2:40.title: Instructions for Setting Up the Tabernacle and Consecrating the Priests
2:40.summary: Yahweh commands Moses to assemble the tabernacle on the first day of the first month, place all its furnishings in proper order, anoint them to make them holy, and consecrate Aaron and his sons as priests for an everlasting priesthood. These instructions establish the sacred space and leadership for Israel's worship.

2:40.title: Moses Completes the Assembly of the Tabernacle
2:40.summary: Moses meticulously follows all of Yahweh's commands, assembling the tabernacle structure, placing every piece of furniture in its designated location, and anointing the priests and all sacred objects. His faithful obedience results in the complete, functional sanctuary ready for Israel's worship.

2:40.title: The Glory of Yahweh Fills the Tabernacle
2:40.summary: The cloud of Yahweh's glory descends upon and fills the completed tabernacle so completely that Moses cannot enter, establishing the tabernacle as the dwelling place of God's presence among Israel. The cloud and fire guide Israel's journeys, signifying God's continuous presence and leadership.

3:1.title: The Lord Calls to Moses
3:1.summary: Yahweh calls to Moses from the Tent of Meeting to deliver instructions about offerings. This introduces the divine source and setting for the regulations that follow.

3:1.title: The Burnt Offering from the Herd
3:1.summary: Instructions for offering a burnt offering from cattle: the worshiper must present a male without blemish, lay hands on its head, and the priests perform the ritual slaughter, blood sprinkling, flaying, and burning to produce a pleasing aroma to the Lord. This offering accomplishes atonement for the one bringing it.

3:1.title: The Burnt Offering from the Flock
3:1.summary: Instructions for offering a burnt offering from sheep or goats, following the same essential procedure as the herd offering with comparable requirements for a male without blemish and priestly ritual actions. The variation accommodates those with smaller livestock.

3:1.title: The Burnt Offering of Birds
3:1.summary: Instructions for the poorest worshipers to offer turtledoves or young pigeons as a burnt offering, with simplified ritual procedures adapted to the small size of birds. This provision ensures all Israelites, regardless of wealth, can make an acceptable burnt offering.

3:2.title: The Basic Meal Offering
3:2.summary: When someone presents a meal offering to Yahweh, it consists of fine flour mixed with oil and frankincense. The priest burns a memorial portion on the altar as a pleasing aroma, while the remainder becomes food for Aaron and his sons as a most holy portion.

3:2.title: Meal Offerings Prepared by Baking and Pan-frying
3:2.summary: Various methods of preparing meal offerings are described: unleavened cakes or wafers baked in an oven, griddle cakes, or pan-prepared offerings, all mixed with oil. The priest presents each to the altar, burns the memorial portion, and the remainder is reserved as a most holy portion for the priests.

3:2.title: Prohibitions and Requirement of Salt in Meal Offerings
3:2.summary: All meal offerings must be prepared without yeast or honey and must be seasoned with salt, which symbolizes the covenant of God. Salt must never be omitted from any offering presented to Yahweh.

3:2.title: The Meal Offering of Firstfruits
3:2.summary: When offering a meal of firstfruits, grain parched with fire and bruised grain from fresh ears are presented with oil and frankincense. The priest burns the memorial portion of the bruised grain and oil with all the frankincense as a pleasing offering to Yahweh.

3:3.title: Peace Offering from the Herd
3:3.summary: Instructions for offering a peace sacrifice from cattle: the animal must be without blemish, presented with a hand-laying ritual, its blood sprinkled on the altar, and specific fat portions burned as a pleasing aroma to Yahweh.

3:3.title: Peace Offering from the Flock (Sheep)
3:3.summary: Instructions for offering a peace sacrifice from sheep or goats: similar procedures apply—hand-laying, blood sprinkling, and burning of fat portions—with the tail fat also included as an offering made by fire.

3:3.title: Peace Offering from Goats
3:3.summary: Instructions for offering a goat as a peace sacrifice follow the same pattern as sheep: the animal is presented without blemish, fat portions are burned for a pleasing aroma, and all fat belongs to Yahweh.

3:3.title: Perpetual Law Against Eating Fat and Blood
3:3.summary: A permanent statute forbids Israelites in all generations from consuming fat or blood, establishing an enduring boundary between what is holy (belonging to God through sacrifice) and what is permitted for human consumption.

3:4.title: Introduction to Sin Offerings
3:4.summary: Yahweh instructs Moses to teach the Israelites about sin offerings for unintentional transgressions against His commandments. This sets the framework for the entire chapter's regulations.

3:4.title: Sin Offering for the Anointed Priest
3:4.summary: When the anointed priest commits an unintentional sin, he must offer a young bull without blemish, bringing its blood into the sanctuary and burning the carcass outside the camp. This ritual purges the guilt that would otherwise affect the entire people.

3:4.title: Sin Offering for the Whole Congregation
3:4.summary: When the entire assembly unintentionally transgresses a commandment, they collectively offer a young bull with the elders laying hands upon it, following the same ritual procedures as for the priest. The whole community receives forgiveness through this corporate sin offering.

3:4.title: Sin Offering for a Ruler
3:4.summary: When a ruler unintentionally sins, he brings a male goat without blemish as his sin offering, following a simplified ritual similar to the communal offering. The priest makes atonement and the ruler receives forgiveness.

3:4.title: Sin Offering for a Common Person
3:4.summary: When an ordinary member of the people commits an unintentional sin, they bring a female goat without blemish, following the established ritual of laying hands, killing, and applying blood to the altar. The priest's atonement grants forgiveness.

3:4.title: Alternative Sin Offering with a Lamb
3:4.summary: As an alternative for those unable to provide a goat, a person may bring a female lamb without blemish for a sin offering, observing the same ritual procedures. This provision ensures all Israelites can obtain atonement regardless of economic means.

3:5.title: Cases of Unintentional Sin
3:5.summary: Four types of unintentional sins are enumerated: failing to report testimony when adjured, touching unclean animals or human uncleanness unknowingly, and making rash oaths. Each sin creates guilt that must be addressed when the person becomes aware of it.

3:5.title: Confession and Lamb Offering
3:5.summary: When guilty of any of these sins, the person must confess and bring a female lamb or goat as a trespass offering, which the priest uses to make atonement.

3:5.title: Offering for the Poor: Doves or Pigeons
3:5.summary: For those unable to afford a lamb, two doves or pigeons may substitute—one for a sin offering and one for a burnt offering—with the priest performing the ritual and pronouncing forgiveness.

3:5.title: Offering for the Poorest: Fine Flour
3:5.summary: For those unable to afford birds, a tenth of an ephah of fine flour serves as a sin offering, with the priest burning a memorial portion and retaining the rest as his portion, extending atonement to the poorest.

3:5.title: Trespass Against Holy Things
3:5.summary: When someone unwittingly wrongs the holy things of Yahweh, they must bring a ram without blemish and restitute the wrong plus one-fifth, with the priest making atonement through the trespass offering.

3:5.title: Guilt and Atonement for Unknown Violations
3:5.summary: Even when someone unknowingly violates Yahweh's commands, they bear guilt and must bring a ram for a trespass offering, which the priest uses to make atonement and secure forgiveness.

3:6.title: Restitution and Atonement for Trespass
3:6.summary: When someone commits fraud or theft against a neighbor, they must fully restore what was taken, add one-fifth more, bring a ram as a trespass offering, and receive priestly atonement for forgiveness. This establishes the protocol for making right economic wrongs and reconciling with God.

3:6.title: The Law of the Burnt Offering
3:6.summary: The burnt offering must remain on the altar fire throughout the night and morning, with the priest maintaining perpetual fire and removing ashes in prescribed garments. The continuous burning symbolizes constant devotion and the priest's holy service.

3:6.title: The Law of the Meal Offering
3:6.summary: A handful of the meal offering with frankincense is burned on the altar as a memorial, while the remainder is eaten by Aaron's sons as unleavened bread in a holy place. This provision sustains the priesthood and sanctifies those who partake of the offering.

3:6.title: The Perpetual Offering of the Anointed Priest
3:6.summary: The high priest must offer a daily meal offering—half in the morning and half in the evening—entirely burned as a sweet aroma to the Lord. Unlike other priests' portions, this offering is wholly consumed and never eaten.

3:6.title: The Law of the Sin Offering
3:6.summary: The sin offering is killed at the burnt offering site and eaten by the officiating priest in a holy place, becoming holy itself; vessels used in preparation are either broken or scoured, and blood brought into the Holy Place means the offering must be burned rather than eaten. This regulations emphasize the extreme holiness of sin atonement.

3:7.title: The Law of the Trespass Offering
3:7.summary: God instructs Moses on the trespass offering procedure: it is most holy, killed where burnt offerings are killed, its blood sprinkled on the altar, its fat portions burned as an offering, and consumed by male priests in a holy place. The trespass offering follows the same law as the sin offering, with the atoning priest receiving the offering.

3:7.title: Priestly Portions from Burnt Offerings and Meal Offerings
3:7.summary: The priest who offers a burnt offering receives the skin; priests who offer meal offerings receive the baked portions, whether mixed with oil or dry, with all of Aaron's sons sharing equally in meal offerings.

3:7.title: Peace Offerings of Thanksgiving
3:7.summary: For a thanksgiving peace offering, the offerer brings unleavened cakes mixed with oil, unleavened wafers, and leavened bread cakes; one cake of each offering goes to the priest who sprinkles the blood, and the meat must be eaten on the day of offering with nothing left until morning.

3:7.title: Vow and Freewill Offerings; Regulations on Cleanliness
3:7.summary: Vow and freewill offerings may be eaten on the day offered and the next day, but any flesh remaining on the third day must be burned; eating flesh on the third day makes the offering unacceptable and the eater bears iniquity. Flesh touched by unclean things is burned, and anyone unclean who eats peace offering flesh shall be cut off from the people.

3:7.title: Prohibition Against Eating Fat and Blood
3:7.summary: Israel is forbidden to eat the fat of bull, sheep, or goat offered as a fire offering; they may not eat any blood of bird or animal anywhere they dwell. Anyone eating such fat or blood shall be cut off from the people.

3:7.title: Priestly Portions from Peace Offerings
3:7.summary: The offerer brings the peace offering with the fat and breast to the priest; the priest burns the fat on the altar while the breast becomes the priest's, and the right thigh goes to the priest as a heave offering. God gives the waved breast and heaved thigh to Aaron and his sons as their perpetual portion from Israel's peace offerings.

3:7.title: Summary: The Anointing Portion of the Priesthood
3:7.summary: The breast and thigh portions constitute the anointing portion for Aaron and his sons from the day they were presented to minister as priests; God commanded this portion be given to them forever throughout their generations. This concludes the laws of burnt offering, meal offering, sin offering, trespass offering, consecration, and peace offerings that God commanded Moses at Mount Sinai.

3:8.title: God's Command to Consecrate Aaron and His Sons
3:8.summary: Yahweh commands Moses to gather Aaron, his sons, consecration materials, and the congregation at the Tent of Meeting. Moses obeys and assembles everyone as instructed.

3:8.title: The Clothing and Dressing of Aaron and His Sons
3:8.summary: Moses washes Aaron and his sons, then dresses them in their priestly garments—coats, sashes, tunics, ephods, breastplate, turban, and holy crown—sanctifying them for service.

3:8.title: The Sin Offering for Aaron and His Sons
3:8.summary: Aaron and his sons lay hands on the bull of the sin offering; Moses kills it, applies the blood to the altar horns, and burns the sacrifice outside the camp to atone for and sanctify them.

3:8.title: The Burnt Offering
3:8.summary: Aaron and his sons lay hands on the ram of burnt offering; Moses kills it, sprinkles the blood, and burns the entire ram as a pleasing aroma to Yahweh.

3:8.title: The Ram of Consecration and Wave Offering
3:8.summary: Moses sacrifices the ram of consecration, marking Aaron and his sons with blood on their ears, thumbs, and toes, then performs a wave offering of fat and bread before Yahweh to complete their dedication.

3:8.title: Final Anointing and Instructions for the Seven-Day Consecration
3:8.summary: Moses anoints Aaron, his sons, and their garments with oil and blood to sanctify them; he instructs them to remain at the Tent of Meeting for seven days, eating the consecration meal and keeping Yahweh's command to avoid death. They obey all that Yahweh commanded.

3:9.title: Moses Commands the Offerings for the Eighth Day
3:9.summary: On the eighth day, Moses instructs Aaron and the elders of Israel to prepare sin offerings, burnt offerings, peace offerings, and a meal offering to present before Yahweh, for on this day Yahweh will appear to them.

3:9.title: Preparation and Instructions for the Offerings
3:9.summary: The congregation brings the offerings before the Tent of Meeting and stands before Yahweh. Moses reminds Aaron that this is what Yahweh commanded and promises that God's glory will appear, then instructs Aaron to offer sacrifices for himself and the people.

3:9.title: Aaron's Sin Offering and Burnt Offering
3:9.summary: Aaron performs the sin offering for himself and the burnt offering according to Yahweh's ordinance, with his sons assisting by presenting blood and handling the proper disposal of parts on and off the altar.

3:9.title: Aaron's Offerings for the People
3:9.summary: Aaron presents the people's sin offering, burnt offering, meal offering, and peace offerings, performing all the prescribed rituals including sprinkling blood, burning fat and organs, and waving the breast and thigh as a wave offering to Yahweh.

3:9.title: The Glory of Yahweh Appears and Consumes the Offerings
3:9.summary: After Aaron blesses the people and both Moses and Aaron emerge from the Tent of Meeting to bless the congregation, the glory of Yahweh appears and divine fire consumes the burnt offering and fat on the altar, causing the people to shout and worship in awe.

3:10.title: Nadab and Abihu Consumed by Fire
3:10.summary: Aaron's sons Nadab and Abihu offer unauthorized fire before the Lord and are consumed by divine fire. Moses explains that God will be shown holy among those who approach Him, and Aaron accepts this judgment in silence.

3:10.title: Removal of the Bodies and Restrictions on Mourning
3:10.summary: Moses directs the removal of Nadab and Abihu's bodies from the sanctuary, and commands Aaron and his remaining sons not to mourn outwardly but to remain at the Tent of Meeting, bearing the Lord's anointing. The surviving priests obey these unusual restrictions to avoid further divine anger.

3:10.title: Laws Regarding Priests' Sobriety and Teaching Duties
3:10.summary: God commands Aaron that priests must abstain from wine and strong drink when entering the Tent of Meeting, establishing this as an eternal statute. This requirement ensures priests can distinguish between holy and common, clean and unclean, and teach Israel God's statutes.

3:10.title: Regulations for Eating Priestly Portions
3:10.summary: Moses instructs the surviving priests regarding their portions from sacrifices: the meal offering must be eaten without yeast in a holy place, and the waved breast and heaved thigh may be eaten in a clean place by priests and their families as their perpetual due. These instructions establish the priests' material provision from the offerings.

3:10.title: Dispute Over the Sin Offering
3:10.summary: Moses discovers the goat of the sin offering was burned rather than eaten and rebukes Eleazar and Ithamar, instructing they should have eaten it in the sanctuary as atonement for the congregation. Aaron defends his sons, noting their grief after witnessing God's judgment, and Moses accepts this explanation as reasonable.

3:11.title: Introduction to Dietary Laws
3:11.summary: God commands Moses and Aaron to tell the Israelites which animals they may eat. The basic principle is established: animals that both part the hoof and chew the cud are clean for consumption.

3:11.title: Unclean Land Animals
3:11.summary: Specific land animals are declared unclean and forbidden for eating because they fail to meet both criteria of parting the hoof and chewing the cud (camel, coney, hare, pig). Contact with their flesh or carcasses is prohibited.

3:11.title: Clean and Unclean Water Creatures
3:11.summary: Water creatures with both fins and scales are clean and may be eaten, while those lacking both are considered abominable and must be avoided, including contact with their carcasses.

3:11.title: Unclean Birds
3:11.summary: A list of specific birds—including eagles, vultures, owls, ravens, herons, storks, and the bat—are declared detestable and forbidden for consumption.

3:11.title: Clean and Unclean Winged Insects
3:11.summary: Flying insects that walk on all fours are abominable, but certain winged insects with legs above their feet for hopping—locusts, katydids, crickets, and grasshoppers—are permitted for eating.

3:11.title: Uncleanness from Touching Unclean Animals
3:11.summary: Contact with or carrying the carcasses of unclean land animals causes ritual uncleanness until evening, requiring washing of clothes. This applies to animals that neither part the hoof nor chew the cud, or those that walk on paws.

3:11.title: Unclean Creeping Things
3:11.summary: Small crawling creatures—weasels, rats, lizards, geckos, and chameleons—are declared unclean. Touching their dead bodies causes uncleanness until evening.

3:11.title: Contamination Rules for Objects and Food
3:11.summary: Objects that dead unclean creatures fall upon become unclean; wooden vessels and textiles must be washed, earthen vessels must be broken, and food or drink touched by unclean creatures is forbidden. However, springs and cisterns remain clean, and dry seeds are unaffected unless water first touches them.

3:11.title: Uncleanness from Clean Animals That Die
3:11.summary: Even animals permitted for eating cause ritual uncleanness if they die of natural causes. Anyone touching or eating such a carcass must wash and remains unclean until evening.

3:11.title: Prohibition of All Creeping Things and Call to Holiness
3:11.summary: All creeping things—whether belly-crawlers, four-footed creatures, or many-footed creatures—are abominable and must not be eaten. The Israelites are called to sanctify themselves and be holy as God is holy, remembering their deliverance from Egypt as the basis for obedience.

3:11.title: Summary and Purpose of Dietary Laws
3:11.summary: The chapter concludes by summarizing the laws governing all animals, birds, water creatures, and creeping things, stating that their purpose is to maintain distinction between clean and unclean, and between creatures that may and may not be eaten.

3:12.title: Purification Laws for Childbirth
3:12.summary: God establishes the period of uncleanness for a woman after childbirth: seven days for a male child (with circumcision on the eighth day) and fourteen days for a female child, followed by extended periods of purification during which the mother cannot touch holy things or enter the sanctuary.

3:12.title: Atonement Offering for Purification
3:12.summary: After completing her purification period, a woman must bring sacrificial offerings to the priest—a lamb for a burnt offering and a bird for a sin offering—or, if unable to afford a lamb, two birds instead; the priest makes atonement and declares her clean.

3:13.title: Introduction to Leprosy Diagnosis
3:13.summary: The Lord instructs Moses and Aaron on how priests are to examine suspected leprosy in the skin. If the hair has turned white and the spot is deeper than the skin surface, the priest pronounces the person unclean.

3:13.title: Examination of a White Spot
3:13.summary: When a bright white spot appears on the skin but is not obviously deeper than the surface and the hair is not white, the priest isolates the person for observation. If after two seven-day periods the spot has not spread and has faded, the person is pronounced clean; if it spreads, the person is declared unclean with leprosy.

3:13.title: Chronic Leprosy and Complete Coverage
3:13.summary: When obvious signs of chronic leprosy appear—white rising, turned hair, and raw flesh—the priest pronounces the person unclean without isolation. However, if leprosy covers the entire skin and turns completely white, the person is paradoxically pronounced clean; but if raw flesh reappears, uncleanness returns. If the raw flesh later turns white, the person is again pronounced clean.

3:13.title: Leprosy Arising from a Boil
3:13.summary: When a white or reddish-white spot develops in the place of a healed boil, the priest examines it to determine if it is leprosy. If white hairs are present and it is deeper than the skin, it is unclean; if not, the priest isolates the person for seven days. If it does not spread, it is merely a scar and the person is clean.

3:13.title: Leprosy Arising from a Burn
3:13.summary: When a bright reddish-white or white spot develops in a burn wound, the priest examines it similarly to other suspected leprosy. If it shows signs of leprosy, the person is unclean; if it is merely the healing burn and does not spread, the person is pronounced clean.

3:13.title: Leprosy of the Head and Beard
3:13.summary: When a plague appears on the head or beard, the priest examines it for signs of leprosy—yellow, thin hair and depth beyond the skin. If the plague is minor, the person is isolated and shaved (except over the itch itself) for observation. If after seven days it has not spread and no yellow hair remains, the person is pronounced clean; if it does spread, the person is unclean. If later black hair grows and the condition heals, the person is again pronounced clean.

3:13.title: Harmless Rash
3:13.summary: When dull white bright spots appear on the body, the priest examines them and determines they are a harmless rash, not leprosy, and the person is clean.

3:13.title: Baldness and Leprosy on the Head
3:13.summary: Natural baldness or forehead baldness alone does not cause uncleanness. However, if a reddish-white plague appears on a bald head or bald forehead and resembles leprosy in the flesh, the priest pronounces the person a leper and unclean.

3:13.title: Social Isolation of the Leper
3:13.summary: The person pronounced unclean with leprosy must wear torn clothes, let their hair hang loose, cover their upper lip, and cry out 'Unclean! Unclean!' to warn others. They must dwell alone outside the camp as long as the plague remains.

3:13.title: Leprosy in Garments and Leather
3:13.summary: When a greenish or reddish plague appears in woolen or linen garments or in leather goods, it is shown to the priest. After seven days of isolation, if the plague has spread, it is a destructive mildew and the garment or leather item must be burned.

3:13.title: Mildewed Garments That May Be Salvaged
3:13.summary: If after examination the plague has not spread in the garment, it is washed and isolated for seven more days. If after washing the plague has faded and has not changed color or spread, it must still be burned. However, if the plague has completely faded after washing, it may be torn out and the item can be washed again and declared clean. If the plague reappears, the item must be burned. This law addresses how to determine if mildewed garments can be saved or must be destroyed.

3:14.title: Cleansing Ritual for Healed Leprosy (First Stage)
3:14.summary: The priest examines a leper who has healed and performs a purification ritual using two clean birds, cedar wood, scarlet yarn, and hyssop. One bird is killed over running water, and the living bird is dipped in its blood before being released, with the priest sprinkling the blood seven times on the healed leper to pronounce him clean.

3:14.title: Personal Cleansing and Waiting Period
3:14.summary: The cleansed leper washes his clothes, shaves all his hair, and bathes in water, then waits seven days outside the camp. On the seventh day, he shaves again and bathes once more to complete his personal purification.

3:14.title: Sacrifice and Anointing for Full Cleansing (Wealthy)
3:14.summary: On the eighth day, the cleansed leper brings prescribed animal and grain offerings before the Lord. The priest offers a trespass offering, applies blood and oil to the leper's right ear, thumb, and toe, then presents sin and burnt offerings, making full atonement to pronounce him completely clean.

3:14.title: Sacrifice and Anointing for Full Cleansing (Poor)
3:14.summary: For those unable to afford the full sacrifice, the priest may accept one lamb for a trespass offering and two birds (doves or pigeons) instead of additional animals. The same anointing ritual is performed with blood and oil on the ear, thumb, and toe, with the priest making atonement before the Lord; this law provides grace for the economically disadvantaged.

3:14.title: Inspection and Treatment of Mildewed Houses
3:14.summary: When mildew appears in a house in Canaan, the priest inspects it after the house is emptied. If the plague persists after seven days or spreads despite repair attempts, the house is torn down. If the plague does not spread after plastering, the priest pronounces the house clean.

3:14.title: Cleansing Ritual for Houses Healed of Mildew
3:14.summary: Using the same purification ritual as for healed lepers—two birds, cedar wood, scarlet yarn, and hyssop—the priest kills one bird over running water and sprinkles the house seven times with its blood and the living bird, then releases the living bird outside the city to complete the atonement and pronounce the house clean.

3:14.title: Summary of Leprosy Laws
3:14.summary: These verses summarize the entire law of leprosy covering various afflictions (leprosy, itch, garment mildew, house mildew, rashes, scabs, and bright spots) and their distinction between unclean and clean conditions, establishing the comprehensive framework for determining ritual purity.

3:15.title: Introduction to Discharge Uncleanness
3:15.summary: God commands Moses and Aaron to instruct Israel about uncleanness caused by bodily discharge. A man with a discharge from his body is unclean whether the discharge is continuous or has stopped.

3:15.title: Laws Regarding a Man's Discharge
3:15.summary: Detailed regulations establish how discharge-uncleanness spreads through contact with beds, sitting places, and persons, requiring washing and bathing; after seven days without discharge plus washing in running water, a man brings two birds for sin and burnt offerings on the eighth day for atonement.

3:15.title: Laws Regarding Seminal Emission
3:15.summary: A man who has an emission of semen must bathe his whole body and is unclean until evening; garments and skin touched by semen must be washed, and if emission occurs during intercourse, both partners must bathe and are unclean until evening.

3:15.title: Laws Regarding Women's Menstrual Impurity
3:15.summary: A woman during her menstrual period is impure for seven days; anything she lies on or sits on becomes unclean, and anyone touching these items or her becomes unclean until evening; a man who has intercourse with her during menstruation becomes unclean for seven days along with his bed.

3:15.title: Laws Regarding Abnormal Bleeding in Women
3:15.summary: A woman with abnormal vaginal bleeding outside her regular period is treated as unclean for the entire duration of discharge; after the bleeding stops, she waits seven days then brings two birds to the priest on the eighth day for sin and burnt offerings to make atonement.

3:15.title: Summary and Purpose of Discharge Laws
3:15.summary: The laws separate Israel from uncleanness so they do not die in their impurity and defile God's tabernacle; the passage concludes by summarizing the laws covering discharges in men and women, seminal emissions, menstruation, and contact with the unclean.

3:16.title: Introduction and Warning about Approaching God's Presence
3:16.summary: After the death of Aaron's sons for approaching God improperly, Yahweh instructs Moses to tell Aaron that he must not enter the Most Holy Place carelessly, for God's presence on the mercy seat is deadly to the unrepentant. This establishes the critical context for the Day of Atonement procedures that follow.

3:16.title: Preparation and the Scapegoat Ritual
3:16.summary: Aaron is instructed to bring specific sacrifices and wear holy linen garments, then to take two goats—one to be sacrificed as a sin offering and one to be the scapegoat—and cast lots to determine their roles. The scapegoat will carry away the people's sins into the wilderness.

3:16.title: Atonement for the Priest and the Holy Place
3:16.summary: Aaron sacrifices the bull for his own sins, then uses incense and blood to make atonement first within the Most Holy Place before the mercy seat, and then for the entire sanctuary and altar, cleansing them from the uncleanness of the people. This restores the holiness of God's dwelling place.

3:16.title: The Scapegoat Bears Away Israel's Sins
3:16.summary: After completing atonement for the sanctuary, Aaron confesses all of Israel's sins over the live goat, transferring them to the animal, which is then sent into the wilderness to bear away the people's iniquities. This symbolic act represents complete removal of sin.

3:16.title: Completion of the Day of Atonement Rites
3:16.summary: Aaron removes his holy garments, bathes, dons regular garments, and offers burnt offerings for himself and the people; the sin offerings are carried outside the camp and burned, and those involved in these final rituals undergo purification. This concludes the sacred atonement ceremony.

3:16.title: The Day of Atonement as Perpetual Statute
3:16.summary: God establishes that on the tenth day of the seventh month, all Israel must afflict their souls and refrain from work so that atonement may be made for them yearly, cleansing them from all sins; this law applies to both native-born and foreigners and is to be observed forever. The passage concludes by affirming that these commands were carried out as Yahweh instructed.

3:17.title: Laws on Sacrificial Slaughter and Prohibition of Goat Idols
3:17.summary: God commands that all slaughter of livestock must occur at the Tent of Meeting as a sacrifice to the Lord, not in the open field or for idolatry. Anyone who kills an animal outside this prescribed manner and fails to bring it to the priest for sacrifice will be cut off from the people, and the people must cease sacrificing to goat idols.

3:17.title: Universal Application to Israelites and Foreigners
3:17.summary: The law against offering sacrifices outside the Tent of Meeting applies equally to native Israelites and foreign residents living among them; violators will be cut off from the people.

3:17.title: Prohibition Against Eating Blood
3:17.summary: God forbids all people—both Israelites and foreigners—from consuming blood because the blood is the life force given by God to atone for souls on the altar. This absolute prohibition reflects blood's sacred role in atonement.

3:17.title: Proper Handling of Hunted Animals
3:17.summary: When hunting animals or birds for food, the hunter must pour out the blood and cover it with dust, honoring the life principle in blood; eating blood incurs the penalty of being cut off from the people.

3:17.title: Uncleanness from Eating Carrion or Torn Animals
3:17.summary: Anyone who eats animals that die naturally or are torn by predators becomes unclean until evening after washing clothes and bathing; failure to purify oneself incurs guilt.

3:18.title: Introduction: God's Authority and the Call to Obedience
3:18.summary: The Lord commands Moses to instruct Israel not to follow the practices of Egypt or Canaan, but instead to observe God's ordinances and statutes, which give life to those who keep them. This establishes the divine authority and purpose behind the laws that follow.

3:18.title: Laws Against Sexual Relations with Close Relatives
3:18.summary: God prohibits sexual relations with immediate and extended family members, including parents, siblings, children, grandchildren, aunts, uncles, in-laws, and multiple wives, establishing clear boundaries to protect family integrity and prevent perversion. These laws define acceptable sexual conduct within the kinship structure.

3:18.title: Laws Against Other Sexual Abominations
3:18.summary: God forbids sexual relations during menstruation, adultery with a neighbor's wife, child sacrifice to Molech, homosexual relations, and bestiality, identifying these as perversions and defilements that profane His name. Each prohibition addresses a specific form of sexual or idolatrous misconduct.

3:18.title: Warning: Consequences of Defiling the Land
3:18.summary: Moses warns that the nations being displaced were defiled by these very abominations, causing the land to vomit them out; Israel must keep God's statutes to avoid the same judgment and defilement, with violators being cut off from their people. This concludes the laws with a sobering reminder of covenant consequences.

3:19.title: Call to Holiness and Rejection of Idolatry
3:19.summary: God commands Moses to tell all Israel to be holy as He is holy, and instructs them to honor parents, keep the Sabbath, and reject idols. This foundational call establishes that holiness requires both reverence for God and obedience to His core commands.

3:19.title: Rules for Peace Offering Sacrifices
3:19.summary: God prescribes regulations for peace offerings, requiring they be eaten within two days and burned if left until the third day; anyone who eats it on the third day profanes the holy thing and bears guilt. This teaches proper respect for sacred offerings and the consequences of ritual negligence.

3:19.title: Leaving Harvest Gleanings for the Poor
3:19.summary: God commands that harvesters leave the corners of fields and fallen fruit unharvested for the poor and foreigners, demonstrating God's concern for the vulnerable. This law embeds compassion and social justice into the economic practices of God's people.

3:19.title: Laws Governing Honesty, Justice, and Love of Neighbor
3:19.summary: God prohibits stealing, lying, deception, false oaths, oppression, wage-theft, slander, and hatred; He commands fair judgment, rebuke of sin, rejection of vengeance, and love of neighbor as oneself. These laws form the moral and relational core of Israel's community life, rooted in fear of God and respect for human dignity.

3:19.title: Laws on Mixing, Sexual Sin, and Atonement
3:19.summary: God forbids crossbreeding animals, sowing mixed seeds, and wearing mixed-material garments; He also prescribes atonement for a man who has sexual relations with a pledged slave woman through a ram offering that the priest presents for forgiveness. These laws teach separation of kinds and provide restoration for sexual transgression.

3:19.title: Fruit Trees and the Fourth-Year Consecration
3:19.summary: When Israel plants fruit trees, the fruit is forbidden for three years; in the fourth year it is holy for praising God, and from the fifth year onward it may be eaten for increased yield. This law teaches patience, consecration, and proper ordering of new agricultural blessings.

3:19.title: Prohibitions on Eating Blood, Sorcery, and Body Mutilation
3:19.summary: God forbids eating meat with blood in it, practicing enchantments or sorcery, cutting hair at the sides of the head, clipping beards, and making cuttings or tattoos in flesh for the dead. These prohibitions distinguish Israel's practices from surrounding pagan customs and protect against spiritual defilement.

3:19.title: Protection of Family Dignity and Rejection of Divination
3:19.summary: God forbids causing daughters to become prostitutes lest the land become profaned with wickedness, commands keeping the Sabbath and revering the sanctuary, and prohibits consulting mediums and wizards. These laws protect family honor, maintain ritual sanctity, and prevent spiritual contamination.

3:19.title: Honor for the Elderly and Justice for Foreigners
3:19.summary: God commands rising before the elderly to show honor, and treats resident foreigners as native-born, commanding love for them as oneself based on Israel's own experience of slavery in Egypt. These laws embed compassion for vulnerable populations into Israel's identity and memory.

3:19.title: Just Weights and Measures, and Final Command to Obey
3:19.summary: God requires honest scales, weights, and measures in all commerce as a mark of righteousness, reminding Israel that He alone is their God who delivered them from Egypt, and calls them to keep all His statutes and ordinances. This conclusion emphasizes that integrity in daily life reflects trust in God and loyalty to the covenant.

3:20.title: Prohibition of Child Sacrifice to Molech and Call to Holiness
3:20.summary: God commands Israel to execute anyone who sacrifices children to Molech and to punish the community if they fail to do so. God calls the people to sanctify themselves and be holy, establishing the foundational principle of separation from idolatry.

3:20.title: Laws Against Sexual Immorality and Perversion
3:20.summary: God establishes severe penalties for various sexual violations including cursing parents, adultery, incest in multiple forms, same-sex relations, and bestiality, all punishable by death or being cut off from the community. These laws aim to preserve family boundaries and sexual order within Israel.

3:20.title: Call to Obedience and Separation from Pagan Nations
3:20.summary: God exhorts Israel to keep all His statutes so the promised land will not reject them as it did the nations before them, and commands them to distinguish between clean and unclean things as a sign of their holiness and separation from other peoples. God's holiness demands that His people reflect His character through ritual and moral purity.

3:20.title: Execution of Mediums and Wizards
3:20.summary: God commands that men and women who are mediums or wizards shall be put to death by stoning, reinforcing the prohibition against divination and contact with the spiritual realm outside of God's ordained means.

3:21.title: Priests' Purity Regarding the Dead
3:21.summary: Priests are forbidden to defile themselves by contact with the dead, except for their nearest relatives. Even then, a priest who is a leader among his people must not profane himself by such contact.

3:21.title: Physical Marks of Priestly Holiness
3:21.summary: Priests must not shave their heads or beards in mourning or make cuts in their flesh, as they are holy to God and bear his name through offering sacrifices. Their sanctification reflects God's own holiness.

3:21.title: A Priest's Daughter Who Prostitutes Herself
3:21.summary: If a priest's daughter defiles herself through prostitution, she brings shame on her father and shall be burned with fire as punishment.

3:21.title: Special Laws for the High Priest
3:21.summary: The high priest has stricter requirements than other priests: he cannot mourn the dead even for parents, must remain in the sanctuary, and must marry only a virgin of his own people to preserve his sanctified lineage.

3:21.title: Physical Blemishes Disqualifying Priests from Offering
3:21.summary: Priests with physical blemishes—blindness, lameness, deformities, skin diseases, or damaged testicles—cannot approach the altar to offer sacrifices, though they may eat holy food. Their blemishes would profane God's sanctuaries.

3:21.title: Moses Communicates the Laws
3:21.summary: Moses relays all these priestly regulations to Aaron, his sons, and the children of Israel.

3:22.title: Priestly Purity Requirements for Eating Holy Things
3:22.summary: God commands Aaron and his sons to maintain ritual purity when handling the holy things consecrated to God, specifying various sources of uncleanness that disqualify priests from eating the holy offerings until they are cleansed. Violations of these requirements result in death, as God alone sanctifies the priesthood.

3:22.title: Who May and May Not Eat the Holy Offerings
3:22.summary: God specifies that only authorized persons—priests, their households, and certain family members—may eat the holy offerings, while foreigners, hired servants, and married priests' daughters are excluded unless specific conditions are met. Priests must guard the holiness of these offerings to avoid bringing guilt upon themselves and the people.

3:22.title: Requirements for Acceptable Sacrificial Animals
3:22.summary: God requires that all burnt offerings and peace offerings be without blemish or defect to be acceptable, with detailed specifications about what deformities disqualify an animal, though certain defective animals may be offered as freewill offerings but not as vows. No animal offered for sacrifice should come from a foreigner, as their defiled status affects the offering.
themes: distinction between vows and freewill offerings

3:22.title: Laws on Age of Sacrificial Animals and Thanksgiving Offerings
3:22.summary: God establishes that sacrificial animals must be at least eight days old and prohibits slaughtering an animal with its young on the same day, while thanksgiving offerings must be eaten the same day they are sacrificed. The passage concludes with God's reminder to keep His commandments and not profane His holy name, identifying Himself as the God who brought Israel out of Egypt.

3:23.title: Introduction to the Appointed Feasts
3:23.summary: Yahweh commands Moses to instruct Israel about the set feasts—holy convocations appointed by God. The seventh day is established as a Sabbath of rest in which no work shall be done.

3:23.title: Passover and Feast of Unleavened Bread
3:23.summary: Yahweh's Passover occurs on the fourteenth day of the first month in the evening, followed by the seven-day Feast of Unleavened Bread with holy convocations on the first and seventh days and daily fire offerings. These commemorative feasts mark Israel's deliverance from Egypt.

3:23.title: Feast of First Fruits and Weeks
3:23.summary: The priest waves a sheaf of first fruits before Yahweh with a lamb offering, followed seven weeks later by the Feast of Weeks with bread, multiple animals sacrificed, and a requirement to leave field corners for the poor and foreigners. These practices express gratitude for harvest and ensure justice for the vulnerable.

3:23.title: Feast of Trumpets
3:23.summary: On the first day of the seventh month, Israel shall observe a day of solemn rest and memorial marked by the blowing of trumpets as a holy convocation with fire offerings to Yahweh. This festival signals the beginning of the sacred fall season.

3:23.title: Day of Atonement
3:23.summary: On the tenth day of the seventh month, the Day of Atonement is observed with complete rest and self-affliction; whoever does not deny themselves or works shall be cut off from the people, as Yahweh makes atonement for Israel. This most solemn observance runs from evening to evening and is eternally binding.

3:23.title: Feast of Tents (Tabernacles)
3:23.summary: Beginning on the fifteenth day of the seventh month, Israel observes a seven-day Feast of Tents with holy convocations and offerings, requiring the people to dwell in booths and carry branches to rejoice before Yahweh. This commemorates God's provision during the wilderness wandering and is an eternal statute.

3:23.title: Moses Declares the Appointed Feasts
3:23.summary: Moses announces to the children of Israel all the appointed feasts of Yahweh that have been commanded, concluding the chapter with the proclamation of the full festival calendar.

3:24.title: The Oil for the Lampstand
3:24.summary: The Lord commands the Israelites to bring pure olive oil so that Aaron may keep the lamps on the gold lampstand burning continually before the Lord as a perpetual statute throughout their generations.

3:24.title: The Bread of the Presence
3:24.summary: The Lord prescribes that twelve cakes of fine flour with frankincense be arranged on the gold table as a memorial offering, to be renewed every Sabbath and eaten by Aaron and his sons in a holy place as an everlasting covenant.

3:24.title: The Blasphemer Stoned
3:24.summary: A half-Israelite man blasphemes the Lord's name during a quarrel in the camp; Moses seeks God's direction and receives the command that the entire congregation must stone him to death, establishing the law that whoever curses God shall be put to death.

3:24.title: Laws of Retaliation and Justice
3:24.summary: The Lord establishes the principle of proportional justice—life for life, fracture for fracture, eye for eye—applying equally to native-born and foreigner, ensuring one standard of law under God.

3:24.title: Execution of the Blasphemer
3:24.summary: Moses relays God's command to the congregation, and they bring the blasphemer outside the camp and execute him by stoning, demonstrating obedience to the Lord's judgment.

3:25.title: The Sabbath Year for the Land
3:25.summary: God commands Israel to observe a Sabbath rest for the land every seventh year, during which fields and vineyards are not worked; the land's natural growth provides food for all people and animals. This practice demonstrates trust in God's provision and honors the land as belonging ultimately to the Lord.

3:25.title: The Year of Jubilee
3:25.summary: After counting seven Sabbath cycles (49 years), the fiftieth year is proclaimed as Jubilee, marked by the trumpet blast on the Day of Atonement; all property returns to original owners, debts are released, and enslaved Israelites are freed to return to their families. God promises abundant harvests in the sixth year to sustain the people through the fallow years.

3:25.title: Redemption of Land
3:25.summary: Land cannot be sold permanently since it belongs to God; Israelites are His tenants with rights to redeem sold property through kinsman-redeemers or by personal prosperity, with all sales ultimately released in the Year of Jubilee. This law prevents permanent disenfranchisement and ensures ongoing connection to ancestral inheritance.

3:25.title: Redemption of Houses and Levite Property
3:25.summary: Houses in walled cities may be redeemed only within one year of sale and are not released in Jubilee, whereas village houses are treated like fields and released in Jubilee; Levite houses can be redeemed at any time and are released in Jubilee, while Levite village fields cannot be sold permanently.

3:25.title: Care for the Poor Israelite
3:25.summary: When an Israelite becomes impoverished, the community must support him as a resident alien without charging interest on loans or profit on food, fearing God and remembering His deliverance from Egypt and gift of the land. This law establishes a moral obligation of generosity rooted in gratitude.

3:25.title: Treatment of Hebrew Servants
3:25.summary: An impoverished Israelite who sells himself into servitude must be treated as a hired worker, not a slave, and must be released in the Year of Jubilee with his family and returned to his inheritance; only foreign slaves from surrounding nations can be held permanently as hereditary property. This law protects Israelite freedom and dignity.

3:25.title: Redemption of an Israelite Sold into Foreign Service
3:25.summary: If an impoverished Israelite sells himself to a foreigner, he may be redeemed by a relative or by himself if he gains wealth; the redemption price is calculated by years remaining until Jubilee based on hired-servant rates, and he shall be released in Jubilee. The passage concludes by affirming that Israelites belong to God alone as His servants, whom He freed from Egypt.

3:26.title: Prohibition of Idolatry and Call to Reverence
3:26.summary: God forbids the Israelites from making idols or carved images and commands them to keep His Sabbaths and revere His sanctuary. These foundational requirements establish proper worship and relationship with God.

3:26.title: Blessings for Obedience
3:26.summary: God promises abundant blessings—plentiful harvests, peace, safety from enemies, supernatural military victory, population growth, and His covenant presence—if the people keep His statutes and commandments. These blessings represent God's faithfulness and the fruit of covenant loyalty.

3:26.title: First Tier of Curses for Disobedience
3:26.summary: If Israel rejects God's commandments and breaks the covenant, He will bring disease, agricultural failure, military defeat, and oppression by enemies. These initial consequences demonstrate God's just response to covenant violation.

3:26.title: Second Tier of Curses—Escalated Punishment
3:26.summary: If Israel continues to rebel despite initial warnings, God will send wild animals, plague, and severe famine that forces radical rationing of bread. The progressive nature of these curses shows God's patience and repeated opportunity for repentance.

3:26.title: Third Tier of Curses—Ultimate Destruction
3:26.summary: If Israel still refuses to obey, God will bring sword, pestilence, destruction of cities and sanctuaries, and scattering among the nations. These severest curses represent the complete dissolution of Israel's covenant security and sovereignty.

3:26.title: The Land's Rest and Israel's Exile
3:26.summary: While Israel is scattered in exile, the land will finally enjoy the Sabbath rest it was denied during their occupation, and the remnant will waste away in their enemies' lands. This passage shows how judgment extends even to the land and reflects ongoing punishment.

3:26.title: Restoration Through Repentance
3:26.summary: If the exiled people confess their sins and accept punishment, God will remember His ancient covenants with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, will not utterly reject them, and will restore them. God's mercy ultimately prevails over judgment for those who repent.

3:26.title: Conclusion: Covenant Ratification
3:26.summary: This verse concludes the legal code of Leviticus by affirming that these statutes, ordinances, and laws were formally established as God's covenant with Israel at Mount Sinai through Moses. It marks the authoritative completion of the legal instruction.

3:27.title: Valuation of Persons Dedicated by Vow
3:27.summary: Yahweh instructs Moses on how to value people who are vowed to the Lord, with different amounts assigned by age and gender. Those too poor to pay the set valuation may be assessed by the priest according to their ability.

3:27.title: Valuation of Unclean Animals and Redemption
3:27.summary: Animals vowed to Yahweh become holy and cannot be exchanged; if an unclean animal is vowed, the priest values it, and the person may redeem it by adding one-fifth to its valuation.

3:27.title: Dedication and Redemption of Houses
3:27.summary: When a house is dedicated to Yahweh, the priest evaluates its value; the dedicator may redeem it by paying the valuation plus one-fifth.

3:27.title: Dedication and Redemption of Fields
3:27.summary: Fields are valued by the seed needed to sow them; if dedicated after the Jubilee, the value is reduced proportionally. Unredeemed fields become priestly property at Jubilee, and fields purchased from others revert to original owners at Jubilee regardless of dedication.

3:27.title: Firstborn Animals and Standard Weights
3:27.summary: All valuations use the sanctuary shekel; firstborn animals already belong to Yahweh and cannot be dedicated, though unclean firstborn may be redeemed plus one-fifth or sold according to valuation.

3:27.title: Devoted Things and Devoted Persons
3:27.summary: Anything completely devoted to Yahweh is most holy and cannot be sold or redeemed; a person who is devoted to destruction must be put to death and cannot be ransomed.

3:27.title: Tithing Commandments
3:27.summary: All tithes of land and herds belong to Yahweh and are holy; if redeemed, one-fifth is added to the value. Tithes cannot be exchanged or inspected for quality and become holy if changed.

3:27.title: Closing Formula
3:27.summary: A concluding statement affirming that all these commandments were spoken by Yahweh to Moses at Mount Sinai for the children of Israel.

4:1.title: God Commands the Census
4:1.summary: Yahweh commands Moses to take a census of all male Israelites aged twenty and older who are able to go to war, with one leader from each tribe to assist in the numbering.

4:1.title: The Twelve Tribal Leaders Appointed
4:1.summary: The names of the twelve tribal leaders are listed—one from each of the tribes of Israel—who will assist Moses and Aaron in conducting the census.

4:1.title: The Census is Conducted
4:1.summary: Moses and Aaron assemble the congregation and number all the males from twenty years old and upward by families and fathers' houses, exactly as Yahweh commanded.

4:1.title: Census Results for the Twelve Tribes
4:1.summary: The individual census totals are recorded for each of the twelve tribes: Reuben (46,500), Simeon (59,300), Gad (45,650), Judah (64,600), Issachar (54,400), Zebulun (57,400), Ephraim (40,500), Manasseh (32,200), Benjamin (35,400), Dan (62,700), Asher (41,500), and Naphtali (53,400).

4:1.title: Total Census Count
4:1.summary: The grand total of all numbered Israelites—counted by the twelve tribal leaders—is six hundred and three thousand five hundred and fifty men able to go to war.

4:1.title: The Levites Excluded and Assigned Duties
4:1.summary: The Levites are excluded from the military census and are instead appointed by God to care for the Tabernacle, its furnishings, and to set up and take down the sanctuary as the people move, encamping around it to protect the congregation from God's wrath.

4:1.title: Israel Obeys God's Instructions
4:1.summary: The children of Israel complete the census and organize themselves according to all that Yahweh commanded through Moses.

4:2.title: God's Command for Camp Organization
4:2.summary: The Lord instructs Moses and Aaron that the Israelites shall camp organized by tribes around the Tent of Meeting, each under their own standard and banner.

4:2.title: The Eastern Camp: Judah, Issachar, and Zebulun
4:2.summary: The three tribes positioned on the east side—Judah (led by Nahshon), Issachar (led by Nethanel), and Zebulun (led by Eliab)—are listed with their numbers totaling 186,400, and they are designated to set out first.

4:2.title: The Southern Camp: Reuben, Simeon, and Gad
4:2.summary: The three tribes positioned on the south side—Reuben (led by Elizur), Simeon (led by Shelumiel), and Gad (led by Eliasaph)—are listed with their numbers totaling 151,450, and they are designated to set out second.

4:2.title: The Levites in the Center
4:2.summary: The Levites and the Tent of Meeting shall travel in the center of the camps, maintaining their position as the camps encamp and set out.

4:2.title: The Western Camp: Ephraim, Manasseh, and Benjamin
4:2.summary: The three tribes positioned on the west side—Ephraim (led by Elishama), Manasseh (led by Gamaliel), and Benjamin (led by Abidan)—are listed with their numbers totaling 108,100, and they are designated to set out third.

4:2.title: The Northern Camp: Dan, Asher, and Naphtali
4:2.summary: The three tribes positioned on the north side—Dan (led by Ahiezer), Asher (led by Pagiel), and Naphtali (led by Ahira)—are listed with their numbers totaling 157,600, and they are designated to set out last.

4:2.title: Summary and Obedience
4:2.summary: The total count of all numbered Israelites is 603,550 (excluding the Levites, who were not numbered as commanded), and the people obey God's organizational instructions perfectly, encamping and setting out according to the Lord's word.

4:3.title: Aaron's Sons and Priestly Succession
4:3.summary: The record of Aaron and Moses begins with the naming of Aaron's four sons. Nadab and Abihu died for offering unauthorized fire before the Lord, leaving Eleazar and Ithamar to serve as priests under their father Aaron.

4:3.title: The Levites Appointed to Serve
4:3.summary: God commands Moses to bring the tribe of Levi near and dedicate them to assist Aaron and his sons in tabernacle service and maintaining God's requirements. The Levites are wholly given to Aaron on behalf of Israel, and only the appointed priests may approach the sanctuary.

4:3.title: The Levites as Redemption for the Firstborn
4:3.summary: God declares that he has taken the Levites instead of all the firstborn of Israel as his own, because all firstborn belong to him from the time he struck down Egypt's firstborn and consecrated Israel's firstborn to himself.

4:3.title: Census and Duties of the Gershonites
4:3.summary: Moses counts the male Levites from one month old and upward, organizing them by their three ancestral families: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari. The Gershonites, numbering 7,500 under Eliasaph, encamp west of the tabernacle and are responsible for the tent structure, coverings, and court furnishings.

4:3.title: Census and Duties of the Kohathites
4:3.summary: The Kohathites number 8,600 males under Elizaphan and encamp on the south side of the tabernacle. They are responsible for the most sacred items—the ark, table, lampstand, altars, and sanctuary vessels—with Eleazar the priest overseeing them.

4:3.title: Census and Duties of the Merarites
4:3.summary: The Merarites number 6,200 males under Zuriel and encamp on the north side of the tabernacle. They are responsible for the structural elements—boards, bars, pillars, sockets, and cords. The total Levite census is 22,000 males from one month old and upward, with Moses and Aaron positioned east before the Tent of Meeting.

4:3.title: Census of Israel's Firstborn Males
4:3.summary: God commands Moses to count all firstborn males of Israel from one month old and upward. The count totals 22,273, which exceeds the number of Levites (22,000), creating a redemption requirement.

4:3.title: Redemption of the Excess Firstborn
4:3.summary: God instructs Moses to use the Levites as substitutes for Israel's firstborn, but since there are 273 more firstborn than Levites, each excess firstborn must be redeemed with five shekels per person. Moses collects 1,365 shekels and gives the redemption money to Aaron and his sons.

4:4.title: Census Instructions for the Kohathites
4:4.summary: Yahweh commands Moses and Aaron to conduct a census of the Kohathite Levites between ages thirty and fifty who are qualified to serve in the Tent of Meeting. This establishes the framework for numbering those eligible for sacred service.

4:4.title: Duties of the Kohathites
4:4.summary: The Kohathites are assigned the sacred responsibility of transporting the most holy objects of the tabernacle—the ark, table of show bread, lampstand, golden altar, and other sanctuary vessels—but they must never look upon these items under penalty of death. Aaron and his sons oversee the covering of these objects before transport, while the Kohathites carry only what has been properly veiled.

4:4.title: Eleazar's Duties and Kohathite Restrictions
4:4.summary: Eleazar the priest is assigned oversight of the sanctuary's oil, incense, meal offerings, and anointing oil, while Aaron and his sons are commanded to personally assign each Kohathite his specific task and burden; the Kohathites are strictly forbidden to even glimpse the holy sanctuary on pain of death.

4:4.title: Census and Duties of the Gershonites
4:4.summary: A census is taken of Gershonite Levites aged thirty to fifty, who are assigned the duty of transporting the tabernacle's curtains, tent coverings, screens, and courtyard hangings; their service is overseen by Ithamar the priest under the direction of Aaron and his sons.

4:4.title: Census and Duties of the Merarites
4:4.summary: A census is taken of Merarite Levites aged thirty to fifty, who are assigned the heavy labor of transporting the tabernacle's framework—boards, bars, pillars, sockets, and courtyard structures; Ithamar the priest oversees their service.

4:4.title: Census Results and Summary
4:4.summary: The actual numbering is conducted: Kohathites number 2,750; Gershonites number 2,630; Merarites number 3,200, for a total of 8,580 Levites eligible for service, all counted according to Yahweh's command through Moses. This census fulfills the organizational structure for tabernacle service.

4:5.title: Removal of the Unclean from the Camp
4:5.summary: God commands Moses to remove from the Israelite camp all those with leprosy, bodily discharges, or contact with the dead, so that God's dwelling place remains holy. The Israelites obey this command completely.

4:5.title: Restitution for Sins Against Others
4:5.summary: God establishes the law of restitution: when someone wrongs another, they must confess, repay the full amount plus one-fifth, or if no kinsman exists to receive it, the payment goes to the priest along with a guilt offering. All holy gifts brought to the priest belong to him.

4:5.title: The Law of the Jealous Husband (Sotah)
4:5.summary: God prescribes a ritual for a husband suspicious of his wife's fidelity when adultery is suspected but unproven: the priest administers a curse-oath with bitter water that will cause physical affliction if she is guilty, or grant her fertility if she is innocent. The husband is freed from wrongdoing; the guilty wife bears the consequences.

4:6.title: The Nazirite Vow: Rules of Separation
4:6.summary: God instructs Moses on the Nazirite vow, a special consecration where a person (male or female) separates themselves to the Lord by abstaining from wine and grape products, allowing their hair to grow uncut, and avoiding contact with the dead. This separation marks them as holy to the Lord throughout the duration of their vow.

4:6.title: Defilement and Restoration of the Nazirite
4:6.summary: If a Nazirite becomes defiled by accidental contact with a dead body, they must shave their head, present prescribed offerings to atone for the defilement, and begin their vow period anew—their previous days of separation are voided. This shows how holiness can be lost and restored through proper restitution.

4:6.title: Completion of the Nazirite Vow and Offerings
4:6.summary: Upon completing their vow period, a Nazirite presents specific offerings (burnt, sin, and peace offerings) along with unleavened bread, shaves their head at the Tent of Meeting, and burns their hair on the peace offering. The priest then performs a wave offering with portions of the sacrifice, after which the Nazirite is released from restrictions and may drink wine again.

4:6.title: The Priestly Blessing
4:6.summary: God commands Aaron and his sons to bless the Israelites with a threefold blessing: may the Lord bless and keep them, make his face shine upon them with grace, and lift his face toward them to give them peace. Through this blessing, the priests invoke God's name on Israel and secure his favor.

4:7.title: The Levites Receive Wagons and Oxen for Tabernacle Service
4:7.summary: After Moses completes the tabernacle's dedication and anointing, the tribal princes offer wagons and oxen for transporting the sanctuary. Moses distributes them to the Levites according to their service responsibilities, with the Kohathites receiving none because they carry the sacred objects on their shoulders.

4:7.title: Nahshon's Dedication Offering for the Altar
4:7.summary: The princes present individual offerings for the dedication of the altar over twelve days, beginning with Nahshon of Judah, whose comprehensive offering includes silver vessels, incense, burnt offerings, a sin offering, and peace offerings.

4:7.title: The Twelve Princes' Dedication Offerings
4:7.summary: Each of the twelve tribal princes presents an identical offering on successive days (Nethanel, Eliab, Elizur, Shelumiel, Eliasaph, Elishama, Gamaliel, Abidan, Ahiezer, Pagiel, and Ahira), establishing the pattern of unity and equal participation in the altar's consecration.

4:7.title: Summary of the Complete Dedication Offerings
4:7.summary: A comprehensive tally records all gifts from the twelve princes for the altar dedication: twelve silver platters and bowls, twelve golden ladles, and numerous sacrificial animals, totaling a substantial communal investment in the sanctuary's consecration.

4:7.title: Moses Hears God's Voice from the Mercy Seat
4:7.summary: When Moses enters the Tent of Meeting, he hears the voice of the Lord speaking from above the mercy seat between the two cherubim, confirming God's presence and readiness to communicate with His servant.

4:8.title: The Lampstand Instructions and Execution
4:8.summary: Yahweh instructs Moses to tell Aaron how to light the seven lamps of the lampstand so they illuminate the area in front of it. Aaron obeys the command, and the passage describes the lampstand's construction as beaten gold work made according to the pattern Yahweh showed Moses.

4:8.title: The Consecration of the Levites
4:8.summary: Yahweh commands Moses to take the Levites from Israel, cleanse them through ritual purification, and present them to the congregation as a wave offering before Aaron. The Levites are consecrated to serve in the Tent of Meeting as substitutes for Israel's firstborn, given to Aaron and his sons to perform the sanctuary service and make atonement for the people.

4:8.title: The Levites' Dedication Ceremony Completed
4:8.summary: Moses, Aaron, and all Israel execute the consecration ceremony exactly as Yahweh commanded, with the Levites purifying themselves and Aaron offering them as a wave offering and making atonement for them. The Levites then begin their service in the Tent of Meeting under Aaron and his sons.

4:8.title: The Age Requirements for Levitical Service
4:8.summary: Yahweh specifies that Levites shall enter active service in the Tent of Meeting from age twenty-five, and at age fifty they shall retire from service work but continue to assist their brothers with duties that do not require active labor.

4:9.title: The Passover Celebration in the Wilderness
4:9.summary: The Lord commands Moses that the Israelites observe the Passover in the first month of their second year in the wilderness, and they obey by celebrating it on the fourteenth day in the evening. This commemorates God's deliverance from Egypt and establishes the ongoing observance of this appointed festival.

4:9.title: Provision for the Unclean and the Foreigner
4:9.summary: Men who are ceremonially unclean due to contact with a dead body ask Moses why they cannot participate in the Passover; God responds by permitting them to observe the Passover a month later (the second month) while maintaining all the festival's requirements, and extends the same provision to foreign residents. This demonstrates God's grace and inclusion while preserving the holiness of the ordinance.

4:9.title: The Cloud as God's Guidance and Presence
4:9.summary: The cloud that covers the tabernacle serves as the visible sign of God's presence—appearing as fire at night—and guides all Israel's movements; whenever the cloud lifts they travel, and whenever it settles they camp, with the duration varying from days to months to years. This pattern of divine guidance teaches the Israelites complete dependence on God's direction and timing.

4:10.title: Instructions for the Silver Trumpets
4:10.summary: God commands Moses to make two silver trumpets for calling the congregation and signaling the camps' movements, with specific blast patterns for gathering, marching, and warfare. The priests are to sound them as a perpetual statute, and they will serve as a memorial before God during worship and festivals.

4:10.title: The Departure from Sinai and the Order of March
4:10.summary: The cloud lifts from the tabernacle in the second year, and Israel begins their journey from Sinai toward Canaan with the cloud guiding them, marching in a prescribed order: the tribes of Judah, Reuben, and Ephraim advance in sequence with their leaders, the Levites transport the tabernacle, and the tribe of Dan brings up the rear. This demonstrates Israel's organized departure and obedience to God's established structure.

4:10.title: Moses Invites Hobab to Join the Journey
4:10.summary: Moses asks his father-in-law Hobab to accompany Israel to the promised land, valuing his desert knowledge and promising him a share in God's blessings. Though Hobab initially refuses, Moses persuades him by emphasizing their mutual benefit and God's favor toward Israel.

4:10.title: The Journey Begins with God's Presence
4:10.summary: Israel departs from Mount Sinai with the ark of the covenant leading them three days ahead to find resting places, while the cloud overshadows them by day and Moses pronounces blessings invoking God's presence to scatter enemies and grant rest to Israel. This passage affirms God's intimate guidance and protection throughout their wilderness journey.

4:11.title: Fire at Taberah
4:11.summary: The people complain to the Lord, and His anger is kindled, sending fire to consume the outskirts of the camp. Moses intercedes in prayer, and the fire is extinguished; the place is named Taberah after this event.

4:11.title: The People's Craving for Meat
4:11.summary: The Israelites weep and long for the meat, fish, and vegetables they ate in Egypt, despising the manna despite its divine provision. Their ingratitude provokes both God's anger and Moses's displeasure.

4:11.title: Moses's Despair
4:11.summary: Overwhelmed by the burden of leading the people, Moses protests to God and asks to be killed rather than face this impossible responsibility. He expresses his anguish and sense of inadequacy for the task.

4:11.title: God's Solution: The Seventy Elders and Flesh
4:11.summary: God commands Moses to gather seventy elders to share the burden of leadership through God's Spirit, and promises the people meat for an entire month as judgment for their complaint and rejection of Him. The abundance of flesh will become loathsome to them.

4:11.title: Moses Questions God's Ability
4:11.summary: Moses expresses doubt about whether sufficient flocks and herds could feed six hundred thousand men for a month. God responds by asking if His hand has become short and asserts that His word will be fulfilled.

4:11.title: The Spirit Rests on the Seventy Elders and Two in Camp
4:11.summary: Moses gathers seventy elders and God's Spirit rests on them, causing them to prophesy; two men (Eldad and Medad) who remained in camp also receive the Spirit and prophesy. When Joshua objects to the unauthorized prophets, Moses expresses the desire that all God's people would be prophets.

4:11.title: The Quails and the Plague
4:11.summary: God sends a wind that brings quails from the sea and lets them fall around the camp; the people gather them abundantly. While the meat is still between their teeth, God's anger strikes the people with a great plague; the place is named Kibroth Hattaavah (Graves of Craving) after the burial of those who died.

4:12.title: Miriam and Aaron's Complaint Against Moses
4:12.summary: Miriam and Aaron criticize Moses for marrying a Cushite woman and challenge whether Yahweh has spoken only through him. The narrator notes that Moses is the most humble man on earth, setting up a contrast with their presumption.

4:12.title: Yahweh Vindicates Moses and Punishes Miriam
4:12.summary: Yahweh summons the three to the Tent of Meeting and declares that Moses holds a unique position of faithfulness and intimacy with God, speaking to him mouth to mouth rather than in visions and dreams. Yahweh's anger is kindled against them for speaking against His servant, and He departs.

4:12.title: Miriam's Leprosy and Moses' Intercession
4:12.summary: When the cloud lifts, Miriam is stricken with leprosy. Aaron begs Moses to intercede for her, acknowledging their foolishness and sin. Moses immediately cries out to God for her healing.

4:12.title: Miriam's Healing and Restoration
4:12.summary: Yahweh grants healing but requires Miriam to be shut outside the camp for seven days as a sign of shame, after which she is brought back. The community waits for her restoration before resuming their journey.

4:13.title: The Command to Send Spies
4:13.summary: God commands Moses to send twelve men, one leader from each tribe, to spy out the land of Canaan. Moses obeys and sends twelve prominent men from the wilderness of Paran.

4:13.title: The Names of the Twelve Spies
4:13.summary: The text lists the names of the twelve spies chosen from each tribe of Israel, including Caleb from Judah and Joshua (formerly Hoshea) from Ephraim, establishing the individuals who will scout the promised land.

4:13.title: Moses' Instructions to the Spies
4:13.summary: Moses instructs the spies to travel through the Negev into the hill country, assess the land's quality, the people's strength, and the cities' fortifications, and to bring back samples of the fruit. He encourages them to be courageous.

4:13.title: The Spies' Survey of Canaan
4:13.summary: The spies travel through Canaan for forty days, exploring from the wilderness of Zin to Hebron and the valley of Eshcol, where they gather enormous fruit samples before returning to report their findings.

4:13.title: The Initial Report: The Land's Abundance and Inhabitants
4:13.summary: The spies return and report that Canaan is indeed a fertile, abundant land flowing with milk and honey, but they also describe the powerful peoples and fortified cities that occupy it, including giants and various nations.

4:13.title: Caleb's Faith and the Majority's Doubt
4:13.summary: Caleb urges the people to take the land immediately, expressing confidence in God's power, but the other spies contradict him with an evil report, claiming the land devours its inhabitants and is filled with giants before whom Israel appears as grasshoppers. This division marks a crisis of faith in the wilderness.

4:14.title: The People's Rebellion and Despair
4:14.summary: After hearing the report of the spies, the entire congregation weeps and murmurs against Moses and Aaron, wishing they had died in Egypt or the wilderness rather than face the powerful inhabitants of Canaan. They propose to appoint a new leader and return to Egypt.

4:14.title: Joshua and Caleb's Appeal and the Divine Intervention
4:14.summary: Moses and Aaron fall in prayer before the assembly while Joshua and Caleb, the faithful spies, tear their clothes and urge the people to trust in God's promise and strength. When the congregation threatens to stone them, the glory of God appears in the Tent of Meeting.

4:14.title: Moses' Intercession for Israel
4:14.summary: God expresses anger at the people's disbelief despite the signs He has shown, threatening to destroy them and make Moses' descendants into a greater nation. Moses intercedes, appealing to God's reputation among the Egyptians and other nations, and pleads for forgiveness based on God's declared character of mercy and patience.

4:14.title: God's Judgment: Wandering in the Wilderness
4:14.summary: God grants Moses' petition and pardons the people, yet pronounces that all adults who have seen His signs and tested Him ten times shall not enter the promised land; only Caleb enters because he followed God fully, while the people must wander forty years in the wilderness.

4:14.title: The Decree of the Forty-Year Wilderness Wandering
4:14.summary: God confirms through Moses and Aaron that all Israelites from twenty years old and above who murmured will die in the wilderness over forty years, while their children will inherit the land they rejected; only Joshua and Caleb are excepted from this judgment.

4:14.title: Death of the Evil Spies
4:14.summary: The ten men who brought back an evil report about the land and incited the congregation's rebellion die by plague before the Lord, while Joshua and Caleb, who gave a faithful report, remain alive.

4:14.title: The People's Failed Attempt to Enter the Land
4:14.summary: After mourning greatly when hearing God's judgment, the people presumptuously attempt to go up to the promised land despite Moses' warning that God is not with them, resulting in their defeat and scattering by the Amalekites and Canaanites.

4:15.title: Laws for Grain and Drink Offerings with Sacrifices
4:15.summary: God instructs Moses on the proper meal and drink offerings to accompany burnt offerings and sacrifices in the promised land, with specific quantities based on the type of animal offered. The law applies equally to both native-born Israelites and resident foreigners, establishing one unified statute before the Lord.

4:15.title: The First Fruits Offering
4:15.summary: God commands the Israelites to offer a wave offering from the first of their dough when they arrive in the promised land, making an initial presentation to the Lord from their bread. This perpetual ordinance acknowledges God's provision and sustains their dependence on his blessing.

4:15.title: Atonement for Unintentional Sins
4:15.summary: God provides a procedure for atonement when the congregation or an individual unintentionally breaks his commandments, requiring appropriate sin offerings and priestly atonement. The law applies equally to native-born Israelites and foreigners, demonstrating God's grace for sins committed in ignorance.

4:15.title: Presumptuous Sin and Execution
4:15.summary: God establishes that anyone who deliberately and arrogantly defies his commandments blasphemes the Lord and must be cut off from the people, facing execution. This law distinguishes between unintentional transgressions (which can be atoned) and willful rebellion (which brings judgment).

4:15.title: The Man Who Gathered Sticks on the Sabbath
4:15.summary: The Israelites discover a man gathering sticks on the Sabbath and bring him to Moses; God commands that he be executed by stoning outside the camp. This narrative demonstrates the seriousness of intentionally breaking God's Sabbath law and shows the community carrying out God's judgment.

4:15.title: The Law of Fringes and Remembrance
4:15.summary: God instructs the Israelites to make fringes with a blue cord on the borders of their garments as a visible reminder to keep his commandments and avoid sinful pursuits.
