5:24.summary: When lending, the creditor must not enter the debtor's house to seize a pledge but must wait for the debtor to bring it out; if the debtor is poor, the pledge must be returned by sunset so he can sleep in his garment, which brings blessing and righteousness.

5:24.title: Protection of Hired Servants' Wages
5:24.summary: An employer must not oppress a poor and needy hired servant, whether Israelite or foreign resident, but must pay his daily wage by sunset, lest the servant cry out to the Lord against the employer.

5:24.title: Individual Accountability for Sin
5:24.summary: Parents shall not be executed for their children's sins, nor children for their parents'; each person shall be put to death only for his own sin.

5:24.title: Justice for Foreigners, Orphans, and Widows with Remembrance of Egypt
5:24.summary: The Israelites must not pervert justice for foreigners or orphans, nor take a widow's garment as pledge; they must remember their own bondage in Egypt and obey these commands.

5:24.title: Laws of Gleaning for the Needy
5:24.summary: When reaping harvest, beating olive trees, or gathering grapes, the Israelites must leave behind forgotten sheaves and unpicked fruit for foreigners, orphans, and widows rather than collecting everything themselves.

5:24.title: Closing Exhortation to Remember Egypt
5:24.summary: The command to leave gleanings is reinforced by recalling that the Israelites themselves were once bondservants in Egypt, whom God redeemed.

5:25.title: Laws Concerning Judicial Punishment
5:25.summary: When disputes are brought to judgment, judges must justify the righteous and condemn the wicked. If a wicked person deserves beating, the judge may order up to forty stripes, but not more, lest excessive punishment degrade the offender's dignity as a fellow Israelite.

5:25.title: Do Not Muzzle the Ox
5:25.summary: An ox working to thresh grain must not be prevented from eating, recognizing the animal's right to sustenance for its labor.

5:25.title: The Levirate Marriage Law
5:25.summary: If a man dies without a son, his brother must marry the widow to produce an heir who will carry on the dead man's name in Israel. If the brother refuses, the widow publicly humiliates him by removing his shoe and spitting in his face, and he becomes known as 'the man whose shoe was untied.'

5:25.title: Law Against a Woman Seizing a Man's Genitals
5:25.summary: If a woman intervenes in a fight between men and grabs her opponent's genitals, her hand must be cut off without mercy as punishment for this transgression.

5:25.title: Laws Against Dishonest Weights and Measures
5:25.summary: Israel is forbidden from using deceptive weights and measures in commerce; they must maintain perfect and just standards. This obedience brings long life in the land, while dishonesty is an abomination to God.

5:25.title: Command to Remember and Destroy Amalek
5:25.summary: Israel must remember how Amalek treacherously attacked the vulnerable and weary Israelites in the wilderness without fear of God. Once God grants Israel rest from all enemies in the promised land, Israel must completely blot out Amalek's memory from under heaven.

5:26.title: The Offering of First Fruits
5:26.summary: When Israel enters and possesses the land God promised, the people are commanded to bring the first fruits of their harvest in a basket to the place where God's name dwells, presenting it before the altar through the priest. This ritual acknowledges God's provision and the fulfillment of His covenant promise.

5:26.title: The Confession of God's Redemptive History
5:26.summary: The worshiper recites Israel's story before God: from Jacob as a wandering Syrian, through Egypt's affliction and oppression, to deliverance by God's mighty hand with signs and wonders, and finally to the promised land. This historical recitation frames the first fruits offering as gratitude for God's faithfulness across generations.

5:26.title: The Tithe of Blessing and the Prayer for Blessing
5:26.summary: Israel is commanded to share their tithes with Levites, foreigners, orphans, and widows in the third year, then declare before God that they have faithfully distributed the holy portion and obeyed all His commands. The people then petition God to look from heaven and bless the nation and land as He swore to their fathers.

5:26.title: The Covenant Renewal and Mutual Declaration
5:26.summary: God commands Israel to keep His statutes and ordinances with full heart and soul; Israel declares Yahweh as their God and commits to walk in His ways; and Yahweh reciprocally declares Israel His treasured possession and promises to exalt them above all nations as a holy people. This passage frames the covenant as a mutual commitment between God and His people.

5:27.title: Instructions to Set Up Stones and Build an Altar
5:27.summary: Moses commands the people to set up large stones plastered with plaster on Mount Ebal after crossing the Jordan, inscribe all the words of the law upon them, and build an altar there for burnt offerings and peace offerings. This public inscription of the law and worship establish a permanent witness to God's covenant and the people's commitment to obedience.

5:27.title: Call to Obedience and Covenant Ratification
5:27.summary: Moses and the Levitical priests call Israel to silence and announce that on this day Israel has formally become the people of Yahweh, obligating them to obey God's commandments and statutes. This declaration solemnizes Israel's covenant relationship and their responsibility to follow God's will.

5:27.title: Assignment of Tribes for Blessings and Curses
5:27.summary: Moses assigns six tribes (Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Joseph, Benjamin) to stand on Mount Gerizim to pronounce blessings, and six tribes (Reuben, Gad, Asher, Zebulun, Dan, Naphtali) to stand on Mount Ebal to pronounce curses. This ritual arrangement dramatizes the consequences of covenant obedience and disobedience.

5:27.title: The Twelve Curses for Covenant Violation
5:27.summary: The Levites pronounce twelve specific curses against those who commit idolatry, dishonor parents, steal land, harm the vulnerable, pervert justice, commit sexual sin, harm neighbors in secret, or fail to uphold the law; the entire assembly affirms each curse with 'Amen.' These curses codify the moral and religious standards of the covenant and invoke God's judgment against those who violate them.

5:28.title: Blessings for Obedience
5:28.summary: Moses promises that if Israel obeys God's commandments completely, God will exalt them above all nations and pour out comprehensive blessings—in city and field, family and livestock, storage and labor, military victory and abundance—establishing them as a holy people whose prosperity will make other nations fear them. Disobedience is forestalled only by steadfast adherence to God's commands without turning to other gods.

5:28.title: Curses for Disobedience
5:28.summary: Moses describes the devastating curses that will overtake Israel if they reject God's commandments: systematic destruction in every domain of life (city, field, family, livestock, labor), military defeat, disease and bodily affliction, humiliation by foreigners, captivity, and becoming a byword among nations. These curses will pursue them relentlessly until they are destroyed, serving as signs and wonders of judgment upon their descendants.

5:28.title: The Extremity of Curse and Final Exile
5:28.summary: Moses warns that because Israel failed to serve God with joy despite abundance, they will be reduced to servitude under enemies in hunger and degradation, besieged until driven to cannibalism, scattered among all peoples with no rest or security, living in constant fear and doubt. The chapter concludes with the ultimate reversal: Israel will be brought back to Egypt in humiliation, enslaved with no one to purchase their freedom.

5:29.title: Moses Recalls God's Covenant Acts and Commands Obedience
5:29.summary: Moses recounts to all Israel the great works Yahweh performed in Egypt and the wilderness—including divine provision for forty years and military victories—and commands them to keep the covenant words so they may prosper. Though Israel witnessed these signs, God has not yet given them hearts to fully know Him, but calls them to faithful obedience.

5:29.title: All Israel Enters into the Covenant
5:29.summary: Moses gathers all Israel—from heads of tribes to wives, children, and foreigners—to formally enter into Yahweh's covenant and oath, establishing them as His people and Himself as their God, fulfilling the promises made to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. This covenant encompasses the entire community across all generations and social stations.

5:29.title: Warning Against Idolatry and the Curse of Covenant Breach
5:29.summary: Moses warns that the covenant extends to future generations and emphasizes that anyone whose heart turns from Yahweh to serve other gods will face God's wrath, divine curses written in the law, and expulsion from the land. Such a person will be set apart for evil and have their name blotted out, serving as a sobering example of covenant unfaithfulness.

5:29.title: The Desolation of the Land as a Sign of Covenant Violation
5:29.summary: Future generations and foreigners will witness the land made desolate by plagues and sickness—like Sodom and Gomorrah—and will recognize this devastation as the result of Israel's forsaking the covenant and serving false gods. The nations will understand that Yahweh's anger and wrath brought judgment, scattering Israel from their land.

5:29.title: The Balance Between God's Hidden Ways and Revealed Law
5:29.summary: Moses concludes by affirming that while the secret purposes of Yahweh belong to Him alone, the revealed words of the law belong to Israel and their children forever as the basis for obedience and covenant keeping. This statement acknowledges both the limits of human knowledge and the sufficiency of God's revealed Word.

5:30.title: Promise of Restoration and Blessing Upon Repentance
5:30.summary: When Israel experiences the curses foretold, they will remember God's words among the nations and return to him with full obedience. God will then restore them from exile, bring them back to their land, circumcise their hearts to love him, defeat their enemies, and bless them abundantly—provided they keep his commandments with wholehearted devotion.

5:30.title: The Commandment is Near and Accessible
5:30.summary: Moses assures Israel that God's commandment is not impossibly distant or mysterious but is near at hand—in their mouth and heart—making it accessible and doable for them.

5:30.title: The Choice Between Life and Death
5:30.summary: Moses presents Israel with a stark choice: obey God's commands, love him, and walk in his ways to receive life and blessing in the promised land, or turn away to worship other gods and face certain death and curse. He calls heaven and earth as witnesses and exhorts them to choose life by clinging to God.

5:31.title: Moses' Farewell and Charge to Israel
5:31.summary: Moses, at 120 years old, announces he cannot enter the promised land but assures Israel that God will go before them and deliver their enemies. He commands them to be strong and courageous, trusting in God's presence and faithfulness.

5:31.title: Joshua Commissioned as Moses' Successor
5:31.summary: Moses publicly commissions Joshua before all Israel to lead them into the promised land, assuring him that God will go before him and never forsake him. This formally establishes Joshua as the new leader.

5:31.title: The Law to Be Read Every Seven Years
5:31.summary: Moses writes the law and instructs the priests and elders to read it publicly every seven years at the Feast of Tents so all Israel—men, women, children, and foreigners—may hear and learn to fear God and obey his commandments.

5:31.title: God's Prophecy of Israel's Future Apostasy
5:31.summary: God summons Moses and Joshua to the Tent of Meeting and predicts that after Moses' death Israel will turn to foreign gods and break the covenant, bringing divine punishment. He commands Moses to write a song as a witness against Israel's future unfaithfulness.

5:31.title: The Song Written and Joshua Commissioned
5:31.summary: Moses writes the song and teaches it to Israel, then formally commissions Joshua with the promise that God will be with him as he leads Israel into the promised land.

5:31.title: The Law Placed in the Ark as a Witness
5:31.summary: After completing the written law, Moses orders the Levites to place it beside the ark as a witness against Israel's anticipated rebellion and corruption. He gathers the elders and calls heaven and earth to witness his warning that Israel will turn from God's way after his death.

5:31.title: The Song Recited to All Israel
5:31.summary: Moses recites the complete song to the entire assembly of Israel, ensuring they hear the divine witness against their future unfaithfulness.

5:32.title: The Song's Introduction and God's Perfect Character
5:32.summary: Moses calls heaven and earth to witness his teaching, comparing his words to rain and dew nourishing the earth. He proclaims God's name and character as perfect, just, and faithful.

5:32.title: Israel's Corruption and God's Gracious Care
5:32.summary: Moses contrasts Israel's perverse ingratitude with God's faithfulness: God chose Israel as His special portion, found them in the wilderness, and nurtured them abundantly like an eagle caring for its young. Yet despite this covenant relationship and material blessing, Israel has been unfaithful.

5:32.title: Jeshurun's Apostasy and Forgetfulness
5:32.summary: Israel (called Jeshurun), grown fat and prosperous from God's blessings, abandons God to worship foreign gods and demons, forgetting the God who gave them birth and salvation.

5:32.title: God's Judgment and Threatened Destruction
5:32.summary: God abhors Israel's perversity and pronounces judgment: He will hide His face, allowing calamity, famine, plague, and exile. Yet He restrains complete destruction lest enemies falsely claim credit for Israel's downfall.

5:32.title: The Folly of Israel's Enemies and Vindication of God's Power
5:32.summary: Moses observes that Israel's enemies lack wisdom to understand that only God's judgment explains Israel's military defeats. God alone holds vengeance and will repay adversaries when Israel's power is exhausted and judgment falls.

5:32.title: God's Final Vindication and Nations' Rejoicing
5:32.summary: God will judge His people with compassion when their strength fails, expose the powerlessness of foreign gods, declare His unique sovereignty over life and death, execute vengeance on His adversaries, and make atonement for His land while the nations rejoice.

5:32.title: The Song Delivered and Its Life-Giving Purpose
5:32.summary: Moses and Joshua present the song to all Israel, and Moses exhorts the people to take the law to heart and teach it to their children, emphasizing that obedience to God's word brings life and long days in the promised land.

5:32.title: Moses' Death Sentence and Mount Nebo
5:32.summary: God commands Moses to ascend Mount Nebo to view the promised land from afar, then die there and be gathered to his people, because he failed to sanctify God at Meribah. Moses will see but not enter Canaan.

5:33.title: Introduction to Moses' Blessing
5:33.summary: Moses, the man of God, blesses the children of Israel before his death, recounting how Yahweh appeared from Sinai with a fiery law and established himself as king over Israel through the giving of the law to Jacob's assembly.

5:33.title: Blessing of Reuben and Judah
5:33.summary: Moses blesses Reuben with survival and prosperity, and blesses Judah with Yahweh's hearing and protection in battle against adversaries.

5:33.title: Blessing of Levi
5:33.summary: Levi receives a blessing for their priestly devotion and covenant faithfulness, particularly their willingness to prioritize God's word over family ties, and they are blessed with the responsibility to teach God's ordinances and offer sacrifices.

5:33.title: Blessing of Benjamin and Joseph
5:33.summary: Benjamin receives God's protection and favor, while Joseph is blessed with abundant agricultural and natural resources from the heavens and earth, along with military strength and dominion through Ephraim and Manasseh.

5:33.title: Blessing of Zebulun, Issachar, and Gad
5:33.summary: Zebulun and Issachar are blessed for their maritime and religious contributions, Gad for his warrior strength and righteousness in executing God's ordinances with Israel.

5:33.title: Blessing of Dan, Naphtali, and Asher
5:33.summary: Dan is blessed as a fierce warrior, Naphtali as satisfied with divine favor and blessed with land, and Asher blessed with children and prosperity, with strength enduring as long as their days.

5:33.title: Conclusion: Israel's Security and Salvation
5:33.summary: Moses concludes by praising the incomparable God who rides the heavens, provides eternal protection, and grants Israel safety, prosperity, and victory over enemies—declaring Israel blessed as a people saved by Yahweh.

5:34.title: Moses Views the Promised Land
5:34.summary: Moses ascends Mount Nebo where the Lord shows him the entire land promised to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob—from Gilead to the Mediterranean, the Negev, and the Jordan Valley. Though allowed to see the land with his own eyes, Moses is forbidden to cross over into it.

5:34.title: The Death of Moses
5:34.summary: Moses dies in Moab according to the Lord's word at the age of 120, yet remains in perfect physical condition with undiminished vigor and sight. His death marks the end of an era and the conclusion of his earthly service to God.

5:34.title: Mourning for Moses and Joshua's Succession
5:34.summary: The Israelites mourn Moses for thirty days in the plains of Moab; afterward, Joshua son of Nun, filled with the spirit of wisdom and commissioned by Moses' laying on of hands, becomes the people's new leader and they obey him as they had obeyed Moses.

5:34.title: Moses' Unparalleled Prophetic Legacy
5:34.summary: The narrative testifies that no prophet like Moses has ever arisen in Israel, emphasizing his unique face-to-face relationship with the Lord and his unmatched performance of signs, wonders, and mighty deeds throughout Egypt and before all Israel.

6:1.title: God's Commission to Joshua
6:1.summary: After Moses' death, God commissions Joshua to lead Israel across the Jordan River into the promised land, assuring him of divine presence and victory. God emphasizes the importance of obedience to the law and courage, promising success if Joshua meditates on and follows all of Moses' commands.

6:1.title: Joshua Prepares the People to Cross the Jordan
6:1.summary: Joshua commands the officers to prepare the people for crossing the Jordan within three days by gathering provisions for the journey into the promised land.

6:1.title: Joshua's Instructions to the Eastern Tribes
6:1.summary: Joshua reminds the Reubenites, Gadites, and half-tribe of Manasseh of Moses' command that they must cross the Jordan armed to help their brothers conquer the land, while their families remain in the territory already given them beyond the Jordan.

6:1.title: The Eastern Tribes Pledge Their Allegiance
6:1.summary: The three tribes affirm their complete commitment to Joshua's leadership, promising to obey his commands as they did Moses', and declaring that anyone who rebels will be put to death.

6:2.title: Joshua Sends Spies to Jericho
6:2.summary: Joshua secretly sends two spies from Shittim to scout Jericho. They lodge at the house of Rahab, a prostitute, but when the king discovers their presence, Rahab hides them on her roof and deceives the royal messengers, sending them on a false chase toward the Jordan.

6:2.title: Rahab's Covenant with the Spies
6:2.summary: Rahab confesses her faith in Israel's God, recounting how Yahweh's mighty deeds at the Red Sea and against the Amorite kings have filled the land with fear. She begs the spies to spare her family in exchange for her kindness, and they swear a covenant to save her household.

6:2.title: The Scarlet Cord Sign and Escape
6:2.summary: The spies escape through Rahab's window via a cord and instruct her to mark her house with a scarlet thread as a sign for protection. Rahab agrees to the terms, and the men advise her to gather her family inside during the coming conquest.

6:2.title: The Spies Return with Good News
6:2.summary: After hiding in the mountains for three days while their pursuers search in vain, the spies return to Joshua and report that Yahweh has delivered the entire land into Israel's hands, as all inhabitants are paralyzed with fear.

6:3.title: Preparation to Cross the Jordan
6:3.summary: Joshua leads Israel from Shittim to the Jordan River and prepares the people to cross. Officers instruct the people to follow the ark of the covenant at a distance, recognizing it as their guide to a way they have never traveled before.

6:3.title: Joshua's Call to Sanctification and God's Assurance
6:3.summary: Joshua calls the people to sanctify themselves and promises God will work wonders among them. God assures Joshua that he will magnify him before Israel as he did Moses, and commands the priests to carry the ark to the Jordan's edge.

6:3.title: Joshua Announces God's Miraculous Power
6:3.summary: Joshua calls the people to hear God's word and declares that the ark passing into the Jordan will prove God's presence and power to drive out all the Canaanite nations. He instructs them to prepare twelve men from each tribe as the miracle is about to unfold.

6:3.title: The Miraculous Crossing of the Jordan
6:3.summary: As the priests bearing the ark step into the Jordan's overflowing waters, God cuts off the water flow upstream, allowing all Israel to cross on dry ground. The priests remain standing in the riverbed as the entire nation passes over opposite Jericho.

6:4.title: The Command to Gather Twelve Stones
6:4.summary: After Israel completely crossed the Jordan, God commands Joshua to have twelve men take twelve stones from the middle of the riverbed as a memorial. These stones will serve as a sign to future generations of God's miraculous deliverance.

6:4.title: The Twelve Men Obey and Carry the Stones
6:4.summary: Joshua calls twelve men, one from each tribe, and commands them to cross into the Jordan, pick up stones, and carry them to the camp. The people obey exactly as Joshua commanded, completing this symbolic act of remembrance.

6:4.title: The Priests Remain Until All Cross; Joshua Magnified
6:4.summary: Joshua also sets up twelve stones in the middle of the Jordan where the priests stood, and the priests remain there until all the people and the ark have crossed. When the priests emerge and the waters return, God magnifies Joshua in the eyes of Israel, causing them to reverence him as they did Moses.

6:4.title: The Waters Return and Israel Encamps at Gilgal
6:4.summary: God commands Joshua to have the priests come out of the Jordan, and as soon as their feet touch dry ground, the waters return and overflow their banks. Israel completes their crossing on the tenth day of the first month and camps at Gilgal.

6:4.title: The Stones Set Up in Gilgal as a Memorial and Teaching Sign
6:4.summary: Joshua sets up the twelve stones in Gilgal and instructs the people to teach their children that God dried up the Jordan just as He had dried up the Red Sea, so that all peoples would know God's mighty hand and Israel would fear Him forever. This memorial preserves testimony to God's power across generations.

6:5.title: The Fear of the Canaanite Kings
6:5.summary: When the kings of Canaan and the Amorites hear that God dried up the Jordan River for Israel to cross, their hearts melt with fear and they lose all courage to resist.

6:5.title: Circumcision at Gilgal
6:5.summary: God commands Joshua to circumcise the new generation of Israelites born in the wilderness, removing the reproach of Egypt and renewing the covenant sign before entering the promised land.

6:5.title: Passover and Transition to Canaan's Produce
6:5.summary: Israel celebrates the Passover at Gilgal and begins eating the produce of Canaan; God's provision of manna, which sustained them for forty years, ceases as they enter their inheritance.

6:5.title: The Commander of the Lord's Army
6:5.summary: Joshua encounters a heavenly figure with a drawn sword near Jericho; the figure identifies himself as the commander of God's army and instructs Joshua to remove his shoes because the ground is holy.

6:6.title: Yahweh's Instructions for Taking Jericho
6:6.summary: Jericho is shut tight against Israel, but Yahweh assures Joshua of victory and gives detailed instructions: the army must march around the city once daily for six days, then seven times on the seventh day with seven priests blowing trumpets, and when the final trumpet sounds, the people must shout and the walls will collapse.

6:6.title: Joshua Relays Orders and the First Procession
6:6.summary: Joshua commands the priests to carry the ark with seven trumpeters, instructs the armed men to lead, and tells all the people to remain silent until given the signal to shout; the ark is carried around the city once on the first day and the people camp for the night.

6:6.title: The Six-Day March Around Jericho
6:6.summary: For six consecutive days, Joshua and the people repeat the same procession—marching once around the city with the ark, priests, and armed men—until on the seventh day they rise early and march around the city seven times.

6:6.title: The Seventh Procession and the Fall of Jericho
6:6.summary: On the seventh circuit of the seventh day, when the priests blow the trumpets, Joshua commands the people to shout; the walls of Jericho collapse and the Israelites enter and conquer the city completely.

6:6.title: Destruction of Jericho and Preservation of Rahab
6:6.summary: The Israelites completely destroy everything in the city with the sword, but Joshua honors his covenant with the prostitute Rahab by sending the two spies to rescue her and her entire family, who are brought out and saved alive to dwell among Israel.

6:6.title: Joshua's Curse and God's Presence
6:6.summary: Joshua pronounces a curse on anyone who would rebuild Jericho, declaring that such rebuilding will cost him his sons; the chapter concludes with the affirmation that Yahweh was with Joshua and his fame spread throughout the land.

6:7.title: Achan's Sin and Israel's Defeat at Ai
6:7.summary: Achan secretly takes devoted things from Jericho, causing God's anger to burn against Israel. When Joshua sends men to attack the small city of Ai, they are defeated and routed, with thirty-six Israelites killed, revealing that hidden sin brings corporate judgment and military failure.

6:7.title: Joshua's Lament and Prayer
6:7.summary: Joshua tears his clothes and falls before God in anguish, asking why God brought Israel across the Jordan only to allow them to be defeated. He grieves that Israel's shame will spread among the Canaanites and damage God's reputation among the nations.

6:7.title: God's Revelation of Israel's Sin and the Method of Judgment
6:7.summary: God commands Joshua to stop lamenting and reveals that Israel has sinned by taking devoted things and hiding them among their possessions. God declares that Israel cannot stand against enemies until the devoted thing is removed, and instructs Joshua to conduct a tribal lottery to identify the guilty party, who will be burned with fire.

6:7.title: The Identification of Achan
6:7.summary: Joshua conducts a systematic lottery process, narrowing from tribes to families to households to individuals, which identifies Achan son of Carmi from the tribe of Judah as the one who has taken the devoted things.

6:7.title: Achan's Confession
6:7.summary: Joshua urges Achan to confess and give glory to God. Achan admits he coveted and stole a beautiful Babylonian robe, silver, and gold, which he hid beneath his tent, revealing how personal desire led to corporate sin.

6:7.title: The Punishment of Achan and Restoration of God's Favor
6:7.summary: Messengers retrieve the stolen items from Achan's tent, and Joshua executes Achan along with all his possessions and family members by stoning and burning them in the valley of Achor. God's anger is turned away and Israel is restored, with a heap of stones marking the place as a memorial to God's judgment.

6:8.title: The Lord's Command and Strategy for Ai
6:8.summary: God assures Joshua not to fear and commands him to take all the people of war to Ai, promising victory. Joshua is instructed to destroy Ai as he did Jericho, but to take the spoil and livestock for themselves, and to set an ambush behind the city.

6:8.title: Joshua's Preparation and Ambush Setup
6:8.summary: Joshua selects thirty thousand mighty warriors and sends them by night to set up an ambush west of Ai, while he personally stays with the main force to execute the deceptive strategy of feigned retreat.

6:8.title: The Battle of Ai Begins
6:8.summary: Joshua musters the people and approaches Ai with the main army from the north while the ambush waits in position; when the king of Ai pursues the apparently fleeing Israelites, the city is left undefended.

6:8.title: The Ambush Succeeds and Ai Is Destroyed
6:8.summary: At Joshua's signal with his javelin, the ambush rises up and seizes the city, setting it on fire; when the men of Ai see the smoke and realize they are trapped between the ambush and the returning Israelites, they are completely destroyed.

6:8.title: The Capture and Execution of Ai's King
6:8.summary: The king of Ai is captured alive and brought to Joshua; after all the inhabitants of Ai are killed in the field and the city is burned, the king is hanged on a tree until evening and then displayed at the city gate with a great heap of stones over him.

6:8.title: Joshua Builds an Altar and Renews the Covenant
6:8.summary: Joshua builds an altar of uncut stones on Mount Ebal, offers sacrifices, writes a copy of the law on stones, and assembles all Israel—divided between Mount Gerizim and Mount Ebal—to hear Joshua read all the words of blessing and curse from the law of Moses.

6:9.title: The Kings Unite Against Israel
6:9.summary: All the Canaanite kings west of the Jordan hear of Joshua's victories and band together with one accord to fight against Israel. Their unified military response demonstrates the threat that Israel's conquest poses to the region's established powers.

6:9.title: The Gibeonites' Deceptive Ruse
6:9.summary: The inhabitants of Gibeon, hearing of Israel's victories at Jericho and Ai, devise a cunning deception: they disguise themselves as ambassadors from a distant land, presenting worn provisions and torn clothing to support their false claim of a long journey. Their elaborate hoax is designed to trick Israel into making a covenant rather than destroying them as they did other Canaanite cities.

6:9.title: Israel's Covenant Without Divine Counsel
6:9.summary: The Israelite leaders sample the Gibeonites' provisions but fail to seek guidance from God, making a peace covenant with them based solely on physical evidence and persuasive speech. This lack of spiritual discernment results in Israel binding itself by oath to preserve the deceiving nation.

6:9.title: The Discovery of the Deception and Israel's Response
6:9.summary: Three days after making the covenant, Israel discovers that the Gibeonites are actually nearby neighbors, not distant foreigners, and the congregation murmurs against the princes for the deception. However, the princes uphold the sworn covenant before God, and the Gibeonites are assigned to become perpetual servants as wood cutters and water drawers rather than being destroyed.

6:9.title: Joshua's Judgment on the Gibeonites
6:9.summary: Joshua confronts the Gibeonites about their deception, and they confess their fear of destruction based on God's command to Moses to conquer the land. Joshua pronounces them cursed to perpetual servitude in the sanctuary, a status they accept, and they are made wood cutters and water drawers for the congregation and God's altar as a permanent consequence of their deception.

6:10.title: The Five Kings Form an Alliance Against Gibeon
6:10.summary: Hearing of Joshua's victories at Ai and the Gibeonites' peace treaty with Israel, Adoni-Zedek of Jerusalem calls together four other Amorite kings to attack Gibeon as punishment for its defection. The five kings unite their armies and march against Gibeon.

6:10.title: Joshua Responds to Gibeon's Plea
6:10.summary: The Gibeonites send urgent messengers to Joshua at Gilgal asking him to rescue them from the five-king coalition. Joshua immediately mobilizes his entire army and marches through the night to reach Gibeon.

6:10.title: The Battle of Gibeon and the Long Day
6:10.summary: God grants Joshua a miraculous victory by confusing the Amorite armies and causing divine hailstones to kill more enemies than the Israelite sword. Joshua calls on God to make the sun and moon stand still to complete the victory, and God answers his prayer with a day like no other in history.

6:10.title: The Pursuit and Capture of the Five Kings
6:10.summary: After returning to camp, Joshua learns the five kings are hiding in a cave at Makkedah. He seals the cave, continues pursuing the fleeing enemy armies until they are utterly destroyed, then returns with all Israel to Makkedah in complete victory.

6:10.title: Execution of the Five Kings
6:10.summary: Joshua brings out the five kings from the cave and commands the Israelite leaders to place their feet on the kings' necks in a sign of complete subjugation. He then executes them, hangs their bodies on trees until evening, and buries them in the cave sealed with great stones.

6:10.title: The Conquest of the Southern Cities
6:10.summary: Joshua systematically captures and destroys Makkedah, Libnah, Lachish, Eglon, Hebron, and Debir, leaving no survivors in any of them and utterly destroying each city and its king, just as he had done to Jericho.

6:10.title: Summary of the Southern Campaign
6:10.summary: Joshua strikes all the land from Kadesh Barnea to Gaza, conquering all kings and peoples in the hill country, Negev, lowland, and slopes, leaving no survivors because Yahweh fought for Israel. He then returns to Gilgal with all Israel.

6:11.title: Coalition of Northern Kings Against Israel
6:11.summary: Jabin king of Hazor organizes a massive coalition of northern Canaanite kings and their armies to unite against Israel, gathering at the waters of Merom. This represents the largest organized resistance to Israel's conquest yet encountered.

6:11.title: God's Promise and Joshua's Obedience
6:11.summary: Yahweh assures Joshua of victory and gives specific instructions to hamstring the horses and burn the chariots, preventing future reliance on military advantage. Joshua obeys the divine command exactly, demonstrating faithful leadership.

6:11.title: Conquest and Destruction of the Northern Cities
6:11.summary: Joshua defeats the coalition, destroys Hazor and all the kings' cities with complete annihilation, and burns only Hazor as instructed. He meticulously obeys Moses' commands through complete devastation of the enemy.

6:11.title: Extent of Conquest and God's Sovereignty
6:11.summary: Joshua captures all the land from Mount Halak to Lebanon, taking all kings after prolonged warfare, with the exception of Gibeon. God hardened the hearts of the remaining kings so they would be completely destroyed as commanded.

6:11.title: Destruction of the Anakim and Rest from War
6:11.summary: Joshua eliminates the Anakim giants from the hill country and throughout the land of Israel, leaving none except remnants in Philistine territory. The complete conquest fulfills God's promise to Moses, and the land settles into peace.

6:12.title: Kings Defeated East of the Jordan
6:12.summary: Moses and the Israelites defeated Sihon king of the Amorites and Og king of Bashan, who ruled extensive territories east of the Jordan. These conquered lands were distributed as possessions to the Reubenites, Gadites, and half-tribe of Manasseh.

6:12.title: Kings Defeated West of the Jordan
6:12.summary: Joshua and the Israelites defeated thirty-one kings in Canaan west of the Jordan, from Baal Gad to Mount Halak, conquering diverse regions and peoples including Hittites, Amorites, Canaanites, and Jebusites. These territories were distributed among the tribes of Israel according to their divisions.

6:13.title: Joshua's Age and the Remaining Land to Conquer
6:13.summary: The Lord tells the aging Joshua that much land remains to be conquered, listing the unconquered territories from the Philistines to the north. Joshua is commanded to divide the remaining land among the nine and a half tribes that have not yet received their full inheritance.

6:13.title: The Inheritance of Reuben, Gad, and Half-Manasseh East of Jordan
6:13.summary: Moses had already given inheritance to the Reubenites, Gadites, and half-tribe of Manasseh on the eastern side of the Jordan, from Aroer to Bashan. The Levites receive no territorial inheritance; instead, the Lord's fire offerings are their inheritance.

6:13.title: The Territory of the Tribe of Reuben
6:13.summary: Detailed boundaries and cities of Reuben's inheritance are listed, extending from Aroer through the plain of Medeba and including all the cities conquered from Sihon king of the Amorites, with the Jordan as their eastern border.

6:13.title: The Territory of the Tribe of Gad
6:13.summary: The territory assigned to Gad is described, including Jazer, the cities of Gilead, half the land of Ammon, and territory extending from Heshbon to the Sea of Chinnereth (Galilee) beyond the Jordan eastward.

6:13.title: The Territory of the Half-Tribe of Manasseh
6:13.summary: The half-tribe of Manasseh receives territory including all of Bashan, the kingdom of Og with sixty cities of Jair, half of Gilead, and cities including Ashtaroth and Edrei, representing the inheritance Moses distributed east of the Jordan.

6:13.title: The Levites' Inheritance—The Lord Himself
6:13.summary: The Levites are given no territorial inheritance because the Lord God of Israel himself is their inheritance, fulfilling what the Lord spoke to them.

6:14.title: Distribution of the Land by Lot
6:14.summary: Eleazar the priest, Joshua, and the tribal leaders distribute the land of Canaan among the nine and a half tribes by lot, following Yahweh's command to Moses. The Levites receive no territorial inheritance but are given cities to dwell in.

6:14.title: Caleb's Request for His Inheritance
6:14.summary: Caleb reminds Joshua of Yahweh's ancient promise made at Kadesh Barnea—that because he alone faithfully followed God among the spies, he would inherit the land he explored. Now at eighty-five, Caleb requests the hill country of Hebron, confident that God will enable him to drive out the Anakim.

6:14.title: Caleb Receives Hebron and the Land Rests
6:14.summary: Joshua blesses Caleb and grants him Hebron as his inheritance, confirming that this land became Caleb's possession because of his wholehearted devotion to God. With Caleb's victory, the land enters a season of peace after warfare.

6:15.title: The Borders of Judah's Territory
6:15.summary: The passage describes in detail the geographical boundaries of Judah's tribal inheritance, traced from the southern border at the Salt Sea and wilderness of Zin, eastward along the Jordan, northward past Jerusalem, and westward to the Mediterranean Sea. These precise borders define Judah's divinely appointed territorial possession.

6:15.title: Caleb's Inheritance and His Daughter's Request
6:15.summary: Caleb receives Hebron as his portion according to God's command, drives out the three sons of Anak, and offers his daughter Achsah as a reward to whoever captures Kiriath Sepher. Othniel takes the city and wins Achsah, who then requests and receives springs of water from her father as a blessing for her desert inheritance.

6:15.title: Cities of Judah in the South
6:15.summary: The text lists twenty-nine cities in Judah's southern region near the border of Edom, organized with their villages, documenting the urban settlements of the tribe's allotted territory.

6:15.title: Cities of Judah in the Lowland
6:15.summary: Fourteen cities in Judah's lowland region are listed with their villages, representing the inhabited centers in this geographical zone.

6:15.title: Cities of Judah in the Shephelah
6:15.summary: Sixteen cities in Judah's Shephelah (the foothills region) are enumerated with their villages, documenting settlements in this transitional area.

6:15.title: Cities of Judah in the Coastal Plain
6:15.summary: Nine cities in Judah's western region near the coast are listed with their villages, marking settlements in the lowland plains.

6:15.title: Philistine Cities in Judah's Territory
6:15.summary: The passage lists Ekron, Ashdod, and Gaza with their surrounding towns and villages extending to the brook of Egypt and the Mediterranean Sea, documenting settlements along Judah's western frontier.

6:15.title: Cities of Judah in the Hill Country
6:15.summary: Eleven cities in Judah's hill country are enumerated with their villages, representing settlements in the mountainous interior region.

6:15.title: Cities of Judah in the Central Hill Country
6:15.summary: Nine cities in Judah's central hill region, including Hebron, are listed with their villages, documenting urban settlements in this area.

6:15.title: Cities of Judah in the Southern Hill Country
6:15.summary: Ten cities in Judah's southern hill country are enumerated with their villages, marking settlements in this mountainous zone.

6:15.title: Cities of Judah Near Jerusalem
6:15.summary: Six cities in the region near Jerusalem are listed with their villages, documenting settlements in proximity to the Jebusite stronghold.

6:15.title: Cities of Judah in the Wilderness Regions
6:15.summary: The passage lists two cities in the region near Kiriath Jearim and six cities in the wilderness region bordering the Dead Sea, including Beth Arabah, En Gedi, and other settlements in the arid zones.

6:15.title: The Jebusites Remain in Jerusalem
6:15.summary: Despite Judah's territorial allocation, the children of Judah were unable to drive out the Jebusites who inhabited Jerusalem, resulting in the two peoples coexisting in the city.

6:16.title: The Allotment of Joseph's Territory
6:16.summary: The land allotted to the children of Joseph is described, extending from the Jordan River at Jericho westward through the hill country to Bethel, then to Luz and the Archites' border, down to the lower Beth Horon and Gezer, ending at the Mediterranean Sea.

6:16.title: The Specific Borders of Ephraim's Inheritance
6:16.summary: The specific territorial boundaries of Ephraim are detailed, including cities and landmarks from Ataroth Addar and Beth Horon in the east to Tappuah and the brook of Kanah in the west, establishing Ephraim's portion within Joseph's larger allotment.

6:16.title: Ephraim's Cities and Failure to Fully Possess the Land
6:16.summary: Ephraim receives several cities within Manasseh's territory but fails to completely drive out the Canaanites from Gezer, who remain as a subjugated labor force among them, foreshadowing Israel's ongoing struggles with incomplete obedience.

6:17.title: Manasseh's Territory and Zelophehad's Daughters' Inheritance
6:17.summary: Manasseh receives his allotted territory as Joseph's firstborn, with Machir and Gilead going to the east of the Jordan. Zelophehad's five daughters petition the leadership and receive an inheritance among their father's brothers, establishing a precedent for women's property rights in Israel.

6:17.title: The Boundaries of Manasseh's Territory
6:17.summary: The specific borders of Manasseh's land are described, running from Asher through Shechem to the sea, with several key cities and fortified towns listed throughout the territory in both Issachar and Asher.

6:17.title: Manasseh's Failure to Fully Conquer the Land
6:17.summary: Despite their allotment, Manasseh is unable to drive out the Canaanite inhabitants of their cities. When Israel gains strength, they subject the Canaanites to forced labor rather than complete expulsion.

6:17.title: Joseph's Sons Complain About Their Allotment
6:17.summary: Ephraim and Manasseh protest to Joshua that their single allotment is insufficient for their great population and God's blessing. Joshua challenges them to clear the forested hill country themselves and assures them they have the strength to drive out even the iron-chariot-equipped Canaanites.

6:18.title: Assembly at Shiloh and Remaining Tribes
6:18.summary: The Israelite congregation gathers at Shiloh and establishes the Tent of Meeting there. Seven tribes still await their land inheritance despite the conquered territory.

6:18.title: Joshua's Command to Survey and Divide the Land
6:18.summary: Joshua urges the remaining tribes to take possession of their inheritance and orders surveyors to map the land into seven portions, which he will then distribute by lot before the Lord at Shiloh. The men complete their survey and Joshua casts lots to divide the land.

6:18.title: Benjamin's Territorial Boundaries
6:18.summary: Benjamin's allotted territory is described in detail, with its borders defined by surrounding tribes and geographical landmarks, bounded by the Jordan River on the east. The tribe receives a strategic position between Judah to the south and Joseph's descendants to the north.

6:18.title: Benjamin's Cities and Villages
6:18.summary: A comprehensive list of Benjamin's towns is provided, totaling twenty-six cities with their villages, including prominent settlements like Jericho, Bethel, and Jerusalem. This completes the formal recording of Benjamin's inheritance.

6:19.title: Simeon's Inheritance
6:19.summary: Simeon receives its tribal inheritance within Judah's territory, receiving thirteen cities plus four additional cities with their villages. Because Judah's allotted portion was too large, Simeon's land is located entirely within Judah's borders.

6:19.title: Zebulun's Inheritance
6:19.summary: Zebulun's tribal territory is defined by specific geographical boundaries and borders, encompassing twelve cities with their villages. The inheritance is established according to families and clearly demarcated boundaries.

6:19.title: Issachar's Inheritance
6:19.summary: Issachar receives its inheritance with clearly marked borders extending to major geographical features, receiving sixteen cities with their villages. The territory is established according to the tribal families.

6:19.title: Asher's Inheritance
6:19.summary: Asher's territory is defined with detailed boundaries including cities near the Mediterranean coast and Sidon, encompassing twenty-two cities with their villages. The inheritance is distributed among the tribal families.

6:19.title: Naphtali's Inheritance
6:19.summary: Naphtali receives its inheritance in the northern region with boundaries extending to the Jordan and other tribal lands, containing nineteen fortified cities with villages. The allocation is made according to the tribal families.

6:19.title: Dan's Inheritance and Northward Migration
6:19.summary: Dan's initial inheritance includes multiple cities near the coast, but finding their territory too small, the Danites conquer Leshem in the north and rename it Dan after their patriarch. This represents both the initial land distribution and Dan's later territorial expansion.

6:19.title: Completion of Land Distribution and Joshua's Inheritance
6:19.summary: The land distribution is completed, and Joshua himself receives an inheritance—the city of Timnathserah in Ephraim's hill country—which he builds and inhabits. The chapter concludes by affirming that Eleazar, Joshua, and the tribal leaders completed the division by lot before the Lord at Shiloh.

6:20.title: The Lord's Command to Establish Cities of Refuge
6:20.summary: The Lord instructs Joshua to designate cities of refuge where those who accidentally kill someone may flee for protection from blood vengeance. The manslayer must present his case to the city elders, who grant him asylum until judgment before the congregation and the death of the high priest, when he may return home.

6:20.title: The Six Cities of Refuge Appointed
6:20.summary: Israel designates six cities of refuge—three on the west side of the Jordan (Kedesh, Shechem, and Hebron) and three on the east side (Bezer, Ramoth, and Golan)—as safe havens for anyone who commits unintentional manslaughter, available to both Israelites and resident aliens.

6:21.title: The Levites' Request for Cities
6:21.summary: The heads of the Levite families petition Eleazar the priest and Joshua, reminding them of God's command through Moses to provide cities for the Levites to dwell in. The children of Israel comply and give the Levites cities with surrounding suburbs as the Lord commanded.

6:21.title: Distribution of Cities by Lot to the Three Levite Families
6:21.summary: The cities are distributed by lot to the three families of Levites—Kohathites (13 cities), Gershonites (13 cities), and Merarites (12 cities)—according to the Lord's command through Moses. The allocation ensures all Levite families receive cities scattered throughout Israel's inheritance.

6:21.title: Levite Cities from Judah, Simeon, and Benjamin
6:21.summary: The Kohathites, who were descendants of Aaron the priests, receive thirteen cities from Judah, Simeon, and Benjamin, including Hebron (a city of refuge), with detailed listings of all nine cities from Judah-Simeon and four from Benjamin. This fulfills the first lot given to Aaron's priestly line.

6:21.title: Levite Cities from Ephraim, Dan, and Manasseh
6:21.summary: The remaining Kohathites receive ten cities distributed by lot from Ephraim (including Shechem, a city of refuge), Dan, and half of Manasseh. The allocation ensures balanced geographic distribution of the non-priestly Kohathite families.

6:21.title: Levite Cities for the Gershonites
6:21.summary: The Gershonites receive thirteen cities from Manasseh (including Golan, a city of refuge), Issachar, Asher, and Naphtali, with each region's contribution detailed. This completes the distribution for the second major Levite family.

6:21.title: Levite Cities for the Merarites
6:21.summary: The Merarites receive twelve cities from Zebulun, Reuben, and Gad, including Ramoth in Gilead as a city of refuge. The detailed accounting completes the distribution of all Levite cities throughout Israel's territory.

6:21.title: Summary and Fulfillment of God's Promises
6:21.summary: A total of forty-eight Levite cities with suburbs are distributed throughout Israel's territory, and the passage concludes by affirming that God fulfilled all His covenantal promises to Israel—giving them the promised land, delivering them from enemies, and granting them rest. Not one word of God's good promises failed.

6:22.title: Joshua Commends and Blesses the Eastern Tribes
6:22.summary: Joshua praises the Reubenites, Gadites, and half-tribe of Manasseh for their faithfulness in warfare, then commissions them to return to their lands beyond the Jordan with a charge to obey God's law and remain devoted to Him.

6:22.title: The Eastern Tribes Depart with Spoil
6:22.summary: Joshua sends the eastern tribes home with their share of plunder and instructs them to divide spoils with their brothers, and they depart from Shiloh toward their inheritance in Gilead.

6:22.title: The Eastern Tribes Build an Altar and Provoke Suspicion
6:22.summary: The eastern tribes build a large altar at the Jordan's edge, which alarms the Israelites in Canaan who suspect rebellion against God, prompting them to assemble for war.

6:22.title: The Delegation Confronts the Eastern Tribes with Accusations
6:22.summary: Phinehas the priest and ten tribal leaders confront the eastern tribes, accusing them of rebellion and unfaithfulness by building an unauthorized altar, and warn of God's judgment similar to the sin of Achan.

6:22.title: The Eastern Tribes Defend Their Altar as a Witness
6:22.summary: The eastern tribes explain that the altar is not for sacrificial offerings but as a permanent witness for future generations that they remain part of God's covenant people despite being separated by the Jordan River.

6:22.title: Reconciliation and Naming of the Altar
6:22.summary: Phinehas and the leaders are satisfied with the explanation and praise God for the eastern tribes' faithfulness, returning to report peace to Israel, and the eastern tribes name the altar 'A Witness Between Us that Yahweh is God.'

6:23.title: Joshua's Farewell Address: God's Victories and the Promised Inheritance
6:23.summary: As Joshua approaches the end of his life after Israel's military victories, he reminds the people that God has defeated their enemies and secured their inheritance. He encourages them that God will continue to drive out the remaining nations and give them the promised land.

6:23.title: Call to Obedience and Covenant Faithfulness
6:23.summary: Joshua urges Israel to obey the law of Moses, avoid pagan practices and idolatry, and hold fast to their relationship with God. He emphasizes that their past military success came through God's power and depends on their continued love and devotion to Him.

6:23.title: Warning Against Intermarriage and Assimilation
6:23.summary: Joshua warns that if Israel intermarries with the remaining pagan nations and turns away from God, He will withdraw His protection, leaving them vulnerable to destruction. The nations they spare will become a source of suffering and lead to their downfall.

6:23.title: Final Appeal: God's Faithfulness and the Threat of Judgment
6:23.summary: Joshua testifies to God's complete faithfulness in fulfilling all His promises and warns that if Israel breaks the covenant and serves other gods, God will bring the threatened curses upon them, destroying them from the land. His departing words balance encouragement with a sobering call to remain faithful.

6:24.title: Joshua Assembles Israel at Shechem
6:24.summary: Joshua gathers all the tribes of Israel and their leaders at Shechem to present themselves before God for a covenant renewal ceremony.

6:24.title: Joshua's Recital of God's Mighty Works
6:24.summary: Joshua recounts Israel's entire history from Abraham through the conquest, emphasizing how God—not Israel's own strength—accomplished every victory and provided the land as a gift. This historical review forms the basis for the covenant renewal.

6:24.title: Joshua's Call to Decision and Personal Commitment
6:24.summary: Joshua challenges Israel to fear God, serve him in sincerity, and abandon idolatry, declaring that he and his household will serve Yahweh regardless of Israel's choice. This sets up a stark either/or decision.

6:24.title: Israel's Affirmation of Covenant Loyalty
6:24.summary: The people respond by affirming their commitment to serve Yahweh alone, recalling his deliverance from Egypt and his victories in conquest, rejecting the alternative of serving other gods.

6:24.title: Joshua's Warning and Israel's Reaffirmation
6:24.summary: Joshua warns that Yahweh is holy and jealous, demanding exclusive loyalty and threatening judgment for apostasy; the people reaffirm their choice to serve Yahweh, and Joshua calls them to witness against themselves.

6:24.title: Covenant Ratification and the Witness Stone
6:24.summary: Joshua formally establishes a covenant with Israel, records it in the book of the law, and erects a stone at Shechem as a perpetual witness to their commitment and a safeguard against future denial of God.

6:24.title: Joshua's Death and Israel's Faithfulness
6:24.summary: Joshua dismisses the people to their inheritances and then dies at age 110; Israel remains faithful during his lifetime and that of the elders, and the chapter concludes with the burial of Joseph's bones and the death of Eleazar, marking the end of an era.

7:1.title: Judah and Simeon Called to Fight
7:1.summary: After Joshua's death, Israel inquires of the Lord who should lead the conquest of Canaan. The Lord designates Judah, and Judah invites his brother Simeon to join in the campaign.

7:1.title: Victory at Bezek and Capture of Adoni-Bezek
7:1.summary: Judah and Simeon defeat the Canaanites and Perizzites at Bezek, capturing their king Adoni-Bezek. They mutilate him in the same manner he had mutilated seventy kings, demonstrating divine justice and retribution.

7:1.title: Judah's Conquests and Caleb's Daughter
7:1.summary: Judah captures Jerusalem and continues conquering cities in the hill country and lowland. Caleb offers his daughter Achsah as a reward to whoever takes Kiriath Sepher; Othniel succeeds and receives her as wife, and she secures water springs as a blessing from her father.

7:1.title: Allied Victories and Caleb's Inheritance
7:1.summary: The Kenites join Judah's campaign; Judah and Simeon destroy Zephath and take Philistine cities. The Lord aids Judah against hill-country inhabitants but cannot drive out valley dwellers with iron chariots, and Caleb receives Hebron as promised.

7:1.title: Incomplete Conquests: Benjamin and Joseph
7:1.summary: Benjamin fails to drive out the Jebusites from Jerusalem; they coexist in the city. Joseph's house captures Bethel by showing mercy to an informant who later founds a city in the Hittite lands.

7:1.title: Widespread Failure to Complete the Conquest
7:1.summary: Multiple tribes—Manasseh, Ephraim, Zebulun, Asher, and Naphtali—fail to drive out Canaanites from their territories, instead subjecting them to forced labor. The Amorites confine the Danites to the hill country, establishing a pattern of incomplete obedience.

7:2.title: The Angel's Rebuke at Bochim
7:2.summary: The angel of Yahweh confronts Israel for breaking covenant by making treaties with the pagan inhabitants and failing to destroy their altars. Because of their disobedience, God declares He will not drive out these nations but will allow them to become thorns in Israel's sides and spiritual snares.

7:2.title: Israel's Weeping at Bochim
7:2.summary: Upon hearing the angel's words, the people weep and cry out in response to the rebuke, naming the place Bochim ('weeping') and offering sacrifices to Yahweh in repentance.

7:2.title: Joshua's Death and the Faithful Generation
7:2.summary: After Joshua dismisses the people to possess their inheritances, Israel faithfully serves Yahweh throughout Joshua's life and the lives of the elder generation who witnessed God's great works. Joshua dies at 110 years old and is buried in Ephraim.

7:2.title: The Cycle Begins: Apostasy and Defeat
7:2.summary: A new generation arises that does not know Yahweh, and Israel turns to serving the Baals and Ashtaroth, provoking God's anger. Yahweh hands them over to spoilers and enemies who oppress them, and wherever they go, God's hand is against them for evil.

7:2.title: The Cycle of Judges and Recurring Apostasy
7:2.summary: When Israel cries out, Yahweh raises up judges who deliver them from their enemies and judge Israel while they are alive. However, each time a judge dies, the people return to greater corruption, serving other gods more stubbornly than before, refusing to abandon their sinful ways.

7:2.title: God's Covenant Penalty and Testing
7:2.summary: Yahweh declares that because Israel has transgressed His covenant and refused to listen to His voice, He will no longer drive out the remaining nations from the land but will leave them to test whether Israel will obey God's way as their fathers did.

7:3.title: The Nations Left to Test Israel
7:3.summary: God deliberately left certain nations in Canaan to test Israel's obedience and to teach the younger generation warfare. These nations—the Philistines, Canaanites, Sidonians, and Hivites—remained to determine whether Israel would keep God's commandments.

7:3.title: Israel's Disobedience and Oppression
7:3.summary: Israel compromised by intermarrying with the surrounding nations and worshiping their gods (Baals and Asheroth), which provoked God's anger. As punishment, God allowed Cushan Rishathaim of Mesopotamia to conquer and oppress Israel for eight years.

7:3.title: Othniel the First Judge
7:3.summary: When Israel cried out to God, He raised up Othniel, Caleb's younger brother, as their savior. The Spirit of Yahweh came upon Othniel, enabling him to defeat Cushan Rishathaim and secure forty years of peace until his death.

7:3.title: Israel's Relapse and Moabite Oppression
7:3.summary: After Othniel's death, Israel again turned to evil, and God allowed King Eglon of Moab—strengthened by Ammonite and Amalekite allies—to oppress them for eighteen years.

7:3.title: Ehud's Assassination of Eglon
7:3.summary: God raised up Ehud, a left-handed Benjamite, to save Israel. Using his disability as an advantage, Ehud gained access to King Eglon by pretending to deliver tribute, then secretly stabbed him with a hidden double-edged sword and escaped.

7:3.title: Ehud's Victory and Eighty Years of Peace
7:3.summary: After Eglon's death, Ehud rallied the Israelites, who seized the Jordan fords and slaughtered ten thousand Moabite warriors, completely subduing Moab. Israel enjoyed eighty years of peace.

7:3.title: Shamgar Delivers Israel from the Philistines
7:3.summary: Shamgar, the son of Anath, became a judge after Ehud and delivered Israel by striking down six hundred Philistines with an oxgoad.

7:4.title: Israel's Oppression Under Jabin
7:4.summary: After Ehud's death, Israel again turns to evil and is sold into the hand of Jabin, king of Canaan, whose military commander Sisera oppresses them with 900 iron chariots for twenty years until they cry out to the Lord.

7:4.title: Deborah Summons Barak
7:4.summary: Deborah, a prophetess and judge of Israel, calls the military commander Barak to lead ten thousand men against Sisera, promising that God will deliver the enemy into his hands; Barak insists she accompany him, and she agrees while prophesying that a woman will receive the honor of Sisera's defeat.

7:4.title: Preparation for Battle
7:4.summary: Barak assembles ten thousand troops from Zebulun and Naphtali at Mount Tabor with Deborah; when Sisera's forces are reported moving toward the river Kishon, Deborah declares that Yahweh has delivered Sisera into Barak's hand and commands him to attack.

7:4.title: The Defeat of Sisera's Army
7:4.summary: Yahweh confuses Sisera and his chariots in battle, causing his army to be defeated by the sword before Barak; Sisera abandons his chariot and flees on foot while Barak pursues and destroys the entire army without mercy.

7:4.title: Sisera's Death at Jael's Tent
7:4.summary: The fleeing Sisera seeks refuge in the tent of Jael, wife of Heber the Kenite, who welcomes him with hospitality; while he sleeps, Jael drives a tent peg through his temples, killing him and fulfilling Deborah's prophecy that a woman would achieve his destruction.

7:4.title: Israel's Victory and Liberation
7:4.summary: Jael reveals Sisera's death to Barak, confirming the victory; as a result, God subdues Jabin and the hand of Israel grows stronger against him until they finally destroy him completely, ending the Canaanite oppression.

7:5.title: Introduction to the Song of Deborah
7:5.summary: Deborah and Barak sing a song of praise to Yahweh, calling all to hear as Deborah proclaims she will sing praise to the God of Israel for the victory granted them.

7:5.title: Yahweh's Mighty Power and Israel's Deliverance
7:5.summary: The song recounts how Yahweh marched forth with cosmic power, causing the earth to tremble, and how after a period of lawlessness and oppression, Deborah arose as a deliverer and the people willingly offered themselves for battle, preparing to rehearse Yahweh's righteous acts.

7:5.title: The Tribes' Response to Battle
7:5.summary: Deborah summons Barak to lead the army and capture prisoners, and the song catalogs which Israelite tribes responded to the call to fight—praising those like Zebulun and Naphtali who jeopardized their lives, while reproaching those like Reuben, Gilead, Dan, and Asher who refused to participate.

7:5.title: The Battle of Taanach and Divine Victory
7:5.summary: The Canaanite kings fought at Taanach but were defeated through Yahweh's intervention—the stars from heaven fought against them, the river Kishon swept away their army, and the angel of Yahweh curses Meroz for failing to aid in the victory.

7:5.title: Jael's Heroic Deed
7:5.summary: Jael, the wife of Heber the Kenite, is blessed above all women for offering hospitality to the fleeing general Sisera, then killing him by driving a tent peg through his head as he rested—securing Israel's victory through her courageous action.

7:5.title: Sisera's Mother's Lament and Final Blessing
7:5.summary: The song concludes by depicting Sisera's mother waiting and wondering why her son's chariot delays, with her ladies assuring her he is taking spoil, then Deborah pronounces a final blessing that Yahweh's enemies perish while those who love him shine like the sun, and the land experiences forty years of peace.

7:6.title: Israel's Oppression by Midian
7:6.summary: Because Israel does evil in God's sight, He delivers them into the hands of the Midianites for seven years. The Midianites devastate the land so thoroughly that Israel is brought very low and cries out to the Lord.

7:6.title: God Sends a Prophet to Rebuke Israel
7:6.summary: In response to Israel's cry, God sends a prophet who reminds them of all He has done for them—delivering them from Egypt and conquering their enemies—and rebukes them for not listening to His voice and fearing foreign gods instead.

7:6.title: The Call of Gideon and the Angel's Sign
7:6.summary: The angel of the Lord appears to Gideon while he threshes wheat in hiding and calls him to save Israel. Though Gideon doubts, he requests a sign; the angel consumes his offering with fire, confirming His divine identity. Gideon builds an altar called 'Yahweh is Peace' in response.

7:6.title: Gideon Destroys the Altar of Baal
7:6.summary: God commands Gideon to tear down his father's altar to Baal and build an altar to the Lord instead. Gideon obeys at night out of fear, and when discovered, his father defends him, arguing that if Baal is a god, he can defend himself. This earns Gideon the name Jerub-Baal.
themes: fear of man versus fear of God

7:6.title: The Spirit Empowers Gideon to Gather Israel
7:6.summary: As the Midianites and their allies gather to invade, the Spirit of the Lord comes upon Gideon. He sounds a trumpet and summons warriors from Manasseh, Asher, Zebulun, and Naphtali to join him against the enemy.

7:6.title: Gideon's Test with the Fleece
7:6.summary: Gideon asks God for a sign—requesting that dew fall only on a fleece of wool while the ground remains dry. God grants this sign, and when Gideon asks for the reverse to be sure, God repeats the miracle, confirming His promise to deliver Israel through Gideon.

7:7.title: The Initial Reduction of Gideon's Army
7:7.summary: Gideon camps by the spring of Harod facing the Midianites. God tells him his army is too large lest Israel take credit for the victory rather than God. Gideon calls for the fearful to leave, and 22,000 depart, leaving 10,000 men.

7:7.title: Testing the Army at the Water
7:7.summary: God instructs Gideon to test the remaining soldiers at the water to further reduce their number. Those who lap water like a dog (300 men) are selected, while those who kneel to drink are dismissed, leaving only 300 for the battle.

7:7.title: Gideon's Encouragement Through the Enemy's Dream
7:7.summary: God tells Gideon to scout the enemy camp; if fearful, he may take his servant. Gideon overhears a Midianite recounting a dream of a barley cake destroying the camp, which his companion interprets as Gideon's sword. Encouraged, Gideon worships and rallies Israel, declaring God has delivered Midian into their hand.

7:7.title: Instructions for the Night Attack
7:7.summary: Gideon divides the 300 men into three companies, giving each trumpets, empty pitchers with torches inside. He instructs them to watch his actions, blow trumpets when he does, and shout 'For Yahweh and for Gideon!' to confuse and terrify the enemy.

7:7.title: The Miraculous Victory at Midian's Camp
7:7.summary: At midnight, Gideon and his men blow trumpets and smash pitchers, revealing torches and shouting. The Midianite army panics and flees in confusion, with God causing them to turn their swords against one another. The enemy army scatters toward Beth Shittah and beyond.

7:7.title: Pursuit and Capture of Midianite Leaders
7:7.summary: Men from Naphtali, Asher, and Manasseh pursue the fleeing Midianites. Gideon summons Ephraim to cut off the enemy at the Jordan. The Ephraimites capture and kill the Midianite princes Oreb and Zeeb, bringing their heads to Gideon beyond the Jordan.

7:8.title: Gideon Appeases the Men of Ephraim
7:8.summary: The men of Ephraim angrily confront Gideon for not calling them to fight against Midian. Gideon skillfully deflects their anger by crediting them with the greater victory, reminding them that God delivered the Midianite princes into their hands.

7:8.title: Gideon's Pursuit and the Cities' Refusal
7:8.summary: Gideon pursues the Midianite kings Zebah and Zalmunna with his three hundred men, asking Succoth and Penuel for provisions. Both cities refuse and mock him, so Gideon threatens to punish them once he returns victorious.

7:8.title: Gideon Defeats Zebah and Zalmunna
7:8.summary: Gideon finds the Midianite army of fifteen thousand men encamped at Karkor and attacks them by surprise, capturing the two kings Zebah and Zalmunna while routing their entire force.

7:8.title: Gideon Punishes Succoth and Penuel
7:8.summary: After his victory, Gideon returns and executes his threatened punishments: he gathers intelligence on Succoth's seventy-seven leaders, teaches them a brutal lesson with thorns and briers, and tears down Penuel's tower while killing its men.

7:8.title: Gideon Executes the Midianite Kings
7:8.summary: Gideon learns that Zebah and Zalmunna had killed men at Tabor, revealing they were his own brothers. When his son Jether refuses to execute them, Gideon kills the kings himself and takes trophies from their camels.

7:8.title: Gideon Refuses Kingship but Makes an Ephod
7:8.summary: Israel offers Gideon a hereditary monarchy, which he refuses, affirming that only Yahweh should rule. However, Gideon then requests the golden earrings from the spoil and fashions them into an ephod in Ophrah, which becomes an object of idolatrous worship and a spiritual snare.

7:8.title: Gideon's Legacy and Israel's Unfaithfulness After His Death
7:8.summary: Midian is subdued and the land enjoys forty years of peace under Gideon's leadership. After his death at an old age, Israel immediately abandons Yahweh and turns to Baal worship, forgetting Gideon's deliverance and showing no kindness to his family.

7:9.title: Abimelech's Rise to Power
7:9.summary: Abimelech convinces the men of Shechem that he, as their kinsman, should rule over them rather than Gideon's seventy sons. Using money from the temple of Baal, he hires mercenaries, murders all his brothers except Jotham who escapes, and is crowned king.

7:9.title: Jotham's Parable of the Trees
7:9.summary: From Mount Gerizim, Jotham delivers a prophetic parable in which noble trees refuse kingship while a worthless bramble accepts it, threatening to consume all. He applies this to Abimelech and Shechem's treachery against Gideon's house, then flees for his life.

7:9.title: God Sends Division and Rebellion
7:9.summary: After ruling three years, an evil spirit from God creates discord between Abimelech and Shechem, fulfilling the curse on the murderers and those who enabled the fratricide. The men of Shechem begin ambushing travelers on the roads.

7:9.title: Gaal's Challenge and Zebul's Treachery
7:9.summary: Gaal the son of Ebed gains the men of Shechem's confidence and openly challenges Abimelech's rule. Zebul, the city's ruler, secretly informs Abimelech and instructs him to ambush Gaal in the morning.

7:9.title: Abimelech Defeats Gaal
7:9.summary: Abimelech sets an ambush in four companies, and when Gaal emerges to confront him, he is defeated and flees. Zebul then expels Gaal and his brothers from the city, leaving Abimelech in control.

7:9.title: Abimelech Destroys Shechem
7:9.summary: The next day Abimelech attacks the city in three companies, slaughters its inhabitants, demolishes it, and sows it with salt—completely destroying the city that had made him king.

7:9.title: The Tower of Shechem Destroyed
7:9.summary: When the survivors flee to the stronghold of the temple of El-berith, Abimelech and his men gather branches from Mount Zalmon, pile them against the tower, and burn it, killing a thousand men and women.

7:9.title: Abimelech's Death at Thebez
7:9.summary: Abimelech attacks Thebez and besieges its tower, but a woman drops an upper millstone on his head, mortally wounding him. He calls his armor-bearer to kill him so he will not be remembered as slain by a woman.

7:9.title: God's Requital and Jotham's Curse Fulfilled
7:9.summary: With Abimelech dead, the Israelites disperse to their homes. God repays Abimelech's wickedness in murdering his brothers and the men of Shechem's treachery, fulfilling Jotham's curse upon them all.

7:10.title: Tola and Jair as Judges
7:10.summary: After Abimelech, two judges arise to lead Israel: Tola judges for 23 years, and Jair judges for 22 years, with his thirty sons riding thirty donkeys and ruling thirty cities in Gilead. These judges bring a period of relative peace and prosperity to Israel.

7:10.title: Israel's Idolatry and Oppression
7:10.summary: Israel again turns to evil, abandoning Yahweh to serve the gods of surrounding nations, prompting God's anger to kindle against them. He sells them into the hands of the Philistines and Ammonites, who oppress Israel for eighteen years, especially the eastern tribes.

7:10.title: Israel's Repentance and God's Rebuke
7:10.summary: Distressed, Israel cries out to Yahweh confessing their sin, but God responds with a rebuke, recounting how He has repeatedly saved them from enemies, yet they continue to forsake Him. He declares He will save them no more and commands them to cry to the gods they have chosen.

7:10.title: Israel's Renewed Repentance and God's Compassion
7:10.summary: Israel makes a complete confession of sin, submitting entirely to God's judgment, and removes all foreign gods to serve Yahweh alone. Moved with compassion for Israel's misery, God's soul is grieved and He responds with mercy.

7:10.title: Israel Prepares for Battle Against Ammon
7:10.summary: As the Ammonites gather and camp in Gilead, the Israelites assemble at Mizpah, and the people's leaders seek someone to lead them into battle, promising that leader will become head over all Gilead. This sets the stage for the emergence of Jephthah as a deliverer.

7:11.title: Jephthah's Rejection and Exile
7:11.summary: Jephthah, a mighty warrior born to a prostitute, is driven out by his half-brothers who deny him inheritance. He flees to Tob where he gathers a band of outcasts around him.

7:11.title: The Elders Recall Jephthah
7:11.summary: When the Ammonites attack Israel, the elders of Gilead seek out Jephthah and offer him leadership if he will lead them in battle. Jephthah negotiates for full authority and the people covenant with him before Yahweh at Mizpah.

7:11.title: Jephthah's Diplomatic Dispute with Ammon
7:11.summary: Jephthah sends messengers to the Ammonite king to negotiate, but the king claims Israel stole Ammonite lands. Jephthah responds with a detailed historical account proving Israel obtained the land through conflict with the Amorites, not Ammon, and appeals to Yahweh as judge. The Ammonite king rejects his words.

7:11.title: Jephthah's Vow and Victory
7:11.summary: Empowered by the Spirit of Yahweh, Jephthah vows that whatever first meets him upon his return will be offered as a burnt offering to Yahweh. He defeats the Ammonites in a great slaughter across twenty cities.

7:11.title: Jephthah's Daughter and the Fulfillment of His Vow
7:11.summary: Jephthah's only daughter comes out to greet him with celebration, but he realizes she is the price of his vow. After allowing her two months to mourn her virginity with her companions, Jephthah fulfills his vow. The incident becomes an annual commemoration in Israel.

7:12.title: Ephraim's Complaint and Jephthah's Defense
7:12.summary: The men of Ephraim confront Jephthah angrily for fighting the Ammonites without summoning them, threatening to burn his house. Jephthah defends his actions, explaining that he called upon them but they refused to help, so he risked his life and God gave him victory.

7:12.title: The Battle of the Fords and the Shibboleth Test
7:12.summary: Jephthah gathers Gilead's men and defeats Ephraim in battle, then stations guards at the Jordan fords to intercept fleeing Ephraimites by testing their pronunciation of 'Shibboleth,' killing 42,000 who fail the test. The incident demonstrates how linguistic and cultural markers become instruments of violence and tribal division.

7:12.title: Jephthah's Judgeship and Death
7:12.summary: Jephthah judges Israel for six years before dying and being buried in one of the cities of Gilead, concluding his tenure as a judge.

7:12.title: Ibzan's Judgeship
7:12.summary: Ibzan of Bethlehem succeeds Jephthah as judge, distinguishing himself by having thirty sons and thirty daughters, marrying the daughters off to foreign men and bringing in thirty foreign wives for his sons, and judges Israel for seven years before dying and being buried at Bethlehem.

7:12.title: Elon's Judgeship
7:12.summary: Elon the Zebulunite judges Israel for ten years and is buried in Aijalon in the land of Zebulun, providing a brief record of another judge's tenure and death.

7:12.title: Abdon's Judgeship
7:12.summary: Abdon son of Hillel the Pirathonite succeeds as judge, notable for his large family of forty sons and thirty grandsons who ride on seventy donkey colts, judges Israel for eight years, and is buried in Pirathon in the Ephraimite hill country. His wealth and progeny exemplify the blessings of a prosperous judge during the period of the judges.

7:13.title: Israel's Oppression and Manoah's Family
7:13.summary: Israel again does evil and is delivered into Philistine hands for forty years. A man named Manoah of Zorah has a barren wife, setting the stage for divine intervention.

7:13.title: The Angel Announces Samson's Birth
7:13.summary: The angel of Yahweh appears to Manoah's barren wife and announces she will bear a son who will be a Nazirite and begin to deliver Israel from the Philistines.

7:13.title: The Woman Reports to Manoah
7:13.summary: Manoah's wife tells her husband about the divine visitor and reiterates the instructions regarding the child's Nazirite status and her own dietary restrictions.

7:13.title: Manoah Seeks Confirmation and Instruction
7:13.summary: Manoah prays for the angel to return and teach them how to raise the child; the angel reappears and confirms the previous message, emphasizing the mother's obligations regarding diet and purity.

7:13.title: The Angel's Miraculous Ascension
