7:13.summary: Manoah attempts to offer hospitality and sacrifice; the angel refuses food but accepts a burnt offering, then ascends in the flame of the altar, revealing his divine nature to the stunned parents.

7:13.title: Samson's Birth and Early Blessing
7:13.summary: Manoah fears death from seeing God, but his wife reassures him based on God's gracious actions; they bear a son named Samson whom Yahweh blesses, and the Spirit of Yahweh begins to move him.

7:14.title: Samson Desires a Philistine Woman
7:14.summary: Samson sees a Philistine woman in Timnah and demands his parents arrange their marriage, ignoring their objection to him marrying an uncircumcised foreigner. Unknown to his parents, God is orchestrating events to give Samson an opportunity to strike against the Philistines who rule Israel.

7:14.title: Samson Kills a Lion and Finds Honey
7:14.summary: On the way to Timnah, a young lion attacks Samson, whom the Spirit of the Lord empowers to tear it apart with his bare hands. Later, Samson discovers a swarm of bees and honey in the lion's carcass, which he eats and shares with his parents without revealing its source.

7:14.title: Samson Proposes a Riddle at the Wedding Feast
7:14.summary: At the wedding feast in Timnah, Samson challenges thirty Philistine men to solve a riddle within seven days, wagering thirty sets of garments on the outcome. The riddle—referencing the honey from the dead lion—proves too difficult for them to solve in three days.

7:14.title: Samson's Wife Betrays the Riddle
7:14.summary: The Philistines threaten to burn Samson's wife and her father's house unless she extracts the riddle's answer from him. After she weeps and pressures him for seven days, Samson reveals the riddle to her, and she immediately tells the Philistines.

7:14.title: Samson's Violent Revenge and Broken Marriage
7:14.summary: The Philistines solve the riddle by exploiting Samson's wife, prompting Samson to kill thirty Philistine men to pay his wager and fulfill his obligation. In retaliation for his betrayal, Samson's wife is given to his best friend, destroying the marriage.

7:15.title: Samson's Wife Given to Another
7:15.summary: Samson returns to visit his wife during harvest time with a gift, but her father refuses him entry, claiming he thought Samson hated her and had given her to his companion instead, offering her younger sister as compensation. Samson declares he will now harm the Philistines without guilt.

7:15.title: Samson Burns the Philistine Crops
7:15.summary: Samson captures three hundred foxes, ties torches between their tails, and releases them into the Philistine grain fields and olive groves, destroying the harvest. The Philistines discover his motive and burn his former wife and her father to death.

7:15.title: Samson's Great Slaughter of the Philistines
7:15.summary: Samson vows further vengeance against the Philistines for burning his wife, strikes them with a great slaughter, and then retreats to hide in the rock of Etam.

7:15.title: Men of Judah Bind Samson
7:15.summary: The Philistines invade Judah seeking Samson; three thousand Judahites go to Etam and agree to bind Samson and deliver him to the Philistines, securing his promise they will not kill him themselves.

7:15.title: Samson Slays a Thousand with a Donkey's Jawbone
7:15.summary: As the Philistines rush forward, God's Spirit empowers Samson to break his bonds; he finds a fresh donkey's jawbone and kills a thousand men with it, then celebrates the victory in a brief song before casting away the weapon.

7:15.title: God Provides Water and Samson Judges Israel
7:15.summary: Exhausted and thirsty after his victory, Samson cries out to God; God miraculously causes water to spring from a hollow place in the rock, reviving him. Samson judges Israel for twenty years during the Philistine period.

7:16.title: Samson in Gaza
7:16.summary: Samson visits a prostitute in Gaza, and when the Philistines lay an ambush to kill him at the gate, he escapes by tearing out the gate doors and carrying them to a distant hilltop. This episode demonstrates his superhuman strength but also his vulnerability to fleshly desires.

7:16.title: Delilah's Three Failed Attempts to Betray Samson
7:16.summary: Samson falls in love with Delilah, whom the Philistine lords bribe to discover the source of his strength. She makes three deceptive attempts using green cords, new ropes, and weaving his hair, each time falsely testing him while an ambush waits in hiding, but Samson breaks free each time. His repeated deception and naiveté foreshadow his eventual downfall.

7:16.title: Samson Reveals His Secret and Loses His Strength
7:16.summary: After Delilah persistently accuses him of mocking her, Samson confesses that his strength comes from his uncut hair as a Nazirite vow to God. Delilah lulls him to sleep, has his hair shaved off, and calls for the Philistines; when Samson awakes, he is unaware that God has departed from him and he is captured. This marks the tragic consequence of his betrayal of his covenant with God.

7:16.title: Samson's Captivity and the Beginning of Restoration
7:16.summary: The Philistines blind Samson, bind him in chains, and force him to grind grain in prison. Yet his hair begins to grow back, symbolizing the possibility of renewed strength and God's potential restoration even in his darkest hour.

7:16.title: The Philistine Victory Celebration
7:16.summary: The Philistine lords gather in Dagon's temple to celebrate their victory, with thousands watching from the roof and floor as they demand that the imprisoned Samson entertain them. This moment of Philistine triumph sets the stage for God's final vindication.

7:16.title: Samson's Final Act of Vengeance and Death
7:16.summary: Samson prays to God for one final strengthening, positions himself between the temple's pillars, and pulls them down, killing himself along with all the Philistine lords and thousands of people—achieving in his death a greater victory than in his life. He is buried by his family, having served as judge of Israel for twenty years.

7:17.title: Micah Restores Stolen Silver and Makes an Idol
7:17.summary: A man named Micah from Ephraim confesses to his mother that he stole eleven hundred pieces of silver from her. His mother blesses him and dedicates the silver to Yahweh to make an engraved and molten image, using two hundred pieces of the silver for this purpose.

7:17.title: Micah Establishes a Household Shrine
7:17.summary: Micah creates a house of gods with an ephod and teraphim, consecrating one of his sons as his priest. The narrator notes that this lawlessness occurs because there is no king in Israel and everyone does what is right in their own eyes.

7:17.title: A Levite Becomes Micah's Priest
7:17.summary: A young Levite from Bethlehem Judah comes to Micah's house seeking shelter, and Micah hires him as his priest, promising him annual payment, clothing, and food. The Levite agrees and becomes a trusted member of Micah's household.

7:17.title: Micah Consecrates the Levite and Expects God's Blessing
7:17.summary: Micah formally consecrates the Levite as his priest and declares confidence that Yahweh will bless him because he now has a legitimate Levite serving at his household shrine. This represents Micah's misguided belief that having a Levite priest validates his idolatrous worship.

7:18.title: The Danites Seek an Inheritance
7:18.summary: The tribe of Dan, lacking their own inherited territory, sends five brave men to scout out new land for settlement. This begins their journey to find a place to dwell.

7:18.title: The Spies Encounter Micah's Levite Priest
7:18.summary: The Danite spies recognize the Levite priest at Micah's house and ask him to seek God's counsel for their mission. The priest assures them their way is before the Lord and will prosper.

7:18.title: The Spies Report on Laish
7:18.summary: The scouts return with news of Laish, a wealthy, undefended city living in security with no powerful neighbors. They encourage their tribe to conquer this vulnerable land that God has supposedly given them.

7:18.title: The Danite Army Mobilizes and Marches
7:18.summary: Six hundred armed Danite men set out from Zorah and Eshtaol, camp at Kiriath Jearim, and proceed toward the hill country of Ephraim to reach Micah's house.

7:18.title: The Danites Steal Micah's Idols and Recruit His Priest
7:18.summary: The five spies inform their brothers of Micah's religious idols and encourage them to take these items. The armed men seize the engraved image, ephod, teraphim, and molten image while the priest stands guard, then persuade the priest to abandon Micah and become the tribal priest instead.

7:18.title: Micah's Futile Pursuit and Surrender
7:18.summary: The Danites depart with the stolen goods and priest, placing their families and livestock ahead. Micah and his neighbors pursue them, but when they confront the Danites about the theft, the Danites threaten them with violence, forcing Micah to turn back powerless.

7:18.title: The Danites Conquer Laish and Rename It
7:18.summary: The Danites attack the peaceful, undefended city of Laish with complete success, burning it to the ground. They rebuild the city and rename it Dan, after their tribal ancestor.

7:18.title: Establishment of Idolatry at Dan
7:18.summary: The Danites set up Micah's stolen engraved image at their new city and establish Jonathan son of Gershom (grandson of Moses) and his descendants as priests to the tribe, perpetuating this idolatrous system throughout the period before the exile.

7:19.title: The Levite and His Unfaithful Concubine
7:19.summary: A Levite from the hill country of Ephraim takes a concubine from Bethlehem, but she abandons him to return to her father's house. The Levite travels to retrieve her, and her father welcomes him warmly, detaining him for three days of hospitality.

7:19.title: The Father-in-Law's Delay
7:19.summary: The Levite attempts to depart on the fourth day, but his father-in-law repeatedly persuades him to stay longer by offering food and fellowship. On the fifth day, the man finally departs with his concubine and servant, refusing to stay in Jebus despite the late hour.

7:19.title: Seeking Shelter in Gibeah
7:19.summary: As darkness falls near Jerusalem, the servant urges them to lodge in the Jebusite city, but the Levite refuses to stay among foreigners and insists on reaching Gibeah in Benjamin's territory. They arrive after sunset and sit in the street, as no one offers them hospitality.

7:19.title: An Old Man's Hospitality
7:19.summary: An old Ephraimite man from the same hill country encounters the travelers in the street and offers them shelter, providing for them generously and restoring their dignity after being rejected by the townspeople.

7:19.title: The Sexual Assault and Tragedy
7:19.summary: Wicked men of Gibeah surround the house demanding the Levite for sexual abuse; the old man and the Levite offer alternatives, but the men seize the concubine and brutally assault her throughout the night. She crawls back to the house at dawn and collapses at the threshold.

7:19.title: The Levite's Horrific Response
7:19.summary: The Levite discovers his concubine dead at the doorway, loads her body on his donkey, and upon reaching home dismembers her corpse into twelve pieces, sending them throughout Israel as a shocking message that provokes outrage and calls for judgment against the crime.

7:20.title: Assembly at Mizpah and the Levite's Account
7:20.summary: All Israel gathers as one at Mizpah to hear how a terrible wickedness occurred. A Levite recounts how men of Gibeah murdered his concubine after assaulting her, and he distributed her dismembered body throughout Israel as a call to justice.

7:20.title: Israel's Covenant Response
7:20.summary: All the people unanimously swear an oath to take no rest until they execute judgment, and begin preparations to march against Gibeah with a unified military force.

7:20.title: Ultimatum to Benjamin and Military Mobilization
7:20.summary: Israel demands Benjamin surrender the guilty men of Gibeah, but Benjamin refuses and instead musters its own fighting force. The account emphasizes Benjamin's skilled warriors, including left-handed slingers, but Israel vastly outnumbers them.

7:20.title: First Two Battles: Israel's Defeats
7:20.summary: Israel seeks God's counsel and is told Judah shall lead first. However, in both the first and second day's battles, Benjamin inflicts devastating defeats on Israel—22,000 then 18,000 men—despite Israel's numerical superiority, demonstrating the uncertainty of war.

7:20.title: Israel's Repentance and Third Inquiry of God
7:20.summary: After the second defeat, Israel weeps, fasts, and offers sacrifices before the ark at Bethel. When they inquire of God again through Phinehas the priest, God promises victory on the third day.

7:20.title: The Third Battle: Israel's Ambush and Victory
7:20.summary: Israel sets a hidden ambush at Gibeah and feigns retreat, drawing Benjamin away from the city as in previous battles. When Benjamin pursues confidently, the ambushers strike the city, signal with smoke, and Israel turns to rout Benjamin completely, killing 25,100 warriors and destroying the city.

7:20.title: Benjamin's Remnant and Israel's Scorched-Earth Campaign
7:20.summary: Six hundred Benjamites escape to the rock of Rimmon and survive there for four months, while Israel systematically destroys all the remaining cities of Benjamin, killing livestock and burning everything.

7:21.title: Israel's Dilemma: The Oath and Benjamin's Need
7:21.summary: The Israelites had sworn not to give their daughters to Benjamin in marriage, but now they mourn Benjamin's near extinction and face an impossible conflict between their oath and the need to preserve the tribe. They gather at Bethel to lament before God, recognizing the crisis created by their own vow.

7:21.title: The Attack on Jabesh Gilead and Procurement of Wives
7:21.summary: Israel discovers that Jabesh Gilead did not participate in the assembly, so they attack the city and capture four hundred young virgin women, whom they give to the surviving Benjamites at Shiloh. Though this partially alleviates the shortage, the women are still insufficient for all who remain.

7:21.title: The Scheme of the Dancing Women
7:21.summary: Unable to break their oath directly, the elders devise a plan: they instruct the Benjamites to hide in the vineyards near Shiloh and seize the dancing women during Yahweh's yearly feast, circumventing the oath by having the men take wives rather than receive them as gifts. The plan succeeds, and the Benjamites secure wives and return to rebuild their cities.

7:21.title: Conclusion: The Absence of Kingship and Moral Anarchy
7:21.summary: Israel disperses to their tribal lands, and the narrator concludes by emphasizing that in those days there was no king in Israel, resulting in each person doing what seemed right in their own eyes. This editorial comment reflects on the chaos and moral relativism that characterizes this dark period in Israel's history.

8:1.title: Elimelech's Family Moves to Moab and Faces Loss
8:1.summary: During a famine in Judah, Elimelech takes his wife Naomi and two sons to Moab seeking survival. After Elimelech's death, his sons marry Moabite women but die within ten years, leaving Naomi bereft of husband and children.

8:1.title: Naomi's Decision to Return Home
8:1.summary: Hearing that God has ended the famine in Judah, Naomi decides to return home and urges her daughters-in-law to stay in Moab and remarry, blessing them for their loyalty to her family.

8:1.title: Naomi Urges Her Daughters-in-Law to Turn Back
8:1.summary: Both daughters-in-law initially insist on going with Naomi, but she persistently argues they should return to Moab because she has no more sons to offer them as husbands, appealing to God's judgment against her. Orpah eventually agrees to leave, but Ruth refuses.

8:1.title: Ruth's Pledge of Loyalty
8:1.summary: Ruth commits herself unconditionally to Naomi with a solemn oath, declaring she will share Naomi's home, people, and God, and remain with her until death. Naomi accepts Ruth's determination and stops trying to send her away.

8:1.title: Naomi and Ruth Arrive in Bethlehem
8:1.summary: Naomi and Ruth return to Bethlehem where they are recognized but Naomi, grieving her losses, asks to be called Mara (bitter) instead, saying God has afflicted her. They arrive at the beginning of barley harvest, marking a new season of hope.

8:2.title: Ruth Seeks Gleaning Work
8:2.summary: Naomi has a wealthy kinsman named Boaz. Ruth asks permission to glean in the fields, and by divine providence, she finds herself working in Boaz's field.

8:2.title: Boaz Shows Favor to Ruth
8:2.summary: Boaz arrives at his field, learns Ruth's identity and reputation from his servant, and then instructs her to stay close to his workers and grants her special protection and provision.

8:2.title: Ruth's Gratitude and Boaz's Recognition
8:2.summary: Ruth humbly questions why she has found favor despite being a foreigner, and Boaz praises her faithfulness to Naomi, commending her for seeking refuge under God's wings.

8:2.title: Ruth's Abundant Harvest
8:2.summary: Boaz invites Ruth to share a meal with the workers and secretly instructs his men to leave extra grain for her; she gleans until evening and collects about an ephah of barley.

8:2.title: Naomi Recognizes God's Plan
8:2.summary: Ruth returns to Naomi with her abundant grain and reveals that Boaz is their kinsman; Naomi recognizes God's continued kindness and advises Ruth to continue gleaning in Boaz's field through the harvest.

8:3.title: Naomi's Plan for Ruth's Future
8:3.summary: Naomi devises a strategy to secure Ruth's future by instructing her to approach Boaz at the threshing floor and appeal to him as a kinsman-redeemer. The plan involves Ruth making herself presentable and then requesting Boaz's protection through the custom of spreading his garment over her.

8:3.title: Ruth Follows Naomi's Instructions
8:3.summary: Ruth obeys her mother-in-law completely and goes to the threshing floor where she finds Boaz eating and drinking with joy. She then lies down at his feet as instructed.

8:3.title: Ruth's Nighttime Appeal to Boaz
8:3.summary: Boaz awakens at midnight, startled to discover Ruth at his feet, and she respectfully asks him to act as kinsman-redeemer. Boaz responds with praise for her character and loyalty, but reveals there is another kinsman closer in line who must be given the opportunity first, though he himself will act if that kinsman refuses.

8:3.title: Boaz's Gift and Discretion
8:3.summary: Ruth remains at Boaz's feet until morning, then departs before others can see her. Boaz gives her six measures of barley as a gift and assurance, instructing her not to return empty-handed to Naomi.

8:3.title: Ruth Reports to Naomi
8:3.summary: Ruth recounts everything to Naomi, including the barley gift, and Naomi confidently assures her that Boaz will resolve the matter that same day, as he is a man of integrity and initiative who will not rest until the kinsman-redeemer issue is settled.

8:4.title: The Redemption Transaction at the Gate
8:4.summary: Boaz publicly brings a closer kinsman to the city gate before elders to offer him the right to redeem Naomi's land. When the kinsman learns that redeeming the land also requires marrying Ruth and raising up her dead husband's name, he declines to protect his own inheritance. He formally cedes his right of redemption to Boaz by removing his shoe in accordance with Israelite custom.

8:4.title: Boaz's Public Declaration and Blessing
8:4.summary: Boaz formally declares to the assembled elders and people that he has purchased Naomi's land and Ruth as his wife to preserve her dead husband's name and inheritance. The community witnesses the transaction and blesses Boaz and Ruth, invoking the names of Rachel, Leah, and Tamar as exemplars of faithfulness and fruitfulness in Israel's history.

8:4.title: The Birth of Obed and Naomi's Restoration
8:4.summary: Boaz takes Ruth as his wife, and the Lord grants her conception and she bears a son. The women celebrate with Naomi, praising the Lord for providing her with a kinsman-redeemer and a grandson who will sustain her in her old age; they name the child Obed, declaring that he is born to restore Naomi's life.

8:4.title: Genealogy: From Perez to David
8:4.summary: The narrative concludes with a genealogical record tracing Obed's descent through Boaz back to Perez (son of Judah and Tamar), and forward through Jesse to King David, establishing the historical and spiritual significance of this union within Israel's royal lineage.

9:1.title: Introduction to Elkanah and His Household
9:1.summary: Elkanah, an Ephraimite from Ramathaim Zophim, has two wives: Hannah (childless) and Peninnah (with children). He regularly travels to Shiloh to worship and sacrifice to Yahweh of Armies at the temple where Eli's sons serve as priests.

9:1.title: Hannah's Sorrow and Peninnah's Provocation
9:1.summary: During sacrificial meals, Elkanah gives Peninnah and her children portions but gives Hannah a double portion out of love, yet Peninnah continually provokes Hannah about her barrenness, causing her deep grief and loss of appetite. Elkanah attempts to console Hannah, asking if his love isn't worth more than sons.

9:1.title: Hannah's Vow and Prayer
9:1.summary: In bitter anguish at the temple, Hannah prays to Yahweh with tears, making a vow that if God grants her a son, she will dedicate him to the Lord's service for his entire life as a Nazirite. Her prayer represents her desperate faith and willingness to surrender her deepest desire to God's purposes.

9:1.title: Eli's Misunderstanding and Blessing
9:1.summary: Eli observes Hannah praying silently with only her lips moving and mistakenly thinks she is drunk; when she clarifies that she pours out her soul in sorrow before God, Eli blesses her with the assurance that God will grant her petition. Hannah's countenance changes, and she finds peace.

9:1.title: The Birth of Samuel
9:1.summary: After worshiping at the temple and returning home, Elkanah and Hannah conceive; Hannah bears a son and names him Samuel, saying 'Because I have asked him of Yahweh,' fulfilling God's answer to her prayer.

9:1.title: Hannah Dedicates Samuel to the Lord
9:1.summary: While Elkanah fulfills his yearly sacrificial vow, Hannah delays going until Samuel is weaned; she then brings him to the temple at Shiloh with offerings and presents him to Eli, reminding the priest of her prayer and explaining that she has now granted him to Yahweh for life. Hannah's dedication embodies her covenantal faith and willingness to surrender her long-desired son to divine service.

9:2.title: Hannah's Prayer of Thanksgiving and Praise
9:2.summary: Hannah sings a prayer of rejoicing in God's salvation, celebrating his holiness and sovereign power to overturn human fortunes. Her song proclaims that God judges all actions and will ultimately give strength to his king and exalt his anointed one.

9:2.title: Samuel's Ministry and Hannah's Blessing
9:2.summary: Samuel begins to minister before Yahweh in the temple while Eli's corrupt sons abuse their priestly office by greedily taking sacrificial meat. Hannah is blessed by Eli and conceives more children, while Samuel grows and finds favor with both God and people.

9:2.title: Eli Rebukes His Wicked Sons
9:2.summary: Eli, now very old, confronts his sons about their wickedness toward Israel and their sexual sins at the Tent of Meeting, but they refuse to listen because God has already decided to bring judgment upon them. Meanwhile, Samuel continues to grow in favor with both God and man.

9:2.title: God's Judgment Against Eli's House
9:2.summary: A man of God delivers a message to Eli proclaiming that God will judge his house for honoring his sons above God's worship, revoking the eternal priesthood promised to his ancestors. God will raise up a faithful priest in place of Eli's line, and Eli's descendants will face poverty and untimely death.

9:3.title: Samuel's Calling: The Setting
9:3.summary: Samuel serves as a young minister to Yahweh in the temple under Eli's care, while divine visions are rare in those days. The scene is set for a supernatural encounter during the night.

9:3.title: Samuel's Confusion: The Voice of God
9:3.summary: Yahweh calls Samuel three times, but Samuel, not yet knowing God's voice, mistakes the calls for Eli's summons. Only after the third call does Eli recognize that God is speaking to the child and instructs Samuel to respond appropriately.

9:3.title: The Lord's Message: Judgment on Eli's House
9:3.summary: Samuel responds properly to God's fourth call and receives a message that shocks Israel—Yahweh will bring judgment upon Eli's entire household forever because of the wickedness of Eli's sons and his failure to restrain them, judgment that no sacrifice can remove.

9:3.title: Samuel Reports the Vision to Eli
9:3.summary: Samuel hesitates to share the troubling vision with Eli but, pressed by the elder priest with an oath, obeys and reveals the entire message. Eli accepts the judgment with resignation, acknowledging God's will.

9:3.title: Samuel Established as a Prophet
9:3.summary: Samuel matures with Yahweh's blessing and protection, and all Israel comes to recognize him as a true prophet of God, established through the Lord's continued revelation to him in Shiloh.

9:4.title: Israel Defeated by the Philistines
9:4.summary: Israel engages the Philistines in battle at Ebenezer but is soundly defeated, with about four thousand men killed in the field. The defeat raises questions about God's presence and protection.

9:4.title: The Ark of the Covenant Brought to Battle
9:4.summary: The Israelite elders, seeking divine intervention, bring the Ark of the Covenant from Shiloh to the camp with the priests Hophni and Phinehas, inspiring great shouts among the people. The people hope the Ark's presence will secure victory against their enemies.

9:4.title: The Philistines' Fear and Resolve
9:4.summary: The Philistines learn that the Ark has arrived and initially fear God's presence, recalling His mighty acts against Egypt, but steel themselves to fight as men rather than submit to servitude. Their courage overcomes their initial terror.

9:4.title: Israel's Catastrophic Defeat and Loss of the Ark
9:4.summary: Despite the Ark's presence, Israel suffers a devastating defeat with thirty thousand footmen slain, the two sons of Eli are killed, and most tragically, the Ark itself is captured by the Philistines. The hoped-for divine intervention becomes instead a sign of God's judgment.

9:4.title: The Messenger Reports the Disaster to Eli
9:4.summary: A Benjamite survivor rushes from the battlefield to Shiloh with torn clothes and dirt on his head to deliver the catastrophic news to the aged priest Eli, reporting Israel's flight, the great slaughter, the deaths of Hophni and Phinehas, and the capture of the Ark. The messenger's appearance and words convey the gravity of the national tragedy.

9:4.title: The Deaths of Eli and Phinehas' Wife
9:4.summary: Upon hearing that the Ark is captured, the elderly Eli falls backward from his seat, breaks his neck, and dies after forty years of judging Israel; Phinehas' pregnant wife, upon learning of the Ark's capture and her husband and father-in-law's deaths, goes into labor and dies after naming her newborn son Ichabod ('the glory has departed') to commemorate Israel's loss of God's presence. The chapter concludes with a lament that God's glory has departed from Israel.

9:5.title: Dagon Falls Before the Ark
9:5.summary: The Philistines capture the ark of God and place it in Dagon's temple, but their idol falls prostrate before it twice, the second time with its head and hands severed. The Philistines thereafter avoid the temple's threshold, demonstrating God's supremacy over their false god.

9:5.title: God's Judgment Spreads Through Philistine Cities
9:5.summary: The Lord's hand brings severe judgment on Ashdod and then Gath, striking the inhabitants with tumors and great confusion. The Philistines recognize God's judgment and attempt to move the ark from city to city seeking relief, but His hand pursues them everywhere.

9:5.title: The Ark Arrives in Ekron; Philistines Plead for Its Return
9:5.summary: When the ark reaches Ekron, the city cries out in terror at the prospect of death, and the assembled Philistine lords decide to send the ark back to Israel rather than face further destruction. The plague and confusion devastate the city, demonstrating that possession of the ark brings death to those who reject God.

9:6.title: The Philistines Seek Counsel on Returning the Ark
9:6.summary: After seven months of plague, the Philistine leaders consult their priests and diviners about how to return the ark of God. They are instructed to send it back with a trespass offering of golden tumors and mice, and are warned not to harden their hearts as Pharaoh did.

9:6.title: Instructions for the Ark's Return and Test
9:6.summary: The Philistines are told to place the ark on a new cart pulled by two nursing cows separated from their calves, with the golden offerings beside it. They agree to let the cows go free, reasoning that if the cows naturally head toward Beth Shemesh, God has caused their suffering.

9:6.title: The Ark's Miraculous Journey to Beth Shemesh
9:6.summary: The Philistines follow the plan, and the cows miraculously walk straight to Beth Shemesh, lowing as they go without turning aside, proving that God—not chance—struck them with the plague.

9:6.title: Beth Shemesh Receives the Ark with Rejoicing
9:6.summary: The people of Beth Shemesh joyfully receive the ark, break up the cart as wood for a burnt offering, sacrifice the cows, and the Levites properly place the ark and golden offerings on a great stone.

9:6.title: Confirmation of Divine Power and Historical Record
9:6.summary: The five Philistine lords witness the return and depart, and the chapter records the five golden tumors and mice sent as restitution—one from each Philistine city—providing historical documentation of God's plague and mercy.

9:6.title: God's Strike on Beth Shemesh and the Ark Moved to Kiriath Jearim
9:6.summary: God strikes fifty thousand seventy men of Beth Shemesh for looking into the ark, prompting the people to acknowledge God's holiness and send messengers asking Kiriath Jearim to take the ark, recognizing they are unworthy to house it.

9:7.title: The Ark Brought to Kiriath Jearim
9:7.summary: The men of Kiriath Jearim retrieve the ark of the Lord and place it in Abinadab's house, with Eleazar consecrated as its keeper. For twenty years the ark remains there while all Israel mourns their separation from God.

9:7.title: Samuel's Call to Repentance and National Renewal
9:7.summary: Samuel calls Israel to wholehearted repentance, commanding them to abandon foreign gods and serve only Yahweh. The people respond by gathering at Mizpah, where they fast, confess their sins, and Samuel judges them.

9:7.title: Yahweh's Deliverance at Mizpah
9:7.summary: When the Philistines attack during Israel's gathering at Mizpah, Samuel intercedes with a burnt offering and Yahweh responds with thunder that confuses the enemy, allowing Israel to pursue and defeat them decisively.

9:7.title: Ebenezer and Lasting Peace
9:7.summary: Samuel erects a stone monument called Ebenezer to commemorate God's help, and Israel experiences lasting freedom from Philistine oppression, recovering their lost cities and establishing peace with neighboring peoples.

9:7.title: Samuel's Leadership and Legacy
9:7.summary: Samuel judges Israel throughout his lifetime, traveling in circuit through Bethel, Gilgal, and Mizpah to administer justice, and establishes his home at Ramah where he builds an altar to Yahweh.

9:8.title: Samuel's Sons as Judges
9:8.summary: Samuel appoints his two sons, Joel and Abijah, as judges over Israel in his old age, but they abandon his righteous example by pursuing bribes and perverting justice. Their corruption sets the stage for Israel's demand for institutional change.

9:8.title: Israel Demands a King
9:8.summary: The elders of Israel come to Samuel requesting that he appoint a king to rule over them like other nations, citing his age and his sons' unfaithfulness. Though displeased, Samuel prays and receives God's instruction to grant their request while warning them of the consequences.

9:8.title: Samuel's Warning About Kingship
9:8.summary: Samuel relays God's word to the people, detailing the oppressive practices a king will impose: conscription of their sons and daughters, seizure of their best lands and resources, forced labor, and taxation that will reduce them to servitude. He warns they will cry out for deliverance but God will not answer.

9:8.title: Israel Rejects Samuel's Warning and Chooses a King
9:8.summary: Despite Samuel's explicit warnings, the people refuse to listen and insist on having a king so they may be like other nations and have him fight their battles. Samuel reports their decision to God, who commands him to grant their request, and Samuel sends the people home.

9:9.title: Introduction of Saul
9:9.summary: Saul is introduced as a handsome, impressive young man from the tribe of Benjamin, superior in appearance to all the people of Israel, descending from a line of mighty men.

9:9.title: Saul Searches for Lost Donkeys
9:9.summary: Saul and a servant search extensively for his father's lost donkeys but cannot find them. When discouraged, the servant suggests consulting a seer in a nearby city, and Saul agrees to seek him out.

9:9.title: Meeting the Young Maidens and Finding Samuel
9:9.summary: As Saul and his servant approach the city, they ask young women about the seer's location and learn that Samuel has just arrived in the city and is about to bless a sacrifice, making this a providential moment to find him.

9:9.title: God's Revelation to Samuel About Saul
9:9.summary: God reveals to Samuel the day before that he will send a man from Benjamin to be anointed as prince over Israel to save the people from the Philistines, and when Samuel sees Saul, God confirms this is the man.

9:9.title: Samuel Receives Saul and Honors Him
9:9.summary: Samuel identifies himself as the seer, invites Saul to dine with him at the high place, and honors him with the choicest portion of food reserved for him, indicating his special significance.

9:9.title: Samuel Prepares to Anoint Saul
9:9.summary: After spending the night together, Samuel and Saul rise early, and Samuel begins to reveal God's word to Saul, setting the stage for his anointing as king.

9:10.title: Samuel Anoints Saul and Gives Him Signs
9:10.summary: Samuel anoints Saul as prince over Israel and provides him with three specific signs that will confirm God's calling: two men reporting the found donkeys, three men offering bread, and a band of prophets whose spirit will come upon him. Samuel instructs Saul to go to Gilgal and wait for him to offer sacrifices.

9:10.title: The Signs Are Fulfilled and Saul Prophesies
9:10.summary: As Saul departs from Samuel, God gives him a new heart and all three promised signs occur that day, culminating in Saul being overwhelmed by God's Spirit and prophesying among the prophets at the high place. His transformation amazes those who knew him, leading to the proverb 'Is Saul also among the prophets?'

9:10.title: Saul Conceals His Kingship from His Uncle
9:10.summary: When Saul's uncle asks where he has been, Saul reports only that he sought Samuel about the donkeys and that they were found, but he keeps secret the matter of the kingdom that Samuel revealed to him. This shows Saul's discretion in not immediately disclosing his anointing.

9:10.title: Saul Is Chosen by Lot Before All Israel
9:10.summary: Samuel gathers Israel at Mizpah and rebukes them for rejecting God by demanding a king, then uses sacred casting of lots to reveal God's chosen king—Saul of Benjamin. Though Saul initially hides among the baggage, he is brought forward and publicly presented as God's choice, with all the people acclaiming him as king.

9:10.title: Samuel Establishes the Kingdom and Saul Returns Home
9:10.summary: Samuel records the regulations of the kingdom before Yahweh and sends the people home; Saul returns to Gibeah accompanied by loyal men whose hearts God has stirred. However, some worthless men despise Saul and refuse to honor him, though he bears their contempt in silence.

9:11.title: Nahash's Ultimatum to Jabesh Gilead
9:11.summary: The Ammonite king Nahash besieges Jabesh Gilead and demands a humiliating covenant requiring the destruction of their right eyes as a reproach to Israel. The elders negotiate a seven-day reprieve to seek help from other Israelites.

9:11.title: Saul's Spirit-Empowered Response
9:11.summary: News of the crisis reaches Saul, and the Spirit of God comes upon him with power, moving him to summon all Israel through a shocking act of authority—cutting oxen and threatening judgment on those who refuse to follow. The people respond in unified obedience.

9:11.title: The Defeat of the Ammonites
9:11.summary: Saul musters a massive army of 330,000 soldiers, delivers an encouraging message to Jabesh Gilead promising deliverance by the next day, and then executes a perfectly coordinated three-pronged attack that completely routs the Ammonites, leaving none of them together.

9:11.title: Saul's Coronation at Gilgal
9:11.summary: The people, grateful for Saul's leadership and victory, seek to execute those who previously doubted him, but Saul shows mercy, attributing the victory to the Lord. Samuel leads the people to Gilgal to formally renew and confirm Saul's kingship with sacrifices and rejoicing.

9:12.title: Samuel's Integrity and Vindication
9:12.summary: Samuel presents himself before all Israel, establishing his advanced age and lineage, and invites the people to testify against him regarding any misconduct. The people unanimously affirm his integrity and honesty throughout his leadership.

9:12.title: The History of God's Salvation
9:12.summary: Samuel recounts Israel's history from Egypt through the wilderness period, detailing how despite God's repeated deliverance through judges like Moses, Aaron, Jerubbaal, Jephthah, and Samuel himself, the people continually forgot God and turned to idolatry. This historical review establishes God's faithfulness and Israel's pattern of unfaithfulness.

9:12.title: The Warning About the Kingship
9:12.summary: Samuel reminds Israel that despite God being their true king, they demanded a human king when threatened by Ammon. He warns that if they and their new king fear and obey God, all will be well; but if they rebel against God's commandment, His judgment will fall upon them as it did on their fathers.

9:12.title: The Sign of Thunder and Rain
9:12.summary: Samuel calls upon God to send thunder and rain during the wheat harvest as a sign of the people's sin in demanding a king. God responds miraculously, and the people are struck with fear, recognizing their wickedness.

9:12.title: Samuel's Assurance and Final Exhortation
9:12.summary: The frightened people ask Samuel to intercede for them, fearing death for their sin. Samuel reassures them not to be afraid, promises God will not forsake them for His name's sake, commits to continue praying for them, and urges them to serve God wholeheartedly with fear, warning that persistent wickedness will bring destruction to both people and king.

9:13.title: Saul's Military Organization and Jonathan's Victory
9:13.summary: Saul establishes his reign and organizes a military force. Jonathan strikes the Philistine garrison at Geba, prompting Saul to call all Israel to arms at Gilgal, signaling the beginning of open conflict with the Philistines.

9:13.title: The Philistine Threat and Israel's Fear
9:13.summary: The Philistines assemble a massive army and encamp at Michmash, causing the Israelites to flee and hide in fear. Saul waits seven days at Gilgal for Samuel as the people scatter in terror.

9:13.title: Saul's Presumption and Loss of the Kingdom
9:13.summary: Impatient for Samuel's arrival and fearing the Philistine threat, Saul presumptuously offers sacrifice himself—a priestly duty forbidden to him. Samuel rebukes him for disobedience and announces that God will remove the kingdom from Saul's line and give it to a man after God's own heart.

9:13.title: Israel's Military Disadvantage and Vulnerability
9:13.summary: After Samuel's departure, Saul musters only six hundred men. The Philistines dominate the land militarily, controlling all metalworking so that only Saul and Jonathan possess swords or spears, leaving Israel defenseless and dependent on their leaders.

9:14.title: Jonathan's Bold Plan Against the Philistines
9:14.summary: Jonathan decides to attack the Philistine garrison with only his armor bearer, trusting that God can save with many or few. His father Saul is unaware of Jonathan's plan, remaining in Gibeah with six hundred men.

9:14.title: Jonathan's Sign and Victory
9:14.summary: Jonathan and his armor bearer establish a sign with the Philistines to confirm God's will. When the Philistines invite them to come up, Jonathan interprets this as God's deliverance and attacks, killing about twenty men in a devastating first assault.

9:14.title: God's Panic and Israel's Victory
9:14.summary: A great trembling and confusion spreads through the Philistine camp and the earth shakes. Saul discovers Jonathan is missing, leads the Israelites into battle where chaos turns the tide, and God grants Israel complete victory.

9:14.title: Jonathan Unknowingly Breaks Saul's Oath
9:14.summary: Saul has sworn the people to fast until evening. Jonathan, unaware of the oath, eats honey from the forest and his strength is renewed. When told of the oath, he criticizes his father's rash vow, arguing that a better slaughter would have resulted if the hungry people could eat.

9:14.title: The People's Sin and Saul's Atonement
9:14.summary: After defeating the Philistines, the exhausted people violate the law by eating meat with blood. Saul recognizes this sin, gathers the people to slaughter and eat their animals properly, and builds his first altar to the Lord.

9:14.title: Jonathan Condemned and Rescued by the People
9:14.summary: Saul desires to pursue the Philistines further but God does not answer him. Through casting lots, Jonathan is identified as having broken Saul's oath and is condemned to death. However, the people intercede for Jonathan, recognizing his great victory, and rescue him from execution.

9:14.title: Summary of Saul's Reign and Family
9:14.summary: A summary is provided of Saul's military victories against surrounding enemies, his family members including his sons and daughters, and his military leadership with Abner as his commander. Saul's reign is marked by continuous warfare against the Philistines and the recruitment of valiant men.

9:15.title: Samuel's Commission to Destroy Amalek
9:15.summary: Samuel delivers God's command to Saul to completely destroy the Amalekites and all their possessions in retribution for their ancient opposition to Israel. This directive represents divine judgment on a persistent enemy and tests Saul's obedience to God's absolute commands.

9:15.title: Saul's Incomplete Execution of God's Command
9:15.summary: Saul gathers a large army and defeats the Amalekites, but preserves King Agag alive and spares the best livestock, claiming to save them for sacrifice rather than destroying everything as commanded. This partial obedience reveals Saul's willingness to rationalize disobedience and prioritize human judgment over God's explicit instructions.

9:15.title: God's Rejection of Saul and Samuel's Confrontation
9:15.summary: God reveals to Samuel that He regrets making Saul king because Saul has turned from following Him, causing Samuel to pray in distress. When Samuel confronts Saul about the bleating sheep and cattle, Saul falsely claims obedience and justifies keeping the spoils for religious sacrifice.

9:15.title: Samuel's Rebuke and Pronouncement of Saul's Rejection
9:15.summary: Samuel confronts Saul with the reality that obedience to God is far more valuable than ritual offerings, declaring that Saul's rebellion and stubbornness are equivalent to witchcraft and idolatry. Samuel announces that because Saul rejected God's word, God has rejected him as king.

9:15.title: Saul's Repentance and Final Parting
9:15.summary: Saul acknowledges his sin out of fear of the people, but Samuel refuses reconciliation, declaring that God will not change His mind and has given the kingdom to another. Despite Saul's emotional plea for honor before the people, the encounter ends with symbolic rupture as Samuel's torn robe represents the kingdom torn from Saul.

9:15.title: Agag's Execution and the Final Separation
9:15.summary: Samuel executes Agag before the Lord, completing the destruction Saul failed to finish, then departs from Saul forever. The chapter closes with mutual sorrow—Samuel mourns for Saul, and God grieves over His choice to make Saul king, marking the definitive end of their relationship.

9:16.title: The Lord Sends Samuel to Anoint a New King
9:16.summary: The Lord commands Samuel to stop mourning for Saul and travel to Bethlehem to anoint a new king from among the sons of Jesse. Samuel fears Saul's response, but the Lord instructs him to cover his mission with a sacrificial pretext.

9:16.title: Samuel Arrives in Bethlehem and Meets the Sons of Jesse
9:16.summary: Samuel arrives in Bethlehem and invites Jesse and his sons to a sacrifice. When he sees Eliab, Samuel assumes he is the chosen one, but God corrects him, revealing that He judges by the heart rather than outward appearance.

9:16.title: Samuel Examines Jesse's Sons and Seeks the Youngest
9:16.summary: Samuel examines seven of Jesse's sons, but the Lord rejects each one. Samuel then asks if all of Jesse's children are present and learns that the youngest, David, is tending sheep and must be brought to the sacrifice.

9:16.title: David Is Anointed as King
9:16.summary: David, the youngest and most handsome son, is brought in, and the Lord immediately confirms he is the chosen one. Samuel anoints David with oil, and the Spirit of the Lord comes powerfully upon him.

9:16.title: Saul's Affliction and the Search for a Musician
9:16.summary: The Spirit of the Lord departs from Saul, and an evil spirit troubles him. Saul's servants suggest finding a skilled harpist to soothe him, and one servant mentions a talented son of Jesse named David.

9:16.title: David Enters Saul's Service and Relieves His Distress
9:16.summary: Saul requests David from his father Jesse, and David enters the king's service as his armor bearer. When the evil spirit troubles Saul, David's skillful harp playing brings relief and healing, establishing David's position in the royal court.

9:17.title: The Philistine Army and Goliath's Challenge
9:17.summary: The Philistine and Israelite armies face each other across the valley of Elah. Goliath, a massive champion, emerges and repeatedly challenges Israel to send a single warrior to fight him, with the loser's entire army becoming enslaved to the winner. When the Israelites hear his defiant words, they are seized with fear and dismay.

9:17.title: David's Arrival at the Camp
9:17.summary: David, the youngest son of Jesse from Bethlehem, is sent by his father to deliver provisions to his three older brothers fighting with Saul's army. Upon arriving at the camp, David hears Goliath's taunting challenge and witnesses the Israelites' fear as the Philistine champion appears before them.

9:17.title: David Volunteers to Fight Goliath
9:17.summary: David questions the soldiers about the reward for defeating Goliath and learns that the king will enrich and honor whoever kills him. When David expresses his willingness to fight the uncircumcised Philistine who defies the living God, his eldest brother Eliab rebukes him out of anger. Nevertheless, David's words are reported to Saul, who summons him.

9:17.title: David Prepares for Battle
9:17.summary: David reassures Saul that he will fight Goliath, recounting how he killed a lion and bear while protecting his father's sheep. Confident that God will deliver him from the Philistine as he was delivered from wild beasts, David refuses Saul's armor and instead takes only his staff, five smooth stones, and his sling to face the giant.

9:17.title: Goliath and David Face Off
9:17.summary: As Goliath approaches and sees the youthful David, he scorns him and curses him by his gods, threatening to feed his flesh to birds and beasts. David responds with a bold declaration that he comes not with weapons but in the name of the Lord of Armies, proclaiming that God will deliver Goliath into his hand and that all will know that victory belongs to the Lord, not to swords and spears.

9:17.title: David Defeats Goliath and Its Aftermath
9:17.summary: David runs toward Goliath, slings a stone that strikes the giant in the forehead and kills him. David then takes Goliath's own sword and cuts off his head, causing the Philistines to flee. The Israelites pursue and plunder the enemy camp, while Saul, amazed by David's victory, inquires about his identity through his commander Abner, establishing David's name and lineage.

9:18.title: Jonathan and David's Covenant of Friendship
9:18.summary: After David finishes speaking with Saul, Jonathan's soul becomes deeply bonded with David, and he loves him as himself. Jonathan makes a covenant with David and gives him his royal robe, armor, sword, bow, and sash as a sign of his devotion and equality.

9:18.title: David's Rising Success and Saul's Growing Jealousy
9:18.summary: David serves Saul with wisdom and success in military matters, gaining favor with all Israel and Saul's servants. However, when women celebrate David's victory over the Philistines with songs praising him above Saul, Saul becomes angry and jealous, viewing David with suspicion from that day forward.

9:18.title: Saul's Murderous Attack and David's Deliverance
9:18.summary: An evil spirit from God comes upon Saul, and he prophesies in his house while David plays music. Saul throws his spear at David twice, intending to kill him, but David escapes. Recognizing that Yahweh is with David but has departed from himself, Saul removes David from his presence, promotes him to captain, and grows increasingly afraid as David continues to prosper.

9:18.title: Saul's Trap: Merab Betrothal
9:18.summary: All Israel and Judah love David, and Saul offers his elder daughter Merab to David as a wife, secretly hoping David will die fighting the Philistines. David humbly questions his worthiness, but Saul gives Merab to another man, Adriel the Meholathite, breaking his promise.

9:18.title: Saul's Second Trap: Michal Betrothal and David's Victory
9:18.summary: When Saul learns that his younger daughter Michal loves David, he sees her as a snare to destroy him, and offers her to David on the condition that he bring one hundred Philistine foreskins. David accepts the challenge, kills two hundred Philistines, and secures Michal as his wife, further cementing his status in Israel.

9:18.title: Saul's Fear and David's Continued Elevation
9:18.summary: Saul sees that Yahweh is with David and that even his daughter Michal loves him, which increases Saul's fear and makes him David's permanent enemy. Despite Saul's opposition, David continues to behave wisely in every encounter with the Philistines, becoming increasingly esteemed throughout the land.

9:19.title: Jonathan Warns David of Saul's Murderous Intent
9:19.summary: Saul commands his servants and son Jonathan to kill David, but Jonathan, who loves David deeply, warns him to hide and promises to intercede with his father. Jonathan's loyalty to David conflicts with his duty to his father, setting the stage for a crucial test of his character.

9:19.title: Jonathan's Intercession: Saul's Oath
9:19.summary: Jonathan argues persuasively to Saul that David is innocent and has served him faithfully, even risking his life against the Philistines and winning a great victory. Saul, moved by Jonathan's words, swears that David will not be killed, and David is restored to his presence.

9:19.title: Saul Attempts to Kill David with His Spear
9:19.summary: After David fights the Philistines with great success, an evil spirit from God comes upon Saul as David plays music before him; Saul hurls his spear at David, trying to pin him to the wall. David narrowly escapes with his life, fleeing into the night.

9:19.title: Michal Helps David Escape and Deceives Saul
9:19.summary: Saul sends messengers to David's house to watch and kill him, but his wife Michal warns David and helps him escape through a window; she then deceives Saul's messengers by placing an idol in the bed and claiming David is sick. When Saul discovers the deception, Michal falsely tells him David threatened to kill her.

9:19.title: David Finds Refuge with Samuel; Saul Encounters God's Spirit
9:19.summary: David flees to Samuel in Ramah, and when Saul sends messengers to capture him, God's Spirit falls upon them and they prophesy instead; this happens repeatedly with each group of messengers and finally with Saul himself, who strips off his clothes and prophesies all day and night. The scene demonstrates God's supernatural protection of David and Saul's inability to resist the Spirit's power.

9:20.title: David's Fear and Jonathan's Initial Reassurance
9:20.summary: David flees to Jonathan in distress, asking what sin he has committed that Saul seeks his life. Jonathan initially denies this, but David insists that Saul is hiding his murderous intent even from Jonathan, and warns that death is imminent.

9:20.title: David's Plan to Test Saul's Intentions
9:20.summary: David proposes a test: he will hide during the new moon feast, and Jonathan will observe Saul's reaction to determine whether evil is truly intended against him. Jonathan agrees to help and promises to report back.

9:20.title: Jonathan's Covenant and Signal Plan
9:20.summary: Jonathan takes David into the field and swears a solemn covenant before God, promising to warn David of danger. They establish a signal using arrows: if the arrows fall short, David is safe; if they go beyond, David must flee. Jonathan invokes God as witness to their eternal bond.

9:20.title: The New Moon Feast and David's Absence
9:20.summary: David hides in the field while Saul hosts the new moon feast. On the second day when David's seat remains empty, Saul questions Jonathan about David's whereabouts. Jonathan gives the prepared excuse about a family sacrifice in Bethlehem.

9:20.title: Saul's Rage and Jonathan's Grief
9:20.summary: Saul's anger ignites against Jonathan, accusing him of choosing David's side and threatening both David's death and Jonathan's own future. In fury, Saul hurls a spear at Jonathan. Jonathan leaves the table in anger and grief, fasting in sorrow for David's sake.

9:20.title: The Arrow Signal Executed
9:20.summary: Jonathan goes to the field with a boy and shoots arrows as arranged. He signals to the boy to retrieve them, calling out that the arrows are beyond him—the sign that David must flee. The boy knows nothing of the covenant between them.

9:20.title: David and Jonathan's Tearful Farewell
9:20.summary: After dismissing the boy, David emerges and the two friends embrace, weeping deeply. Jonathan affirms their covenant sworn before God and sends David away in peace, vowing that their bond and that of their descendants will endure forever.

9:21.title: David Receives Bread and a Sword at Nob
9:21.summary: David flees to the priest Ahimelech at Nob, claiming to be on a secret mission for the king. He requests food and is given the holy showbread after confirming his men are ceremonially clean, and receives Goliath's sword as a weapon.

9:21.title: Doeg the Edomite Witnesses David
9:21.summary: An ominous detail: Saul's servant Doeg, a chief herdsman, is present at the sanctuary and witnesses David's request for a weapon. David takes Goliath's sword, recognizing its unique power and significance.

9:21.title: David Flees to Achish and Feigns Madness
9:21.summary: David flees Saul's territory and seeks refuge with Achish, king of Gath, an enemy Philistine city. When recognized as the legendary warrior, David feigns madness by acting erratically, prompting Achish to reject him as a useless lunatic.

9:22.title: David Gathers Followers at Adullam
9:22.summary: David escapes to the cave of Adullam where his family joins him, and he attracts a following of about four hundred men who are distressed, in debt, or discontented, becoming their captain.

9:22.title: David Secures His Parents and Receives Gad's Counsel
9:22.summary: David places his parents under the protection of the king of Moab, and the prophet Gad directs him to leave the stronghold and go to the forest of Hereth in Judah.

9:22.title: Doeg Accuses David Before Saul
9:22.summary: Saul, paranoid about conspiracy, questions his servants about David's support; Doeg the Edomite informs the king that he saw David receiving help from the priest Ahimelech at Nob, including food and Goliath's sword.

9:22.title: Saul Condemns Ahimelech and the Priests
9:22.summary: Saul summons the priest Ahimelech and accuses him of conspiring with David; though Ahimelech defends his innocence and David's loyalty, Saul sentences him and all his father's house to death.

9:22.title: The Massacre of the Priests of Nob
9:22.summary: Saul's guards refuse to execute the priests, but Doeg carries out the slaughter, killing eighty-five priests and destroying the city of Nob with all its inhabitants; only Abiathar, a son of Ahimelech, escapes and flees to David.

9:22.title: David Takes Responsibility and Comforts Abiathar
9:22.summary: Abiathar reports the priests' slaughter to David, who acknowledges his responsibility for their deaths and assures Abiathar of protection, promising him safety under David's care.

9:23.title: David Delivers Keilah from the Philistines
9:23.summary: David learns that Keilah is under Philistine attack and inquires of God whether to intervene. Though his men fear the risk, God assures David of victory, and David defeats the Philistines and saves the city.

9:23.title: David Flees Keilah When Saul Threatens
9:23.summary: When Saul learns David is trapped in walled Keilah, he plans to besiege the city. David consults God through the priest Abiathar and learns that the townspeople will betray him to Saul; he and his six hundred men escape before Saul arrives.

9:23.title: Jonathan Renews His Covenant with David
9:23.summary: While David hides in the wilderness of Ziph pursued by Saul, Jonathan seeks him out and strengthens his faith in God's promise that David will be king. The two renew their covenant before the Lord.

9:23.title: The Ziphites Betray David to Saul
9:23.summary: The men of Ziph inform Saul of David's exact hiding place and offer to deliver him up. Saul praises them and asks them to scout further to ensure they have the right location before he pursues.

9:23.title: God Saves David by Diverting Saul with Philistine Raiders
9:23.summary: As Saul closes in on David in the wilderness of Maon, trapping him on a mountain, a messenger arrives reporting a Philistine raid on the land. Saul abandons the pursuit to deal with the Philistines, allowing David to escape to the strongholds of En Gedi.

9:24.title: Saul Pursues David to En Gedi
9:24.summary: After returning from fighting the Philistines, Saul learns that David is hiding in the wilderness of En Gedi and sets out with three thousand men to capture him. David and his men are concealed deep within a cave near some sheep pens.

9:24.title: David Spares Saul in the Cave
9:24.summary: When Saul enters the cave to relieve himself, David's men urge him to kill his enemy, but David cuts only the hem of Saul's robe and is immediately convicted by his conscience. David prevents his men from attacking Saul, recognizing that Saul is the Lord's anointed.

9:24.title: David Reveals Himself and Declares His Innocence
9:24.summary: David exits the cave and calls after Saul, showing him the torn robe as evidence that he had the opportunity to kill him but chose not to, and appeals to God to judge between them. David protests his innocence and affirms his loyalty despite Saul's persecution.

9:24.title: Saul's Repentance and Reconciliation
9:24.summary: Saul recognizes David's voice, is moved to tears, and acknowledges that David is more righteous than he is, having shown mercy when he could have killed him. Saul confesses his wrongdoing, predicts David's future kingship, and requests that David spare his descendants, to which David agrees.

9:25.title: Samuel's Death and David's Retreat
9:25.summary: Samuel dies and is buried in Ramah, prompting David to flee into the wilderness of Paran. The narrative then introduces Nabal, a wealthy but cruel man from Maon, and his wife Abigail, a woman of intelligence and beauty.

9:25.title: Nabal's Rejection of David's Request
9:25.summary: David hears that Nabal is shearing sheep and sends ten young men to request provisions, reminding Nabal that David's men protected his shepherds without harm. Nabal rudely refuses the request, insulting David and claiming not to know who he is.

9:25.title: David's Wrathful Response and Abigail's Warning
9:25.summary: Enraged by Nabal's rejection, David arms four hundred men to destroy Nabal and his household, but a young servant warns Abigail of the impending disaster, praising David's men for their protection and character.

9:25.title: Abigail's Intervention and Reconciliation
9:25.summary: Abigail quickly prepares abundant gifts and meets David on the road, falling before him in humble supplication. Her wise words acknowledge Nabal's foolishness, appeal to God's protection of David, and remind him that vengeful bloodshed would burden his future kingship, causing David to bless her and abandon his destructive plan.

9:25.title: Nabal's Death and God's Judgment
9:25.summary: Abigail returns home to find Nabal drunk and feasting; she waits until morning to tell him what transpired, and when she does, his heart fails in terror. God strikes Nabal dead about ten days later, vindicating David without his own hand.

9:25.title: David's Marriage to Abigail and His Wives
9:25.summary: David praises God for avenging his reproach and sends messengers to propose to Abigail, who accepts humbly and becomes his wife. The passage concludes by noting that David also marries Ahinoam and mentions that Saul has given David's previous wife Michal to another man.

9:26.title: Saul Pursues David to the Wilderness of Ziph
9:26.summary: The Ziphites inform Saul of David's location in the hill of Hachilah. Saul musters three thousand men and camps in the wilderness, but David discovers his arrival and scouts Saul's position, finding him sleeping with his army encamped around him.

9:26.title: David Spares Saul's Life in the Camp
9:26.summary: David and Abishai enter Saul's camp at night; Abishai offers to kill the sleeping king, but David refuses, declaring that Saul is the Lord's anointed and must not be harmed by human hand. Instead, David takes only Saul's spear and water jar as proof, departing undetected as a deep sleep from God prevents anyone from waking.

9:26.title: David Confronts Saul and Receives His Blessing
9:26.summary: From a safe distance, David calls out to Abner and Saul, exposing their negligent guard and proving his power over Saul by displaying the spear and water jar. David appeals to the king, defending his innocence and expressing trust in the Lord's justice. Saul acknowledges his sin, confesses he has played the fool, and blesses David, promising to cease pursuit.

9:27.title: David Flees to Gath
9:27.summary: Despairing of ever escaping Saul's pursuit in Israel, David decides to seek refuge among the Philistines, relocating with his six hundred men to King Achish of Gath. When Saul learns of David's flight to Gath, he ceases his pursuit.

9:27.title: David Settles in Ziklag
9:27.summary: David requests a place in the countryside rather than the royal city, and Achish grants him the city of Ziklag. David and his men dwell there for a year and four months.

9:27.title: David's Raids and Deception
9:27.summary: While living in Philistine territory, David conducts raids against Israel's enemies (Geshurites, Girzites, and Amalekites) but deceives Achish by claiming to attack Judean territories, eliminating all witnesses to hide his true activities. Achish becomes convinced David has turned against Israel and will serve him forever.

9:28.title: David's Pledge to Achish
9:28.summary: Achish commands David to join the Philistine army against Israel, and David cryptically pledges his service. David appears trapped between his loyalty to Israel and his survival among the Philistines.

9:28.title: Samuel Dead; Armies Gather
9:28.summary: Samuel's death leaves Israel without prophetic guidance, while both the Philistine and Israelite armies assemble for decisive battle at Gilboa and Shunem.

9:28.title: Saul's Fear and Desperate Search
9:28.summary: Terrified by the Philistine army and abandoned by God's silence through all legitimate means, Saul seeks out a medium at Endor in violation of his own laws.

9:28.title: The Medium of Endor Summons Samuel
9:28.summary: Saul disguises himself and persuades the fearful medium to conjure Samuel's spirit; she sees Samuel rising from the earth, recognizes the king, and Saul bows before the apparition.

9:28.title: Samuel's Judgment: Saul's Doom Pronounced
9:28.summary: Samuel rebukes Saul for disturbing him and declares that God has abandoned Saul, torn the kingdom away and given it to David, and that Saul and his sons will die in tomorrow's battle.

9:28.title: The Medium's Mercy and Saul's Departure
9:28.summary: Devastated by Samuel's prophecy, Saul collapses in terror; the compassionate medium provides him bread and encouragement, and he departs into the night to face his final battle.

9:29.title: The Philistine Army Gathers
9:29.summary: The Philistines assemble their full military forces at Aphek while the Israelites camp at Jezreel's spring. David and his men march with Achish at the rear of the Philistine forces.

9:29.title: The Philistine Princes Question David's Loyalty
9:29.summary: The Philistine commanders challenge Achish about allowing David to fight alongside them, fearing he will betray them in battle and pointing to the songs celebrating David's military victories over Saul. Their suspicion threatens to expose David's precarious position as a fugitive living among Israel's enemies.

9:29.title: Achish Dismisses David from Battle
9:29.summary: Achish acknowledges David's personal integrity and faithfulness but yields to the Philistine princes' demands, ordering David to leave camp peacefully to avoid angering the other commanders. David is thus prevented from fighting against his own people.

9:29.title: David's Protest and Departure
9:29.summary: David protests his dismissal to Achish, questioning what fault has been found in him, but Achish reaffirms David's goodness while remaining firm that the princes will not permit him to fight. Achish instructs David to depart early in the morning with his men.

9:29.title: David Departs and the Philistines Advance
9:29.summary: David and his men obey Achish's command and depart early in the morning to return to Philistine territory, while the main Philistine army marches toward Jezreel to face Israel. David's removal from the battle proves providential, keeping him from fighting against his own nation.

9:30.title: The Amalekite Raid on Ziklag and David's Distress
9:30.summary: David and his men return to Ziklag to find it burned and their families taken captive by the Amalekites. The people grieve deeply and threaten to stone David, but David strengthens himself by trusting in God.

9:30.title: David Inquires of the Lord
9:30.summary: David seeks God's guidance through the priest Abiathar and the ephod, asking whether he should pursue the Amalekites. God assures him of victory and the recovery of all that was taken.

9:30.title: The Pursuit and the Egyptian Guide
9:30.summary: David pursues the Amalekites with 400 men, leaving 200 behind at the brook Besor. They find an Egyptian servant abandoned by the Amalekites and, after restoring him, convince him to guide them to the enemy camp.

9:30.title: The Battle and Recovery of the Spoil
9:30.summary: David discovers the Amalekites celebrating with plunder and defeats them completely, recovering all the stolen goods, his wives, and the flocks and herds taken from the region.

9:30.title: David's Equitable Division of Spoil
9:30.summary: David meets the 200 exhausted men left behind and overrules selfish soldiers who want to deny them a share of the spoil. He establishes a lasting statute that those who guard supplies deserve equal reward as those in battle.

9:30.title: David Distributes Spoil to the Elders of Judah
9:30.summary: David sends portions of the spoil as gifts to the elders and friends throughout Judah and neighboring regions, strengthening his political alliances and support as he moves toward kingship.

9:31.title: The Death of Saul and His Sons
9:31.summary: The Philistines defeat Israel in battle on Mount Gilboa, killing Saul's sons and wounding Saul himself. Rather than face capture and abuse by the uncircumcised Philistines, Saul commands his armor bearer to kill him; when the armor bearer refuses, Saul falls on his own sword, and his armor bearer follows him in death.

9:31.title: The Philistines Desecrate Saul's Body
9:31.summary: The Philistines find Saul and his sons dead, mutilate Saul's body by cutting off his head and stripping his armor, display the armor in their temple, and fasten his body to the wall of Beth Shan as a trophy of victory.

9:31.title: The Men of Jabesh Gilead Honor Saul
9:31.summary: The brave men of Jabesh Gilead, remembering Saul's past kindness to their city, retrieve his body and those of his sons from the Philistine wall, cremate them with respect, and bury the bones with mourning and fasting.

10:1.title: A Messenger Brings News of Saul's Defeat
10:1.summary: An Amalekite messenger arrives at David's camp in Ziklag with news that Saul and Jonathan have been killed in battle against the Philistines. David questions the messenger to learn details of what happened.

10:1.title: The Amalekite's Account of Saul's Death
10:1.summary: The Amalekite describes encountering the wounded Saul on Mount Gilboa, how Saul asked him to kill him, and how he did so and took Saul's crown and bracelet as proof. His account raises questions about how he came to possess the insignia of Israel's king.

10:1.title: David and His Men Mourn the Dead
10:1.summary: David and all his men tear their clothes and mourn deeply for Saul, Jonathan, and the fallen people of Israel, fasting until evening as a sign of their grief. Despite David's complicated relationship with Saul, he honors the dead and the nation's loss.

10:1.title: David Executes the Amalekite
10:1.summary: David questions the Amalekite's identity, then condemns him to death for daring to lay hands on the Lord's anointed king. The Amalekite is executed for his confession that he killed Saul, regardless of his claim that he was granting a mercy killing.

10:1.title: David's Lament for Saul and Jonathan
10:1.summary: David composes and sings an elegy mourning Saul and Jonathan, celebrating their martial prowess, their noble character, and their deep bond, while lamenting their deaths in battle. The lament is to be taught to the people of Judah and preserved in the book of Jashar.

10:2.title: David Anointed King over Judah
10:2.summary: David inquires of God and is directed to Hebron, where he is anointed king over the house of Judah. He sends a blessing to the men of Jabesh Gilead for honoring Saul with burial, declaring his own kingship and urging them to be strong.

10:2.title: Ishbosheth Made King over Israel
10:2.summary: Abner, commander of Saul's army, establishes Saul's son Ishbosheth as king over Israel and the northern territories, while David reigns over Judah alone for seven and a half years, creating a divided kingdom.

10:2.title: The Battle at Gibeon and Asahel's Death
10:2.summary: Forces under Abner (supporting Ishbosheth) and Joab (supporting David) meet at Gibeon, where a combat begins between young men, escalates into fierce battle, and results in Asahel's death when he recklessly pursues the fleeing Abner despite warnings.

10:2.title: Ceasefire and Asahel's Burial
10:2.summary: As pursuit continues into the evening, Abner appeals to Joab to end the destructive conflict, Joab agrees and sounds the trumpet to halt pursuit, and the chapter closes with the casualties counted and Asahel buried in Bethlehem.

10:3.title: David's Growing Strength and Sons Born in Hebron
10:3.summary: While war continues between the houses of Saul and David, David's power increases while Saul's weakens. David's six sons are born to him in Hebron through different wives, establishing his dynasty.

10:3.title: Abner's Quarrel with Ishbosheth
10:3.summary: Abner, commanding general of Saul's house, grows powerful during the war, but when Ishbosheth accuses him of impropriety with Saul's concubine, Abner angrily swears to transfer the kingdom to David. Ishbosheth, terrified, cannot answer him.

10:3.title: Abner Negotiates with David and Returns Michal
10:3.summary: Abner sends messengers to David proposing an alliance to unite Israel under him, requiring that David first receive his wife Michal from Saul's house. David agrees, and Ishbosheth surrenders Michal, though her first husband Paltiel grieves as she is taken away.

10:3.title: Abner Persuades Israel and Makes Covenant with David
10:3.summary: Abner convinces the elders of Israel and Benjamin that David is God's chosen king to save Israel from enemies, then meets David in Hebron, feasts with him, and promises to unite all Israel under David's rule before departing in peace.

10:3.title: Joab Murders Abner in Revenge
10:3.summary: When Joab learns that Abner has been peacefully dismissed, he suspects deception and secretly recalls him from the well of Sirah. Joab then privately murders Abner in Hebron's gate to avenge his brother Asahel's death in a previous battle.

10:3.title: David Disavows Abner's Murder and Mourns Him
10:3.summary: David publicly denounces himself and his kingdom from guilt in Abner's death, cursing Joab's household instead. David commands mourning rites, weeps at Abner's grave, and laments him as a great man who should not have fallen foolishly, winning the people's approval that the king is blameless.

10:4.title: The Collapse of Ishbosheth's Courage and Introduction of Mephibosheth
10:4.summary: Upon hearing of Abner's death, Ishbosheth loses his strength and Israel is thrown into confusion. The passage introduces Rechab and Baanah, captains under Ishbosheth, and explains how Mephibosheth, Jonathan's son, became lame while fleeing the news of Saul and Jonathan's deaths.

10:4.title: The Assassination of Ishbosheth
10:4.summary: Rechab and Baanah enter Ishbosheth's house during his midday rest, strike him down, behead him, and flee with his head into the night. This murderous act removes the last obstacle to David's complete rule over Israel.

10:4.title: David's Justice Against the Assassins
10:4.summary: Rechab and Baanah bring Ishbosheth's head to David, expecting reward, but David rejects their deed as wicked murder and executes them as punishment, ultimately giving Ishbosheth's head proper burial in Abner's tomb. David's response demonstrates his commitment to justice and his refusal to accept the throne through treachery.

10:5.title: All Israel Anoints David King
10:5.summary: The tribes of Israel come to David in Hebron, acknowledging their shared kinship and recalling that God had chosen him to shepherd Israel. David makes a covenant with the elders and is anointed king over all Israel.

10:5.title: David's Reign: Duration and Division
10:5.summary: David reigns for forty years total, beginning at age thirty—seven and a half years in Hebron over Judah, then thirty-three years in Jerusalem over all Israel and Judah.

10:5.title: David Captures Jerusalem from the Jebusites
10:5.summary: David and his men conquer Jerusalem (Zion) from the Jebusites, who mockingly claimed their blind and lame could defend it; David makes it his capital and rebuilds it.

10:5.title: David's Growing Power and Recognition
10:5.summary: David's power increases as God is with him; King Hiram of Tyre sends materials and craftsmen to build David's house, and David recognizes that God has exalted his kingdom for Israel's sake.

10:5.title: David's Sons Born in Jerusalem
10:5.summary: David takes additional wives and concubines in Jerusalem and fathers several sons there, including Solomon, Nathan, and others whose names are listed.

10:5.title: David's First Victory Over the Philistines
10:5.summary: When the Philistines learn of David's kingship, they attack; David consults God, receives assurance of victory, and defeats them at Baal Perazim, where God 'breaks through' the enemy like a breach of waters.

10:5.title: David's Second Victory Over the Philistines
10:5.summary: The Philistines attack again; God instructs David to circle around and attack from a different position, with the sound of marching in the mulberry trees as a sign that God has gone before him. David obeys and defeats the Philistines from Geba to Gezer.

10:6.title: David's Procession to Bring the Ark
10:6.summary: David gathers thirty thousand chosen men of Israel and sets out to bring the ark of God from Abinadab's house to Jerusalem on a new cart, accompanied by joyful music and celebration.

10:6.title: Uzzah's Death and the Ark's Stay with Obed-Edom
10:6.summary: When the cattle stumble, Uzzah reaches out to steady the ark and is struck dead by God's anger, causing David to fear and leave the ark at Obed-Edom's house for three months, where God blesses the household.

10:6.title: David Successfully Brings the Ark to Jerusalem
10:6.summary: Hearing of God's blessings on Obed-Edom, David retrieves the ark and brings it into the city of David with sacrifices, joyful dancing, and the sound of trumpets.

10:6.title: The Ark Installed and David's Blessing to the People
10:6.summary: As the ark enters David's tent, he offers sacrifices and blesses all Israel; he then distributes food to all the people, both men and women, before they return to their homes.

10:6.title: Michal's Contempt and David's Response
10:6.summary: Michal reproaches David for his undignified dancing before the people, but David defends his worship as an act before God and declares his willingness to be even more humble; Michal remains childless as a result.

10:7.title: David's Desire to Build a House for God
10:7.summary: After God grants David rest from his enemies, David expresses to the prophet Nathan his desire to build a permanent house for the ark of God, since he himself dwells in a cedar palace. Nathan initially encourages him to pursue this plan.

10:7.title: The Davidic Covenant
