20:19.title: Integrity Over Foolishness
20:19.summary: A poor person who walks in integrity is better than a fool with perverse speech. Foolish zeal without knowledge and hasty actions lead people astray, and human foolishness ultimately rages against God.

20:19.title: Wealth, Friendships, and Poverty
20:19.summary: Wealth attracts many friends while poverty isolates people, even from family and close companions. The poor person's pleas for help go unheeded as those around them distance themselves.

20:19.title: Wisdom, Falsehood, and Justice
20:19.summary: Those who pursue wisdom and understanding love themselves and find good, while false witnesses and liars face inevitable punishment and destruction.

20:19.title: Foolishness, Authority, and Rulers
20:19.summary: Luxury is unsuitable for fools, and servants should not rule over princes. A king's wrath is terrible, but his favor brings blessing like gentle dew.

20:19.title: Family Troubles and Divine Provision
20:19.summary: A foolish son brings calamity to his father and a quarrelsome wife brings constant distress, but a prudent wife is a gift from God that surpasses inherited wealth.

20:19.title: Laziness and Obedience
20:19.summary: Idleness leads to deep sleep and hunger, while keeping God's commandments preserves the soul; contempt for God's ways leads to death.

20:19.title: Compassion and Discipline
20:19.summary: Helping the poor is lending to God and will be rewarded, and disciplining one's son offers hope for his future and protects him from death.

20:19.title: Anger, Rescue, and Counsel
20:19.summary: A hot-tempered person must face consequences, and repeatedly rescuing such a person creates ongoing problems. Listening to counsel and instruction brings future wisdom.

20:19.title: God's Purpose and Human Kindness
20:19.summary: While people make many plans, God's counsel ultimately prevails. Kindness makes a person desirable and is more valuable than dishonesty or poverty.

20:19.title: Fear of God and Sloth
20:19.summary: Fearing Yahweh leads to life, contentment, and rest from trouble. In contrast, the sluggard is too lazy even to feed himself.

20:19.title: Correction and Instruction
20:19.summary: Flogging a scoffer teaches the simple, and rebuking the understanding brings knowledge. Abandoning instruction causes one to stray from knowledge.

20:19.title: Corrupt Witnesses and Divine Penalties
20:19.summary: Corrupt witnesses mock justice while the wicked greedily consume iniquity; scoffers and fools face divinely prepared punishment and beatings.

20:20.title: Avoiding Alcohol, Anger, and Quarreling
20:20.summary: Wine and beer lead to foolishness and loss of wisdom. The king's anger is dangerous and provokes death, while honor comes from avoiding conflict rather than engaging in it like a fool.

20:20.title: Diligence, Discernment, and Faithfulness
20:20.summary: The lazy person refuses to work and ends in poverty; wisdom draws out hidden counsel from the heart. True faithfulness is rare despite many claims of loyalty.

20:20.title: Righteousness, Judgment, and Sinlessness
20:20.summary: The righteous person's integrity blesses his descendants, and a just king eliminates evil through careful observation. No one can claim true sinlessness before God.

20:20.title: Honesty in Commerce and God's Knowledge
20:20.summary: False weights and measures are abominable to God; even children reveal their true nature through their actions. God has given us hearing and sight to discern truth.

20:20.title: Diligence Brings Abundance; Knowledge Is Precious
20:20.summary: Loving sleep leads to poverty, but staying alert brings satisfaction. Wise speech is more valuable than gold or jewels.

20:20.title: Warnings Against Bad Debts and Deception
20:20.summary: Those who guarantee debts for strangers or wayward people face loss; deceitful gain may taste sweet at first but brings painful consequences.

20:20.title: Wise Council and the Danger of Gossip
20:20.summary: Plans succeed through careful counsel and strategic wisdom; those who spread secrets and gossip should be avoided entirely.

20:20.title: Honoring Parents and Avoiding Quick Wealth
20:20.summary: Cursing parents brings complete darkness and destruction to one's life. Wealth gained hastily at the start will not be blessed in the end.

20:20.title: Trust in God Rather Than Revenge
20:20.summary: Instead of seeking revenge for evil, one should wait on God for deliverance. God detests dishonest commerce and false measures.

20:20.title: Divine Providence and Rash Vows
20:20.summary: God directs human steps, and no one can fully understand his ways. Making hasty dedications or vows without reflection is a trap.

20:20.title: The King's Justice and God's Searching Spirit
20:20.summary: A wise king separates the wicked and punishes them severely. God's spirit within humanity searches the deepest parts of the heart.

20:20.title: Love and Strength in Leadership and Life
20:20.summary: Love and faithfulness protect and sustain a king's throne. Youth's glory is strength while age's splendor is gray hair; hardship cleanses away evil.

20:21.title: God's Sovereignty and the Primacy of Justice
20:21.summary: The Lord directs even kings' hearts according to His will, and though people justify their own ways, God alone truly evaluates the heart. Righteous conduct and justice matter more to God than religious ritual.

20:21.title: The Contrast Between Wickedness and Righteousness
20:21.summary: Pride and arrogance characterize the wicked and lead to sin, while diligence produces prosperity and innocence manifests in upright conduct. The wicked's violent ways ultimately drive them away from good.

20:21.title: Contentious Relationships and Moral Accountability
20:21.summary: Better to live in hardship alone than with a quarrelsome spouse; the wicked harbor evil intent while the righteous show compassion. Those who ignore the poor's cry will similarly be ignored in their own distress.

20:21.title: The Power of Gifts and the Peril of Folly
20:21.summary: Secret gifts can appease anger, while the righteous rejoice in justice but fools rush toward ruin. Abandoning wisdom leads to death and the grave.

20:21.title: Pleasure, Poverty, and Wisdom's Treasure
20:21.summary: Love of pleasure and indulgence leads to poverty, while God uses the wicked to ransom the righteous. The wise household is precious while the fool dissipates wealth.

20:21.title: The Righteous Path and Humble Wisdom
20:21.summary: Pursuing righteousness and kindness yields life and honor; the wise gain influence through understanding, while guardians of their speech avoid trouble. The proud scoffer works in arrogant presumption.

20:21.title: Laziness, Generosity, and Steadfastness
20:21.summary: The sluggard's lethargy destroys him while the righteous give generously; false witnesses perish but the upright establish their ways despite opposition. The wicked harden themselves but God sustains the just.

20:21.title: Final Trust in Divine Victory
20:21.summary: Human wisdom, understanding, and counsel cannot prevail against the Lord; though horses are readied for battle, victory ultimately belongs to God alone.

20:22.title: The Value of a Good Name and Fear of Yahweh
20:22.summary: A good reputation and divine favor are more valuable than wealth. Both rich and poor are made by God, and humility combined with reverence for Yahweh leads to true wealth, honor, and life.

20:22.title: Prudence, Discipline, and Generosity
20:22.summary: The wise person avoids danger through foresight while the foolish suffer consequences; discipline shapes children for life, and generosity to the poor brings blessing.

20:22.title: Consequences of Speech, Character, and Economic Justice
20:22.summary: Removing mockers brings peace; gracious speech gains favor; God watches knowledge and destroys the unfaithful; laziness, adultery, and oppression of the poor all lead to ruin.

20:22.title: The Teacher's Appeal to Listen and Learn
20:22.summary: The wise teacher calls the student to internalize wisdom's words and trust in Yahweh; the thirty sayings of counsel are meant to teach truth and provide reliable answers.

20:22.title: Justice for the Poor and Warnings About Debt
20:22.summary: God defends the poor against exploitation; avoid entanglement with hot-tempered people and dangerous debt obligations, lest you lose everything and ensnare your soul.

20:22.title: Property Rights and Diligence
20:22.summary: Respect ancestral boundaries and property laws; a person skilled in work will gain honor and serve kings rather than obscure masters.

20:23.title: Caution When Dining with Rulers
20:23.summary: When eating with someone of high status, one should be mindful and self-controlled, resisting the temptation of their lavish food, which may come with hidden strings or obligations. Restraint protects one from compromising integrity for material comfort.

20:23.title: Warning Against Pursuing Wealth
20:23.summary: One should not exhaust themselves chasing riches, but instead exercise wisdom and restraint, recognizing that wealth is fleeting and illusory—it vanishes like an eagle flying away. The pursuit of money at all costs is ultimately futile.

20:23.title: The Stingy Host's Deceptive Hospitality
20:23.summary: One should avoid accepting food from a stingy person whose heart is not genuinely hospitable; such a meal will be regretted and remembered with shame. False hospitality leaves a bitter aftertaste and damages one's reputation.

20:23.title: Don't Waste Wisdom on Fools
20:23.summary: Sharing wise counsel with a fool is pointless, as he will only despise and reject the wisdom offered. One must choose one's audience wisely.

20:23.title: Protection of the Fatherless
20:23.summary: One must not move ancient boundary markers or exploit the vulnerable fatherless, for God Himself is their strong Defender and will bring judgment against their oppressors. God actively protects the defenseless.

20:23.title: Commit to Learning
20:23.summary: One should dedicate oneself wholeheartedly to receiving instruction and gaining knowledge, opening both heart and ears to wisdom. Learning requires active, engaged receptiveness.

20:23.title: Corporal Discipline of Children
20:23.summary: Parental correction and physical discipline of a child will not cause harm but rather rescue the child from destructive paths and eternal judgment. Loving discipline is an act of spiritual rescue.

20:23.title: Parental Joy in a Wise Child
20:23.summary: A parent's heart is gladdened and rejoices when a child demonstrates wisdom and speaks what is right and true. A child's righteousness brings deep joy to those who raised them.

20:23.title: Fear the Lord Rather Than Envy Sinners
20:23.summary: One should not envy those who prosper through sin, but instead maintain reverence for God throughout one's days, trusting in a future hope and reward. Devotion to God offers lasting fulfillment that envy cannot.

20:23.title: Son, Be Wise and Stay on the Right Path
20:23.summary: The father calls his son to listen, pursue wisdom, and guard his heart by remaining on the path of righteousness. Inner vigilance protects outward conduct.

20:23.title: Warning Against Gluttony and Drunkenness
20:23.summary: One should avoid excessive wine and overeating, as these vices lead to poverty and degradation, leaving a person clothed in shame and rags. Indulgence destroys both wealth and dignity.

20:23.title: Honor Your Parents in Their Age
20:23.summary: One must listen to and respect one's father and mother, particularly showing care and honor to one's mother in her old age. Family loyalty and gratitude are lifelong obligations.

20:23.title: Purchase Truth at Any Cost
20:23.summary: One should acquire truth, wisdom, discipline, and understanding and guard them carefully, valuing them above all else and never selling them for convenience or gain. These virtues are priceless treasures.

20:23.title: The Blessing of Righteous and Wise Children
20:23.summary: A father delights greatly in a righteous child, and a mother rejoices when she has borne a wise son or daughter. Children's virtue brings lasting joy to parents.

20:23.title: Give Your Heart and Follow the Right Way
20:23.summary: The father appeals to his son to surrender his heart fully and keep his eyes fixed on the father's ways, implying trust and commitment to a righteous path. Wholehearted devotion shapes behavior.

20:23.title: The Destructive Pit of Sexual Immorality
20:23.summary: A prostitute and an unfaithful woman are like dangerous traps—a deep pit and a narrow well—waiting to ensnare and destroy men, multiplying unfaithfulness. Sexual vice destroys individuals and communities.

20:23.title: The Destructive Effects of Wine
20:23.summary: Excessive wine brings misery, quarrels, injuries, and shame, appearing attractive but ultimately biting like a snake and leaving one disoriented and helpless as if drowning. The consequences of alcohol abuse are devastating and dehumanizing.

20:23.title: The Alcoholic's Denial and Compulsion
20:23.summary: A drunkard, numbed by alcohol, dismisses being beaten and craves another drink, trapped in denial and compulsion despite the harm. Addiction blinds one to reality and enslaves the will.

20:24.title: Avoid the Company of Evil Men
20:24.summary: Do not envy or desire fellowship with those who do evil, for their hearts are bent on violence and mischief. Their very nature is corrupting and should be avoided.

20:24.title: Wisdom Builds and Strengthens
20:24.summary: Through wisdom, understanding, and knowledge, a household is established and filled with treasure; wise counsel and many advisors bring strength and victory. Wisdom is the foundation of both material and strategic success.

20:24.title: Folly and the Fool
20:24.summary: A fool cannot access wisdom and remains silent in public forums; those who scheme evil are called plotters, and folly itself is sinful and brings contempt. Foolishness and scheming are inherently corrupt.

20:24.title: Duty to Rescue the Innocent
20:24.summary: Weakness in times of crisis reveals small strength, but one must actively rescue those heading toward death and injustice; God sees all hearts and will judge each person according to their works. Righteous action in defense of the innocent is a moral duty before God.

20:24.title: Wisdom's Sweet Reward
20:24.summary: Wisdom is sweet to the soul, like honey to the taste, and pursuing it brings hope and a lasting reward. The discovery of wisdom offers both present pleasure and future assurance.

20:24.title: The Resilience of the Righteous
20:24.summary: The wicked should not ambush or destroy the righteous, for though the righteous may fall many times, they rise again, while the wicked are brought down by disaster. Righteousness has an inherent resilience that wickedness lacks.

20:24.title: Do Not Gloat Over Your Enemy's Fall
20:24.summary: Do not rejoice when your enemy falls or is destroyed, lest God see it and become displeased, turning his wrath from the enemy to you. Malicious satisfaction over another's ruin invites God's judgment.

20:24.title: The Futility of Envying the Wicked
20:24.summary: Do not fret or be envious of evildoers, for they have no future reward and their light will be extinguished. Envy of the wicked is pointless because their end is darkness.

20:24.title: Fear God and the King; Avoid Rebellion
20:24.summary: Fear God and respect the king's authority; do not join with those who rebel against established order. Both God and earthly authority bring sudden calamity upon those who resist them.

20:24.title: Justice and Honest Judgment
20:24.summary: Showing partiality in judgment is wrong; declaring the guilty innocent brings curse and disgrace, but those who convict the guilty will be blessed abundantly. True justice requires impartial judgment and brings reward.

20:24.title: The Value of an Honest Answer
20:24.summary: A truthful response is as pleasant and valuable as a kiss on the lips. Honesty brings relational warmth and genuine connection.

20:24.title: Proper Order of Labor and Building
20:24.summary: Establish your fields and prepare your outdoor work before building your house. Proper priority and sequence in labor ensure security and success.

20:24.title: Against False Testimony and Retaliation
20:24.summary: Do not testify falsely against your neighbor or lie with your lips; do not repay evil with evil or render harm according to what was done to you. Justice and integrity require refusing both false witness and vengeful retaliation.

20:24.title: The Sluggard's Field: A Lesson in Sloth
20:24.summary: Observing the overgrown, neglected field of a lazy man teaches a hard lesson about the consequences of laziness and idleness; even small amounts of sleep and folded hands lead to poverty that overtakes like a robber. Sloth produces inevitable ruin.

20:25.title: Title and Theme: God's Concealment and Royal Inquiry
20:25.summary: Solomon's proverbs were copied by Hezekiah's scribes, establishing their authority. God's glory lies in concealing matters while kings' glory is in discovering them, yet royal hearts remain ultimately unsearchable.

20:25.title: Purification and Proper Conduct Before Authority
20:25.summary: Just as removing dross purifies silver and removing the wicked strengthens a king's throne, one must conduct oneself humbly before rulers rather than self-exalting, so that advancement comes through invitation rather than presumption.

20:25.title: Discretion in Disputes and Confidentiality
20:25.summary: One should not hastily bring disputes to court, but resolve matters privately with one's neighbor while guarding confidences, lest public shame and lasting damage to reputation result.

20:25.title: The Value of Well-Spoken Words and Faithful Messengers
20:25.summary: Timely, apt words are precious like gold and silver; wise reproof given to the willing ear is ornamental; and a faithful messenger brings refreshment to those who sent him, much like cold snow in harvest.

20:25.title: False Boasting and Excessive Familiarity
20:25.summary: One who falsely boasts of gifts is empty like clouds without rain; similarly, overindulgence in honey or over-frequent visits to a neighbor's house breed contempt rather than favor.

20:25.title: Harmful Speech and Unsuitable Actions
20:25.summary: False testimony wounds like weapons; unfaithful confidence in trouble fails like a bad tooth; and singing to the sorrowful is as inappropriate and counterproductive as removing a garment in winter or pouring vinegar on soda.

20:25.title: Kindness to Enemies and the Consequences of Slander
20:25.summary: Feeding and watering one's hungry enemy demonstrates virtue and invites divine reward; conversely, a backbiting tongue provokes anger just as the north wind brings rain.

20:25.title: Solitude Over Conflict; Self-Control and Integrity
20:25.summary: It is better to dwell alone or in a corner than with a contentious woman; good news from afar refreshes like cold water; a righteous person should not compromise before the wicked; and pursuing honor is as unwise as excess honey, for a man without self-restraint is like a defenseless city.

20:26.title: Folly Cannot Be Honored
20:26.summary: Honor given to a fool is as inappropriate as snow in summer or rain at harvest, just as a fool needs discipline like animals need correction. These images show the natural unfitness of honoring those who lack wisdom.

20:26.title: The Paradox of Answering a Fool
20:26.summary: One must navigate carefully when dealing with a fool: answering him according to his folly risks becoming like him, yet failing to answer allows him to remain confident in his own wisdom. Both responses present dangers that require discernment.

20:26.title: The Harm Caused by Employing Fools
20:26.summary: Entrusting a fool with responsibility leads to harm and destruction, just as giving a parable to a fool renders it useless and hiring a fool for work brings injury. Fools lack the discretion and competence needed for meaningful tasks.

20:26.title: The Incorrigible Fool and the Self-Deceived
20:26.summary: A fool who repeats his folly is like a dog returning to vomit—he cannot learn from error. Yet worse than an obvious fool is the person wise in his own eyes, for he cannot be reached by correction.

20:26.title: The Sluggard's Excuses and Self-Deception
20:26.summary: The sluggard invents obstacles to avoid work and lies in bed unchanging, yet considers himself wiser than those of actual wisdom. His self-deception and laziness prevent him from productive action despite overestimating his own judgment.

20:26.title: The Danger of Meddling and Deception
20:26.summary: Interfering in another's quarrel is as dangerous as grabbing a dog by the ears, and the person who deceives his neighbor while claiming to joke is like a madman shooting deadly weapons. Both actions cause unpredictable and serious harm.

20:26.title: Gossip and Strife Feed Each Other
20:26.summary: Quarrels require gossip to sustain them just as fire requires fuel; without wood a fire dies, and without gossip strife diminishes. A contentious person kindles conflict the way coals ignite wood.

20:26.title: The Danger of Deceptive Speech
20:26.summary: Whispered words penetrate deeply and deceptively, and the lips of an evil-hearted person hide malice behind charm. A malicious man disguises his true nature in speech, but his wickedness will eventually be exposed before all.

20:26.title: Evil Returns Upon Its Author
20:26.summary: Those who dig pits for others fall into them, and those who set plots in motion find the consequences return upon themselves. A lying tongue and flattering mouth ultimately harm those who use them and bring ruin to others.

20:27.title: Humility and Self-Praise
20:27.summary: Do not boast about the future or praise yourself, for you cannot know what tomorrow holds and self-commendation is foolish. Let others recognize your worth rather than promoting yourself.

20:27.title: The Weight of Provocation and Value of True Friendship
20:27.summary: A fool's provocation is more burdensome than physical weights, and jealousy is more dangerous than anger. True friends give honest criticism while enemies offer false comfort.

20:27.title: Contentment, Home, and Companionship
20:27.summary: Satisfaction depends on inner condition, not external circumstances—the full despise honey while the hungry find bitter things sweet. A person displaced from home is restless, but good counsel and pleasant company bring heart-joy.

20:27.title: Choosing Friends and Avoiding Disaster
20:27.summary: Maintain loyalty to friends and their families; in crisis, a nearby neighbor is more valuable than a distant relative. The prudent anticipate danger and protect themselves, while the simple-minded ignore warnings and suffer.

20:27.title: Warnings Against Foolish Pledges and Contentious Partners
20:27.summary: Take security from those who pledge collateral for strangers, especially seductresses. A loud morning greeting becomes a curse, and a contentious wife is as unbearable as incessant rain—impossible to control.

20:27.title: Mutual Sharpening and Self-Knowledge
20:27.summary: Friends refine each other as iron sharpens iron, improving character and appearance. Just as water reflects a physical face, the heart reveals a person's true nature and character.

20:27.title: Insatiable Desires and Unchangeable Foolishness
20:27.summary: The grave and destruction are never satisfied, just as human desire is endless and insatiable. No amount of correction can reform a fool—beating him in a mortar cannot remove his foolishness.

20:27.title: Diligence in Pastoral Care and Lasting Wealth
20:27.summary: Pay close attention to your flocks and herds, for wealth is temporary and royal status does not endure; those who tend their livelihood gain provision. Seasonal agricultural abundance—fresh grass, lambs, and goats—supplies clothing, currency, and food for all household members.

20:28.title: The Boldness of the Righteous vs. the Fear of the Wicked
20:28.summary: The wicked flee without pursuit due to their guilty conscience, while the righteous display lion-like courage. A land in rebellion suffers from multiple rulers, but understanding and knowledge bring stability.

20:28.title: Law, Justice, and Moral Clarity
20:28.summary: A needy person who oppresses others causes destruction like violent rain; those abandoning God's law defend the wicked, while those keeping it oppose them. Only those seeking God understand true justice.

20:28.title: Integrity Over Wealth and Family Honor
20:28.summary: A poor person walking in integrity is better than a rich person living perversely. A wise son keeps the law, while keeping company with gluttons brings shame to one's father.

20:28.title: Exploitation and Listening to God's Law
20:28.summary: Wealth gained through excessive interest ultimately goes to the compassionate poor; ignoring God's law makes even prayers an abomination to Him.

20:28.title: Divine Justice and Human Perception
20:28.summary: Those who lead the upright into evil fall into their own traps while the blameless inherit good. The rich person's self-perceived wisdom is exposed by the poor person of true understanding.

20:28.title: Righteousness Brings Glory; Sin Requires Confession
20:28.summary: When the righteous succeed, there is great honor; when the wicked gain power, people hide in fear. Concealing sins prevents prosperity, but confession and repentance bring mercy.

20:28.title: Fear of God and Wicked Rulers
20:28.summary: The person who continually fears God is blessed, while the hard-hearted person falls into trouble. A wicked ruler over defenseless people is as devastating as a roaring lion or charging bear.

20:28.title: Judgment, Ill-Gotten Gain, and Bloodguilt
20:28.summary: A tyrannical ruler lacks judgment; one rejecting dishonest gain lives long. A person burdened by taking life becomes a fugitive destined to wander until death with no support.

20:28.title: Blamelessness, Labor, and Futile Pursuits
20:28.summary: The blameless person enjoys safety while one with perverse ways falls suddenly. Hard work on the land brings abundance, but chasing fantasies leads to poverty.

20:28.title: Faithfulness, Greed, and Partiality
20:28.summary: A faithful person is richly blessed while one eager to get rich will face punishment. Showing partiality is wrong, yet people will do injustice even for a piece of bread.

20:28.title: The Stingy Pursuer of Riches and the Power of Rebuke
20:28.summary: A stingy person rushes after wealth unaware that poverty awaits them. Honest rebuke is more valued than flattery and brings greater favor.

20:28.title: Theft from Family and Trust in God
20:28.summary: Robbing one's parents while claiming it's not wrong makes one a partner with destruction. A greedy person stirs up conflict, but trusting in God brings prosperity.

20:28.title: Self-Trust, Wisdom, Generosity, and the Perishing Wicked
20:28.summary: Trusting in oneself is foolishness; trusting in wisdom brings safety. Giving to the poor prevents lack and curses, while the righteous flourish when the wicked are destroyed.

20:29.title: Consequences of Stubbornness and Leadership
20:29.summary: Stubborn rebellion against correction leads to sudden destruction, while righteous leadership brings joy to people, whereas wicked rule causes suffering. These verses contrast the outcomes of personal defiance and public governance.

20:29.title: Wisdom, Integrity, and Social Justice
20:29.summary: Loving wisdom honors one's father while pursuing vice squanders wealth; just rulers build stable nations while corrupt ones destroy them; flattery and evil trap the perpetrator, but the righteous escape and celebrate, caring for the poor with genuine concern. This section emphasizes integrity in personal choices, governance, and social responsibility.

20:29.title: Wise Speech and Good Governance
20:29.summary: Mockers create turmoil in cities while wise people resolve anger; foolish disputes yield no peace; integrity is threatened by the violent; and corrupt leaders surround themselves with wicked officials. These verses highlight how wisdom brings peace while folly and corruption breed chaos.

20:29.title: God's Impartial Justice and Parental Discipline
20:29.summary: God grants sight and dignity to both poor and oppressor alike; a just king's throne endures forever; correction and discipline guide children toward wisdom and bring parental joy. These verses affirm God's universal justice and the transformative power of proper discipline.

20:29.title: Law, Obedience, and Correction
20:29.summary: Without divine guidance people abandon restraint, but law-keepers are blessed; stubborn servants cannot be corrected by words alone; hasty speakers show greater folly than fools; and indulging a servant from youth creates dependency. These verses stress the necessity of law, the limits of verbal correction, and the dangers of enabling.

20:29.title: Pride, Anger, Fear, and Moral Integrity
20:29.summary: Anger and wrath multiply sin and strife; pride brings humiliation while humility brings honor; complicity in theft harms one's own soul and prevents truthful testimony; fear of others is a trap while trust in God provides safety; many seek ruler's favor but true justice comes from God; the dishonest and righteous remain opposed. This closing section summarizes key virtues and vices underlying relational and spiritual well-being.

20:30.title: Agur's Acknowledgment of Divine Transcendence
20:30.summary: Agur confesses his profound ignorance and lack of wisdom, then poses a series of rhetorical questions about God's cosmic power and sovereignty that highlight the vastness of divine knowledge beyond human comprehension.

20:30.title: The Perfection and Inviolability of God's Word
20:30.summary: God's word is declared flawless and protective for those who trust in him, with a stern warning against adding to or distorting his revealed word.

20:30.title: Agur's Prayer for Provision and Integrity
20:30.summary: Agur requests two things: freedom from falsehood and a moderate provision of food, recognizing that either excess wealth or poverty could lead him to deny God or compromise his integrity.

20:30.title: Warnings Against Slander and Depictions of a Depraved Generation
20:30.summary: A prohibition against slandering servants is followed by portraits of four generations characterized by disrespect for parents, spiritual blindness, arrogance, and violent exploitation of the vulnerable.

20:30.title: Things Never Satisfied and Consequences of Disrespecting Parents
20:30.summary: An image of insatiable greed is presented through the leach's daughters and four never-satisfied things, followed by a warning that mocking one's parents invites divine judgment through nature.

20:30.title: Wonders Beyond Understanding and the Adulteress
20:30.summary: Four phenomena—the flight of an eagle, movement of a serpent, course of a ship, and courtship of a man—are declared incomprehensible, with the adulteress illustrating shameless moral obliviousness.

20:30.title: Four Things That Cause the Earth to Tremble
20:30.summary: Four scenarios depicting social disorder and upheaval—an unfit servant ruling, a foolish man becoming satisfied, an unloved woman marrying, and a servant becoming heir—cause instability that the earth cannot bear.

20:30.title: Four Small Creatures Remarkably Wise
20:30.summary: Despite their weakness, four small animals—ants, conies, locusts, and lizards—demonstrate wisdom through industry, prudence, coordination, and resilience, earning them places even in kings' palaces.

20:30.title: Four Creatures Stately in Their March
20:30.summary: Four beings—the lion, greyhound, male goat, and king—are presented as majestic and commanding in their bearing, moving with authority and dignity that compels respect and submission.

20:30.title: The Wisdom of Restraint in Speech and Anger
20:30.summary: Agur counsels silencing oneself through covering the mouth when pride or evil thoughts arise, using the image of churning and wounding to show how forced anger inevitably produces conflict and harm.

20:31.title: King Lemuel's Mother's Instruction on Kingship and Justice
20:31.summary: Lemuel's mother teaches him to avoid destructive pursuits like excessive wine and womanizing, which lead kings astray from upholding justice. Instead, she instructs him to use wine to comfort the suffering and to speak up for the voiceless and oppressed.

20:31.title: The Woman of Noble Character (The Virtuous Woman)
20:31.summary: An acrostic poem praising the exemplary wife and woman whose excellence surpasses mere physical beauty—she is industrious, generous, wise, strong, and God-fearing, bringing honor to her family and earning lasting respect and praise. Her true value lies not in charm or appearance but in her character, fear of the Lord, and the fruit of her labors.

21:1.title: Title and Theme: The Preacher's Proclamation of Vanity
21:1.summary: The Preacher, identified as the son of David and king in Jerusalem, declares that all is vanity—emphasizing this truth through repetition. This opening statement sets the central theme of the entire book.

21:1.title: The Cyclical Nature of Life and the Insignificance of Human Labor
21:1.summary: The Preacher observes that human labor yields no lasting gain, as all existence moves in endless, repetitive cycles—generations pass, natural phenomena recur without variation, and nothing is truly new under the sun. Human achievement and memory are ultimately forgotten and insignificant.

21:1.title: The Preacher's Personal Quest and Conclusion
21:1.summary: The Preacher describes his unique position as king and his deliberate pursuit of wisdom to understand all human activity, yet he concludes that despite his investigation, all remains vanity and a chasing after wind. Even the most accomplished and wise person cannot escape this fundamental futility.

21:1.title: The Limitations of Wisdom and the Sorrow It Brings
21:1.summary: The Preacher reflects on the constraints of human ability—what is crooked cannot be made straight, and what is lacking cannot be numbered—and reveals that his pursuit of wisdom and knowledge, while surpassing all predecessors, ultimately leads only to grief and sorrow. Increased understanding only deepens human suffering.

21:2.title: Testing Pleasure and Mirth
21:2.summary: The Preacher decides to test himself with pleasure and laughter to see if they provide meaning, while his heart guides him with wisdom. He questions whether mirth and laughter accomplish anything worthwhile.

21:2.title: Accumulation of Wealth and Accomplishments
21:2.summary: The Preacher catalogs his great works, possessions, and achievements—grand buildings, gardens, servants, treasure, and entertainment—withholding nothing his eyes or heart desired. Despite his wisdom remaining with him, he found temporary joy in all his labor.

21:2.title: All Accomplishments Are Vanity
21:2.summary: Upon reflection, the Preacher recognizes that all his works and labor are meaningless and futile—merely chasing after wind with no lasting profit.

21:2.title: Wisdom Cannot Overcome Death
21:2.summary: Though wisdom excels folly as light exceeds darkness, the Preacher realizes that death comes to both the wise and the fool alike, and neither is remembered forever. The advantage of wisdom is ultimately nullified by the common fate of mortality.

21:2.title: Despair Over Unrewarded Labor
21:2.summary: The Preacher comes to hate life itself because all labor under the sun is grievous and meaningless; he must leave his works to an unknown successor who may be a fool, and his days are filled with sorrow and toil with no rest. All of this constitutes vanity and evil.

21:2.title: The Only Good: Simple Enjoyment from God
21:2.summary: The Preacher concludes that the only worthwhile thing is for a person to eat, drink, and enjoy good in their labor—a gift from God's hand. Those whom God favors receive wisdom, knowledge, and joy, while sinners labor only to accumulate for others, which is vanity.

21:3.title: A Time for Every Purpose Under Heaven
21:3.summary: The Preacher presents a comprehensive catalog of opposite human experiences and activities, asserting that God has appointed a specific time for each purpose. This poetic enumeration demonstrates the cyclical and balanced nature of life's seasons and activities.

21:3.title: The Burden of Work and God's Hidden Design
21:3.summary: The Preacher questions the profit of human labor given the burden God has placed on mortals, yet acknowledges that God makes everything beautiful in its time and has set eternity in human hearts, though His complete work remains unknowable. This reflects the tension between God's hidden purposes and human limitation.

21:3.title: The Gift of Joy and God's Eternal Work
21:3.summary: The Preacher concludes that the best course is to rejoice, do good, and enjoy the fruits of one's labor as God's gift, knowing that whatever God does is eternal and unchanging. He notes that God seeks to repeat what has passed, emphasizing divine constancy and human mortality.

21:3.title: Injustice in the World and Divine Judgment
21:3.summary: The Preacher observes wickedness in places of justice and righteousness, but affirms his conviction that God will judge both the righteous and the wicked in due time. This expresses faith in ultimate divine justice despite present injustice.

21:3.title: Humans and Animals: The Test of Mortality
21:3.summary: The Preacher reflects that God tests humanity so they may recognize their equality with animals in mortality—both breathe the same breath, both return to dust, and no one knows whether the human spirit rises while the animal's descends. This emphasizes human vulnerability and the mystery of what lies beyond death.

21:3.title: Rejoicing in One's Portion
21:3.summary: The Preacher concludes that a person's best good is to rejoice in their own works, since this is their appointed portion and no one can show them what comes after death. This final statement returns to the theme of finding contentment in present life rather than seeking unknowable future knowledge.

21:4.title: The Oppressed Have No Comforter
21:4.summary: The Preacher observes widespread oppression and suffering under the sun, with the oppressed having no one to comfort them while their oppressors hold power. He concludes that the dead and even the unborn are better off than those suffering in this world.

21:4.title: Ambition and Envy Lead to Vanity
21:4.summary: The Preacher observes that all labor and achievement stem from envy and competition with one's neighbor, which is empty and futile; he affirms that modest contentment is better than exhausting striving for more.

21:4.title: The Isolation of the Solitary Laborer
21:4.summary: The Preacher observes a man working alone without family, whose endless labor never satisfies him, leaving him to question the purpose of his toil and deprivation—another example of vanity and misery.

21:4.title: The Value of Companionship and Community
21:4.summary: The Preacher argues that two people are better than one because they share labor's rewards and can help each other in hardship, warmth, and defense; a threefold cord symbolizes the strength and stability of community.

21:4.title: The Rise and Fall of a Youth King
21:4.summary: The Preacher observes that a poor, wise young man can rise from prison to become king, replacing an old, foolish king; yet the masses who initially supported the youth eventually abandon him, showing that even popular success is ultimately vain and fleeting.

21:5.title: Reverence and Restraint in Worship
21:5.summary: The Preacher instructs that listening to God with reverence is superior to thoughtless sacrifice, and warns against hasty or excessive words before God, comparing foolish speech to the confusion of dreams.

21:5.title: The Importance of Keeping Vows
21:5.summary: The Preacher emphasizes that vows made to God must be fulfilled promptly, warning that it is better not to vow than to break a vow and incur God's anger, and reiterates the need to fear God amid vanity.

21:5.title: Oppression and Hierarchy of Power
21:5.summary: The Preacher acknowledges that oppression exists in society due to a hierarchy of officials, each accountable to a higher authority, yet the earth's produce ultimately belongs to all.

21:5.title: The Insatiability of Wealth and Its Effects
21:5.summary: Those who love money and abundance are never satisfied, and while the laborer sleeps peacefully, the rich are kept awake by their abundance, revealing the futility and anxiety of wealth.

21:5.title: Wealth as a Burden and Human Mortality
21:5.summary: The Preacher describes the tragedy of hoarding wealth to one's own harm, noting that riches can be lost and that humans leave life as they entered it—naked and empty-handed, gaining nothing from their labor.

21:5.title: The Good Life: Eating, Drinking, and Enjoying God's Gift
21:5.summary: In contrast to the miser's darkness and frustration, the Preacher affirms that it is good and proper to eat, drink, and enjoy the fruits of one's labor as God's gift, bringing joy and satisfaction in the present.

21:6.title: The Evil of Possessing Wealth Without Power to Enjoy It
21:6.summary: The Preacher observes a grave injustice: a person whom God grants riches, wealth, and honor lacks the ability to enjoy these possessions, while a stranger consumes them instead. This represents a profound vanity and spiritual disease.

21:6.title: The Stillborn Child's Advantage
21:6.summary: A man with a hundred children and many years of life, yet without satisfaction or proper burial, is worse off than a stillborn child who experiences no toil and finds rest in darkness. The stillborn never saw the sun or knew hardship, gaining rest rather than suffering.

21:6.title: The Universal Problem of Unsatisfied Desire
21:6.summary: Whether one lives a thousand years or merely exists, all people who fail to enjoy good end up in the same place; all human labor merely sustains hunger without satisfaction, and the wise person gains no greater advantage than the fool. Looking is better than endless desiring, yet both are vanity.

21:6.title: The Limits of Human Knowledge and Contention
21:6.summary: What has been has always been named and known; human nature is fixed and unchanging, and no one can contend with one greater than himself. Many words create only vanity, and no one can know what truly benefits a person in life or what comes after death.

21:7.title: The Value of Mourning Over Celebration
21:7.summary: A good reputation and contemplation of death are more valuable than superficial pleasures, for reflection on mortality and sorrow refines the heart while foolish mirth leads astray.

21:7.title: Wisdom's Rebuke Versus Foolish Laughter
21:7.summary: Accepting wise correction is far superior to hearing foolish praise, since the fool's laughter is meaningless and extortion or bribes corrupt even the wise.

21:7.title: Patience and Perspective on the Present
21:7.summary: Patience and humility matter more than pride, hasty anger is foolish, and one should not nostalgically wish for a better past without wisdom.

21:7.title: Wisdom as Superior Inheritance and Defense
21:7.summary: Wisdom surpasses material inheritance and provides both defense and life-preservation, making it the most excellent possession.

21:7.title: God's Inscrutable Design
21:7.summary: God's work is beyond human correction, and He has deliberately balanced prosperity and adversity so that humanity cannot comprehend His full purposes.

21:7.title: The Paradox of Righteousness and Wisdom
21:7.summary: The righteous sometimes perish while the wicked flourish, so one should avoid both excessive self-righteousness and extreme wickedness; fear of God leads to balance and wisdom.

21:7.title: Wisdom's Strength and Universal Sinfulness
21:7.summary: Wisdom provides more strength than worldly power, yet no person on earth is entirely righteous and sin-free.

21:7.title: Don't Judge Others' Words Too Harshly
21:7.summary: One should not take every idle word spoken against oneself seriously, recognizing that everyone, including oneself, has cursed and spoken ill of others.

21:7.title: The Search for Wisdom and Understanding
21:7.summary: Despite seeking to be wise, the Preacher found that true wisdom and understanding remain elusive and deeply hidden, though he discerned that wickedness is stupidity and foolishness is madness.

21:7.title: The Danger of Seductive Woman and God's Original Design
21:7.summary: Some women are spiritually dangerous traps, and only those who fear God escape such snares; God made humanity upright, but humans have devised many sinful schemes to depart from that uprightness.

21:8.title: Wisdom and Obedience to Authority
21:8.summary: The wise person's understanding shines through their bearing, and they know when to obey the king's commands out of respect for God's oath. One must be cautious in the king's presence and not question his supreme authority, as he answers to no one.

21:8.title: Timing, Knowledge, and Human Powerlessness
21:8.summary: Those who keep wise counsel avoid harm and understand the proper time and way for all things, yet humans remain ignorant of the future and powerless over death and the spirit. Despite human attempts at power, one person can still harm another under the sun.

21:8.title: The Problem of Injustice and Apparent Meaninglessness
21:8.summary: The Preacher observes that the wicked are buried and forgotten despite their prominence, yet because justice is delayed, people persist in evil. The righteous sometimes suffer like the wicked and vice versa, which the Preacher identifies as ultimate vanity and absurdity.

21:8.title: Joy in Labor as Life's Best Good
21:8.summary: Since humans cannot fully comprehend God's work despite all investigation and wisdom, the best response is to find joy in eating, drinking, and finding gladness in one's labors—the gifts God provides. Human wisdom and effort ultimately cannot unlock the mysteries of divine work.

21:9.title: The Common Fate of All People
21:9.summary: The Preacher reflects that righteous and wicked alike face the same ultimate fate—death—which is an evil reality that troubles human hearts. All distinctions of morality and conduct collapse under the finality of shared mortality.

21:9.title: Why Life is Better Than Death
21:9.summary: Though death comes to all, the living have at least the hope and consciousness of existence, while the dead experience nothing and are entirely forgotten. Life, however uncertain, retains value that death completely erases.

21:9.title: Carpe Diem—Joy in Life's Transience
21:9.summary: The Preacher urges the living to embrace simple pleasures—eating, drinking, and loving—as their legitimate portion in life, since God has already approved these pursuits. Joy amid vanity is not escapism but acceptance of the human lot.

21:9.title: Work With Diligence While You Can
21:9.summary: The Preacher exhorts full engagement in present labors, since Sheol offers no opportunity for work, wisdom, or achievement after death. Wholehearted effort now is the only rational response to mortality.

21:9.title: The Limits of Human Achievement and Foresight
21:9.summary: Success in running, battle, or acquiring wealth does not depend on human ability alone but on time and chance beyond anyone's control; people cannot even know when calamity will strike. Human weakness and unpredictability expose the vanity of striving.

21:9.title: The Parable of the Poor Wise Man
21:9.summary: A poor wise man saves a besieged city through wisdom, yet receives no honor or recognition for his deed. The Preacher concludes that wisdom is superior to strength and weapons, yet society often despises and ignores the wise, especially the poor, while one fool can undo much good.

21:10.title: The Corrupting Power of Folly
21:10.summary: Small acts of folly can ruin great wisdom and honor, just as dead flies spoil fine perfume. A fool's foolishness is evident in his conduct and self-proclamation wherever he goes.

21:10.title: Wise Conduct Before Authority and Social Disorder
21:10.summary: When facing an angry ruler, gentleness and restraint defuse tension rather than escalating conflict. The Preacher observes the disorder of his time: fools elevated to positions of honor while the worthy are humbled.

21:10.title: Dangers of Carelessness and Unpreparedness
21:10.summary: Reckless actions bring harm to the actor—digging pits, breaking walls, and carving stones all result in injury. Even the skilled charmer gains nothing if the snake bites before being charmed, showing the limits of expertise without proper conditions.

21:10.title: The Wise and Foolish Use of Words
21:10.summary: A wise person's words are gracious and benefit others, while a fool destroys himself through his own speech. Fools multiply words excessively and presume to know the future, which is beyond human knowledge.

21:10.title: The Effects of Foolish Labor and Wise Governance
21:10.summary: A fool's ceaseless labor exhausts him because he lacks basic wisdom and direction. A nation blessed with a noble king who leads with proper habits and strength is far happier than one ruled by a child-king whose princes feast recklessly.

21:10.title: Idleness, Pleasure, and Prudent Speech
21:10.summary: Slothfulness causes homes to decay and leak, yet feasting and wine bring joy and money enables all things—a paradox of life's pleasures. Nevertheless, one must guard speech carefully, avoiding curses against king or rich, lest private words be reported by unseen listeners.

21:11.title: Generosity and Charity Despite Uncertainty
21:11.summary: The Teacher encourages generous giving and sharing with many, not knowing what calamities may come. This teaches that benevolence and charitable giving are virtues even when outcomes are uncertain.

21:11.title: Sowing Without Complete Knowledge
21:11.summary: Natural processes occur according to their own laws regardless of human observation; the farmer must sow and reap without knowing which effort will succeed, teaching that action must proceed despite human ignorance and inability to control outcomes. Work persistently in both morning and evening, accepting that some efforts will prosper and others may not.

21:11.title: Enjoy Life While Remembering Its Brevity
21:11.summary: Light and life are precious and sweet, and one should rejoice in years lived; yet this joy must be tempered by remembrance of the many dark days coming, for all is ultimately vanity. The passage balances appreciation for life's goodness with realistic acknowledgment of suffering and mortality.

21:11.title: Youth's Pleasure Pursued Under Divine Judgment
21:11.summary: Young people are encouraged to rejoice and follow their hearts' desires during youth, yet with the sobering knowledge that God will bring all actions into judgment. Remove sorrow and evil from life, recognizing that youthful vitality and beauty are fleeting.

21:12.title: Remember Your Creator in Youth Before the Decay of Old Age
21:12.summary: The Preacher urges remembrance of God in youth before the inevitable physical decline and death comes. Using metaphorical language about darkening lights, trembling limbs, failing senses, and the return of dust to earth and spirit to God, he depicts the natural progression toward mortality and the futility of earthly pleasure.

21:12.title: The Preacher's Wisdom and Teaching Authority
21:12.summary: The Preacher reaffirms that all is vanity while affirming his role as a wise teacher who studied, pondered, and organized proverbs to communicate truth. He describes the power of wise words as goads and nails—penetrating and enduring tools for instruction given through one shepherd.

21:12.title: Final Exhortation: Fear God and Keep His Commandments
21:12.summary: The Preacher concludes by warning against endless study and book-making as wearisome pursuits, instead directing his reader to the essential duty: fear God and keep His commandments, knowing that all works—hidden or revealed—will be brought into divine judgment.

22:1.title: Title of the Work
22:1.summary: The Song of Solomon is introduced as Solomon's composition, establishing the work's authorship and significance.

22:1.title: The Beloved's Longing for Her Lover
22:1.summary: The Beloved expresses her intense desire for her lover, describing his affection as surpassing wine, while friends celebrate their love. This opening establishes the passionate longing and mutual admiration central to the song.

22:1.title: The Beloved Describes Her Dark Complexion
22:1.summary: The Beloved acknowledges her dark skin, stating she is lovely despite—or because of—her sun-darkened appearance, explaining she was made to work in vineyards and neglected her own care. This assertion defends her beauty against potential judgment from the daughters of Jerusalem.

22:1.title: The Beloved Seeks Her Lover; The Lover Responds
22:1.summary: The Beloved asks her lover where he tends his flock at midday, desiring to be near him rather than wandering uncertain; the Lover responds by inviting her to follow the sheep and join him by the shepherds' tents. Their exchange reflects mutual longing and companionship.

22:1.title: The Lover Praises the Beloved's Beauty
22:1.summary: The Lover compares the Beloved to a prized chariot horse and praises her adorned cheeks and neck; friends offer to create golden and silver ornaments for her. This passage celebrates the Beloved's beauty through rich imagery and communal affirmation.

22:1.title: Mutual Expressions of Fragrance and Affection
22:1.summary: The Beloved describes how her perfume filled the air while the king was present, and characterizes her beloved as precious fragrances—myrrh and henna blossoms—that she keeps close to her heart. The passage uses sensory imagery to convey intimacy and value.

22:1.title: Mutual Beauty and Their Shared Dwelling
22:1.summary: The lovers exchange affirmations of each other's beauty—the Lover comparing the Beloved's eyes to doves while describing their resting place, and the Beloved affirming his beauty and describing their home with precious materials. The passage concludes with imagery of permanence and comfort in their shared space.

22:2.title: Mutual Admiration and Comparison
22:2.summary: The lovers exchange compliments, each describing the other's beauty and uniqueness compared to all others. Their declarations establish the exclusivity and desirability of their love.

22:2.title: The Banquet of Love and Physical Affection
22:2.summary: The beloved describes being brought to a banquet hall and held tenderly, overwhelmed by love, while cautioning the daughters of Jerusalem not to rush or force love prematurely. This passage celebrates intimate affection while warning against hastening love's proper timing.

22:2.title: The Beloved's Arrival in Spring
22:2.summary: The lover anticipates and witnesses the beloved's arrival, described with the vitality and movement of springtime when winter has passed and the earth blooms with new life. The beloved invites the lover to arise and depart together, celebrating the season of love's fullness.

22:2.title: Tender Expressions and the Lover's Request
22:2.summary: The lover calls to his beloved in the refuge of the mountains, praising her beauty and voice, while the beloved expresses possession of her lover and asks him to return at evening. The passage concludes with mutual longing and commitment despite the brevity of their time together.

22:3.title: The Beloved's Nighttime Search
22:3.summary: The speaker searches for her beloved through the night and city streets, encountering watchmen before finally finding him and bringing him to her mother's house. This depicts the longing and joy of romantic love pursued and fulfilled.

22:3.title: Charge to the Daughters of Jerusalem
22:3.summary: The speaker adjures the daughters of Jerusalem not to awaken love prematurely, emphasizing that love must develop in its proper time. This warns against forcing or rushing the natural progression of romantic love.

22:3.title: Solomon's Magnificent Procession
22:3.summary: A description of King Solomon's ornate carriage approaching from the wilderness, surrounded by armed guards and constructed with precious materials, celebrated by the daughters of Zion on his wedding day. This depicts royal splendor and the joy of the wedding celebration.

22:4.title: The Lover Praises the Beloved's Beauty
22:4.summary: The lover celebrates the beloved's physical beauty in detailed imagery, from her eyes and hair to her lips and breasts, declaring her completely beautiful and flawless. This extended praise poem emphasizes the lover's admiration and desire for intimate union.

22:4.title: Invitation and Expressions of Enchantment
22:4.summary: The lover invites the beloved to come away with him from Lebanon and surrounding mountains, expressing how completely she has captivated his heart and overwhelmed his senses with her love and fragrance. His praise focuses on how she surpasses all other pleasures.

22:4.title: The Garden of the Beloved and Mutual Invitation
22:4.summary: The lover describes the beloved as a locked and private garden, orchard, and fountain filled with precious spices and fruits, symbols of her exclusivity and fertility, then the beloved awakens the winds to spread her fragrance and invites her lover to enter and enjoy her garden. The passage concludes with mutual desire and consummation.

22:5.title: The Lover's Garden Celebration
22:5.summary: The beloved declares his arrival in the garden of his love and celebrates their union with sensory imagery of spices, honey, wine, and milk, while friends are invited to rejoice in the abundance of their love.

22:5.title: The Beloved's Knock and the Lover's Lost Opportunity
22:5.summary: While the beloved sleeps, her lover knocks urgently at night, but her hesitation in responding causes him to leave before she opens the door, leading to her distressed search and encounter with hostile watchmen who beat and rob her.

22:5.title: The Beloved's Plea to the Daughters of Jerusalem
22:5.summary: Distressed and lovesick, the beloved entreats the daughters of Jerusalem to tell her lover how deeply she loves him if they find him.

22:5.title: The Friends' Question
22:5.summary: The daughters of Jerusalem challenge the beloved, asking what makes her beloved so special that she would adjure them so earnestly on his behalf.

22:5.title: The Beloved's Praise of Her Lover
22:5.summary: The beloved provides an elaborate, sensory-rich description of her lover's physical beauty and excellence, from his complexion and hair to his limbs and overall appeal, concluding that he is altogether lovely and her beloved friend.

22:6.title: The Beloved's Whereabouts and Mutual Possession
22:6.summary: The chorus asks the Shulamite where her beloved has gone, and she responds that he has gone to his garden to feed among the flowers. She declares their mutual belonging: she is his and he is hers.

22:6.title: The Lover's Praise of His Beloved
22:6.summary: The beloved praises the Shulamite's beauty by comparing her to great cities and armies, describing her physical features with poetic imagery. He declares her uniqueness and perfection above all other women, even queens and concubines.

22:6.title: The Lover's Contemplation and Desire
22:6.summary: The chorus asks who this beautiful woman is, appearing like dawn, moon, and sun. The lover describes going into a garden to inspect its growth but being overcome by desire and transported with his royal people.

22:6.title: The Chorus's Call and the Lover's Question
22:6.summary: The chorus repeatedly calls the Shulamite to return so they may gaze upon her, and the lover questions why they desire to look at her as at a ceremonial dance.

22:7.title: The Lover Describes the Beloved's Beauty
22:7.summary: The male lover offers an elaborate, poetic description of his beloved's physical beauty from her feet upward, comparing her graceful form to precious materials, natural features, and architectural structures. This intimate praise celebrates her elegant appearance and affirms her desirability.

22:7.title: Expressions of Passion and Delight
22:7.summary: The lover continues his passionate celebration of the beloved, expressing delight in her stature and declaring his intention to embrace her, comparing her to fruit and wine. His language becomes increasingly intimate, conveying physical longing and sensual joy.

22:7.title: Mutual Belonging and Romantic Invitation
22:7.summary: The beloved affirms her devotion to her lover and his desire for her, then invites him to join her in the countryside where she offers herself to him amid nature's beauty and fertility. This closing passage expresses mutual commitment and intimate invitation within a pastoral setting.

22:8.title: Longing for Public Affection and the Refrain on Love
22:8.summary: The beloved wishes her lover were like a brother so she could publicly express her affection without shame, describing intimate moments she would share with him. She then repeats the refrain urging not to awaken love prematurely.

22:8.title: Love's Unquenchable Strength
22:8.summary: The lovers are reunited, with the woman stirring passion under the apple tree where her beloved was born. The passage celebrates that love is as powerful as death itself and cannot be extinguished by any force.

22:8.title: The Little Sister and the Beloved's Maturity
22:8.summary: Others speak of having a young, immature sister and plan how to protect her future; the beloved responds that she has matured into a strong wall, finding favor and peace in her beloved's sight.

22:8.title: The Vineyard: Possession and Devotion
22:8.summary: Solomon's vast commercial vineyard is contrasted with the beloved's personal vineyard, which she gives entirely to her lover, symbolizing her singular devotion to him above all worldly wealth.

22:8.title: The Final Appeal and Consummation
22:8.summary: The beloved calls to her lover in the gardens and invites him to flee with her to the mountains of spices, concluding the poem with an image of joyful union and escape together.

23:1.title: The Indictment of Rebellious Judah
23:1.summary: Isaiah presents his vision concerning Judah and Jerusalem, calling heaven and earth to witness Yahweh's complaint that Israel, unlike animals who know their masters, has rejected and rebelled against God. The nation is sick from head to foot with sin and suffers desolation, yet a small remnant remains by God's mercy, spared from complete destruction like Sodom and Gomorrah.

23:1.title: Yahweh Rejects Empty Sacrifice and Calls for Justice
23:1.summary: God addresses the rulers and people as if they were Sodom and Gomorrah, rejecting their multiplied sacrifices, religious observances, and prayers as worthless because they are accompanied by bloodguilt and evil. Instead, Yahweh calls them to cease wrongdoing, learn to do good, and practice justice by defending the oppressed, orphans, and widows.

23:1.title: An Offer of Redemption Through Obedience
23:1.summary: Yahweh extends an invitation to reason together and offers the possibility of complete forgiveness—sins as scarlet shall become white as snow—contingent upon willing obedience and repentance. Refusal and rebellion will result in judgment by the sword.

23:1.title: The Corruption of Jerusalem and Its Purification
23:1.summary: Jerusalem, once a faithful city full of justice, has become corrupt—her silver turned to dross, her rulers become thieves and bribe-takers who neglect the vulnerable. Yahweh promises to purge away the dross, restore righteous judges and counselors, and transform the city into 'the city of righteousness.'

23:1.title: Redemption for the Converted, Destruction for Transgressors
23:1.summary: Zion will be redeemed through justice, and her repentant converts through righteousness, but transgressors and those who forsake Yahweh will be destroyed together. The wicked, once strong as oak trees, will wither and burn like tinder consumed by fire with none to quench it.

23:2.title: The Mountain of the Lord in the Last Days
23:2.summary: Isaiah describes a vision of Zion's future exaltation when God's house will be established as the highest place, drawing all nations to receive instruction in God's ways and experience universal peace. The law will go forth from Jerusalem, and nations will cease their wars, transforming weapons into agricultural tools.

23:2.title: Israel's Abandonment of God
23:2.summary: Isaiah rebukes Judah for forsaking God and filling their land with foreign divination practices, material wealth, military power, and idolatry. Their self-sufficiency and worship of human-made idols have brought them low, and judgment is declared against them.

23:2.title: The Day of Yahweh's Judgment Against Human Pride
23:2.summary: Isaiah describes the terrifying day when God will execute judgment, humbling all human pride and exaltation—whether found in natural majesty (cedars, mountains), human achievement (towers, walls, ships), or personal arrogance. Only Yahweh will be exalted on that day.

23:2.title: The Destruction of Idols and False Trust
23:2.summary: When God rises to shake the earth, idols will completely vanish as people flee in terror into caves and crevices, casting away their precious idols of silver and gold. The passage concludes by warning against trusting in humanity, whose brief life is worthless compared to God.

23:3.title: The Removal of Judah's Leaders and Support
23:3.summary: The Lord will strip Jerusalem and Judah of all their sustenance and leadership—removing mighty men, judges, prophets, and wise counselors—leaving only incompetent children in positions of power, creating chaos and desperation among the people.

23:3.title: Judg­ment Due to Open Rebellion and Sin
23:3.summary: Jerusalem and Judah fall because their words and deeds rebel against the Lord; they openly parade their sin like Sodom without shame, and the righteous will be rewarded while the wicked face disaster for their own deeds.

23:3.title: The Lord's Indictment of Judah's Leaders
23:3.summary: The Lord rises to judge, specifically confronting the elders and leaders who have exploited and oppressed the poor, enriching themselves from the vulnerable while leading the people into error.

23:3.title: The Humbling of Zion's Daughters
23:3.summary: Because the women of Zion are haughty and proud, adorning themselves with luxurious ornaments and jewelry, the Lord will strip them of all beauty and finery, replacing their splendor with sores, baldness, sackcloth, and shame.

23:3.title: The Desolation of Jerusalem
23:3.summary: The men and warriors of Jerusalem will fall by the sword in battle, and the city gates will mourn as Jerusalem itself becomes desolate and sits abandoned on the ground.

23:4.title: Seven Women Seek One Man
23:4.summary: In a future day of judgment, seven women will desperately seek marriage to one man, willing to provide for themselves if only they can bear his name and lose their shame. This reflects the devastating population loss and social desperation resulting from God's judgment.

23:4.title: The Branch of the Lord and the Cleansing of Zion
23:4.summary: After judgment, God's branch will bring beauty and glory to the survivors of Israel, and those remaining in Zion will be called holy after the Lord purges away filth and bloodshed through justice and burning. This announces a future restoration and sanctification of God's people.

23:4.title: The Glory and Shelter Over Zion
23:4.summary: The Lord will establish a miraculous cloud by day and flaming fire by night over Mount Zion as a canopy and shelter, providing both shade from heat and refuge from storms. This imagery of God's protective presence recalls the wilderness wandering and promises divine care for the redeemed community.

23:5.title: The Song of the Vineyard
23:5.summary: Isaiah presents a parable of God's vineyard (Israel) that received every care but produced worthless fruit, inviting judgment. The passage reveals that God expected justice and righteousness but found oppression and distress instead.

23:5.title: First Woe: Greed and Its Consequences
23:5.summary: Isaiah pronounces judgment on those who accumulate property greedily and neglect God's works, warning that their lands will be desolate and their multitudes will perish. Yet God will be exalted in justice while the arrogant are humbled.

23:5.title: Woes Against Moral Corruption and Rejection of God
23:5.summary: Isaiah pronounces multiple woes against those who mock God's judgment, pervert morality by calling evil good, trust in their own wisdom, and corrupt justice through bribery. Their rejection of God's law will result in complete destruction.

23:5.title: God's Judgment Through Foreign Invasion
23:5.summary: God's burning anger against his people manifests through a swift military invasion by a distant nation, described as an unstoppable force that will seize prey and bring darkness and distress upon the land. The passage emphasizes God's unrelenting judgment.

23:6.title: Isaiah's Vision of the Lord's Glory
23:6.summary: In a vision, Isaiah sees the Lord seated on a high throne in the temple, attended by seraphim who proclaim His holiness. The scene is marked by overwhelming glory that causes the temple to shake and fill with smoke, emphasizing God's transcendent majesty and the vastness of His presence.

23:6.title: Isaiah's Confession and Cleansing
23:6.summary: Confronted by God's holiness, Isaiah confesses his unworthiness and that of his people, recognizing their unclean lips. A seraphim then purifies him by touching his lips with a coal from the altar, declaring his iniquity removed and sins forgiven, preparing him for service.

23:6.title: Isaiah's Commission and the Message of Judgment
23:6.summary: God calls Isaiah to be a prophet, and Isaiah readily responds; however, God's message is one of spiritual hardening and judgment—the people will hear but not understand, see but not perceive. Though destruction will come, a remnant (the holy seed) will remain, preserving hope beyond judgment.

23:7.title: The Threat of the Aramean and Israelite Alliance
23:7.summary: King Rezin of Syria and King Pekah of Israel march against Jerusalem, causing fear throughout King Ahaz and his people. Though they cannot conquer the city, their alliance terrifies Judah.

23:7.title: Isaiah's Message of Reassurance to Ahaz
23:7.summary: The Lord sends Isaiah to meet King Ahaz with his son and delivers an oracle assuring him that the two allied kings will not succeed; Syria will fall within 65 years, but Judah will be established if Ahaz believes. The message offers divine reassurance against overwhelming political pressure.

23:7.title: The Sign of Immanuel
23:7.summary: When Ahaz refuses to ask for a sign, Isaiah announces that the Lord will give one anyway: a virgin will conceive and bear a son named Immanuel, and before the child reaches maturity, the two threatening kings will be gone. This miraculous sign assures Judah of God's presence and protection.

23:7.title: Coming Judgment and Devastation
23:7.summary: Isaiah warns of severe judgment coming through the king of Assyria, describing how the land will be stripped bare, cultivated fields will become briars and thorns, and only subsistence living will remain. This judgment will fall upon Judah as the ultimate consequence of unfaithfulness, despite the promise of Immanuel.

23:8.title: The Sign of Maher-Shalal-Hash-Baz
23:8.summary: Isaiah is commanded to write a prophetic name on a tablet and take witnesses, then bears a son with the prophetess whose name signifies that Damascus and Samaria will be plundered by Assyria before the child can speak. This serves as a sign of imminent judgment on Israel's northern neighbors and warning to Judah.

23:8.title: The Flood Waters of Assyria
23:8.summary: Yahweh explains that Judah's rejection of gentle waters and trust in pagan alliances will bring the overwhelming flood of Assyrian invasion, yet declares that God is with His people and no counsel against them will stand. The passage announces both judgment for unfaithfulness and assurance of God's ultimate presence and protection.

23:8.title: Fear God, Not Men
23:8.summary: Isaiah is instructed not to fear the conspiracies and threats of the people but to revere Yahweh alone as holy and to be feared, since God will be both a sanctuary for those who trust Him and a snare for those who reject Him. The passage warns of stumbling and capture for those who resist God's word.

23:8.title: The Prophet's Faithful Witness
23:8.summary: Isaiah commits to preserving God's testimony and law among his disciples, waiting for the Lord despite His hidden face, and declares that he and his children are signs and wonders testifying to God's presence among Israel. This passage affirms the prophet's role as a faithful witness during a time of judgment.

23:8.title: Consult God's Law, Not the Dead
23:8.summary: Isaiah condemns consulting mediums and necromancers instead of God and His law, warning that those who reject God's word will face darkness, distress, and anguish with no morning light. The passage calls people to trust in God's revelation rather than seeking guidance from the dead or pagan practices.

23:9.title: The Coming of the Messiah and Everlasting Peace
23:9.summary: Isaiah proclaims that the people in darkness will see a great light—a child born who will be called Wonderful Counselor, Mighty God, Everlasting Father, and Prince of Peace. His government will increase without end, established on David's throne through justice and righteousness forever.

23:9.title: The Lord's Word Against Israel's Pride
23:9.summary: The Lord sends a word against Israel, and the people of the northern kingdom boast in their pride that they will rebuild what was destroyed. In response, God will raise up enemies—Syrians and Philistines—to devour Israel, and His anger remains kindled.

23:9.title: Israel's Unrepentance and God's Continued Anger
23:9.summary: The people do not turn back to God who struck them, so the Lord will cut off from Israel both leaders and followers, the noble and the deceptive prophet. God will show no compassion on the young, fatherless, and widows because the nation is profane and evil, and His anger remains unturned.

23:9.title: Wickedness Consumes the Land and Its People
23:9.summary: Wickedness burns like wildfire through the land, consuming it and reducing the people to fuel for the fire. The survivors turn against one another in desperation and fratricidal violence, with tribes devouring their own flesh, and God's anger remains unturned.

23:10.title: Woe to Unjust Lawmakers
23:10.summary: Isaiah pronounces judgment against those who write oppressive laws and deprive the poor and vulnerable of justice. Their wealth and power will not save them in the day of God's visitation and judgment.

23:10.title: Assyria as God's Instrument of Judgment
23:10.summary: God identifies Assyria as the rod of his anger, sent to punish the nations, yet Assyria acts with its own prideful motives and boasts of conquering cities as an instrument of human ambition rather than divine purpose. Assyria plans to conquer Jerusalem as it has conquered other nations and their idols.

23:10.title: God Will Judge Assyria's Pride
23:10.summary: After the Lord completes his work of judgment on Mount Zion and Jerusalem, he will punish the king of Assyria for his arrogant boasting that his own strength and wisdom achieved his conquests, treating nations as a bird gathering abandoned eggs. God promises to humble the nation's willful pride and haughty ambition.

23:10.title: The Folly of the Tool Exalting Itself
23:10.summary: Isaiah uses vivid imagery to show the absurdity of Assyria exalting itself as if a tool could glorify itself above the one who uses it; God will consume Assyria's glory and forest through fire, reducing the nation to so few survivors that their number could be counted like remaining forest trees. The tool cannot boast against the hand that wields it.

23:10.title: A Remnant Shall Return
23:10.summary: In that day, the surviving remnant of Israel will cease relying on Assyria and will trust in the Lord their God; though Israel's population is vast, only a remnant will return, as God has determined complete destruction measured by righteous judgment. The Lord will accomplish a full and final end in all the earth.

23:10.title: Deliverance from the Assyrian Yoke
23:10.summary: The Lord reassures Zion not to fear Assyria's rod and staff, for his indignation against them will soon be accomplished and his anger will turn to destroy Assyria instead, as in ancient miracles at Midian and Egypt. The yoke of Assyrian oppression will be broken and lifted from Israel's shoulders.

23:10.title: Assyria's Advance on Jerusalem
23:10.summary: The passage depicts the Assyrian army's rapid military advance toward Jerusalem, passing through multiple towns in sequence, striking fear and flight among the inhabitants, until the army arrives at Nob and threatens Mount Zion and Jerusalem itself. The vividly named places show the enemy's unstoppable progress.

23:10.title: God's Judgment Cuts Down the Mighty
23:10.summary: The Lord, Yahweh of Armies, will cut down Assyria's lofty might—the tall trees and thick forests—removing the shield of those who trust in human strength; even mighty Lebanon falls before God's judgment. The proud and arrogant will be brought low by the Mighty One.

23:11.title: The Messiah's Character and Spirit
23:11.summary: A righteous ruler will emerge from Jesse's line, endowed with the sevenfold Spirit of the Lord, and will judge with perfect righteousness and equity rather than appearance, striking down the wicked while protecting the vulnerable.

23:11.title: The Peaceable Kingdom
23:11.summary: In the Messiah's reign, all creation will be transformed into a state of perfect peace where natural predators and prey live together in harmony, and violence ceases entirely because the knowledge of God fills the earth.

23:11.title: The Banner for the Nations and Gathering of the Remnant
23:11.summary: The Messiah will become a beacon to which the nations turn, and the Lord will gather the scattered remnant of Israel and Judah from all lands and corners of the earth.

23:11.title: Reunification and Expansion of Israel
23:11.summary: Internal divisions between Ephraim and Judah will cease, Israel will defeat its surrounding enemies and expand its territory, the Lord will open a pathway through impossible obstacles as He did in the exodus, and a highway will be prepared for the returned remnant.

23:12.title: Thanksgiving for God's Comfort and Salvation
23:12.summary: The speaker declares gratitude to God for turning away his anger and providing comfort, affirming trust in God as strength, song, and salvation. This expresses the spiritual transformation from judgment to restoration that God promises to his people.

23:12.title: Drawing Water from the Wells of Salvation
23:12.summary: The redeemed community will joyfully draw water from the wells of salvation, a metaphor for receiving the abundant blessings and life-giving grace that flow from God's redemptive work.

23:12.title: A Call to Universal Praise and Witness
