23:12.summary: God's people are commanded to give thanks, call upon his name, and declare his deeds to all nations, celebrating his excellence and greatness, especially among Zion. This passage emphasizes the missionary dimension of redemption—that God's salvation should be proclaimed and celebrated throughout the earth.

23:13.title: The Burden of Babylon and the Summons to War
23:13.summary: Isaiah receives a vision concerning Babylon's judgment. God musters nations and armies from distant lands as instruments of his wrath to destroy the entire land, summoning warriors to advance against the cities.

23:13.title: The Day of Yahweh: Cosmic and Terrestrial Devastation
23:13.summary: The day of Yahweh arrives with cosmic upheaval and universal terror—darkness covers the heavens, the earth trembles, and all creation witnesses God's judgment against human pride and wickedness. The entire created order responds to divine wrath against the arrogant and wicked.

23:13.title: The Slaughter and Suffering of Babylon's People
23:13.summary: In the chaos of invasion, Babylonians flee like hunted animals to their homelands, only to face death by the sword. The Medes, indifferent to plunder, show no mercy—slaughtering the young, children, and unborn, ransacking homes and assaulting women.

23:13.title: Babylon's Complete Desolation and Ruin
23:13.summary: Babylon, once the jewel of kingdoms and pride of the Chaldeans, will be utterly destroyed like Sodom and Gomorrah—uninhabitable forever, inhabited only by wild beasts and desolate creatures. Her former glory and splendor will be replaced by only ruins and the cries of jackals in what were once magnificent palaces.

23:14.title: Restoration and Reversal of Israel's Fortune
23:14.summary: Yahweh will show compassion on Jacob and restore Israel to their land, where foreigners will join them and they will possess their former oppressors as servants. This promises a complete reversal of captivity and domination.

23:14.title: The Parable Against the King of Babylon
23:14.summary: When Yahweh gives Israel rest from their suffering, they will compose a mocking song against the fallen king of Babylon, celebrating how the oppressor's cruelty has ended and the whole earth now rejoices in peace. Even nature—the trees and cedars—celebrate his downfall.

23:14.title: The King of Babylon Brought Down to Sheol
23:14.summary: The underworld itself stirs to meet the fallen king, and the dead rulers challenge him, mocking his weakness; his splendor and pride are destroyed, and despite his arrogant boasts of ascending to heaven and exalting himself above God, he is brought down to the depths of the pit. This depicts the ironic reversal of his ambitions through divine judgment.

23:14.title: The Fate of the Oppressor: Dishonor and Oblivion
23:14.summary: Those who see the king's fallen form will stare in amazement, barely recognizing the man who once made the earth tremble; he will be denied proper burial and his offspring will be eliminated, erasing his name and legacy forever. This represents the ultimate dishonor and the complete erasure of his dynasty.

23:14.title: Yahweh's Judgment Against Babylon
23:14.summary: Yahweh declares He will cut off the king's children and eliminate Babylon entirely—removing its name, remnant, and descendants—and will reduce it to desolate ruin. This is Yahweh's definitive pronouncement of the city's total destruction.

23:14.title: Yahweh's Eternal Purpose for the Nations
23:14.summary: Yahweh swears that as He has planned, so it will happen; He will break the Assyrian in His land and free His people from oppression, fulfilling the plan He has determined for all the earth. This affirms the certainty and irresistibility of God's purposes against all hostile powers.

23:14.title: Oracle Against Philistia and Affirmation of Zion
23:14.summary: This burden was given in the year King Ahaz died; it warns Philistia not to rejoice at the broken rod of Assyria, for a worse threat will emerge; smoke from the north signals invasion and destruction, yet Yahweh has founded Zion as a refuge for the afflicted. The passage pivots from judgment on Israel's enemies to hope for God's people in Zion.

23:15.title: The Sudden Destruction of Moab
23:15.summary: Moab's major cities—Ar and Kir—are destroyed overnight, prompting the people to flee to high places in mourning, shaving their heads and dressing in sackcloth as signs of grief and despair.

23:15.title: The Spread of Devastation and Despair
23:15.summary: Moab's cities cry out in anguish as the destruction spreads; the armed men tremble in fear, while the land itself becomes barren and desolate, with water sources dried up and vegetation withered.

23:15.title: The Flight and Final Judgment
23:15.summary: The remnant of Moab's survivors flee, carrying what wealth they can across the wilderness, while their cries of anguish spread throughout the land, and God brings additional judgment—lions—upon those who escape.

23:16.title: Appeal for Refuge and Justice for Moab's Fugitives
23:16.summary: The prophet calls for lambs to be sent as tribute and urges that Moab's scattered people be given shelter and protection from destruction. Justice and righteousness will establish a stable throne where the oppressed find safety.

23:16.title: The Establishment of a Righteous Throne
23:16.summary: A messianic vision describes a throne established in loving kindness where a ruler will sit in truth, execute justice, and pursue righteousness. This represents God's ideal government rooted in the Davidic covenant.

23:16.title: Lament Over Moab's Pride and Fall
23:16.summary: The prophet laments Moab's coming destruction, contrasting its present pride and arrogance with the devastation of its vineyards and fertile lands. Joy and celebration will cease as judgment brings ruin to the nation.

23:16.title: Futility of Moab's Prayer and Prophecy of Decline
23:16.summary: Moab's attempts to pray and seek deliverance at the sanctuary will be unsuccessful, and within three years the nation's glory and vast multitude will be reduced to a small, feeble remnant. This confirms Yahweh's word against Moab.

23:17.title: The Burden Against Damascus and Syria
23:17.summary: Damascus and the cities of Syria will be destroyed and become desolate, their fortifications will fall, and they will be reduced to the same diminished state as Israel. This prophecy announces God's judgment on these rival nations.

23:17.title: Jacob's Glory Will Diminish
23:17.summary: Israel's glory and strength will waste away like a harvested field, leaving only scattered gleanings—a small remnant will survive God's judgment. The image emphasizes both the severity of the coming judgment and the mercy of a surviving remnant.

23:17.title: Israel Will Return to Their Maker
23:17.summary: In that day of judgment, Israel will turn their eyes toward God rather than their idols and man-made altars, and their fortified cities will become desolate like abandoned ruins. This reflects both judgment and the potential for spiritual restoration.

23:17.title: Forgetfulness Leads to Spiritual Barrenness
23:17.summary: Israel has forgotten God and their strength, instead planting foreign gods and idolatrous practices; their efforts will bear no fruit, and their harvest will fail in times of grief and sorrow. Spiritual unfaithfulness results in material and spiritual desolation.

23:17.title: The Nations Will Be Scattered by God
23:17.summary: Many nations will rush against Israel like roaring seas, but God will rebuke them and scatter them like chaff before the wind; by evening they will be terror-stricken, and by morning they will be gone. God's intervention ensures the destruction of those who plunder His people.

23:18.title: The Land Beyond the Rivers and Its Ambassadors
23:18.summary: Isaiah addresses a distant, powerful nation beyond Ethiopia that sends messengers by sea, describing a tall, awesome people whose land is divided by rivers. This sets the stage for God's judgment upon this proud nation.

23:18.title: A Call to Watch and God's Patient Observation
23:18.summary: All people are summoned to witness God's coming judgment signaled by banners and trumpets, while God declares He will watch from His dwelling place with calm certainty before acting. This emphasizes God's sovereign timing and the world-wide significance of His intervention.

23:18.title: The Pruning and Devastation of the Nation
23:18.summary: Before harvest is complete, God will prune away the nation's branches with pruning hooks, leaving the remnants to be consumed by wild birds and beasts in summer and winter. This vivid image depicts total destruction and the humiliation of the once-proud people.

23:18.title: The Remnant's Gift and Conversion
23:18.summary: After judgment, the surviving tall and awesome people will bring tribute to Yahweh at Mount Zion, acknowledging His power and converting to worship the God of Armies. This shows the redemptive outcome of judgment: the conversion and submission of the nations.

23:19.title: The Lord's Judgment on Egypt
23:19.summary: The Lord rides on a swift cloud to judge Egypt, causing the idols to tremble and the hearts of Egyptians to melt. He will stir up internal conflict among the Egyptians, destroy their counsel, and deliver them into the hand of a cruel and fierce king.

23:19.title: The Desolation of Egypt's Waters and Agriculture
23:19.summary: The waters of the Nile will fail, rivers will become foul, and all agricultural life dependent on the Nile will wither and die. Fishermen, workers in flax and linen, and all laborers will mourn and languish as their livelihoods are destroyed.

23:19.title: The Folly of Egypt's Wise Men
23:19.summary: The princes and wisest counselors of Egypt are revealed as fools and deceived, unable to understand God's purposes against their nation. God has mixed a spirit of perverseness into Egypt, causing them to stagger like a drunken man in all their works.

23:19.title: Egypt's Terror and Fear
23:19.summary: The Egyptians will become like women, trembling and afraid at the shaking of God's hand, while Judah becomes a terror to Egypt because of God's determined plans against her.

23:19.title: Egypt's Conversion and Restoration
23:19.summary: In that day, five Egyptian cities will speak Canaanite language and worship the Lord; altars and pillars will be erected in Egypt as signs of God's presence. The Egyptians will cry out to the Lord for deliverance, whom He will provide, and through striking and healing, Egypt will return to and worship the Lord.

23:19.title: The Highway of Nations and Universal Blessing
23:19.summary: A highway will connect Egypt and Assyria, allowing the formerly hostile nations to worship together. Israel will join Egypt and Assyria as a threefold blessing in the earth, as God calls Egypt 'my people,' Assyria 'the work of my hands,' and Israel 'my inheritance.'

23:20.title: Isaiah's Symbolic Nakedness as a Sign Against Egypt and Ethiopia
23:20.summary: When Sargon of Assyria conquers Ashdod, God commands Isaiah to walk naked and barefoot for three years as a living sign and wonder. This symbolic action prophesies that Assyria will lead away the captives of Egypt and Ethiopia in the same humiliating condition, stripping them of their dignity and power.

23:20.title: The Despair of Nations Trusting in Egypt
23:20.summary: Egypt and Ethiopia, whom surrounding nations had trusted for deliverance, will themselves be captured and shamed by Assyria, leaving the coastal peoples who fled to them for help in despair and with no escape from the Assyrian king. The prophecy demonstrates the futility of trusting in human powers rather than God.

23:21.title: The Burden of the Wilderness of the Sea—Vision of Babylon's Fall
23:21.summary: Isaiah receives a grievous vision of destruction coming like a whirlwind from the wilderness, with Elam and Media attacking. The prophet is overwhelmed with anguish and fear at the vision, while enemies prepare for battle, turning his longed-for peace into terror.

23:21.title: The Watchman's Report—The Fall of Babylon
23:21.summary: The Lord commands Isaiah to post a watchman to report what he sees; the watchman describes approaching troops and proclaims the fall of Babylon and destruction of her idols. Isaiah declares this message from the Lord of Armies to Israel as a harvested grain offering.

23:21.title: The Burden of Dumah—Darkness and Dawn
23:21.summary: Someone from Seir calls to the watchman asking about the night, and the watchman responds that both morning and night are coming—urging them to inquire further and return. The cryptic answer suggests both judgment and hope are approaching.

23:21.title: The Burden on Arabia—Judgment on Kedar
23:21.summary: Caravans of Dedanites will lodge in the Arabian forest, where inhabitants provide water and bread to fugitives fleeing the sword and battle. Within a year, the Lord will diminish the glory and mighty warriors of Kedar, leaving only a remnant.

23:22.title: The Valley of Vision: Jerusalem's Destruction Foretold
23:22.summary: Isaiah grieves over Jerusalem's impending destruction, where the people have fled to rooftops in confusion and despair rather than facing battle with weapons. The prophet laments the day of treading down, perplexity, and broken walls that the Lord will bring upon the city.

23:22.title: Human Preparation Without Faith
23:22.summary: As enemy forces gather with chariots and horsemen at Jerusalem's gates, the people frantically fortify walls, gather water, and demolish houses for defense, yet they fail to look to God who orchestrated these events long ago. Their practical preparations reveal their lack of trust in the Lord's ultimate plan.

23:22.title: Mourning Rejected, Revelry Condemned
23:22.summary: The Lord calls the people to weep, mourn, and repent, but instead they feast, drink, and say "let us eat and drink, for tomorrow we will die." Their defiant hedonism in the face of judgment becomes an unforgivable sin that will not be pardoned.

23:22.title: The Fall of Shebna
23:22.summary: Isaiah is commanded to denounce Shebna, the treasurer and palace official, who has presumed to carve out a grand tomb for himself as if he were royalty. The Lord will violently remove him from his position of power and cast him away in shame.

23:22.title: The Installation of Eliakim
23:22.summary: The Lord will replace the fallen Shebna with his servant Eliakim, clothing him with authority, giving him the key of David's house, and establishing him as a father figure to Jerusalem and Judah. All the glory of his father's house will depend upon him like vessels hung on a nail.

23:22.title: The Removal of the Nail
23:22.summary: Yet even Eliakim, though fastened firmly as a nail in a sure place, will eventually fail and be cut down; the burden he bears will fall and be removed, revealing that only God's purposes endure eternally. No human leader, however righteous, escapes the cycle of weakness and judgment.

23:23.title: The Burden Against Tyre: Her Desolation and Former Glory
23:23.summary: Isaiah pronounces judgment against Tyre, the great maritime trading city whose wealth came from commerce across the Mediterranean and the Nile. The once-prosperous city that supplied nations is now laid waste.

23:23.title: The Reason for Tyre's Judgment: God's Plan Against Human Pride
23:23.summary: Isaiah explains that God Himself has ordained Tyre's destruction to humble the city's pride and bring her merchants and princes—who were honored among the nations—into disgrace. Sidon and Tyre's arrogance will be exposed.

23:23.title: The Fulfillment of Judgment: Flight and Homelessness
23:23.summary: The oppressed inhabitants are commanded to flee across the sea to distant lands, yet find no refuge or rest anywhere, as God's judgment extends across all kingdoms through His power over the seas and strongholds.

23:23.title: Historical Example of Ruin: The Chaldean Stronghold
23:23.summary: Isaiah references the Chaldean stronghold (associated with Assyrian conquest) as an example of how towers and palaces are destroyed, calling the maritime merchants to wail as their own stronghold faces the same fate.

23:23.title: Tyre's Seventy-Year Desolation and Restoration to Shame
23:23.summary: After seventy years of oblivion, Tyre will be remembered—but as a forgotten prostitute singing for wages—and will return to her commercial pursuits, trafficking with all nations in a state of spiritual degradation rather than restored honor.

23:23.title: The Redemptive Purpose: Tyre's Wealth for God's People
23:23.summary: Ultimately, Tyre's merchandise and wealth will be consecrated to the Lord and used to provide abundant food and clothing for those who worship before Him, transforming her commerce from pride to provision for the holy.

23:24.title: The Lord's Judgment on the Earth
23:24.summary: The Lord will empty and lay waste the earth, scattering its inhabitants with impartial judgment. This comprehensive destruction is certain because the Lord has spoken it.

23:24.title: The Earth's Mourning and Decay
23:24.summary: The earth and its people mourn and fade away due to transgression of God's laws and covenant; all signs of celebration and joy cease as cities are destroyed and desolated. The land's sorrow reflects humanity's guilt and the curse of judgment.

23:24.title: Remnant Praise and Prophetic Lament
23:24.summary: Like scattered olives after harvest, a remnant will lift their voices to glorify the Lord's majesty from distant lands; yet the prophet expresses anguish at the treacherous dealing of the wicked. Praise rises amid judgment, but betrayal continues.

23:24.title: Inescapable Terror and Final Collapse
23:24.summary: Those inhabiting the earth face inescapable judgment—fear, pit, and snare surround them with no escape. The earth itself will collapse violently and permanently, reeling like a drunkard and unable to rise again due to sin's weight.

23:24.title: The Lord's Final Vindication and Reign
23:24.summary: The Lord will punish both celestial and earthly powers, gathering them as prisoners; ultimately the Lord will reign on Mount Zion while the sun and moon fade, and glory appears before his elders. God's sovereignty is established and his enemies are subdued.

23:25.title: Praise for God's Wonderful and Faithful Works
23:25.summary: The prophet exalts God for accomplishing his planned purposes with complete faithfulness, having destroyed enemy cities so thoroughly they will never be rebuilt. This causes strong peoples and awesome nations to glorify and fear God.

23:25.title: God as Refuge for the Oppressed
23:25.summary: God has been a stronghold and refuge for the poor and needy in their distress, protecting them from storms and heat like shade from the sun, and will silence the noise and songs of oppressors. The passage emphasizes God's protective care for the vulnerable.

23:25.title: The Messianic Feast and Removal of the Veil
23:25.summary: On God's mountain, all peoples will celebrate a feast of rich foods and fine wines, and God will remove the veil covering all nations and destroy death forever, wiping away tears and reproach. The redeemed will recognize God as their savior and rejoice in his salvation.

23:25.title: The Humbling of Moab and Enemy Nations
23:25.summary: God's hand will rest on his mountain while Moab will be trampled down like straw in mud, and their pride and handiwork will be humbled as their fortress walls are demolished and brought to dust. This demonstrates God's judgment on arrogant nations.

23:26.title: Song of Trust in God's Protection
23:26.summary: In the future day of restoration, Judah will sing of God as their strong fortress and source of salvation, calling the righteous to enter through open gates and trust in the Lord who keeps their minds in perfect peace.

23:26.title: The Humbling of the Lofty City
23:26.summary: God brings down proud cities and those dwelling on high, laying them low to dust where even the poor and needy can tread upon them in vindication.

23:26.title: Longing for God and His Righteous Ways
23:26.summary: The righteous desire God's judgments and renown with soul and spirit, recognizing that only through His name and ordaining of peace can true righteousness be established and all works be accomplished.

23:26.title: Deliverance from Other Lords and the Dead
23:26.summary: Though other lords once ruled over Israel, the people will acknowledge only God's name; God has visited judgment on the wicked dead so thoroughly that no memory of them remains.

23:26.title: National Growth Yet Incomplete Deliverance
23:26.summary: God has increased the nation and expanded its borders, yet despite their distress and prayer during chastening, their efforts for deliverance have been in vain—like bearing only wind, with no salvation achieved in the earth.

23:26.title: Resurrection and Divine Judgment
23:26.summary: God promises that the dead shall live and rise, calling the inhabitants of dust to awake and sing; the people are urged to hide during God's coming judgment on the earth's inhabitants for their iniquity and to witness the disclosure of bloodshed.

23:27.title: The Punishment of Leviathan
23:27.summary: Yahweh will use his sword to punish and slay leviathan and the sea dragon, symbolizing the defeat of chaos and evil forces. This represents God's ultimate triumph over all that opposes His kingdom.

23:27.title: The Song of the Vineyard and God's Care
23:27.summary: Israel is depicted as a pleasant vineyard that Yahweh keeps and waters constantly, protecting it from harm; those who resist God's strength are invited to make peace with Him instead of facing His wrath. This passage emphasizes God's tender care for His people and His offer of reconciliation.

23:27.title: Jacob's Restoration and Forgiveness
23:27.summary: Jacob and Israel will flourish and fill the earth with fruit, and through God's measured discipline their iniquity will be forgiven as they abandon idolatry and false worship. This passage promises national restoration following repentance and the removal of sin.

23:27.title: The Desolation of the Fortified City
23:27.summary: A fortified city lies forsaken and desolate, its wood consumed by cattle and burned by women, showing God's judgment on a people lacking understanding. This depicts the complete abandonment and destruction that comes from rejecting God's compassion.

23:27.title: The Gathering of Israel
23:27.summary: Yahweh will thresh the land from the Euphrates to Egypt and gather the children of Israel one by one; a great trumpet will sound to call those scattered in Assyria and Egypt to return and worship God on His holy mountain in Jerusalem. This passage promises the final ingathering and restoration of God's people to their homeland and to worship.

23:28.title: Woe Against the Drunkards of Ephraim
23:28.summary: Isaiah pronounces judgment against the proud, drunken leaders of Ephraim, whose glory will be destroyed like a fading flower. The Lord will cast down their fading splendor and trample their crown of pride.

23:28.title: The Lord as Crown and Strength
23:28.summary: In that day, the Lord will become a crown of glory and a diadem of beauty for the remnant of his people, providing justice for judges and strength for those who resist invasion. This promise contrasts with the fallen crown of Ephraim and offers restoration and divine protection.

23:28.title: Blindness and Stumbling of Priests and Prophets
23:28.summary: Priests and prophets are intoxicated and unable to judge or teach, yet God offers rest and refreshment which they refuse to hear. Consequently, God will speak to them in a foreign language through stammering lips, and they will fall backward, broken and captured.

23:28.title: The Covenant with Death Annulled
23:28.summary: Isaiah addresses the scoffing rulers of Jerusalem who boast of their covenant with death and refuge in lies, but the Lord declares He will lay a precious cornerstone in Zion and sweep away their false refuge with judgment. Their agreement with Sheol will be annulled and terror will overtake them continually.

23:28.title: The Bed Too Short and God's Extraordinary Work
23:28.summary: The people cannot escape judgment—their bed is too short and blanket too narrow for comfort. The Lord will rise up in anger to accomplish His unusual and extraordinary work, and warns the scoffers not to strengthen their bonds through continued mockery.

23:28.title: The Parable of the Farmer's Wisdom
23:28.summary: Isaiah calls the people to listen to God's voice, then illustrates through the image of a wise farmer that God has ordered all things according to right judgment and marvelous wisdom. Just as the farmer uses different methods for different crops, God applies appropriate measures to His people according to His excellent counsel.

23:29.title: The Siege and Deliverance of Ariel (Jerusalem)
23:29.summary: Jerusalem (Ariel) will be besieged and brought low, speaking weakly from the dust, but God will suddenly deliver her by destroying her enemies, whose multitude will vanish like a dream when the hungry or thirsty awaken.

23:29.title: Spiritual Blindness and God's Marvelous Work
23:29.summary: God has poured out a spirit of deep sleep on the people, sealing all vision from them because their worship is merely external while their hearts are far from him; yet God will perform a marvelous work that confounds the wisdom of their wise men.

23:29.title: Woe to Those Who Hide Counsel from God
23:29.summary: Those who hide their plans in darkness and question God's knowledge demonstrate absurd pride, comparable to created things denying their maker's existence and understanding.

23:29.title: Future Transformation and Reversal of Fortune
23:29.summary: In a coming day, the deaf will hear, the blind will see, the humble will rejoice, and the ruthless and wicked will be destroyed, while those who perverted justice through false testimony and slander will be cut off.

23:29.title: Jacob's Future Redemption and Sanctification
23:29.summary: God, who redeemed Abraham, promises that Jacob will no longer be ashamed; his children—the work of God's hands—will sanctify God's name and stand in awe of him, while those who err in spirit will gain understanding.

23:30.title: Woe to Those Seeking Egypt Instead of God
23:30.summary: Isaiah condemns Judah's leaders for making alliances with Egypt without consulting God, seeking human strength instead of divine guidance. Their reliance on Pharaoh will bring them shame and confusion rather than protection.

23:30.title: The Futility of the Egyptian Alliance and Rejection of God's Prophets
23:30.summary: Isaiah describes the difficult journey to Egypt laden with treasures sent to a nation that cannot help, and condemns the people for rejecting God's prophets and demanding false, pleasing prophecies instead of truth. The people actively resist God's guidance and desire only comfortable lies.

23:30.title: The Breaking of Judah's Confidence
23:30.summary: God warns that their rejection of His word and trust in oppression will result in sudden, total collapse like a shattered vessel, and their military strategy of fleeing on horses will fail as they are pursued relentlessly. Their strength will crumble, leaving them isolated and exposed.

23:30.title: God's Mercy for Those Who Wait and Return
23:30.summary: Despite judgment, God promises grace and restoration for those who wait upon Him and return, including the gift of wise teachers, guidance in the path, agricultural abundance, and ultimate healing and transformation in a day of great restoration. The entire creation will be renewed with extraordinary light and provision.

23:30.title: God's Judgment on Assyria and Deliverance of Israel
23:30.summary: God's judgment comes against the Assyrian oppressor with overwhelming force and anger, while Israel responds with songs of joy as God defeats their enemy with divine power. The final destruction of Assyria is portrayed as burning like a prepared pyre, prepared beforehand by God.

23:31.title: Woe to Those Who Trust in Egypt Instead of God
23:31.summary: Isaiah condemns those who seek help from Egypt and trust in military power rather than looking to the Holy One of Israel. God will bring disaster upon evildoers, and human military strength—whether Egyptian horses or chariots—cannot withstand God's judgment.

23:31.title: God's Protection of Jerusalem
23:31.summary: God promises to defend Mount Zion and Jerusalem with unwavering courage, comparing His protection to a lion fearless before shepherds and to birds hovering protectively over their nest. God will guard, deliver, and preserve His people.

23:31.title: Call to Repentance and Idolatry Rejected
23:31.summary: Israel is called to return to God from whom they have deeply revolted, and in the day of salvation they will cast away their idols of silver and gold—the false gods they have made with their own hands. Repentance involves rejecting idolatry and returning wholly to the Lord.

23:31.title: The Fall of Assyria
23:31.summary: The Assyrian king will be destroyed not by human military might but by God's sword, causing him to flee; his young men will be conscripted and his princes terrified. God's fire in Zion and furnace in Jerusalem will accomplish what human strength cannot.

23:32.title: The Righteous King and Moral Transformation
23:32.summary: A future king will reign in righteousness and bring justice, while people will gain spiritual clarity and understanding. The fool and scoundrel will lose their former status as true values are restored.

23:32.title: The Contrast Between Fool and Noble
23:32.summary: The fool speaks folly and oppresses the hungry and thirsty with wickedness and lies, while the noble devises and pursues noble things. This passage illustrates the fundamental moral divide between the wicked and the righteous.

23:32.title: Warning of Judgment on Complacent Women
23:32.summary: Isaiah calls careless women to repent, warning them of coming desolation within a year as harvests fail and cities are destroyed. The land will be stripped bare with thorns and briars, and once-joyful places will become forsaken ruins.

23:32.title: Restoration Through God's Spirit
23:32.summary: When God's Spirit is poured out from on high, the wilderness will become fruitful, justice and righteousness will flourish, and God's people will dwell in peace, safety, and quiet rest. This promises ultimate restoration after judgment.

23:32.title: Blessing on the Faithful Agriculturalists
23:32.summary: Despite hail and destruction that may flatten forests and level cities, those who sow faithfully by all waters and work their fields are blessed. The passage affirms God's blessing on faithful labor even amid judgment.

23:33.title: Woe to the Destroyer and Betrayer
23:33.summary: Isaiah pronounces judgment against those who destroy and betray others, declaring that they will themselves be destroyed and betrayed when their deeds are complete. This announces God's principle of divine retribution and justice.

23:33.title: Prayer for God's Gracious Presence and Promise of Restoration
23:33.summary: The community appeals to Yahweh for grace and strength in times of trouble, and God responds with assurance that He dwells on high and will fill Zion with justice, providing stability, salvation, wisdom, and the fear of the Lord as treasure. This expresses both human dependence on God and divine covenant faithfulness.

23:33.title: Lament Over Land Devastation and Broken Covenant
23:33.summary: Isaiah describes the desolation caused by covenant-breaking enemies: ambassadors weep, highways are empty, and the land mourns with forests stripped bare. This depicts the catastrophic consequences of rejected covenant and military invasion.

23:33.title: God's Self-Exaltation and Judgment on the Nations
23:33.summary: Yahweh declares His intention to arise and be exalted, pronouncing that the enemy's plans will come to nothing (like chaff and stubble) and that peoples will be burned away like lime and thorns. God's power will consume those who oppose Him.

23:33.title: The Righteous Remnant Spared and Secure
23:33.summary: God calls all people to witness His mighty deeds, then describes the righteous person who walks blamelessly and refuses corruption—such a one will dwell securely on high with assured provision and safety. This contrasts the fate of the wicked with the blessing of the morally upright.

23:33.title: Vision of the King and Deliverance from Foreign Oppression
23:33.summary: The redeemed will behold the king in his beauty and see distant lands restored; the terror of the past and the incomprehensible foreign oppressor will be gone. This promises an eschatological vision of peace and the removal of hostile occupation.

23:33.title: Zion Restored: God's Eternal Presence and Healing
23:33.summary: Isaiah describes Zion as a peaceful, permanent dwelling where God is present as judge, lawgiver, and king, protected by rivers that prevent invasion, and where the people dwell free from sickness and forgiven of sin. This portrays the ultimate restoration of God's people in security and divine favor.

23:34.title: Call to Hear Yahweh's Judgment
23:34.summary: Isaiah summons all nations and peoples to listen as God announces His universal rage against the nations and their complete destruction. The prophet establishes the cosmic significance of the judgment about to be declared.

23:34.title: The Sword of Yahweh Against Edom
23:34.summary: Isaiah depicts the graphic devastation wrought by God's sword upon Edom, describing mountains melting in blood, the sky dissolving, and a great sacrifice-slaughter in Bozrah as an act of divine vengeance on behalf of Zion. This vivid imagery emphasizes the totality and horror of God's judgment against this particular nation.

23:34.title: Edom's Eternal Desolation
23:34.summary: The land of Edom becomes a perpetually burning wasteland, transformed into burning pitch that smolders forever, inhabited only by unclean animals and wild creatures while its palaces and fortresses lie overgrown with thorns. The once-proud civilization is utterly erased, with no nobles or princes remaining.

23:34.title: Desert Animals Possess the Ruined Land
23:34.summary: Wild creatures and unclean animals make the desolate land their home according to God's ordained plan, with each creature finding its mate and dwelling place as decreed by Yahweh's command and spirit. The passage affirms that this devastation and the animals' occupation are all written in God's book and appointed by His measuring line.

23:35.title: The Desert Blooms in Glory
23:35.summary: The wilderness and desert will transform into a place of abundant beauty and rejoicing, displaying God's glory as splendidly as Lebanon, Carmel, and Sharon. This depicts the future restoration and renewal that God's people will witness.

23:35.title: Encouragement to the Fearful and Weak
23:35.summary: The prophet exhorts the weak and fearful to be strengthened and courageous, assuring them that God will come with vengeance against their enemies and will save them. This call to courage is grounded in confidence in God's protective power.

23:35.title: Miracles of Healing and Restoration
23:35.summary: The blind will see, the deaf will hear, the lame will leap, and the mute will sing as God's restorative power manifests. The barren desert will become a fertile place with abundant water, reversing the desolation and enabling life to flourish.

23:35.title: The Holy Way of the Redeemed
23:35.summary: A sacred highway will be established for God's redeemed people, from which the unclean and wicked are excluded, and on which danger and predators will not exist. This road represents the secure path God provides exclusively for those who belong to Him.

23:35.title: The Return of the Ransomed with Everlasting Joy
23:35.summary: The redeemed will return to Zion singing with everlasting joy and gladness, while sorrow and suffering depart forever. This concluding vision captures the ultimate fulfillment of God's redemptive plan for His people.

23:36.title: Sennacherib's Invasion and Envoy
23:36.summary: In the fourteenth year of King Hezekiah's reign, the Assyrian king Sennacherib attacks and captures the fortified cities of Judah, then sends his commander Rabshakeh with a large army to Jerusalem to demand surrender.

23:36.title: Rabshakeh's First Appeal to Judah's Officials
23:36.summary: Judah's officials meet Rabshakeh, who mocks their military confidence, ridicules their reliance on Egypt, demands submission to Assyria, and claims that even the Lord commanded him to destroy the land.

23:36.title: Request to Speak in Aramaic Rejected
23:36.summary: Judah's officials request that Rabshakeh speak in Aramaic rather than Hebrew to avoid demoralizing the people on the walls, but Rabshakeh refuses and vows to speak directly to the common people.

23:36.title: Rabshakeh's Propaganda Speech to the People
23:36.summary: Rabshakeh addresses the people on the wall in Hebrew, warning them not to trust Hezekiah or the Lord for deliverance, offering them peace and prosperity if they surrender, and challenging whether any god has ever rescued a nation from Assyria's hand.

23:36.title: Judah's Officials Report to Hezekiah
23:36.summary: The officials remain silent in obedience to Hezekiah's command, then return to the king in distress, tearing their clothes and reporting Rabshakeh's threatening words.

23:37.title: Hezekiah's Distress and Isaiah's First Message of Reassurance
23:37.summary: Upon hearing Rabshakeh's blasphemous message, King Hezekiah enters the temple in mourning and sends officials to the prophet Isaiah for intercession. Isaiah responds with God's promise that He will turn the Assyrian king back to his own land where he will fall by the sword.

23:37.title: Sennacherib's Second Threatening Message
23:37.summary: Rabshakeh reports to Sennacherib that he is fighting at Libnah, then the king sends another threatening letter to Hezekiah, boasting of Assyria's military power and the failure of other nations' gods to deliver them from Assyrian conquest.

23:37.title: Hezekiah's Prayer Before Yahweh
23:37.summary: After reading Sennacherib's letter, Hezekiah spreads it before the Lord in the temple and prays, acknowledging God's supremacy over all kingdoms and asking Him to save Judah so that all earth may know that Yahweh alone is God.

23:37.title: Isaiah's Prophetic Message Against Sennacherib
23:37.summary: Isaiah delivers God's word, mocking Sennacherib's pride and boasts, and revealing that God knows all his ways and raging against Him; God will humble him and turn him back by the way he came through a bridle in his nose.

23:37.title: The Sign of Recovery and God's Promise of Deliverance
23:37.summary: God gives Hezekiah a sign of Judah's restoration through a three-year cycle of recovery, and promises that the Assyrian king will not enter Jerusalem—God will defend the city for His own sake and for the sake of His servant David.

23:37.title: The Miraculous Deliverance and Sennacherib's Death
23:37.summary: The angel of Yahweh strikes down 185,000 Assyrian soldiers in a single night, causing Sennacherib to withdraw to Nineveh where he is later killed by his own sons in the house of his god, fulfilling God's word through Isaiah.

23:38.title: Hezekiah's Illness, Prayer, and Healing
23:38.summary: King Hezekiah receives a death sentence from Isaiah but turns to God in fervent prayer, reminding Him of his faithful walk. God hears his prayer, grants him fifteen additional years, promises deliverance from Assyria, and provides a miraculous sign: the sun's shadow on a sundial retreats ten steps backward.

23:38.title: Hezekiah's Song of Thanksgiving
23:38.summary: Hezekiah reflects on his illness, describing his despair at facing death and separation from God's presence, but then celebrates God's deliverance and restoration of his life. He commits to living carefully and praising God, recognizing that only the living can praise and testify to God's truth.

23:38.title: Practical Treatment and Hezekiah's Question
23:38.summary: Isaiah prescribes a poultice of figs as medical treatment for Hezekiah's boil, and Hezekiah asks for confirmation of the sign that he will be able to worship at the temple again.

23:39.title: Merodach Baladan's Embassy and Hezekiah's Display
23:39.summary: The Babylonian king Merodach Baladan sends letters and a gift to King Hezekiah after hearing of his recovery from illness. Hezekiah, pleased with the embassy, proudly displays all his treasures, precious metals, spices, oil, armor, and the entirety of his wealth and dominion.

23:39.title: Isaiah Interrogates Hezekiah About the Visitors
23:39.summary: The prophet Isaiah questions Hezekiah about the foreign visitors and what they witnessed. Hezekiah admits that the Babylonian messengers have seen everything in his house and treasury.

23:39.title: Isaiah's Prophecy of Babylonian Captivity
23:39.summary: Isaiah delivers God's judgment: all of Hezekiah's treasures and those of his ancestors will be carried away to Babylon, and his own descendants will be taken as eunuchs to serve in the Babylonian palace. This is Yahweh's direct word of consequence for Hezekiah's prideful display.

23:39.title: Hezekiah's Resigned Response
23:39.summary: Hezekiah accepts Isaiah's word as good and finds comfort in the knowledge that there will be peace and truth during his own lifetime, showing resignation to God's judgment while being spared immediate destruction.

23:40.title: God's Comfort to Jerusalem
23:40.summary: God commands his people to be comforted, announcing that Jerusalem's warfare has ended and her sins have been pardoned through double recompense. This opens the section of comfort and restoration after exile.

23:40.title: Prepare the Way of the Lord
23:40.summary: A voice calls in the wilderness to prepare a level highway for God by exalting valleys and lowering mountains, so that God's glory will be revealed to all flesh. This prophetic call describes both literal and spiritual preparation for God's coming.

23:40.title: The Transience of Flesh and Permanence of God's Word
23:40.summary: A voice announces that all flesh is like withering grass and fading flowers, but God's word stands forever, contrasting human mortality and weakness with divine permanence and reliability. This establishes God's word as the basis for the comfort promised to Jerusalem.

23:40.title: Good News: Your God Comes in Strength and Gentleness
23:40.summary: The messenger announces to Zion and Jerusalem that God comes as a mighty ruler with his reward, yet he also tenderly feeds and leads his flock like a compassionate shepherd. This reveals God's paradoxical nature of both power and gentleness.

23:40.title: God's Incomparable Greatness and Wisdom
23:40.summary: A rhetorical declaration establishes that God alone measured the waters and heavens, needs no counsel, and is incomparably greater than all creation—nations are like drops in a bucket and less than nothing before him. This affirms God's supreme wisdom, knowledge, and dominion.

23:40.title: The Folly of Idolatry
23:40.summary: The passage contrasts God's incomparability with human attempts to fashion idols from wood and precious metals, showing the absurdity of trying to represent the divine through material objects. This condemns idolatry as inadequate and foolish.

23:40.title: God's Sovereignty Over Creation and History
23:40.summary: God calls Israel to remember his foundational nature as he who sits above the earth, stretches out the heavens like a tent, brings princes to nothing, and calls the stars by name through his might. This reinforces that God rules all creation and history with absolute authority.

23:40.title: Trust in God's Strength and Renewal
23:40.summary: God addresses Israel's despair and doubt, declaring that he, the everlasting Creator, does not faint or weary, and he gives strength to the weak; those who wait on him will renew their strength and rise on eagles' wings. This concludes with the promise of restoration for faithful trust.

23:41.title: God's Sovereignty Over Nations
23:41.summary: God calls the nations to silent judgment, presenting himself as the one who raises up a righteous leader from the east to conquer nations and kings. He establishes his eternal sovereignty as the first and last, the source of all generations and historical events.

23:41.title: Idolaters' Response and Futile Efforts
23:41.summary: The islands and ends of the earth tremble in fear at God's power, and the people encourage one another to rebuild their idols through human craftsmanship. Their collaborative effort to strengthen idols contrasts with their powerlessness before God.

23:41.title: God's Comfort to Israel
23:41.summary: God addresses Israel as his chosen servant, reminding them of their election from Abraham and their calling from the ends of the earth, and repeatedly assures them not to fear because he will strengthen and help them. All who oppose Israel will be defeated and will vanish like nothing.

23:41.title: Israel Transformed into an Instrument of Judgment
23:41.summary: God reassures the weak and insignificant Jacob-Israel not to fear, promising that their Redeemer will make them into a sharp, powerful threshing instrument to thresh mountains and scatter their enemies like chaff. Israel will find joy and glory in the Lord through this transformation.

23:41.title: God's Care for the Afflicted and Nature's Transformation
23:41.summary: God promises to care for the poor and thirsty in the wilderness, transforming barren lands into watered, fertile places filled with trees, so that all may recognize God's creative power and faithfulness. The restored creation testifies to God's work and Israel's God.

23:41.title: God's Challenge to Idols
23:41.summary: God challenges the idols to present their case, demanding they declare future events or prove their power to do good or evil. He pronounces judgment that idols are nothing, their works are nothing, and those who choose them are abominable.

23:41.title: God's Foreknowledge and Idols' Worthlessness
23:41.summary: God announces he has raised up a ruler from the north who calls on his name and will conquer nations, demonstrating that only God knows the future and declares it beforehand. In contrast, the idols and their advisors are silent and powerless, their works are vanity, and their images bring only confusion.

23:42.title: The Servant's Mission of Justice
23:42.summary: God presents His chosen servant, empowered by the Spirit, who will bring justice to the nations with gentleness rather than force, remaining steadfast until justice prevails throughout the earth. This servant will handle the broken and weak with care while faithfully establishing God's righteous purposes.

23:42.title: God's Sovereign Call and Revelation
23:42.summary: God, the Creator of all things, has called the servant in righteousness to be a covenant and light to the nations, opening blind eyes and freeing prisoners. God alone is Lord, will not share His glory with idols, and announces new things before they occur.

23:42.title: A Call to Universal Praise
23:42.summary: All creation—from the sea to the mountains, from the desert to the islands—is summoned to sing a new song and declare God's praise throughout the earth. This jubilant response honors God's salvation and revelation.

23:42.title: God's Mighty Intervention and Deliverance
23:42.summary: God, like a warrior, rises up with zeal to overcome His enemies and accomplish His purposes, breaking forth after long silence with power. He will guide the blind and transform the landscape, removing obstacles and leading people on paths they do not know.

23:42.title: The Blindness of Idolatry and Israel's Spiritual Deafness
23:42.summary: Those who trust in idols will be turned back in disappointment, while Israel, despite being God's messenger and servant, is spiritually blind and deaf, seeing but not perceiving, hearing but not listening. This indictment reveals the tragic spiritual condition of God's own people.

23:42.title: Israel's Judgment for Breaking the Covenant
23:42.summary: God, for His righteousness' sake, magnified the law, yet Israel was robbed and plundered because they sinned against Him and refused to walk in His ways. God poured out His fierce anger and judgment upon them through warfare and burning, but they failed to recognize or repent of these consequences.

23:43.title: God's Promise of Protection and Redemption
23:43.summary: Yahweh assures Jacob/Israel not to fear because they have been redeemed, called by name, and belong to God. Through trials and dangers—waters, rivers, fire—God will be present and protect them, and will gather the scattered people from all directions back to himself.

23:43.title: Israel as God's Witnesses
23:43.summary: God summons Israel as blind and deaf people to witness his supremacy over all nations and gods. Israel is chosen as Yahweh's witnesses to testify that he alone is God, the savior, and that none can resist his power or rescue from his hand.

23:43.title: The Overthrow of Babylon
23:43.summary: Yahweh, Israel's Redeemer and King, declares that for Israel's sake he will send armies to Babylon and bring down the Chaldeans, causing their ships of rejoicing to become vessels of flight.

23:43.title: The New Thing God Will Do
23:43.summary: God, who made a path in the sea for the exodus, announces he will do a new thing—making a way in the wilderness and rivers in the desert to provide for his chosen people, so that all creation will honor him as they praise his name.

23:43.title: Israel's Unfaithfulness and God's Forgiveness
23:43.summary: God confronts Israel for neglecting prayer, withholding worship, and burdening him with sins rather than offerings, yet declares he will blot out their transgressions for his own sake. However, because of their fathers' and teachers' transgressions, God will profane the sanctuary and bring curse and shame upon Israel.

23:44.title: God's Promise to Jacob: The Outpouring of the Spirit
23:44.summary: The Lord addresses Jacob/Israel as His chosen servant and promises to pour out water, His Spirit, and blessings on their descendants, who will flourish and openly claim their identity as God's people. This is a promise of spiritual renewal and multiplication for the covenant people.

23:44.title: The Unique Supremacy of the Lord
23:44.summary: The Lord declares His absolute uniqueness and supremacy—He is the first and the last, the only God and Rock, and the one who establishes history and declared future events to His witnesses. This assurance is meant to replace fear with confidence in His singular power and knowledge.

23:44.title: The Futility and Folly of Idolatry
23:44.summary: Isaiah depicts the absurdity of idolatry: craftsmen labor to make gods from wood and metal, using part of the same tree or ore for fuel and cooking, then worship what they have created. Their hearts are blinded and deceived, unable to recognize the logical contradiction of their own actions.

23:44.title: Redemption and Restoration of Israel
23:44.summary: God calls Israel to remember that they are His servant, formed and redeemed by Him; He has blotted out their sins and invites them to return to Him, prompting all creation to sing in celebration of Jacob's redemption and God's glory. This is a call to repentance grounded in God's prior act of forgiveness.

23:44.title: God's Power Over All Things and Cyrus's Role
23:44.summary: The Lord reaffirms His absolute power—stretching the heavens, spreading the earth, and frustrating false wisdom—and declares His purposes through His servants; He specifically names Cyrus as His shepherd who will enable Jerusalem to be rebuilt and the temple to be restored. This passage links divine sovereignty to concrete historical action on behalf of Israel.

23:45.title: Yahweh's Commission to Cyrus
23:45.summary: God addresses Cyrus, His anointed servant, promising to go before him, overcome all obstacles, and grant him treasures and victory so that all nations may know that Yahweh alone is God. Though Cyrus does not yet know God, He calls him by name for the sake of Israel His chosen people.

23:45.title: The Sovereignty of God Over All Creation
23:45.summary: God declares His absolute sovereignty over all creation, forming light and darkness, making peace and calamity, and calling the heavens to pour down righteousness and salvation. God alone is the source of all cosmic order and moral redemption.

23:45.title: Rebuke of Human Presumption and God's Creative Authority
23:45.summary: God rebukes those who question or contest His authority, comparing humanity to clay pots that have no right to question the potter. God affirms His role as Creator and Maker, stating that He raised up Cyrus in righteousness to rebuild the city and free the exiles.

23:45.title: The Nations' Submission and Israel's Vindication
23:45.summary: Foreign nations and their wealth will be drawn to Israel and submit to God, acknowledging that God is with His people and no other god exists. Those who make idols will be confused, but Israel will experience everlasting salvation and vindication.

23:45.title: God's Righteous Revelation and Invitation to the Nations
23:45.summary: God affirms that He created the habitable earth and openly declares righteousness, inviting all survivors of the nations to assemble and recognize that only Yahweh is God—just, a Savior, and alone without equal. He has announced these things from ancient times.

23:45.title: Universal Call to Look to God and Bow Before Him
23:45.summary: God calls all peoples to look to Him and be saved, having sworn that all creation will bow before Him and confess His righteousness and strength. All Israel will be justified and glorified in Yahweh alone.

23:46.title: The Futility of Idols
23:46.summary: Bel and Nebo, the gods of Babylon, bow down and cannot bear their own weight, while their idols become burdensome loads that cannot save those who carry them. This demonstrates the powerlessness of idols compared to the living God.

23:46.title: God's Faithfulness from Birth to Old Age
23:46.summary: God calls Israel to remember that He has carried them from birth through every stage of life, and promises to continue bearing and delivering them into their old age. This affirms God's constant, sustaining care across the entire lifespan of His people.

23:46.title: The Absurdity of Crafted Idols
23:46.summary: God challenges His people to compare Him with handmade idols that are crafted from gold and silver, carried by humans, set in place, and unable to speak or save anyone. The passage ridicules the irrationality of worshiping created objects instead of the Creator.

23:46.title: God's Sovereignty and Foreknowledge
23:46.summary: God summons transgressors to remember His past works and unique divine nature: He alone knows the future, declares the end from the beginning, and accomplishes His counsel through agents like the ravenous bird from the east (likely Cyrus). This passage establishes God's absolute control over history and His infallible purposes.

23:46.title: God's Righteousness and Salvation Drawing Near
23:46.summary: God calls the stubborn and unrighteous to listen as He brings His righteousness and salvation near to them without delay, placing salvation in Zion as Israel's glory. This is a promise of God's redemptive grace extended even to those far from righteousness.

23:47.title: Babylon's Humiliation and Judgment
23:47.summary: The virgin daughter of Babylon is commanded to sit in dust and endure hard labor, stripped of her dignity and splendor. God declares He will take vengeance and expose her nakedness and shame without mercy.

23:47.title: God's Redemption and Babylon's Arrogance
23:47.summary: The passage affirms that Yahweh of Armies, the Holy One of Israel, is the Redeemer who allowed His people to suffer under Babylon's merciless rule, while Babylon arrogantly assumed eternal dominion without considering the consequences of her actions.

23:47.title: Swift Judgment on Babylon's Self-Reliance
23:47.summary: Babylon, secure in her pleasures and self-centered confidence, will suddenly lose her children and husband in a single day because she trusted in her own wickedness and claimed self-sufficiency. Evil will overtake her unexpectedly, and she cannot prevent it.

23:47.title: The Failure of Babylon's Sorceries and Counselors
23:47.summary: Babylon's enchantments, sorceries, astrologers, and stargazers—in which she has labored since youth—will prove powerless to save her. These counselors will burn like stubble, unable to deliver her, and her trading partners will scatter, leaving her with no savior.

23:48.title: Israel Called but Insincere
23:48.summary: God addresses the house of Jacob/Israel, who claim the name of the Lord and call themselves from the holy city, yet do so without truth and righteousness. They rely on God's name while their hearts remain distant.

23:48.title: God's Prior Declarations and Israel's Stubbornness
23:48.summary: God explains that He announced former things long ago and fulfilled them suddenly to prove His power, knowing Israel's stubborn and stiff-necked resistance and tendency toward treachery. He did this lest they credit their idols or claim prior knowledge.

23:48.title: God's Restraint and Refining
23:48.summary: For His name's sake and His glory, God defers His anger and refines Israel through affliction rather than destroying them, claiming He will not share His glory with idols. His self-interest is bound up in preserving Israel's existence and His own reputation.

23:48.title: God's Sovereignty and the Servant of Babylon
23:48.summary: God calls Jacob and Israel to listen, declaring Himself as the first and last, the one who laid the earth's foundation and spread the heavens. He announces that the one He loves will accomplish His pleasure against Babylon and the Chaldeans.

23:48.title: God's Open Counsel and Conditional Blessing
23:48.summary: God invites Israel to draw near and hear that He has not hidden His purposes but spoken openly since the beginning, and the Lord has sent Him with His Spirit. He laments that if Israel had heeded His commandments, they would have enjoyed abundant peace, righteousness, and countless descendants.

23:48.title: Redemption from Babylon and the Wicked's Lack of Peace
23:48.summary: God commands Israel to flee Babylon and announce to the earth that Yahweh has redeemed Jacob, recalling how He sustained them in the wilderness with water from the rock. Yet He closes with a solemn declaration that there is no peace for the wicked.

23:49.title: The Servant's Call and Commission
23:49.summary: The servant announces that Yahweh called him from the womb, prepared him as a sharp sword and polished shaft, and declared he will be glorified through Israel. This establishes the servant's divine commission and identity as God's instrument.

23:49.title: The Servant's Struggle and Exaltation
23:49.summary: The servant expresses weariness from seemingly fruitless labor but trusts that his vindication rests with God, who promises to expand his mission from restoring Israel to becoming a light to all nations and winning the worship of kings. God affirms the servant's worth despite human contempt and rejection.

23:49.title: Restoration and Ingathering of Israel
23:49.summary: Yahweh promises to help the servant in the acceptable time, establishing him as a covenant for the people to restore the land and free the captives, who will be guided and sustained on their journey home from all directions. This depicts the restoration and return of the dispersed people of Israel.

23:49.title: Comfort and Reassurance to Zion
23:49.summary: Creation is called to celebrate God's comfort of his people, but Zion protests that she has been abandoned and forgotten; Yahweh responds with an unshakeable promise of remembrance engraved on his hands and assures her that her oppressors will depart while her children return. God's everlasting compassion and protective care overcome despair.

23:49.title: Zion's Restoration and Population
23:49.summary: Yahweh promises that Zion will be clothed with returning children like a bride adorned, and the once-desolate land will become too small to hold its inhabitants, leaving Zion amazed at how the children she thought lost have been restored. This depicts the reversal of Zion's barrenness and exile into abundance.

23:49.title: Nations Serve and Honor Zion
23:49.summary: Yahweh will signal the nations to bring Zion's sons and daughters home, and kings and queens will serve as her nursing parents, bowing to her in submission as recognition of God's power and faithfulness. This depicts the exaltation of Zion as nations honor her.

23:49.title: God's Deliverance and the Defeat of Oppressors
23:49.summary: Yahweh declares he will rescue captives from their mighty oppressors and contend against those who contend with Zion, turning oppressors' violence against themselves as judgment while all flesh recognizes Yahweh as Savior and Redeemer. This affirms God's power to overthrow tyranny and vindicate his people.

23:50.title: God's Power to Redeem Despite Israel's Sin
23:50.summary: God challenges Israel to show evidence that He has divorced or sold them, clarifying that their separation resulted from their own sins and transgressions, not from God's weakness. God demonstrates His undiminished power through cosmic acts—drying up the sea and covering the heavens in darkness.

23:50.title: The Servant's Obedience and Confidence
23:50.summary: The Servant describes his training by God to speak words of encouragement to the weary and his willing submission to suffering—giving his back to strikers and his face to shame—sustained by confidence that God will vindicate him. Despite humiliation, the Servant resolves to face his trials with unwavering determination.

23:50.title: The Servant's Vindication and God's Justice
23:50.summary: The Servant affirms that God's justification is near and challenges any accuser to come forward, confident that all opponents will fade away like worn garments consumed by moths. God's justice will ultimately triumph over all who oppose the Servant.

23:50.title: A Call to Trust and Warning to the Self-Reliant
23:50.summary: The passage calls on those who fear God and obey His servant to trust in God's name even in darkness, offering hope to the faithful; it warns those who kindle their own fires and rely on their own plans that they will lie down in sorrow as a result of their self-sufficiency.

23:51.title: Call to Remember Abraham and God's Comfort
23:51.summary: God calls the righteous to remember their origins in Abraham and Sarah, and to recognize how God has comforted Zion by transforming her wasteland into a paradise of joy and thanksgiving. This exhortation establishes the foundation for faith by pointing to God's past faithfulness.

23:51.title: The Eternal Righteousness and Salvation of God
23:51.summary: God declares that His righteousness and salvation are near, will be established as light to the nations, and will endure forever despite human reproach and the decay of all earthly things. The passage contrasts the temporary nature of human opposition with God's eternal justice.

23:51.title: Awake and Remember God's Mighty Acts
23:51.summary: The prophet calls on God's arm to awake and remember His ancient victories over chaos and Egypt, so that the redeemed might return to Zion with everlasting joy and singing. This recalls God's redemptive power in history as grounds for present hope.

23:51.title: Do Not Fear: God Your Maker Comforts You
23:51.summary: God reassures His people not to fear mortal oppressors, reminding them that He is their Maker and Comforter who created the heavens and earth, has put His words in their mouths, and calls them His people. The passage emphasizes God's supremacy over human threats and His intimate commissioning of His servants.

23:51.title: Jerusalem's Suffering and Desolation
23:51.summary: Jerusalem is addressed as having drunk the cup of God's wrath and now lies devastated, with her children fainting in the streets under judgment while having no one to guide or comfort her. This passage graphically depicts the city's affliction as a consequence of divine judgment.

23:51.title: The Cup of Wrath Removed and Redirected
23:51.summary: God announces that He has taken the cup of His wrath from Jerusalem's hand and will place it instead into the hands of her oppressors who had trampled her. This reversal of fortunes promises liberation and vindication for the afflicted people.

23:52.title: Zion Called to Awake and Arise
23:52.summary: Jerusalem is summoned to awaken and put on strength and beautiful garments as a holy city, shaking off the dust of captivity and breaking free from oppression. The imagery calls God's people to recognize their restored dignity and freedom.

23:52.title: Redemption Without Price; God's Name Vindicated
23:52.summary: God declares that His people, sold for nothing into captivity, will be redeemed without payment, and through their deliverance the Lord's name—blasphemed by their oppressors—will be recognized and honored among all nations. This passage promises both liberation and the vindication of God's character.

23:52.title: The Messenger of Good News and Salvation
23:52.summary: The passage celebrates the arrival of messengers bringing news of peace and salvation, announcing God's reign over Zion, while watchmen sing for joy at God's return and all nations witness His mighty salvation. This pericope expresses universal joy and recognition of God's redemptive power.

23:52.title: The Departure from Captivity in Safety
23:52.summary: God calls the redeemed to depart from captivity in ritual cleanness, reassuring them that their exodus will not be hurried or fearful because the Lord will lead them forward and protect their rear. This passage emphasizes God's protective presence during the journey of restoration.

23:52.title: The Exalted and Disfigured Servant
23:52.summary: God's servant will act wisely and be exalted to great heights, yet His appearance will be marred beyond recognition; despite this disfigurement, He will startle nations and bring understanding to kings who had never heard of Him. This passage presents the paradox of the servant's humiliation and exaltation with transformative power.

23:53.title: The Servant's Unimpressive Appearance
23:53.summary: The prophet opens with a question about who will believe the message of God's arm being revealed, then describes the servant's humble, unattractive origin and rejection by people. Despite growing like a tender plant from dry ground, he is despised and without the beauty or form that would commend him to others.

23:53.title: The Servant Bears Sickness and Sin
23:53.summary: Though the servant carries human sickness and suffering, people mistakenly believe he is punished by God for his own sin; in reality, he is pierced and crushed for human transgressions, making peace possible through his wounds. The passage establishes the substitutionary nature of his suffering—he bears the iniquity of all people who, like sheep, have strayed from God's way.

23:53.title: The Servant's Silent Suffering and Unjust Death
23:53.summary: Like a lamb led to slaughter, the servant remains silent under oppression and affliction, accepting judgment without complaint or resistance. He is unjustly condemned and executed, buried among the wicked despite having committed no violence and spoken no deceit.

23:53.title: The Servant's Vindication and Exaltation
23:53.summary: God ordains the servant's suffering as an offering for sin, after which he will see his offspring, prolong his days, and prosper in God's purpose. Justified by his knowledge and made righteous, the exalted servant will be honored among the great, having poured out his soul in death to bear the sins of many and intercede for transgressors.

23:54.title: Sing, O Barren Woman
23:54.summary: The desolate and barren are called to rejoice and expand, for they will bear many children and their descendants will possess the nations and rebuild devastated cities. This announces restoration and multiplication beyond all expectation.

23:54.title: Do Not Fear: Your Maker Is Your Husband
23:54.summary: God reassures the people not to fear shame or disappointment, revealing himself as their husband and redeemer who has temporarily withdrawn but now returns with everlasting mercy and compassion. The brief season of abandonment yields to covenant faithfulness.

23:54.title: The Covenant of Peace Shall Not Depart
23:54.summary: God swears that just as he promised the waters of Noah would not cover the earth again, so he will not be angry with his people anymore; mountains may move but his steadfast love and covenant of peace are eternal. This seals divine commitment through the most solemn oath.

23:54.title: The Rebuilt City of Precious Stones
23:54.summary: God will restore the afflicted city with foundations of sapphires, walls and gates of precious jewels, and all its children will be taught by God in righteousness and peace, far from oppression and terror. Spiritual and material restoration merge in an image of divine beauty and security.

23:54.title: No Weapon Formed Against You Shall Prevail
23:54.summary: Though enemies may gather, they will fall; God created both the craftsman and the destroyer, but no weapon formed against God's servants will succeed, and they will condemn every accusing tongue. This is the heritage and righteousness of the Lord's servants.

23:55.title: The Invitation to Freely Receive God's Blessings
23:55.summary: God invites all who are thirsty and poor to come freely receive food, wine, and milk without payment, and promises an everlasting covenant with sure mercies like those given to David. This invitation extends to nations who do not yet know God, who will come running because of His glory.

23:55.title: The Call to Repentance and Return
23:55.summary: God urges people to seek Him while He is near and calls the wicked to forsake their ways and return to Him, promising abundant mercy and pardon for all who repent.

23:55.title: The Superiority and Power of God's Thoughts and Word
23:55.summary: God declares that His thoughts and ways are infinitely higher than human thoughts and ways, and compares His word to rain that never returns empty but accomplishes its purpose and prospers in all it is sent to do.

23:55.title: The Restoration and Transformation of Creation
23:55.summary: God promises that His people will depart with joy and peace, and all creation will rejoice and be transformed—thorns becoming fir trees and briars becoming myrtles—as an eternal sign of God's redemptive work.

23:56.title: Call to Justice and Righteousness
23:56.summary: Yahweh commands his people to practice justice and righteousness, promising that salvation and divine justice are imminent. Those who obey—especially by keeping the Sabbath and avoiding evil—are blessed.

23:56.title: Inclusion of Foreigners and Eunuchs in God's Covenant
23:56.summary: Yahweh assures foreigners and eunuchs that those who keep his covenant and Sabbath will not be excluded from his people but will receive an everlasting name and be welcomed into his house of prayer. God promises to gather all the outcasts and make his temple a house of prayer for all peoples.

23:56.title: Condemnation of Blind Watchmen and Corrupt Shepherds
23:56.summary: Isaiah denounces the leaders of Israel as blind, ignorant watchmen and greedy, self-serving shepherds who cannot protect the people and care only for their own pleasure and gain. The passage warns of their spiritual failure and moral corruption.

23:57.title: The Righteous Taken Away from Evil
23:57.summary: The righteous perish and are taken away from the world, entering into peace and rest, while few recognize or mourn their passing. Their removal from earthly life is presented as deliverance from coming evil.

23:57.title: Rebuke of Idolatry and False Alliances
23:57.summary: God condemns the people's idolatrous practices, unfaithful covenants with false gods, and relentless pursuit of worldly comfort despite their weariness, declaring that their works will not profit them. Only those who take refuge in God will inherit His holy mountain and find true peace.

23:57.title: God's Comfort and Healing to the Contrite
23:57.summary: God promises to remove obstacles from His people's path and declares that He dwells with the contrite and humble in spirit, offering healing, restoration, and peace to those near and far who turn to Him. God creates words of peace and healing for all who repent.

23:57.title: No Peace for the Wicked
23:57.summary: The wicked are compared to a restless, troubled sea that cannot find peace, and God definitively declares that there is no peace for those who reject Him. This serves as a stark contrast to the peace offered to the contrite and righteous.

23:58.title: The Prophet's Call to Declare Israel's Sin
23:58.summary: Isaiah is commanded to cry out loudly and declare to Israel their disobedience and sins. Yet paradoxically, the people outwardly seek God daily and appear as a righteous nation, creating a stark contrast between appearance and reality.

23:58.title: False Fasting Exposed
23:58.summary: The people question why God ignores their fasts and self-affliction, but God reveals their fasts are hypocritical—they fast while pursuing strife and wickedness, and their religious practices lack genuine spiritual intent. External acts of piety without righteous hearts are unacceptable to God.

23:58.title: The True Fast God Chooses
23:58.summary: God defines the authentic fast He desires: freeing the oppressed, breaking chains of injustice, feeding the hungry, sheltering the homeless, and clothing the naked. True fasting means active compassion and justice toward the vulnerable, not mere physical self-denial.

23:58.title: Blessings That Follow Righteous Fasting
23:58.summary: When Israel practices true fasting through justice and mercy, God promises transformative blessings: their light will shine, healing and righteousness will come, God will answer their prayers, and they will become rebuilders of a broken society, restoring what was lost. Obedience brings restoration and divine blessing.

23:58.title: Honoring the Sabbath and Final Promise
23:58.summary: God calls Israel to honor the Sabbath by refraining from their own pursuits and pleasures, making it holy and a delight rather than a burden. Those who keep the Sabbath holy will experience supreme joy in God and inherit the blessings promised to Jacob's descendants.

23:59.title: God's Power Is Not Limited; Sin Separates
23:59.summary: The prophet affirms that God's power to save and ability to hear are not diminished, but rather the people's iniquities have created a barrier between them and God, causing Him to hide His face from them. This establishes the theological framework: the problem is not God's inability but human sin.

23:59.title: Catalog of Societal Sin and Corruption
23:59.summary: The passage catalogues the people's widespread moral corruption: violence, lies, injustice, and evil pursuits dominate their hands, lips, and paths, preventing them from knowing peace or justice. Their wickedness is endemic and pervasive throughout society.

23:59.title: The Darkness of Divine Judgment and Hopelessness
23:59.summary: The community laments that justice is far away, righteousness does not reach them, and despite seeking light and salvation they walk in darkness and obscurity—they grope blindly and groan like grieving animals. This describes the spiritual and social consequences of their sin.

23:59.title: Confession of Transgression and Collapse of Truth
23:59.summary: The community confesses their multiplied transgressions, their denial of God, and their rebellion, while truth itself has fallen in the streets and the righteous become victims; God's displeasure is evident because justice has vanished. This passage moves from accusation to collective acknowledgment and despair.

23:59.title: God's Intervention: The Divine Warrior Brings Salvation
23:59.summary: Finding no human intercessor, God Himself acts as a warrior, clothing Himself in righteousness and vengeance to save and repay according to deeds; His coming is unstoppable like a rushing stream, and His name will be feared from west to east. God's justice and power override human failure.

23:59.title: Promise of the Redeemer and Eternal Covenant
23:59.summary: God promises that a Redeemer will come to Zion for those who turn from disobedience, and establishes an everlasting covenant wherein His Spirit and words will remain in the mouths of the faithful and their descendants forever. This concludes the passage with hope and restoration.

23:60.title: Arise and Shine: God's Glory Upon Zion
23:60.summary: The prophet calls Jerusalem to arise and shine because God's glory has risen upon it, causing nations and kings to be drawn to its light even as darkness covers the earth. This is a call to recognize and embody the transformative presence of God's glory.

23:60.title: The Gathering of Treasures and Peoples
23:60.summary: Jerusalem is commanded to look around and see her scattered children and distant peoples returning with abundant wealth from across the known world—camels bearing gold and frankincense, ships of Tarshish bringing silver and gold—all coming to glorify her and her God. This depicts the restoration of Israel's fortunes through the voluntary submission and generosity of nations.

23:60.title: Foreigners Build Zion's Walls and Serve Her
23:60.summary: Former enemies and foreigners shall rebuild Jerusalem's walls and serve her rulers, while those nations that refuse to serve her will be utterly destroyed, demonstrating God's reversing of past judgment into mercy and the vindication of His people. This shows the radical shift from oppression to honor through divine favor.

23:60.title: The Beautification of the Sanctuary and Humbling of Enemies
23:60.summary: The finest materials from distant lands—the glory of Lebanon's timber—shall beautify God's sanctuary, while former oppressors bow down and acknowledge Jerusalem as the holy city of God, completing her exaltation and vindication. This symbolizes both the material splendor and spiritual majesty of restored Zion.

23:60.title: From Forsaken to Eternal Excellency and Peace
23:60.summary: God promises to transform Jerusalem from a despised, desolate place into an eternal source of joy for generations, nourished by nations and restored to righteousness, with peace and justice replacing violence and destruction. This describes the complete reversal of Jerusalem's condition and the establishment of God's just rule.

23:60.title: Eternal Light and the Flourishing of God's Planting
23:60.summary: God Himself shall be Jerusalem's everlasting light and glory, eliminating mourning and sorrow, while her righteous people inherit the land forever as God's planted seed, multiplying from small to great and bringing glory to the Lord. This concludes the vision with the eternal blessedness of a redeemed and perfected people.

23:61.title: The Anointed One's Mission of Comfort and Restoration
