23:61.summary: The Spirit of the Lord empowers the speaker to bring good news to the humble, bind up the brokenhearted, proclaim liberty to captives, and comfort all who mourn with joy and praise replacing their grief. This establishes the servant's mission to transform the spiritual condition of God's people from despair to righteousness.

23:61.title: Restoration of the Land and Elevation of God's People
23:61.summary: The redeemed people will rebuild ruined cities and be served by foreigners, while they themselves are elevated to priestly status as ministers of God inheriting the wealth of nations. This describes the reversal of Israel's fortunes through divine restoration and exaltation.

23:61.title: Double Blessing and Covenant Promises for the Redeemed
23:61.summary: God replaces shame with double honor and joy for His people, establishing an everlasting covenant of justice and blessing whose effects will be recognized by all nations as evidence of God's favor. This affirms the permanence and visibility of divine restoration.

23:61.title: The Speaker's Rejoicing in Salvation and Righteousness
23:61.summary: The speaker rejoices in God's salvation, clothed in righteousness like a bridegroom adorned for celebration, and declares that righteousness and praise will spring forth universally as naturally as plants grow from the earth. This personal testimony of joy concludes with affirmation of God's universal glory.

23:62.title: God's Commitment to Zion's Vindication
23:62.summary: God declares He will not rest until Jerusalem's righteousness shines forth as brightness and salvation as a burning lamp. The nations will witness Zion's righteousness and glory, and she will receive a new name from the Lord's mouth, becoming a crown of beauty and royal diadem in His hand.

23:62.title: New Names and the Marriage of God and Zion
23:62.summary: Jerusalem will no longer be called Forsaken or Desolate, but will receive new names—Hephzibah and Beulah—signifying God's delight in her. Her relationship with God is portrayed as a joyous marriage, where the Bridegroom (God) rejoices over the bride (Zion) as a young groom rejoices over his virgin bride.

23:62.title: Watchmen and Intercessory Prayer for Jerusalem
23:62.summary: God appoints watchmen on Jerusalem's walls who will cry out day and night without ceasing, calling on believers to give God no rest until He establishes Jerusalem and makes her a praise throughout the earth. This depicts the role of persistent prayer and vigilance in God's purposes for the city.

23:62.title: God's Oath to Restore Zion's Harvests
23:62.summary: God swears by His right hand and mighty arm that He will no longer allow Zion's grain and wine to be consumed by enemies and foreigners, but those who labor for and gather the harvest will eat and drink it themselves in God's sanctuary while praising Him. This promise assures Zion of protection and the fruit of her labor.

23:62.title: Preparation for Zion's Salvation and Redemption
23:62.summary: A call goes forth to prepare the way and clear the highway for God's people, and Yahweh proclaims to the ends of the earth that Zion's salvation is coming with God's reward. Zion and her people will be called the holy people and the redeemed of the Lord—no longer forsaken but sought out—marking the completion of restoration.

23:63.title: The Victorious Warrior from Edom
23:63.summary: A mysterious figure returns from Edom with blood-stained garments, identifying himself as the Lord who has trodden the winepress of judgment alone, executing vengeance and redemption through his wrath against the peoples.

23:63.title: God's Mercies and Mighty Acts in Israel's Past
23:63.summary: The prophet recalls God's loving kindness and mercies toward Israel, celebrating how God compassionately saved and sustained his people through their affliction, leading them through the wilderness like a shepherd guides his flock.

23:63.title: A Prayer for God's Return and Compassion
23:63.summary: The community cries out to God from their affliction, questioning why he has withdrawn his compassion and allowed their enemies to trample his sanctuary, pleading for him to return and remember his people as his chosen inheritance.

23:64.title: Prayer for God's Powerful Intervention
23:64.summary: The prophet urgently pleads for God to tear open the heavens and come down with overwhelming power, causing mountains to quake and making His name known to all nations. He recalls that God has done awesome, unexpected things in the past that no one has heard of or seen—a God unlike any other who acts for those who wait upon Him.

23:64.title: Confession of Sin and Separation from God
23:64.summary: The prophet acknowledges that while God meets those who practice righteousness and remember Him, Israel has sinned persistently and now faces God's anger and withdrawn presence. The people have become spiritually unclean, their righteousness is worthless, and they fade away like leaves scattered by the wind of their own iniquities.

23:64.title: Appeal to God as Father and Potter
23:64.summary: Despite their sin, the prophet appeals to God's covenant relationship, calling Him Father and describing Israel as clay in the potter's hand—the work of His own hands. He begs God not to remain angry forever or remember their iniquity, reminding Him that they are still His people.

23:64.title: Lament over Jerusalem's Desolation
23:64.summary: The prophet laments that the holy cities, including Zion and Jerusalem, have become wilderness and desolation, and the beautiful temple where their fathers praised God has been burned with fire. He ends with an urgent question: Will God remain silent in the face of such suffering, or will He finally act to deliver His people?

23:65.title: God's Outstretched Hands to Rebellious People
23:65.summary: God made himself available to those who didn't seek him, yet Israel remained rebellious, practicing idolatry and abominations that provoked his judgment. God declares that he will repay their iniquities and those of their fathers into their own bosom.

23:65.title: Preservation of God's Servants
23:65.summary: As new wine is preserved for its blessing, God will spare his servants from complete destruction and bring forth a faithful remnant from Jacob and Judah to inherit the mountains and dwell in restored lands.

23:65.title: The Fate of Those Who Forsake God
23:65.summary: Those who abandon God and turn to idolatry will face the sword and slaughter because they rejected his call and chose evil over his delight.

23:65.title: Contrast Between God's Servants and the Wicked
23:65.summary: God's servants will experience abundance, joy, and singing while the wicked experience hunger, thirst, sorrow, and obliteration; the righteous will be blessed and remember God as the source of truth.

23:65.title: The New Heavens and New Earth
23:65.summary: God will create new heavens and earth where Jerusalem becomes a place of joy with no weeping, death, or labor in vain; the faithful will enjoy long lives, build homes, plant vineyards, and dwell in peace where even predators live in harmony.

23:66.title: True Worship: God's Rejection of False Sacrifice
23:66.summary: God declares that no human temple can contain Him, for He created all things; instead, He looks upon the humble, contrite, and obedient. Those who offer sacrifices without genuine faith—treating all rituals as equivalent—provoke God's judgment because they have deliberately rejected His word and chosen their own ways.

23:66.title: Zion's Miraculous Birth and God's Faithfulness
23:66.summary: God comforts those persecuted for His name's sake, assuring them that Jerusalem will experience unprecedented, miraculous restoration—a nation born in an instant. God's power to bring forth is absolute and will not fail, guaranteeing the fulfillment of His purposes for Zion.

23:66.title: Rejoicing in Jerusalem's Comfort and Renewal
23:66.summary: All who love Jerusalem are called to rejoice in her restoration, nursing from her abundance and receiving comfort as a child from a mother. The faithful will witness visible signs of God's favor toward His servants and His wrath against His enemies, as their very bones flourish with renewed life.

23:66.title: God's Judgment by Fire on the Unfaithful
23:66.summary: The Lord comes in fiery judgment against all flesh, particularly those who practice idolatry and ritual defilement while rejecting God's ways. Those who sanctify themselves for false worship and consume forbidden things will be consumed and destroyed together.

23:66.title: God's Global Gathering and Commissioning of Witnesses
23:66.summary: God declares He will gather all nations to witness His glory, sending survivors to distant lands to proclaim His fame to those who have never heard. The faithful from among the nations will be brought as an offering to Jerusalem, and from them God will establish priests and Levites for His service.

23:66.title: Eternal Worship and Final Judgment
23:66.summary: In the new heavens and earth, God's people and name will endure eternally, with all flesh perpetually coming to worship. Yet those who transgressed against God will face unending judgment—their bodies a perpetual testimony to divine justice and the consequences of rebellion.

24:1.title: Superscription and Historical Setting
24:1.summary: Jeremiah, son of Hilkiah from the priestly town of Anathoth, receives God's word during the reigns of Josiah, Jehoiakim, and Zedekiah, spanning from the thirteenth year of Josiah's reign until Jerusalem's fall and exile.

24:1.title: Jeremiah's Call and Commission
24:1.summary: God calls Jeremiah to be a prophet to the nations, assuring him of his preordained purpose, empowering him with words, and commissioning him to both destroy and build according to God's purposes. Jeremiah initially objects due to his youth, but God overcomes his fear with presence and strength.

24:1.title: Vision of the Almond Branch
24:1.summary: God shows Jeremiah a vision of an almond branch and reveals that He watches over His word to perform it, confirming the reliability of His promises and the certainty of coming judgment.

24:1.title: Vision of the Boiling Caldron and Coming Judgment
24:1.summary: God shows Jeremiah a second vision of a boiling caldron tilting from the north, symbolizing judgment from northern kingdoms who will besiege Jerusalem because Judah has forsaken God and turned to idolatry.

24:1.title: Jeremiah's Strengthening for Opposition
24:1.summary: God instructs Jeremiah to boldly deliver His message without fear, promising to make him spiritually unshakeable like a fortified city and assuring him that though he will face fierce opposition, God will deliver him.

24:2.title: God's Call to Jeremiah and Memory of Israel's Early Faithfulness
24:2.summary: Yahweh commands Jeremiah to proclaim to Jerusalem that He remembers Israel's devotion in youth and their journey through the wilderness, when they were set apart as holy firstfruits to Him. Those who harm Israel will be judged.

24:2.title: The Indictment: Israel's Fathers Abandoned God
24:2.summary: God calls Israel to hear His complaint: though He brought them from Egypt and gave them a fertile land, their fathers found no fault in Him yet abandoned Him for worthless idols and defiled the land. God announces His judgment will extend to their descendants.

24:2.title: The Unnatural Exchange: Trading Glory for Worthlessness
24:2.summary: God points out that no nation has ever abandoned its gods, yet Israel has traded their glory for unprofitable idols—like forsaking living waters to dig broken cisterns that cannot hold water. This incomprehensible folly calls forth heavenly horror.

24:2.title: Consequences of Apostasy: Becoming Prey and Self-Deception
24:2.summary: Israel, though not enslaved, has become prey to invaders and experienced desolation and burning cities because they forsook Yahweh; their own wickedness and backsliding condemn them, yet they refuse to acknowledge the evil and bitterness of their sin.

24:2.title: Persistent Rebellion: A Vine Turned Corrupt
24:2.summary: Despite God's ancient deliverance from slavery and planting Israel as a noble vine, they have become corrupt and given themselves wholly to idolatry and unfaithfulness, pursuing foreign allies like a driven animal in heat, refusing to restrain themselves from their destructive path.

24:2.title: Universal Shame: Leaders and People All Corrupted
24:2.summary: The entire leadership—kings, princes, priests, and prophets—along with the common people are ashamed like a thief caught stealing, having bowed to wood and stone while rejecting God, yet calling on Him only in crisis. God challenges them to contend with His accusations.

24:2.title: Failed Discipline and Inexplicable Forgetfulness
24:2.summary: God's discipline of Israel's children produced no correction; instead they were devoured by the sword while false prophets spoke lies. Yet Israel has forgotten God with a forgetfulness as unnatural as a bride forgetting her wedding attire.

24:2.title: Indictment Complete: Injustice, Denial, and Final Rejection
24:2.summary: Israel has become expert in pursuing false lovers and has stained themselves with innocent blood, yet they deny their guilt; God declares He will judge them for this denial, and their alliances with Egypt and Assyria will ultimately shame and fail them as God rejects those in whom they trust.

24:3.title: Israel's Unfaithfulness and Call to Repentance
24:3.summary: God uses the metaphor of an unfaithful wife to describe Israel's spiritual adultery with idols, yet calls them to return to Him as their Father. Despite their persistent wickedness and refusal to be ashamed, God appeals to them to cry out to Him again.

24:3.title: The Contrast Between Israel and Judah
24:3.summary: God shows Jeremiah how Israel (the northern kingdom) played the harlot on high places and was divorced for her adultery, yet Judah (the southern kingdom) saw this judgment and learned nothing, following the same treacherous path. Judah's half-hearted return to God proves she is even more guilty than her sister.

24:3.title: God's Call to Return and Promise of Restoration
24:3.summary: God commands Jeremiah to proclaim to backsliding Israel that He will show mercy and not keep anger forever if they acknowledge their transgression, and promises to give them faithful shepherds and bring them to Zion. The invitation emphasizes God's role as a merciful husband willing to restore His people.

24:3.title: Restoration and Messianic Hope
24:3.summary: God envisions a future when Israel will be so fully restored that the ark of the covenant will no longer be needed, and Jerusalem itself will be God's throne where all nations gather; the reunited house of Israel and Judah will inherit the land and call God 'Father' without turning away. This passage depicts a transformation of worship and relationship centered on God's direct presence rather than symbols.

24:3.title: Israel's Treachery and Weeping
24:3.summary: God again expresses deep pain at Israel's treacherous departure from Him, comparing it to a wife abandoning her husband, while a voice on the heights reveals Israel's weeping and petitions as they recognize how they have perverted their way and forgotten God. The passage captures both accusation and the beginning of their awakening.

24:3.title: Israel's Confession and Plea for Healing
24:3.summary: The backsliding children confess their need for God's healing, acknowledge that their salvation comes only from Yahweh (not from human help or idols), and lament how shame and idolatry have consumed the fruits of their fathers' labor throughout generations. They humbly lie down in shame and confusion, confessing that they and their ancestors have never obeyed God's voice.

24:4.title: Call to Return and Repentance
24:4.summary: God promises that if Israel returns and removes abominations, they will be established and become a blessing to nations. The condition for restoration is sincere repentance and covenant faithfulness.

24:4.title: Prepare the Ground and Circumcise Your Hearts
24:4.summary: God calls Judah and Jerusalem to prepare themselves spiritually by removing obstacles to faith and performing inner transformation, lest divine wrath consume them. The metaphor shifts from agricultural preparation to circumcision of the heart, demanding genuine internal change.

24:4.title: Warning of Invasion from the North
24:4.summary: A trumpet call summons Judah to flee to fortified cities as God announces the arrival of evil and great destruction from the north. The urgency and panic signal imminent military invasion.

24:4.title: The Destroyer Arrives and Spreads Terror
24:4.summary: A lion-like destroyer of nations emerges to lay waste the land and cities, and God's fierce anger brings confusion and despair to kings, princes, priests, and prophets. The invasion devastates both leaders and people alike.

24:4.title: Jeremiah's Complaint and God's Response
24:4.summary: Jeremiah protests that God deceived the people by promising peace while allowing destruction; God responds that judgment will come like a scorching wind to pronounce sentence against them. This reveals the gap between false prophets' messages and divine intent.

24:4.title: The Approaching Whirlwind and Call to Cleanse
24:4.summary: The enemy approaches with the speed and destructiveness of a whirlwind, prompting Jeremiah's cry of despair; God appeals to Jerusalem to wash away wickedness before it is too late. Despite impending doom, a final call to repentance is offered.

24:4.title: The Watchers from Afar and Jerusalem's Rebellion
24:4.summary: News spreads from northern regions of hostile watchers approaching from distant lands who surround the cities of Judah. God identifies this as the consequence of Jerusalem's rebellion against him.

24:4.title: Wickedness Brings Bitter Consequences
24:4.summary: God declares that Judah's own ways and deeds have brought judgment upon them, and this wickedness reaches into the very depths of their hearts. Judgment is self-inflicted through their own choices.

24:4.title: Jeremiah's Anguish Over War and Destruction
24:4.summary: Jeremiah cries out in personal anguish at the sounds of war and the rapid destruction sweeping across the land, unable to find peace as disaster follows disaster. His emotional devastation mirrors the land's physical devastation.

24:4.title: Vision of Cosmic Desolation and Complete Ruin
24:4.summary: God reveals through Jeremiah a vision of universal devastation: the earth becomes waste, the heavens darken, mountains tremble, humans flee, cities crumble, and the land becomes a wilderness under God's fierce anger. The vision extends from human foolishness to cosmic judgment.

24:4.title: Total Desolation Yet Not Total Destruction
24:4.summary: God announces that the entire land will be desolate but God will not make a complete end of it; the earth and heavens will mourn, reflecting God's unchangeable and deliberate judgment. Judgment is severe but not final annihilation.

24:4.title: Universal Flight from Invasion
24:4.summary: Every city empties as people flee from invaders, hiding in thickets and mountains while towns stand abandoned and desolate. The invasion's terror drives complete evacuation.

24:4.title: Jerusalem's Beauty Cannot Save Her
24:4.summary: Though desolate Jerusalem adorns herself with scarlet, gold, and paint to attract lovers, they despise her and seek her death; she cries out in anguish like a woman in travail, faint before murderers. No beauty or self-adornment can prevent her ruin.

24:5.title: Search for a Righteous Person
24:5.summary: God instructs Jeremiah to search Jerusalem's streets for even one righteous person who acts justly and seeks truth, promising pardon if found. Yet despite their oaths invoking God's name, the people swear falsely, revealing the hollowness of their claims.

24:5.title: Hardened Hearts and Coming Judgment
24:5.summary: God laments that despite striking and consuming the people, they refuse correction and have hardened their hearts like rock. As punishment for their many transgressions and backsliding, God will send wild beasts—a lion, wolf, and leopard—to destroy those who venture out.

24:5.title: Adultery and Unfaithfulness
24:5.summary: God questions how He can pardon a people whose children have forsaken Him, sworn by false gods, and committed adultery and sexual immorality after being abundantly fed. God declares His right to punish such a wicked nation.

24:5.title: Partial Destruction and False Prophets
24:5.summary: God commands destruction of Jerusalem's walls and branches, but not complete annihilation, because both Israel and Judah have dealt treacherously with Him. The people deny God's reality and reject warnings of judgment, and false prophets become wind with no word from God.

24:5.title: The Invading Nation and Its Devastation
24:5.summary: Because the people have spoken such words, God will make His word like fire consuming them as wood. God will bring a mighty, ancient nation whose language they don't understand to devour their crops, livestock, vineyards, and fortified cities.

24:5.title: Exile as Consequence of Forsaking God
24:5.summary: Though God will not completely destroy Judah during the invasion, when the people ask why God has done this, they will be told it is because they forsook God and served foreign gods—so now they will serve foreigners in a land not their own.

24:5.title: A Call to Fear God and Acknowledge His Power
24:5.summary: Jeremiah is commanded to declare to Israel and Judah that foolish people without understanding refuse to fear or tremble before God despite His awesome power over nature—yet they have a rebellious heart and don't acknowledge that God provides rain and sustains the harvest.

24:5.title: Sin's Consequence and Wickedness of the Wealthy
24:5.summary: The people's iniquities have turned away God's blessings and withheld good from them. Wicked people among them act as predators, setting traps to catch people; their houses are full of deceit, and though they have grown rich and fat, they neglect justice for the fatherless and needy.

24:5.title: Final Indictment: False Prophecy and Complicit People
24:5.summary: God declares His right to punish such wickedness, noting that something horrible has occurred in the land: false prophets prophesy lies and priests rule by their own authority, while the people love having it so, setting up a system of spiritual corruption with no one to challenge it.

24:6.title: Warning of Invasion from the North
24:6.summary: Jeremiah warns the people of Benjamin to flee Jerusalem as an enemy approaches from the north. God declares He will destroy the daughter of Zion as enemy armies surround the city with their flocks and camps.

24:6.title: God's Command to Destroy Jerusalem
24:6.summary: The invading armies prepare to attack Jerusalem at noon and night, destroying her palaces. God commands them to cut down trees and build siege works, calling Jerusalem wholly oppressive and full of violence; He will glean her remnant like a vine harvest.

24:6.title: Jeremiah's Anguish and God's Judgment on All
24:6.summary: Jeremiah laments that Israel's ear is uncircumcised and they reject God's word, so he is filled with God's wrath and must pour it out on all people—children, young men, husbands, wives, and the aged. God will dispossess them of houses, fields, and wives.

24:6.title: Condemnation of Greed and False Comfort
24:6.summary: From least to greatest, all are given to covetousness, and prophets and priests deal falsely by proclaiming 'Peace, peace!' when there is no peace. They feel no shame and will fall when God visits them in judgment.

24:6.title: Refusal of the Good Way and Its Consequences
24:6.summary: God urges Israel to stand in the ways, ask for the old paths, and walk in the good way to find rest, but they refuse to listen to His watchmen or His trumpet. Therefore, He will bring evil as the fruit of their rejected thoughts, declaring that even religious offerings are unacceptable; stumbling blocks and perishing await all people.

24:6.title: The Northern Enemy and Call to Mourning
24:6.summary: A great, cruel nation from the north comes with bow and spear, riding as an army against Zion. The people's hands grow feeble with fear; they cannot go into the field or walk safely. Jeremiah calls the daughter of his people to clothe herself in sackcloth and ashes and mourn bitterly, for the destroyer comes suddenly.

24:6.title: The Refiner's Test and Rejection of Israel
24:6.summary: God has made Jeremiah a tester of metals and a fortress among the people to know and test their way, but they are all grievous rebels dealing corruptly—brass and iron. The bellows blow fiercely but the wicked are not plucked away, and they will be called rejected silver because Yahweh has rejected them.

24:7.title: Jeremiah's Commission and Call to Repentance
24:7.summary: The word of the Lord comes to Jeremiah, commanding him to stand at the temple gate and proclaim that Judah must amend their ways and execute justice. Only through genuine moral reform—treating the vulnerable fairly and abandoning idolatry—will God allow them to dwell safely in the land.

24:7.title: The Futility of False Trust and Warning of Judgment
24:7.summary: Jeremiah exposes the people's self-deception: they commit grave sins—theft, murder, adultery, idolatry—yet trust that the temple's name will protect them. God warns that He will destroy the temple as He did Shiloh, casting Judah out of His sight because they persistently refuse to listen.

24:7.title: Prohibition of Intercession and Declaration of Divine Wrath
24:7.summary: God forbids Jeremiah to pray for the people because their rebellion is complete. Despite widespread idolatry to the queen of heaven, they provoke only themselves to shame; God's wrath will be poured out upon all creation in the land, consuming everything without mercy.

24:7.title: God's True Requirement and History of Disobedience
24:7.summary: God declares that sacrifices are worthless; He demands only obedience and covenant loyalty. From the exodus onward, the people have refused to listen to God's voice and the prophets He sent, growing worse rather than better in their stubbornness and rebellion.

24:7.title: The Nation's Incurable Deafness and Moral Decay
24:7.summary: Jeremiah is forewarned that his message will fall on deaf ears; the nation refuses instruction and truth has vanished from their speech, marking them as beyond recovery.

24:7.title: Judgment Upon Jerusalem and the Valley of Slaughter
24:7.summary: Jerusalem must mourn her rejection by God; the people have defiled the temple with abominations and practiced child sacrifice at Topheth. In judgment, the valley will become a burial ground overflowing with corpses, and all joy will cease from the cities as the land becomes desolate.

24:8.title: Desecration of Bones and Choice of Death
24:8.summary: God will allow the bones of Judah's leaders and people to be exhumed and desecrated before the heavenly bodies they worshiped, leaving them unburied. The survivors will find death preferable to the life of exile and suffering God has imposed.

24:8.title: Israel's Stubborn Refusal to Repent
24:8.summary: God questions why Jerusalem refuses to return to Him despite the natural order and divine law, noting that the people show no remorse and their leaders' claimed wisdom is false. Their rejection of God's word and their deceitful practices demonstrate spiritual blindness.

24:8.title: Universal Corruption and Coming Loss
24:8.summary: From least to greatest, all people—prophets, priests, and ordinary citizens—are consumed by greed and deal falsely; therefore God will dispossess them of their wives, fields, and harvests. The land will be stripped of its fruit and fertility.

24:8.title: The Invasion and God's Judgment
24:8.summary: The people flee to fortified cities seeking safety, but God has silenced them and given them bitter water to drink for their sin; the sound of enemy horses approaches from Dan, and God will send serpents as instruments of His judgment. Hope for peace and healing proves futile.

24:8.title: Jeremiah's Lament for His People
24:8.summary: Jeremiah grieves deeply for Jerusalem's suffering, hearing the cries of the distant exiles questioning God's presence and asking why He allowed idolatry; the prophet laments that the harvest has passed without salvation and despairs whether healing can come to his wounded people.

24:9.title: Jeremiah's Lament Over Judah's Corruption
24:9.summary: Jeremiah expresses anguish over the spiritual and moral decay of his people, who are characterized by deception, treachery, and refusal to know God. Their pervasive lying and deceit have become ingrained in their society, making even neighborly relationships unsafe.

24:9.title: God's Coming Judgment on the Nation
24:9.summary: Yahweh announces He will refine and test the people through judgment because of their corruption. God will not overlook their sins but will execute vengeance on a nation whose words are as deadly as arrows.

24:9.title: The Devastation of the Land
24:9.summary: Jeremiah describes the complete desolation that will come upon Judah and Jerusalem—mountains will echo with mourning, cities will become ruins, and the surviving people will be scattered among foreign nations and pursued by the sword. This judgment results from their abandonment of God's law and pursuit of idolatry.

24:9.title: A Call to Mourning and the Horror of Death
24:9.summary: Jeremiah summons professional mourning women to lament the coming destruction, describing how death will penetrate homes and kill young and old, leaving bodies unburied on the fields. The vivid imagery emphasizes the totality and horror of the coming judgment.

24:9.title: True Boasting in God Alone
24:9.summary: God calls His people to abandon pride in wisdom, strength, and wealth, instead finding their glory in knowing God and understanding His character of loving-kindness, justice, and righteousness. True honor comes only from relationship with the Lord.

24:9.title: Universal Judgment on Circumcised and Uncircumcised Nations
24:9.summary: Yahweh announces that He will judge all nations—both those physically circumcised and those uncircumcised—including Egypt, Judah, Edom, Ammon, and Moab, because ultimately all nations, including Israel, are uncircumcised in heart. External covenant signs mean nothing without internal spiritual obedience.

24:10.title: Warning Against Learning the Ways of the Nations
24:10.summary: Yahweh commands Israel not to adopt pagan customs or fear the signs and omens the nations fear. Jeremiah describes the vanity of idolatry—how idols are crafted from trees and decorated with precious metals but cannot speak, move, or act, making them powerless objects of worship.
themes: fear of God versus fear of idols

24:10.title: Yahweh's Incomparable Greatness
24:10.summary: Jeremiah proclaims Yahweh's supreme greatness, power, and uniqueness compared to all earthly rulers and wise men. In contrast to the lifeless idols, Yahweh alone is the true, living, and eternal King whose wrath causes the earth to tremble and nations to fall.

24:10.title: Idols Shall Perish; Yahweh Created All
24:10.summary: Jeremiah declares that false gods who did not create the heavens and earth will perish, while Yahweh created all things by his power, wisdom, and understanding, controlling even the weather and heavens. Jacob's God is incomparable and chosen—Israel is his inheritance, distinct from all idolatrous nations.

24:10.title: Siege and Scattering: Judgment on Judah
24:10.summary: Yahweh commands the besieged people to gather their possessions as he will hurl the land's inhabitants into distress. Jeremiah laments the destruction of his tent, the loss of his children, the failure of shepherds to seek Yahweh, and the impending desolation coming from the north.

24:10.title: Prayer for Mercy and Justice
24:10.summary: Jeremiah acknowledges that human destiny is not within human control and appeals to Yahweh for correction in measure, not in anger. He asks Yahweh to pour out wrath on the nations that do not know him and have devoured and destroyed Jacob.

24:11.title: The Covenant and Its Violation
24:11.summary: Yahweh commands Jeremiah to proclaim the covenant to Judah and Jerusalem, reminding them of the oath sworn to their fathers and the command to obey. Despite Yahweh's persistent pleading through the prophets, the people refused to obey and walked in the stubbornness of their evil hearts, so judgment came upon them.

24:11.title: The Conspiracy and Coming Judgment
24:11.summary: Yahweh reveals a conspiracy among Judah and Jerusalem: they have turned back to the iniquities of their forefathers and broken the covenant by serving other gods and offering incense to Baal. Though they will cry out in their trouble, Yahweh will not listen, and the nation, once like a beautiful olive tree, will be destroyed by fire.

24:11.title: Jeremiah's Persecution and Appeal
24:11.summary: Yahweh reveals to Jeremiah the plots against him, and Jeremiah, innocent as a lamb led to slaughter, appeals to Yahweh the righteous judge to execute vengeance on those who have devised evil against him.

24:11.title: Judgment on the Men of Anathoth
24:11.summary: The men of Anathoth, Jeremiah's hometown, threaten him not to prophesy in Yahweh's name or face death; Yahweh responds with judgment, pronouncing that their young men will die by the sword and their children by famine, with no remnant left.

24:12.title: Jeremiah's Complaint: Why Do the Wicked Prosper?
24:12.summary: Jeremiah lodges a complaint with God, acknowledging His righteousness while questioning why the treacherous flourish and remain at ease. God knows Jeremiah's faithful heart and is urged to judge the wicked like sheep for slaughter.

24:12.title: The Land Mourns and Betrayal by Family
24:12.summary: The land and its creatures suffer because of the wickedness of its inhabitants who deny God's judgment, and even Jeremiah's own family and kinsmen have dealt treacherously with him despite their false words. The prophet is warned not to trust them.

24:12.title: God's Lament Over His Rejected Heritage
24:12.summary: God describes abandoning His beloved inheritance (Israel/Judah) to her enemies, and expresses His grief and anger; the land has become desolate through destructive shepherds, and those who sow wheat reap thorns because of God's fierce anger. The heritage has turned hostile and barren.

24:12.title: Judgment on Neighbors and Conditional Restoration
24:12.summary: God promises to uproot the evil nations neighboring Israel who have touched His people's inheritance, but offers restoration and reintegration if these nations learn to swear by Yahweh's name; however, those who refuse will be uprooted and destroyed. Divine judgment is certain, but mercy is conditionally available.

24:13.title: The Parable of the Ruined Belt
24:13.summary: Jeremiah is commanded to buy a linen belt, hide it by the Euphrates River, and retrieve it after many days to find it ruined. God explains that just as the belt clings to a man's waist, He had bound Israel and Judah to Himself for praise and glory, but they refused to listen and will be destroyed.

24:13.title: The Parable of the Wine Bottles
24:13.summary: Jeremiah announces that God will fill all the inhabitants of Judah—kings, priests, prophets, and Jerusalem's people—with drunkenness, causing them to dash against one another in destruction without pity or mercy.

24:13.title: Call to Humility and Lament for the Flock
24:13.summary: Jeremiah urges the people to listen, reject pride, and give glory to God before darkness overtakes them, warning of stumbling in judgment; if they refuse, the prophet declares his soul will weep for their pride and for God's captive flock.

24:13.title: Humiliation of the Royal House and Nation
24:13.summary: Jeremiah addresses the king and queen mother, commanding them to humble themselves as their crowns fall and the southern cities are shut up; Judah is wholly carried away captive, and their shame and violence result from their great iniquity.

24:13.title: Inability to Change and Final Judgment
24:13.summary: God declares that Judah cannot change their evil nature any more than an Ethiopian can change his skin or a leopard its spots, so He will scatter them like stubble; their forgotten covenant, false trust, adulteries, and abominations will be exposed in shameful judgment.

24:14.title: The Drought and Its Effects
24:14.summary: A severe drought afflicts Judah, causing widespread mourning and desperation as people search for water and animals perish from lack of sustenance. The passage depicts the physical devastation of the land and the suffering of all creation due to the absence of rain.

24:14.title: The People's Prayer for Mercy
24:14.summary: Despite their sins and many backslidings, the people appeal to Yahweh as their hope and savior, acknowledging their guilt while pleading with him not to abandon them in their time of trouble. They paradoxically call upon God while recognizing their unworthiness.

24:14.title: Yahweh's Rejection and Jeremiah's Commission
24:14.summary: Yahweh announces that the people have repeatedly wandered and refused to obey, so he will not accept their prayers, fasting, or sacrifices; instead, he will consume them through sword, famine, and pestilence. Jeremiah is commanded not to intercede for them.

24:14.title: False Prophets and Their Condemnation
24:14.summary: Jeremiah objects that the prophets are promising peace and deliverance, but Yahweh reveals these prophets are liars who speak from their own hearts without divine commission; they themselves will perish by sword and famine, and those who believe them will face the same destruction. This exposes the danger of false prophecy in misleading the people.

24:14.title: Jeremiah's Lament Over Judah's Wound
24:14.summary: Jeremiah is called to weep continually over the virgin daughter of his people who has suffered a grievous wound; he describes the land filled with the slain and sick, with even prophets and priests lacking knowledge, and cries out in anguish over God's apparent rejection of Judah. The passage expresses the prophet's compassionate intercession despite the people's sin.

24:14.title: The People's Final Plea for Restoration
24:14.summary: The people confess their wickedness and that of their fathers, begging Yahweh not to reject them or break his covenant, and they appeal to him as the only source of rain and salvation, declaring their willingness to wait upon him. This concluding prayer acknowledges both guilt and God's exclusive power to deliver.

24:15.title: The Irreversible Judgment on Judah
24:15.summary: God declares that even the intercession of Moses and Samuel cannot save this people; He appoints four forms of destruction—sword, beasts, birds, and scattering among nations—because of Manasseh's sins and Judah's persistent unfaithfulness.

24:15.title: Jeremiah's Lament and God's Promise of Strength
24:15.summary: Jeremiah bitterly complains of his isolation and rejection, cursed by all though he lent to no one; God responds that He will strengthen the prophet and make his enemies plead with him, yet warns that Judah's treasures will be plundered and fire will burn them.

24:15.title: Jeremiah's Prayer for Vindication and His Complaint
24:15.summary: Jeremiah appeals to God to remember him, avenge his persecutors, and not withdraw in longsuffering; he recalls his devotion to God's word and his separation from worldly joy, yet pleads why his suffering remains incurable and unhealed.

24:15.title: God's Conditional Promise of Restoration and Protection
24:15.summary: God promises that if Jeremiah returns to faithfulness and separates the precious from the vile, He will restore him as His mouth to the people; God will make him a fortified bronze wall against whom enemies cannot prevail, and will deliver him from the wicked.

24:16.title: Jeremiah Forbidden to Marry and the Fate of Children
24:16.summary: God commands Jeremiah not to marry or have children because those born in the land will die terrible deaths—unlamented, unburied, and consumed by sword, famine, and wild animals. This symbolic prohibition demonstrates the severity of coming judgment.

24:16.title: Jeremiah Forbidden to Mourn or Celebrate
24:16.summary: God commands Jeremiah to abstain from mourning rituals and celebrations, including funerals and feasts, because He has withdrawn peace and compassion from the people. All joy—including wedding celebrations—will cease from the land.

24:16.title: The People's Question and God's Answer: Why Judgment Comes
24:16.summary: When the people ask why God has pronounced such evil, Jeremiah is to explain that their ancestors abandoned God for other gods, and they themselves have sinned even more gravely by stubbornly following their evil hearts. As punishment, they will be exiled to a foreign land where they must serve other gods.

24:16.title: Promise of Restoration and Divine Justice
24:16.summary: God promises that future generations will commemorate not the exodus from Egypt but the restoration from exile among the nations, as He brings His people back to their land. He will judge them doubly for their sin and pollution of the land, yet restoration remains assured.

24:16.title: God's Power Revealed to All Nations
24:16.summary: Jeremiah acknowledges God as his strength and refuge, expressing confidence that all nations will eventually recognize the futility of false gods and turn to the true God. God will reveal His hand and might, making all people know that He alone is Yahweh.

24:17.title: The Indelible Sin of Judah
24:17.summary: Jeremiah describes Judah's sin as permanently engraved on their hearts and altars, and warns that God will strip away their land and force them into exile as punishment for kindling His eternal anger.

24:17.title: Two Ways: The Curse of Trusting Man and the Blessing of Trusting God
24:17.summary: A wisdom passage contrasts the cursed person who trusts in human strength—withering like a desert plant—with the blessed person who trusts in the Lord—flourishing like a tree by water and bearing fruit in drought.

24:17.title: God Knows the Heart; Unjust Gain Will Not Endure
24:17.summary: Jeremiah acknowledges that the human heart is deceitful and corrupt beyond understanding, but God searches and judges all hearts according to their deeds; ill-gotten riches will ultimately abandon the wicked person.

24:17.title: The Sanctuary and the Forsaking of Living Waters
24:17.summary: God's glorious sanctuary stands as a sign of hope for Israel, yet those who abandon the Lord—the spring of living waters—will be ashamed and their names written in dust.

24:17.title: Jeremiah's Prayer for Healing and Vindication Against Persecutors
24:17.summary: Jeremiah pleads with God for healing and salvation, defends his faithful ministry despite mockery, asks God not to be a terror to him but his refuge, and calls down judgment upon his persecutors.

24:17.title: The Test of Sabbath Obedience: Blessing or Burning
24:17.summary: God commands Jeremiah to preach at Jerusalem's gates that the people must honor the Sabbath by carrying no burden; if they obey, the city will flourish with worship and royal continuity, but if they disobey, fire will consume the palaces.

24:18.title: The Potter's House Vision
24:18.summary: God commands Jeremiah to go to the potter's house, where he observes a potter reshaping a marred vessel on the wheel. This visual lesson demonstrates God's sovereign power to reshape nations and peoples according to His will.

24:18.title: The Potter's Power Over Nations
24:18.summary: God explains that He has absolute authority over nations like a potter over clay, able to judge and destroy or build and plant them based on their response to His word and obedience to His voice. Nations that repent receive mercy; those who disobey face withdrawal of promised blessing.

24:18.title: Judah's Stubborn Refusal
24:18.summary: Jeremiah is commanded to call Judah and Jerusalem to repent and amend their ways, but they stubbornly refuse, declaring their intention to follow their own desires and the stubbornness of their evil hearts instead.

24:18.title: The Horror of Israel's Apostasy and Judgment
24:18.summary: God declares that Israel's abandonment of Him for false gods is unnatural and horrifying, causing them to stumble from the ancient paths and resulting in desolation, scattering, and divine judgment as God turns His face from them in their calamity.

24:18.title: Jeremiah's Prayer Against His Enemies
24:18.summary: After recounting that enemies plot to silence Jeremiah through slander and false accusation, Jeremiah appeals to God, reminding Him of his faithful intercession on their behalf, and urgently petitions God to judge his persecutors with famine, sword, and death without forgiveness or mercy.

24:19.title: Jeremiah Commanded to Buy a Bottle and Go to Hinnom
24:19.summary: God commands Jeremiah to purchase a potter's earthen bottle and take elders of the people and priests to the valley of Hinnom to deliver a prophetic message.

24:19.title: Prophecy of Judgment on Jerusalem
24:19.summary: Jeremiah proclaims that God will bring calamity upon Jerusalem because the people have forsaken God, worshiped idols, and committed atrocities including child sacrifice; judgment will include military defeat, cannibalism during siege, and desecration of the dead.

24:19.title: The Breaking of the Bottle as a Sign
24:19.summary: Jeremiah is to break the bottle before the witnesses as a symbolic action representing God's irreversible destruction of Jerusalem and its people, comparing their fate to broken pottery that cannot be repaired, with all houses defiled by idolatry becoming like Topheth.

24:19.title: Jeremiah's Proclamation in the Temple Court
24:19.summary: Jeremiah returns from Hinnom to the temple court and reiterates God's judgment against Jerusalem and its towns, attributing the calamity to the people's stubborn refusal to hear God's words.

24:20.title: Pashhur's Persecution of Jeremiah and His Prophecy of Judgment
24:20.summary: Pashhur, a priest and chief officer in the temple, hears Jeremiah's prophecy and beats him, placing him in stocks. The next day Jeremiah announces that God has renamed Pashhur "Magormissabib" (terror on every side) and declares that Pashhur, his household, and all Judah will be captured, plundered, and destroyed by Babylon.

24:20.title: Jeremiah's Lament and Confidence in God's Vindication
24:20.summary: Jeremiah complains that God persuaded him to prophesy, resulting in mockery and derision from all around him, yet he cannot stop speaking God's word despite the internal burning compulsion. Though surrounded by enemies who plot his downfall, he declares confidence that the Lord stands with him as a mighty one and will shame his persecutors, then calls to praise God for deliverance.

24:20.title: Jeremiah's Curse Upon His Birth
24:20.summary: In profound despair, Jeremiah curses the day of his birth and the man who brought news of his birth to his father, wishing he had been killed in the womb so his mother could have been his grave. He questions why he was born only to experience labor, sorrow, and shame.

24:21.title: Yahweh's Response to Zedekiah's Inquiry: Jerusalem's Doom
24:21.summary: King Zedekiah sends officials to ask Jeremiah if God will help against the Babylonian siege. Jeremiah relays God's devastating answer: God will turn the weapons against Judah, fight against them directly, and deliver the survivors into Nebuchadnezzar's hand without mercy.

24:21.title: The Choice Between Life and Death
24:21.summary: God presents the people with a stark choice: remain in Jerusalem and die by sword, famine, and plague, or surrender to the Chaldeans and live. God has determined that the city will fall to Babylon and be burned.

24:21.title: Warning to the House of David: Call for Justice
24:21.summary: God commands the royal house of David to execute justice and protect the oppressed, warning that failure to do so will result in God's unquenchable wrath and destruction of their dwellings. God promises to punish them according to their deeds.

24:22.title: Call to the House of the King: Justice or Desolation
24:22.summary: Jeremiah is commanded to deliver God's word to the king of Judah, calling him to practice justice, protect the vulnerable, and refrain from violence. If the king obeys, the dynasty will prosper; if he refuses, the royal house will become a desolate wilderness, and neighboring nations will recognize it as God's judgment for covenant-breaking and idolatry.

24:22.title: Prophecy Against Shallum: No Return from Captivity
24:22.summary: Jeremiah instructs the people not to mourn for the dead king but to lament for Shallum (Jehoahaz), who was led into captivity and will never return to his native country—he will die in exile.

24:22.title: Woe Against Jehoiakim for Exploitation and Injustice
24:22.summary: Jeremiah condemns Jehoiakim for building his house through unrighteousness and injustice—exploiting laborers without pay and pursuing luxury through cedar and paint—rather than following his father Josiah's example of doing justice and knowing God. The king's heart is consumed with covetousness, bloodshed, oppression, and violence.

24:22.title: Jehoiakim's Shameful Death and Captivity of His Advisers
24:22.summary: God declares that Jehoiakim will receive no honorable mourning, but will be buried ignominiously like a donkey and cast out beyond Jerusalem's gates; his advisers (lovers) will be destroyed or carried into captivity, leaving him ashamed and bringing pangs upon the land.

24:22.title: Oracle Against Coniah: Rejection and Exile
24:22.summary: Although Coniah (Jehoiachin) were God's signet ring on His right hand, God will pluck him away and deliver him into the hands of Nebuchadnezzar and the Chaldeans, exiling him and his mother to an unknown land from which they will never return, despite their longing.

24:22.title: Coniah Declared Childless and Without Prosperity
24:22.summary: God summons the earth to hear His word: Coniah is to be recorded as childless, a despised broken vessel in whom no one delights, and none of his descendants will ever prosper or sit on David's throne to rule Judah, severing the line of royal succession.

24:23.title: Judgment Against Unfaithful Shepherds and Promise of Restoration
24:23.summary: Yahweh condemns the shepherds of Israel who have scattered and neglected the flock, then promises to gather the remnant and appoint faithful shepherds who will care for the people. The passage moves from judgment to restoration, assuring that the flock will be fruitful and secure under proper leadership.

24:23.title: The Righteous Branch and Future Salvation
24:23.summary: Yahweh promises to raise up a righteous descendant of David who will reign wisely, execute justice, and bring salvation and safety to Judah and Israel. This future restoration will be so complete that people will cease to reference the exodus from Egypt and instead celebrate Yahweh's deliverance from exile.

24:23.title: The Prophetic Lament Over Profane Prophets and Priests
24:23.summary: Jeremiah grieves over the spiritual condition of the land, where both prophets and priests are profane, adultery and false oaths are rampant, and wickedness pervades even Yahweh's house. Their slippery path will lead to their downfall as Yahweh brings judgment upon them.

24:23.title: Condemnation of False Prophets in Samaria and Jerusalem
24:23.summary: Yahweh condemns the prophets of Samaria for prophesying by Baal and leading Israel astray, and the prophets of Jerusalem for committing adultery, speaking lies, and enabling wickedness. Their ungodliness will be punished with bitterness and gall as judgment spreads throughout the land.

24:23.title: Warning Against False Prophets and Their Vain Visions
24:23.summary: Yahweh warns the people not to listen to false prophets who speak from their own hearts and offer peace to those who reject Yahweh's word. The wrath of Yahweh is unleashed like a whirling storm upon the wicked, and in the end times people will understand that these prophets did not speak from Yahweh's council.

24:23.title: False Prophets Sent Not by Yahweh
24:23.summary: Yahweh contrasts the unauthorized prophets who run without being sent and speak without being commissioned, with what would have happened had they truly stood in his council: they would have turned the people from their evil ways. Their lack of divine commission and their failure to call people to repentance expose their falseness.

24:23.title: Yahweh's Omniscience and Omnipresence
24:23.summary: Yahweh asserts his all-knowing and all-present nature, declaring that nothing can be hidden from him and that he fills both heaven and earth. This affirmation of divine omniscience establishes the basis for his knowledge of the false prophets' deceptions.

24:23.title: False Dreams and Delusions of Deceitful Prophets
24:23.summary: Yahweh condemns prophets who claim false dreams and spread their delusions to the people, causing them to forget Yahweh's name just as their ancestors abandoned his name for Baal. These dream-based deceptions persist in the hearts of false prophets who deceive one another and the nation.

24:23.title: Distinction Between True and False Prophecy
24:23.summary: Yahweh commands that true prophets speak his word faithfully while false dreamers may tell their dreams, emphasizing the vast difference between authentic divine word and human deception. Yahweh's word is compared to fire and a hammer that breaks rock—powerful, effective, and transformative.

24:23.title: Yahweh's Judgment Against Multiple Categories of False Prophets
24:23.summary: Yahweh declares himself against prophets who steal his words from one another, those who speak falsely using his name, and those who prophesy lying dreams and lead the people into error. None of these false prophets were sent or commanded by Yahweh, and they profit the people not at all.

24:23.title: Rejection of the 'Burden of Yahweh'
24:23.summary: When the people or prophets inquire about the 'burden of Yahweh,' they are to be told that Yahweh will cast them off, and anyone who invokes this phrase will face personal and familial punishment. The term itself becomes a marker of those who have perverted Yahweh's words.

24:23.title: Instructions for Proper Inquiry About Yahweh's Word
24:23.summary: Instead of speaking of the 'burden of Yahweh,' the people should ask one another what Yahweh has answered and spoken, seeking his authentic word. This instructional passage redirects the people toward genuine inquiry and away from the hollow religious language of false burden-bearing.

24:23.title: Final Judgment for Using the 'Burden' Formula
24:23.summary: Yahweh repeats his command not to use the 'burden of Yahweh' phrase, and warns that persistent violation will result in utter rejection, casting off from his presence, and everlasting reproach upon them and the city. This final judgment emphasizes the severity of perverting Yahweh's words and refusing to heed his correction.

24:24.title: Jeremiah's Vision of Two Baskets of Figs
24:24.summary: Yahweh shows Jeremiah two baskets of figs set before the temple—one with very good figs and one with very bad figs that cannot be eaten. When asked what he sees, Jeremiah accurately identifies the baskets and their condition.

24:24.title: The Good Figs: Promise to the Captives
24:24.summary: Yahweh interprets the good figs as representing the exiles of Judah sent to Babylon, whom He will regard favorably, restore to their land, and transform through a renewed covenant relationship. God promises to give them a heart to know Him and make them His people.

24:24.title: The Bad Figs: Judgment on Those Who Remain
24:24.summary: Yahweh interprets the bad figs as representing King Zedekiah, the remaining princes, and those in Jerusalem and Egypt who rejected exile. They will be scattered among the kingdoms as a curse and consumed by sword, famine, and pestilence.

24:25.title: Jeremiah's Long Message of Repentance Rejected
24:25.summary: Jeremiah addresses Judah in the fourth year of King Jehoiakim, recounting how for twenty-three years he has repeatedly called the people to repent and turn from idolatry, but they have consistently refused to listen. His persistent warnings went unheeded despite his faithfulness in delivering God's word.

24:25.title: Judgment: Nebuchadnezzar's Conquest and Seventy Years of Exile
24:25.summary: Because Judah rejected God's prophets, the Lord will send Nebuchadnezzar and the northern armies to devastate the land, make it desolate, and enslave the nations for seventy years. After that period, God will punish Babylon and all nations according to their deeds.

24:25.title: The Cup of God's Wrath Distributed to All Nations
24:25.summary: God commands Jeremiah to take a symbolic cup of divine wrath and cause all nations to drink from it, symbolizing their coming judgment and destruction by the sword. The vision encompasses Judah, Egypt, Philistia, Edom, Tyre, Arabia, and all kingdoms of the earth, showing that even refusal to accept judgment will not spare them.

24:25.title: The Day of the Lord: Roaring Judgment and Desolation
24:25.summary: Jeremiah is commanded to prophesy that the Lord will roar from heaven in judgment, contending with all nations and flesh, leaving the slain unburied across the earth. The shepherds and leaders of Judah will wail as their pastures are laid waste and their land becomes desolate by God's fierce anger.

24:26.title: The Lord's Command to Jeremiah
24:26.summary: God commands Jeremiah to stand in the temple court and proclaim to all Judah that if they do not repent and listen to His law and prophets, He will make the temple like Shiloh and curse the city. The message offers conditional hope: repentance may turn away God's judgment.

24:26.title: Jeremiah's Arrest and Accusation
24:26.summary: After Jeremiah delivers God's message in the temple, the priests, prophets, and people seize him and demand his death for prophesying against the temple and city. The princes arrive to investigate the commotion.

24:26.title: Jeremiah's Defense
24:26.summary: Jeremiah addresses the princes and people, affirming that God sent him to speak these words and calling them to repent. He submits himself to their judgment but warns them that killing him will bring innocent blood upon themselves and the city.

24:26.title: Jeremiah's Acquittal by the Elders
24:26.summary: The princes and people declare Jeremiah worthy of life, and certain elders cite the precedent of the prophet Micah, who similarly prophesied against Jerusalem in Hezekiah's time; Hezekiah repented rather than killed the prophet, and God relented of the disaster.

24:26.title: The Contrast: Uriah's Fate
24:26.summary: To contrast Jeremiah's deliverance, the narrative recounts how another prophet, Uriah, also prophesied against Jerusalem but was hunted down by King Jehoiakim, fled to Egypt, was extradited, and executed by the king.

24:26.title: Ahikam's Protection of Jeremiah
24:26.summary: God's protection of Jeremiah is secured through the intervention of Ahikam son of Shaphan, whose support prevents the people from handing Jeremiah over to death.

24:27.title: The Sign of the Yoke and God's Sovereignty Over Nations
24:27.summary: God commands Jeremiah to make bonds and yokes and send them to neighboring kings through messengers to Zedekiah, symbolizing that God has given all lands into Nebuchadnezzar's hand as His servant. God's power over creation and history means He determines which ruler receives dominion.

24:27.title: The Promise of Survival Through Submission
24:27.summary: Nations that refuse to serve Nebuchadnezzar will face sword, famine, and pestilence; but those who submit to his yoke will be allowed to remain in their land and live. The choice between destruction and preservation is presented as dependent on accepting God's judgment through Babylon.

24:27.title: Warning Against False Prophets and Exhortation to Zedekiah
24:27.summary: Jeremiah directly tells King Zedekiah to submit to Babylon and live, warning him not to believe prophets who claim he need not serve the king of Babylon, as these false prophets are not sent by God but speak lies that will lead to destruction. God did not send these prophets, and they will cause the people and themselves to perish.

24:27.title: Judgment on False Prophets and Restoration of Temple Vessels
24:27.summary: Jeremiah warns priests and all people not to believe prophets claiming the temple vessels will soon return from Babylon, calling this a lie; he urges submission to preserve the city. God affirms that the remaining temple vessels will indeed be carried to Babylon, but will eventually be returned in the day of God's visitation.

24:28.title: Hananiah's False Prophecy of Liberation
24:28.summary: The prophet Hananiah publicly declares that God will break Babylon's yoke within two years, return the temple vessels, and restore King Jeconiah and the captives. This directly contradicts Jeremiah's warnings of extended exile and submission to Nebuchadnezzar.

24:28.title: Jeremiah's Cautious Response and Test of True Prophecy
24:28.summary: Jeremiah publicly acknowledges Hananiah's words while cautiously noting that true prophets are historically known by whether their predictions come true, especially prophets of peace whose words must be verified. He appeals to the principle that false prophecy can be distinguished from true prophecy by its fulfillment.

24:28.title: Hananiah's Symbolic Act and Defiant Proclamation
24:28.summary: Hananiah dramatically breaks the wooden yoke from Jeremiah's neck and proclaims that God will similarly break Nebuchadnezzar's yoke within two years, using a symbolic action to reinforce his false message. Jeremiah silently departs.

24:28.title: God's Word Against Hananiah: Iron Yokes Replace Wooden Ones
24:28.summary: The Lord directs Jeremiah to tell Hananiah that his breaking of the wooden yoke means God will replace it with iron yokes—making Babylon's bondage even more severe and inescapable. The metaphor shifts from wood to iron to indicate intensified judgment.

24:28.title: Jeremiah Condemns Hananiah and His Death
24:28.summary: Jeremiah declares that Hananiah is not sent by God and has deceived the people with lies, prophesying that Hananiah will die that year as punishment for speaking rebellion against the Lord. Hananiah dies in the seventh month, confirming Jeremiah as the true prophet.

24:29.title: Introduction to Jeremiah's Letter to the Exiles
24:29.summary: Jeremiah sends a letter to the Jewish exiles in Babylon, addressed to the elders, priests, prophets, and people, delivered by envoys from King Zedekiah. This sets the historical context for God's communication to the captive community.

24:29.title: God's Command to Build and Seek Peace in Exile
24:29.summary: God commands the exiles to settle in Babylon by building houses, planting gardens, marrying, and multiplying, and to seek the peace of the city for their own welfare. This reveals God's intent for the exiles to establish stable lives rather than await quick return.

24:29.title: Warning Against False Prophets in Babylon
24:29.summary: God warns the exiles not to be deceived by false prophets and diviners in their midst, as they prophesy lies in God's name and have not been sent by Him. This protects the community from spiritual deception during their vulnerable time of captivity.

24:29.title: Promise of Return and Restoration After Seventy Years
24:29.summary: After seventy years in Babylon, God promises to restore the exiles to Jerusalem and gather them from all nations where they have been scattered, demonstrating His good intentions toward them. God invites them to seek Him with their whole hearts and promises to be found by them.

24:29.title: Judgment on Those Who Remained in Jerusalem
24:29.summary: God pronounces judgment on King Zedekiah and those who remained in Jerusalem, declaring they will face sword, famine, and pestilence, and be scattered among the nations, because they rejected God's prophetic word. This contrasts their fate with the promised restoration of the exiles.

24:29.title: Condemnation of False Prophets Ahab and Zedekiah
24:29.summary: God identifies two false prophets, Ahab and Zedekiah, who prophesy lies in God's name and commit adultery, declaring they will be executed by King Nebuchadnezzar and become a curse among the exiles. Their deaths serve as a sign of God's judgment against false prophecy.

24:29.title: Shemaiah's Challenge to Jeremiah's Authority
24:29.summary: Shemaiah the Nehelamite writes letters to Jerusalem's priest Zephaniah and others, criticizing the priest for not rebuking Jeremiah and opposing his message that the captivity will be long and that exiles should build lives in Babylon. This reveals opposition to Jeremiah's prophetic word among the exiles' leadership.

24:29.title: God's Judgment Against Shemaiah the False Prophet
24:29.summary: After the priest Zephaniah reads Shemaiah's letter to Jeremiah, God pronounces judgment that Shemaiah will be punished along with his descendants and will not live to see the restoration God promises to His people, because he spoke rebellion against the Lord. This vindicates Jeremiah's true prophecy against its opponents.

24:30.title: Command to Write the Restoration Promise
24:30.summary: Yahweh commands Jeremiah to write down all His words, introducing a message of restoration: He will bring back Israel and Judah from captivity to their ancestral land. This written record serves as a testimony to God's redemptive purpose despite the people's current exile.

24:30.title: The Anguish of Jacob's Trouble
24:30.summary: Yahweh describes the coming Day of Judgment as an unprecedented time of agony—Israel writhing like a woman in labor—yet assures that Jacob will be saved from this terrible ordeal. The passage emphasizes both the severity of the crisis and the certainty of deliverance.

24:30.title: Breaking the Yoke and Covenant Restoration
24:30.summary: Yahweh promises to break Israel's bondage and yoke, freeing them from servitude so they may serve Him alone and their restored Davidic king. He assures Jacob not to fear, pledging personal presence, complete salvation, and the restoration of peace and security.

24:30.title: The Incurable Wound and Its Cause
24:30.summary: Yahweh acknowledges Israel's wound as incurable and their pain as the direct result of their own sins and iniquity; no one comes to their aid because they have been wounded by God as punishment for their transgressions. The passage explains suffering not as arbitrary but as the consequence of covenant-breaking.

24:30.title: Judgment on Oppressors and Healing for Zion
24:30.summary: Yahweh promises that all who have devoured and despoiled Israel will themselves be devoured and taken captive, while He will restore health and healing to Israel, whom the nations had rejected and abandoned. This marks the reversal of Israel's fortunes and the vindication of the oppressed.

24:30.title: Rebuilding, Multiplication, and Covenant Renewal
24:30.summary: Yahweh promises to restore Jacob's tents, rebuild Jerusalem with joy and thanksgiving, multiply the population, establish their children securely, raise up a native prince who will approach Him, and renew the covenant relationship: 'You shall be my people, and I will be your God.' This envisions complete national and spiritual restoration.

24:30.title: The Storm of Yahweh's Wrath
24:30.summary: Yahweh's fierce wrath sweeps forth as an unstoppable storm that will burst upon the wicked, and His anger will not return until He has fully executed His intentions against evildoers; in the latter days Israel will understand these judgments. This closing affirms that God's justice will be complete and comprehensible in the future.

24:31.title: God's Everlasting Love for Israel
24:31.summary: God declares that He will be the God of all Israel's families and they shall be His people. He reminds them that He has loved them with an everlasting love and has drawn them with loving kindness.

24:31.title: The Restoration and Joy of Israel
24:31.summary: God promises to rebuild Israel, restoring the virgin nation to beauty and joy with vineyards, songs, and dances. The scattered people will return from distant lands, and their mourning will turn to celebration as they experience God's abundance and comfort.

24:31.title: Rachel Weeping and Comfort for the Exiles
24:31.summary: A voice of lamentation is heard in Ramah as Rachel weeps for her lost children, but God calls her to cease weeping and assures her that her children will return from enemy lands and come back to their own borders.

24:31.title: Ephraim's Repentance and God's Compassion
24:31.summary: God hears Ephraim's repentance and acknowledgment of his correction, and responds with overwhelming compassion, remembering him as a beloved son and promising mercy.

24:31.title: The Call to Return and God's Renewal
24:31.summary: God calls the backsliding virgin of Israel to set up road signs and return to her cities, announcing that He has created something new in the earth—a woman encompassing a man. He satisfies the weary and sorrowful souls, and finds His rest sweet in this renewal.

24:31.title: God's Watchfulness to Build and Plant
24:31.summary: God promises to sow Israel and Judah with the seed of man and animal, watching over them to build and plant as He once watched to destroy. Each person will bear responsibility for their own sin, ending the proverb about fathers' sins affecting children.

24:31.title: The New Covenant
24:31.summary: God announces He will make a new covenant with Israel and Judah, not like the old one they broke, but one where He writes His law on their hearts and they all know Him intimately, with complete forgiveness of their iniquity.

24:31.title: The Permanence of Israel's Covenant
24:31.summary: God swears by the sun, moon, stars, and sea that Israel will endure as a nation forever, and that He will not cast off Israel's seed despite their deeds, as long as those ordinances remain in place.

24:31.title: The Rebuilt and Holy Jerusalem
24:31.summary: God promises that Jerusalem will be rebuilt from the tower of Hananel to the corner gate, with the valley of the dead bodies becoming holy to the Lord, never to be plucked up or destroyed again.

24:32.title: The Setting: Jeremiah's Imprisonment and Prophecy
24:32.summary: During the Babylonian siege of Jerusalem in the tenth year of Zedekiah's reign, Jeremiah is imprisoned in the court of the guard for prophesying that the city will fall to Nebuchadnezzar and that Zedekiah will be captured. The king has shut him up because his message contradicts the hope for deliverance.

24:32.title: The Symbolic Purchase of the Field
24:32.summary: God commands Jeremiah to purchase a field in Anathoth from his cousin Hanamel, and Jeremiah obeys by buying it for seventeen shekels of silver and recording the transaction with witnesses. He then gives the deeds to Baruch with instructions to preserve them, as a sign that houses and fields will again be bought in the land after its restoration.

24:32.title: Jeremiah's Prayer of Questioning
24:32.summary: After delivering the deed to Baruch, Jeremiah prays to God, acknowledging His power over creation and His mighty deeds in Israel's history, but then expressing confusion about why he is being commanded to buy land when the city is about to fall to the Chaldeans. His prayer moves from praise to a lament about the apparent contradiction between God's command and the visible reality of judgment.

24:32.title: God's Declaration of Judgment and the Causes of Exile
24:32.summary: God responds by affirming His power over all flesh and declaring that He will indeed give the city to Nebuchadnezzar, detailing the burning and destruction to come. He explains that this judgment results from Israel's persistent idolatry, rebellion, and refusal to obey His voice despite His patient teaching, including their abominable worship of Baal and Molech.

24:32.title: The Promise of Restoration and Redemption
24:32.summary: God promises that beyond the judgment and exile, He will gather His people from all lands, restore them to their place, establish an everlasting covenant, give them a unified heart to fear Him, and restore the land so that fields are bought and sold again. This promise of restoration gives meaning to Jeremiah's symbolic purchase and assures that God's ultimate purpose is redemption, not destruction.

24:33.title: The Word of Yahweh and the Call to Prayer
24:33.summary: Yahweh's word comes to Jeremiah a second time while he is imprisoned in the guard's court, commanding him to call upon God, who promises to answer and reveal great and hidden things. This establishes the framework for the restoration message that follows.

24:33.title: Promise of Healing and Restoration
24:33.summary: Though the cities of Judah are destroyed and filled with corpses from God's judgment, Yahweh promises to heal, cure, and reveal abundance of peace and truth, restoring both Judah and Israel and cleansing them from iniquity. The restored city will become a name of joy and praise among all nations.

24:33.title: The Voice of Joy Returns to the Desolate Land
24:33.summary: In the waste places where Judah and Jerusalem are now silent and empty, the sounds of joy, celebration, and thanksgiving will be heard again, and shepherds will tend their flocks throughout all the cities of the land. This vivid image depicts the complete reversal of the current devastation.

24:33.title: The Righteous Branch and the Salvation of Judah
24:33.summary: Yahweh will perform the good word spoken to Israel and Judah by raising up a righteous Branch from David who will execute justice and righteousness, bringing salvation and security to Jerusalem. The city will be called 'Yahweh our Righteousness,' signifying God's covenant fulfillment.

24:33.title: The Eternal Covenant with David and the Levites
24:33.summary: Yahweh confirms that David's line will never lack a descendant to sit upon Israel's throne, and the Levitical priests will never lack ministers to continually offer sacrifices before God. These covenants represent God's permanent commitment to His chosen instruments of rule and worship.

24:33.title: The Unbreakable Covenant Sealed by Creation
24:33.summary: Yahweh declares that His covenant with David and the Levites is as unbreakable as the natural cycles of day and night and as immeasurable as the stars and sand, guaranteeing that David's seed and the Levitical priesthood will be multiplied forever. This uses the constancy of creation itself as an oath.

24:33.title: Yahweh's Answer to the People's Despair
24:33.summary: In response to the people's claim that God has rejected the two families of Israel and Judah, Yahweh reaffirms that His covenant of day and night (creation order) and His decree to give rulers from David's seed over Abraham's descendants will stand firm, and He will have mercy by restoring their captivity. God's faithfulness to His covenants proves He has not abandoned His people.

24:34.title: Jeremiah's Word to Zedekiah During the Siege
24:34.summary: God commands Jeremiah to tell King Zedekiah that Jerusalem will fall to Nebuchadnezzar and he will be captured, though he will die peacefully like his predecessors. Jeremiah delivers this message while Babylon's army besieges Jerusalem and its remaining fortified cities.

24:34.title: The Broken Covenant to Free Slaves
24:34.summary: King Zedekiah makes a covenant to free all Hebrew slaves, and the people obey and release them; however, they later renege on this commitment and force the freed servants back into bondage. This violation of their own covenant becomes the basis for God's judgment.

24:34.title: God's Judgment for Profaning the Covenant
24:34.summary: God condemns Judah for breaking their covenant to free slaves, comparing their betrayal to the ancient law given at the Exodus and their own recent pledge made in God's house. As punishment, God proclaims liberty—meaning liberation to sword, plague, and famine—and will give all who violated the covenant into the hands of their enemies, returning the Babylonian army to utterly destroy Judah and its cities.

24:35.title: The Object Lesson of the Rechabites
24:35.summary: God commands Jeremiah to bring the Rechabite family into the temple and offer them wine to test their obedience to their ancestral covenant. Jeremiah obeys the command and sets wine before them.

24:35.title: The Rechabites' Faithful Refusal
24:35.summary: The Rechabites refuse the wine, explaining that their ancestor Jonadab commanded them never to drink wine, build houses, or plant crops, but to live in tents permanently—a commandment they have faithfully kept throughout their lives and even during the Babylonian invasion. Their consistent obedience to their father's word demonstrates their integrity.

24:35.title: The Comparison and Judgment on Judah
24:35.summary: God uses the Rechabites' obedience as a contrast to Judah's disobedience, noting that while the Rechabites faithfully keep their father's commands, Judah refuses to listen to God's repeated calls through the prophets to repent and return. Because of this stubborn rejection, God pronounces judgment against Jerusalem and all Judah.

24:35.title: Promise of Blessing to the Rechabites
24:35.summary: As a reward for their faithfulness in keeping Jonadab's commandment, God promises that the Rechabites will never lack a descendant to serve before Him forever, ensuring their house will endure and be honored. Their obedience is vindicated and eternally blessed.

24:36.title: The Lord Commands Jeremiah to Write a Scroll
24:36.summary: God instructs Jeremiah to write all His spoken words against Israel, Judah, and the nations on a scroll, with the hope that Judah will hear, repent, and be forgiven.

24:36.title: Baruch Writes and Reads the Scroll
24:36.summary: Jeremiah dictates God's words to Baruch, his scribe, and because Jeremiah is confined, instructs Baruch to read the scroll publicly in the temple on a fast day so the people might repent.

24:36.title: Baruch Reads in the Temple
24:36.summary: During a national fast in Jerusalem, Baruch reads Jeremiah's scroll aloud in the temple courtyard where all the people can hear God's words.

24:36.title: The Princes Hear and Respond
24:36.summary: When Micaiah reports the scroll's words to the royal princes, they become afraid, summon Baruch to read it to them, and then order him and Jeremiah to hide to protect them from the king's wrath.

24:36.title: King Jehoiakim Burns the Scroll
24:36.summary: The princes report the scroll's contents to King Jehoiakim, who burns it piece by piece in contempt for God's word; even the pleas of three princes to spare it go unheeded.

24:36.title: God's Judgment Against Jehoiakim
24:36.summary: Although the king orders the arrest of Jeremiah and Baruch, God hides them; God then pronounces judgment: Jehoiakim will have no heir to the throne, his body will be unburied, and he and his people will suffer the full measure of God's threatened judgment.

24:36.title: A New Scroll Written with Added Words
