24:36.summary: Jeremiah dictates the words to Baruch again on a new scroll, with many additional words added, demonstrating that God's word cannot be destroyed despite human opposition.

24:37.title: Zedekiah's Reign and Jeremiah's Freedom
24:37.summary: Zedekiah becomes king of Judah under Babylonian appointment, but he and the people reject God's word through Jeremiah. Despite this rejection, Jeremiah remains free to move among the people.

24:37.title: Jeremiah's Prophecy Against False Hope
24:37.summary: When Pharaoh's army arrives and the Chaldeans temporarily lift their siege, Jeremiah delivers a harsh word from God: Pharaoh will withdraw and the Babylonians will return to destroy Jerusalem, regardless of any military advantage. The people are warned against self-deception about their certain defeat.

24:37.title: Jeremiah's Arrest at Benjamin Gate
24:37.summary: As the Chaldean siege lifts temporarily, Jeremiah leaves Jerusalem to claim property in Benjamin, but is arrested at the gate by Captain Irijah on false charges of defection. The princes beat him and imprison him in the house of Jonathan the scribe.

24:37.title: Zedekiah's Secret Inquiry and Jeremiah's Conditional Release
24:37.summary: After many days in the dungeon, Zedekiah secretly summons Jeremiah and asks if there is a word from God; Jeremiah repeats that the king will be delivered to Babylon. Jeremiah pleads not to be returned to the deadly dungeon, and Zedekiah transfers him to the court of the guard where he receives daily bread until it runs out.

24:38.title: Jeremiah Imprisoned in the Dungeon
24:38.summary: Four princes hear Jeremiah prophesying that the city will fall to Babylon and demand his death, claiming he demoralizes the soldiers. King Zedekiah, powerless to resist, allows them to cast Jeremiah into a muddy dungeon where he sinks in the mire.

24:38.title: Ebedmelech Rescues Jeremiah
24:38.summary: An Ethiopian eunuch named Ebedmelech learns of Jeremiah's plight and appeals to the king, who grants permission to rescue the prophet. Ebedmelech gently retrieves Jeremiah from the dungeon using rags and cords, and restores him to the court of the guard.

24:38.title: Jeremiah's Secret Counsel to Zedekiah
24:38.summary: The king privately consults Jeremiah, who urges him to surrender to Babylon to save his life and the city, warning that refusal will result in the city's burning and the capture of his family. Jeremiah assures the fearful king that God's word will protect him if he obeys.

24:38.title: Jeremiah Conceals the King's Weakness
24:38.summary: The king instructs Jeremiah to keep their conversation secret, and when the princes interrogate the prophet, Jeremiah protects Zedekiah by giving a false account. Jeremiah remains imprisoned in the court of the guard until Jerusalem falls.

24:39.title: The Siege and Breach of Jerusalem
24:39.summary: Nebuchadnezzar's Babylonian forces besiege Jerusalem in the ninth year of King Zedekiah's reign, and after eighteen months a breach is finally made in the city walls in the eleventh year. This marks the beginning of the fulfillment of Jeremiah's prophecies of judgment.

24:39.title: The Capture and Punishment of Zedekiah
24:39.summary: Babylonian officials take control of the city, and King Zedekiah attempts to flee but is captured in the plains of Jericho and brought before Nebuchadnezzar at Riblah. Zedekiah is forced to witness the execution of his sons and nobles, then is blinded and taken in chains to Babylon.

24:39.title: The Destruction of Jerusalem and Treatment of the People
24:39.summary: The Babylonians burn the royal palace and homes, destroy the city walls, and deport most of the population to Babylon, though the poorest inhabitants are left behind to tend vineyards and fields. This represents both judgment on the city and God's provision for a remnant.

24:39.title: Jeremiah's Release and Protection
24:39.summary: Nebuchadnezzar personally orders that Jeremiah be treated well and protected, recognizing God's hand upon the prophet. The prophet is released from the guard's court and entrusted to Gedaliah's care, where he lives among the people.

24:39.title: The Promise to Ebed-Melech
24:39.summary: While still imprisoned, Jeremiah receives a word from the Lord for Ebed-Melech the Ethiopian eunuch, assuring him that though judgment will come on the city, his life will be spared because of his trust in God. The promise demonstrates God's faithfulness to those who trust Him even in the midst of catastrophe.

24:40.title: Jeremiah Released by Nebuzaradan
24:40.summary: After Jerusalem's fall, the Babylonian captain of the guard releases Jeremiah from chains, explaining that God brought judgment because Judah sinned and disobeyed. Jeremiah is offered the choice to go to Babylon or remain in Judah, and is directed to Gedaliah the governor with provisions.

24:40.title: Remnant Gathers Around Gedaliah
24:40.summary: Jeremiah joins Gedaliah at Mizpah, and military captains recognize Gedaliah as Babylon's appointed governor. Gedaliah assures them safety under Babylonian rule, and Jews scattered in surrounding lands return to Judah to rebuild their lives under his administration.

24:40.title: Warning of Conspiracy Against Gedaliah
24:40.summary: Johanan and other captains warn Gedaliah that the Ammonite king has sent Ishmael to assassinate him, threatening the survival of the restored Judean community. Gedaliah refuses to believe the warning and rejects Johanan's offer to eliminate the threat preemptively.

24:41.title: Ishmael's Assassination of Gedaliah
24:41.summary: Ishmael, a royal official, treacherously kills Gedaliah the governor along with the Jews and Chaldean soldiers at Mizpah during a shared meal. This violent act eliminates the puppet governor placed by Babylon and destabilizes the remnant community.

24:41.title: Ishmael's Massacre of Worshippers
24:41.summary: Two days after killing Gedaliah, Ishmael deceptively lures eighty pilgrims traveling to Jerusalem to Mizpah and massacres them, throwing their bodies into a pit. His violence extends beyond political opponents to include innocent worshippers seeking to honor God.

24:41.title: Ishmael's Flight and Johanan's Pursuit
24:41.summary: Ishmael captures the remaining people at Mizpah, including the king's daughters, as captives and flees toward Ammon, but Johanan the son of Kareah pursues and defeats him at Gibeon. The captives are freed and return to Johanan, while Ishmael escapes with only eight men.

24:41.title: Johanan's Gathering of Survivors and Flight to Egypt
24:41.summary: Johanan collects all the survivors recovered from Ishmael—men, women, children, and eunuchs—and leads them to Geruth Chimham near Bethlehem, planning to flee to Egypt out of fear of Babylonian retaliation for Gedaliah's death. The chapter ends with the community in crisis, fearing judgment from Nebuchadnezzar.

24:42.title: The Remnant's Request for Guidance
24:42.summary: The surviving leaders and people of Judah approach Jeremiah and earnestly request that he pray to God for them, asking for divine guidance on what they should do. They solemnly covenant that they will obey whatever word God sends through Jeremiah, whether it seems good or evil.

24:42.title: God's Promise to Those Who Stay
24:42.summary: After ten days, God's word comes to Jeremiah with a conditional promise: if the people remain in the land, God will build them up, plant them securely, and grant them mercy and compassion. They are called not to fear the king of Babylon, for God promises deliverance.

24:42.title: Warning Against Flight to Egypt
24:42.summary: God pronounces judgment on those who reject His word and insist on fleeing to Egypt to escape war and famine, declaring that the very disasters they fear will pursue them there instead. God's wrath will be poured out on them as it was on Jerusalem, making them objects of horror and curse.

24:42.title: Final Warning and Condemnation
24:42.summary: Jeremiah declares God's final testimony against the people, exposing their hypocrisy: they asked him to pray and promised obedience, yet they have decided to go to Egypt anyway in contradiction to God's clear word. Their death by sword, famine, and pestilence in Egypt is now certain.

24:43.title: Rejection of Jeremiah's Warning
24:43.summary: After Jeremiah delivers God's message forbidding the people to go to Egypt, Azariah, Johanan, and other proud leaders accuse him of speaking falsely and claim that Baruch is manipulating him against their interests. They refuse to accept that God has sent this warning.

24:43.title: Flight to Egypt Despite God's Command
24:43.summary: Johanan and all the captains of the forces disobey God's voice and lead all the remnant of Judah—including men, women, children, the king's daughters, and the prophet Jeremiah—into Egypt, settling in Tahpanhes. Their deliberate rejection of Yahweh's instruction sets the stage for judgment.

24:43.title: Jeremiah's Symbolic Act and Prophecy Against Egypt
24:43.summary: God commands Jeremiah to hide large stones in the mortar of Pharaoh's house and prophesy that Nebuchadnezzar will set his throne upon them, conquer Egypt, and destroy its gods and temples. This symbolic act demonstrates that even Egypt—where the people fled for refuge—will fall under Babylonian judgment.

24:44.title: God's Indictment of Judah's Idolatry
24:44.summary: Jeremiah addresses the Jews in Egypt, reminding them of Jerusalem's destruction caused by their burning incense and serving foreign gods despite the prophets' warnings. God's wrath was poured out on Judah and the cities lie desolate as evidence of their disobedience.

24:44.title: Warning Against Continued Idolatry in Egypt
24:44.summary: God warns the Jewish remnant in Egypt that by continuing to burn incense to other gods, they are committing evil against themselves and will face destruction through sword, famine, and plague until none remain. Their flight to Egypt will not save them from divine judgment.

24:44.title: The People's Defiant Response
24:44.summary: The Jews in Egypt—men, women, and entire communities—openly reject Jeremiah's message and vow to continue burning incense to the queen of heaven as their ancestors did, claiming they prospered then and suffer now because they stopped. They assert their right to worship as they choose.

24:44.title: Jeremiah's Response: The Cause of Their Suffering
24:44.summary: Jeremiah confronts the people, declaring that God remembers their idolatrous incense-burning and that their present desolation is directly caused by their sin and disobedience to God's law. Their suffering is not punishment for stopping idolatry, but the consequence of their continued transgression.

24:44.title: God's Sarcastic Permission and Promise of Judgment
24:44.summary: God sarcastically grants the Jews permission to fulfill their vows to worship the queen of heaven, then pronounces that His name will be banished from Egypt and all Judah there will be consumed by sword and famine, with only a few survivors to prove that God's word stands true. The surviving remnant will serve as witnesses to God's fulfilled judgment.

24:44.title: The Sign of Judgment: Pharaoh Hophra's Fall
24:44.summary: God provides a sign of His impending judgment by announcing that Pharaoh Hophra will be delivered into the hands of his enemies, just as Zedekiah was given to Nebuchadnezzar. This parallel event will confirm to the survivors that God's words against them will surely come to pass.

24:45.title: Baruch's Complaint and Despair
24:45.summary: Jeremiah delivers a message to his scribe Baruch, who laments that God has added sorrow to his pain and left him without rest. Baruch expresses his exhaustion and weariness despite his faithful service to the prophet.

24:45.title: God's Assurance to Baruch Amid Judgment
24:45.summary: God responds to Baruch's complaint by reminding him that judgment is coming on all the land, and calls him to surrender personal ambitions; yet God promises to preserve Baruch's life as a reward for his faithfulness. The message reorients Baruch from self-pity to acceptance of God's sovereign plan and provision.

24:46.title: Introduction to Prophecies Against the Nations
24:46.summary: Jeremiah receives God's word concerning the nations, specifically introducing a prophecy against Egypt and Pharaoh Necoh's army defeated by Nebuchadnezzar at Carchemish.

24:46.title: The Battle of Carchemish and Egypt's Defeat
24:46.summary: A call to prepare for battle is followed by a vision of Egyptian forces being routed and fleeing in terror before Babylon's king; the day is proclaimed as the Lord's day of vengeance and sacrifice.

24:46.title: Egypt's Incurable Shame
24:46.summary: Egypt is personified as a wounded maiden unable to heal despite medicines, and her humiliation becomes known throughout the nations as mighty warriors fall together.

24:46.title: Proclamation of Nebuchadnezzar's Invasion
24:46.summary: Jeremiah is commanded to declare throughout Egyptian cities that the sword of Babylon approaches; the Egyptians are driven by the Lord and realize Pharaoh is powerless to save them.

24:46.title: The Inexorable Advance of Babylon
24:46.summary: Nebuchadnezzar is compared to great mountains in his inevitable coming; Egypt, a beautiful heifer, shall be devastated as invaders cut through her defenses like woodcutters through a forest, leaving her desolate.

24:46.title: Divine Judgment on Egypt's Gods and Leaders
24:46.summary: The Lord declares judgment on Amon, Pharaoh, Egypt's gods and kings, delivering them into Nebuchadnezzar's hands; yet afterward Egypt shall be restored and inhabited as in ancient days.

24:46.title: Comfort and Hope for Israel
24:46.summary: Despite Egypt's destruction, Israel is assured of God's protection and deliverance from captivity; the Lord will utterly destroy the nations where Israel is scattered but will preserve and correct Israel with measure.

24:47.title: Superscription: Word Concerning the Philistines
24:47.summary: Jeremiah receives God's word of judgment against the Philistines, given before Pharaoh's attack on Gaza.

24:47.title: Vision of Invading Waters and Destruction
24:47.summary: God describes an overwhelming flood of invasion from the north that will devastate Philistine cities and their inhabitants, destroying their allies and remnant. The imagery depicts total military conquest and civilian despair.

24:47.title: Lament over Philistine Cities and God's Sword
24:47.summary: The prophet laments the devastation of Gaza and Ashkelon, then addresses God's sword of judgment, asking when it will cease; God responds that His sword cannot rest because He has commanded it against the Philistine seacoast.

24:48.title: The Destruction of Moab's Cities
24:48.summary: God announces the destruction of Moab's major cities, including Nebo, Kiriathaim, and Heshbon, and calls its people to flee. The judgment is comprehensive and inescapable, with no city spared from the coming devastation.

24:48.title: The Curse on Negligent Judgment and Moab's False Confidence
24:48.summary: God curses those who withhold the sword from bloodshed in executing His judgment, while comparing Moab's complacency and trust in false god Chemosh to Israel's past shame. Moab's settled, undisturbed condition has left it vulnerable to judgment.

24:48.title: The Military Defeat and Humiliation of Moab
24:48.summary: Moab's warriors are defeated and slaughtered; the nation experiences rapid collapse and is broken down. The surrounding nations are called to lament Moab's fall, as the strong nation is reduced to desolation.

24:48.title: Judgment on Moab's Cities and Power
24:48.summary: God's judgment extends to all the cities of Moab, both near and far, and Moab's horn (symbol of power) and arm are broken. The comprehensive nature of the judgment demonstrates God's complete judgment on the nation.

24:48.title: Moab's Pride and Religious Judgment
24:48.summary: God will make Moab drunk in derision for magnifying itself against Yahweh, and will cause wine and agricultural abundance to cease. Moab's religious practices and pride in its gods are brought to nothing.

24:48.title: The Signs and Extent of Moab's Mourning
24:48.summary: All of Moab experiences deep mourning with shaved heads, cut beards, and cuttings—signs of extreme grief covering cities and streets. Moab becomes a broken vessel and a terror to all surrounding nations.

24:48.title: The Final Military Collapse and Inescapable Doom
24:48.summary: God comes against Moab like an eagle; strongholds fall and warriors lose courage. Moab faces inescapable doom through fear, the pit, and snare, with fire consuming the nation from every direction.

24:48.title: The Final Lament and Promise of Restoration
24:48.summary: God laments Moab's destruction and the captivity of its people, but concludes with a promise to restore Moab's captives in the latter days. The judgment is complete, but restoration remains possible through God's ultimate mercy.

24:49.title: Judgment Against Ammon
24:49.summary: Yahweh condemns Ammon for taking Gad's territory and promises to bring war against Rabbah, destroying their cities and scattering their people. Though judgment comes, Yahweh will eventually restore the captives of Ammon.

24:49.title: Judgment Against Edom
24:49.summary: Yahweh denounces Edom's pride and false wisdom, declaring that their land will be completely devastated like Sodom and Gomorrah, with no survivors remaining. Edom's confidence in their mountain fortresses will prove futile against God's judgment.

24:49.title: The Coming Destroyer Against Edom
24:49.summary: God describes a powerful enemy rising up like a lion to attack Edom's strongholds, causing their mighty warriors to become as fearful as women in labor. The destruction will be so complete that the earth trembles at the news.

24:49.title: Judgment Against Damascus
24:49.summary: Damascus is overtaken by panic and sorrow upon hearing the evil news; the city weakens and flees, with its young men falling in the streets and fire consuming its palaces. The city of praise and joy will be abandoned.

24:49.title: Judgment Against Kedar and Hazor
24:49.summary: Nebuchadnezzar of Babylon, acting as God's instrument, is commissioned to destroy the Arabian tribes of Kedar and Hazor, plundering their tents, flocks, and camels, and scattering the people to all winds. Hazor will become a desolate dwelling place of jackals.

24:49.title: Judgment and Future Restoration of Elam
24:49.summary: God will break Elam's military power (their bow), scatter them to the four winds, and bring terror and the sword upon them. Yet in the latter days, God will restore Elam's captives and establish His throne in their land.

24:50.title: The Fall of Babylon Announced
24:50.summary: God's word against Babylon is proclaimed to the nations: Babylon will be taken, her gods will be defeated, and her land will be desolate when an enemy nation comes from the north. This announces the imminent judgment and complete overthrow of the great imperial power.

24:50.title: Israel's Return and Restoration
24:50.summary: In that time, Israel and Judah will return together seeking the Lord and turning toward Zion, entering an everlasting covenant with Him. The passage reflects on Israel as lost sheep scattered by unfaithful shepherds, but now ready to be restored to God's fellowship.

24:50.title: God Marshals Nations Against Babylon
24:50.summary: Israel is called to flee Babylon as God stirs up great nations from the north to destroy her completely; Babylon will become prey and desolation because of her exploitation and pride, with her people scattered and her agriculture destroyed. This depicts the comprehensive military judgment executed by divine will.

24:50.title: Israel's Deliverance and Forgiveness
24:50.summary: Israel, hunted and broken by Assyria and Babylon, will be restored by God to fertile pastures and full satisfaction, and in that day all Israel's iniquities will be pardoned and forgotten. This assures the remnant of complete restoration and divine forgiveness after judgment.

24:50.title: The Destruction of Babylon
24:50.summary: God commands the destruction of Babylon through invading armies, describing her as caught in a snare and broken like a hammer, with her young men falling in her streets and fire consuming her cities; the proud one will stumble and none will raise him up. This lengthy passage emphasizes Babylon's complete and irreversible downfall.

24:50.title: God as Israel's Redeemer and Babylon's Doom
24:50.summary: While Israel and Judah remain oppressed captives, their strong Redeemer (the Lord) will plead their cause and bring rest to the earth by disquieting Babylon's inhabitants; Babylon will be struck by the sword, drought, and desolation, becoming inhabitable like Sodom and Gomorrah. This passage shifts focus between Israel's vindication and Babylon's total ruin.

24:50.title: The Enemy Advance and Babylon's Terror
24:50.summary: A great people and many kings from the earth's uttermost parts come from the north as a fierce, merciless army; the king of Babylon hears of them and his hands grow feeble with anguish, and God will appoint a chosen one to lead the attack, after which the noise of Babylon's fall will shake the earth and be heard among all nations. This concluding section depicts the final terror and capitulation of Babylon before overwhelming divine judgment.

24:51.title: God's Judgment Against Babylon
24:51.summary: The Lord declares he will raise a destroying wind against Babylon and send strangers to plunder it. The archers and warriors of Babylon will be destroyed utterly in the land of the Chaldeans.

24:51.title: Israel's Deliverance and Call to Flee
24:51.summary: Though Israel and Judah have sinned, God has not forsaken them and calls his people to flee Babylon before divine vengeance falls. God promises to vindicate his people and declares that Babylon, once a golden cup of intoxication to the nations, cannot be healed.

24:51.title: The Medes Called to Destroy Babylon
24:51.summary: The Lord stirs up the spirit of the Median kings to destroy Babylon as vengeance for the defiling of his temple. God swears he will fill Babylon with warriors and bring about its destruction.

24:51.title: God's Power and Idolatry's Futility
24:51.summary: A hymn celebrates God's omnipotent creation and control over nature, contrasting his eternal power with the worthlessness of idols that are mere human delusions. Jacob's inheritance stands eternally secure in the Lord of Armies.

24:51.title: The Instrument of God's Judgment
24:51.summary: God addresses his people as his battle axe and weapon of war, through whom he will break in pieces nations, kingdoms, horses, chariots, and all people. God will render to Babylon all the evil it has done to Zion.

24:51.title: Babylon's Complete Destruction
24:51.summary: God declares he is against Babylon, the destroying mountain, and will roll it down from the rocks and burn it. Not a single stone shall be taken from Babylon for building; it shall remain desolate forever.

24:51.title: The Nations Gathered Against Babylon
24:51.summary: A call goes out to prepare the nations and kingdoms of Ararat, Minni, and Ashkenaz, along with Median governors, to march against Babylon. The land trembles as God's purposes against Babylon are set to make it utterly desolate.

24:51.title: Babylon's Swift Collapse
24:51.summary: Babylon's mighty men lose heart and remain in their strongholds as their strength fails. Messengers run throughout the city reporting that it has been taken on every side, the gates are seized, and the warriors are terrified.

24:51.title: Babylon Likened to a Threshing Floor
24:51.summary: God compares Babylon to a threshing floor about to be trodden, and the inhabitants of Zion and Jerusalem call for God's vengeance on Babylon for the violence, devourment, and humiliation of God's people by Nebuchadnezzar.

24:51.title: God's Vengeance on Babylon
24:51.summary: The Lord promises to plead Israel's cause and take vengeance by drying up Babylon's sea and springs. Babylon will become desolate heaps and a den of jackals; God will intoxicate its people into perpetual sleep and lead them like lambs to slaughter.

24:51.title: Babylon's Final Ruin
24:51.summary: The fall of mighty Babylon, once praised throughout the earth, is lamented as the sea covers it and its cities become desolate wastelands where neither man nor animal dwells.

24:51.title: Judgment on Babylon's Gods and Vindication of Israel
24:51.summary: God will execute judgment on the idol Bel and force it to disgorge what it has swallowed; the wall of Babylon shall fall. The heavens and earth will sing for joy over Babylon's destruction as the slain of all lands fall at Babylon in recompense for the slain of Israel.

24:51.title: Call to Remember and Lament Over the Temple
24:51.summary: God's people who have escaped are called to flee, remember the Lord from afar, and bring Jerusalem to mind. They confess their shame at hearing reproach and having strangers enter God's sanctuaries, yet God promises judgment on Babylon's idols and the groaning of the wounded throughout her land.

24:51.title: No Escape from God's Judgment
24:51.summary: Though Babylon would mount to the sky and fortify itself, destroyers shall come from God. The sound of great destruction fills the land as the Lord lays Babylon waste, intoxicates her princes and wise men into perpetual sleep, and overthrows her broad walls and high gates, rendering all the nations' labor for naught.

24:51.title: Jeremiah's Symbolic Act Against Babylon
24:51.summary: Jeremiah commands Seraiah, a chief quartermaster, to read aloud all the words of judgment against Babylon written in a book, then bind a stone to it and cast it into the Euphrates River while declaring that Babylon shall sink and not rise again because of the evil God will bring upon it.

24:52.title: Zedekiah's Reign and Evil
24:52.summary: Zedekiah becomes king at age twenty-one and reigns for eleven years in Jerusalem, but does evil like Jehoiakim before him. His wickedness causes God to cast Judah out from His presence, and Zedekiah rebels against the king of Babylon.

24:52.title: The Fall of Jerusalem and Capture of Zedekiah
24:52.summary: Nebuchadnezzar besieges Jerusalem for over two years; when the city walls are breached, Zedekiah flees but is captured, brought before the Babylonian king, forced to watch his sons and princes executed, blinded, and imprisoned in Babylon until his death.

24:52.title: Destruction of the Temple and Its Furnishings
24:52.summary: Nebuzaradan, captain of the Babylonian guard, burns the temple, the royal palace, and all the houses of Jerusalem, then systematically strips and demolishes the temple's bronze and precious metal vessels, pillars, and decorative elements, cataloging their dimensions and ornamentation.

24:52.title: Execution of Judah's Leaders
24:52.summary: Nebuzaradan captures the chief priest, second priest, temple officers, royal counselors, scribes, and other officials from Jerusalem, brings them to Riblah, and executes them all, marking the complete political and religious collapse of Judah.

24:52.title: Census of the Captive Jews
24:52.summary: A detailed accounting records the number of Jews carried into captivity by Nebuchadnezzar across multiple deportations, totaling 4,600 people removed from the land over successive years.

24:52.title: Release and Favor of Jehoiachin
24:52.summary: In his first year, the new Babylonian king Evilmerodach releases the imprisoned Judean king Jehoiachin from prison, treats him kindly, elevates him above other captive kings, and provides him a daily allowance for the rest of his life.

25:1.title: Jerusalem's Desolation and Abandonment
25:1.summary: Jerusalem, once great and full of people, now sits alone like a widow, weeping bitterly with no one to comfort her as all her lovers and friends have become her enemies and betrayed her.

25:1.title: Judah's Captivity and Suffering
25:1.summary: Judah has been taken into captivity due to affliction and servitude, finding no rest among the nations; the city's religious and social life has ceased, and her leaders flee without strength before their pursuers.

25:1.title: Jerusalem's Sin and Shame
25:1.summary: Jerusalem remembers her former pleasures while experiencing present misery, and the speaker recognizes that her grievous sin has made her unclean and brought about her shameful downfall with no comforter.

25:1.title: Desecration and Desperate Hunger
25:1.summary: The adversary has plundered all of Jerusalem's treasures and defiled her sanctuary, while her desperate people trade their valuables for bread to survive.

25:1.title: The Speaker's Cry of Anguish and God's Judgment
25:1.summary: The speaker appeals to passersby, asking if any sorrow compares to hers, describing how God has sent affliction like fire, bound her with the yoke of transgression, and delivered her to enemies and destruction.

25:1.title: Weeping, Isolation, and Acknowledgment of Righteousness
25:1.summary: The speaker weeps for her afflicted children and the loss of comfort, while acknowledging that Yahweh is righteous and her own rebellion against His commandment has brought this desolation upon her people.

25:1.title: False Hope, Final Lament, and Imprecation
25:1.summary: The speaker laments how her allies deceived her and leaders died while seeking food, expresses distress over rebellion and sword violence, and calls upon God to bring judgment upon her enemies as He has upon her for her transgressions.

25:2.title: God's Judgment Upon Zion and Jerusalem
25:2.summary: The Lord, in anger, has destroyed the beauty and strongholds of Israel and Judah, treating Jerusalem as an enemy. He has defiled the sanctuary, broken down the walls and gates, and allowed the king and princes to be scattered among nations without divine guidance.

25:2.title: The Suffering and Mourning of Zion's People
25:2.summary: The elders and virgins of Jerusalem sit in silent mourning, covered in dust and sackcloth, while children and infants starve in the streets, crying to their mothers for food that cannot be found.

25:2.title: Jerusalem's Desolation and the Enemy's Triumph
25:2.summary: Jerusalem's wounds are beyond healing, her prophets have given false visions and failed to expose her sin, and all who pass by mock her ruin. The Lord has fulfilled his judgment, allowing enemies to rejoice and triumph over the once-beautiful city.

25:2.title: A Call to Urgent Intercession and Lament
25:2.summary: The walls of Zion are called to cry out to the Lord day and night, and the people are urged to pour out their hearts in prayer for their starving children. The passage concludes by asking God to witness the complete devastation—priests and prophets slain, young and old dead in the streets, and those the speaker raised consumed by enemies.

25:3.title: The Speaker's Affliction and Despair
25:3.summary: A suffering individual describes the overwhelming afflictions God has brought upon him—physical torment, imprisonment, mockery, and emotional devastation. The passage depicts total desolation and loss of hope in God's mercy.

25:3.title: The Turn to Hope and God's Compassion
25:3.summary: The speaker recalls God's steadfast love and new mercies each morning, finding hope in God's faithfulness despite present suffering. The passage reflects that affliction is not God's desire and calls the sufferer to patient endurance and submission.

25:3.title: Communal Confession and Continued Lament
25:3.summary: The community confesses its transgressions and calls for repentance while lamenting God's apparent abandonment, anger, and refusal to hear prayers. The speaker weeps over the destruction and suffering of the people.

25:3.title: Personal Persecution and Appeal for Divine Judgment
25:3.summary: The speaker recounts relentless persecution and mortal danger from enemies, then appeals to God who has already heard and redeemed him. The passage concludes with a request that God execute judgment against the enemies' malice.

25:4.title: The Degradation of Zion's Precious Ones
25:4.summary: The gold of Jerusalem's sanctity has become dim and the precious sons of Zion, once comparable to fine gold, are now esteemed as cheap earthen vessels. This opening lament establishes the shocking contrast between what Zion once was and its present degraded state.

25:4.title: The Horrors of Siege and Famine
25:4.summary: The people of Zion suffer devastating famine and hunger during the siege, with nursing children dying of thirst, the once-delicate nobility wasting away, and even desperate mothers consuming their own children. This section depicts the extreme physical suffering and moral degradation resulting from Jerusalem's destruction.

25:4.title: Yahweh's Wrath Executed Through Enemy and Sin
25:4.summary: Yahweh's fierce anger has been poured out on Zion through fire and the invading enemy, a calamity unbelieved by the nations and caused by the sins of corrupt prophets and priests who shed innocent blood. The priests themselves are now scattered and defiled, rejected even by other nations.

25:4.title: Futile Hope and the Fall of the Anointed King
25:4.summary: Jerusalem's people watched in vain for help from a nation that could not save them, while relentless pursuers hunted them down and captured their king—the anointed of Yahweh—in whom they had hoped to find refuge. The collapse of their last earthly hope marks the completion of their desolation.

25:4.title: Judgment on Edom and Restoration Promise for Zion
25:4.summary: Edom is warned that the cup of divine punishment will pass to her as well, while Zion's punishment is declared complete and her captivity ended, with a promise that Yahweh will visit and uncover Edom's sins. The passage moves from Zion's suffering to assurance of restoration and Edom's future judgment.

25:5.title: A Plea for Divine Remembrance of Judah's Suffering
25:5.summary: The community cries out to God, reminding Him of their humiliation and loss—their land taken by foreigners, their families broken, their resources depleted, and their subjection to foreign powers. They acknowledge that their fathers' sins have been passed down to them, and they bear the consequences of ancestral iniquity.

25:5.title: The Depths of National Degradation and Mourning
25:5.summary: The lament catalogs the complete reversal of social order and joy in the community: servants now rule over them, people risk their lives for food, physical suffering from famine is evident, women are violated, princes are executed, young people are enslaved to hard labor, and all celebration has ceased into mourning. Life has become characterized by terror, exhaustion, and the absence of hope.

25:5.title: Confession of Sin and Desolation of Zion
25:5.summary: The community acknowledges their own sinfulness as the cause of their calamity—the crown of their glory has fallen and Zion, once a holy mountain, now lies desolate and abandoned. Their shame is complete, and their sacred place is profaned.

25:5.title: Appeal for Restoration and Lament of Divine Rejection
25:5.summary: The prayer contrasts God's eternal, unchanging nature with His apparent abandonment of His people, asking why He has forgotten and forsaken them for so long. The final plea is for restoration and renewal, yet it ends with the stark acknowledgment that God has utterly rejected them and remains consumed with anger—leaving the lament unresolved.

26:1.title: The Call of Ezekiel
26:1.summary: In the thirtieth year of his life, during the fifth year of King Jehoiachin's captivity by the river Chebar, Ezekiel the priest experiences a vision when the heavens open and God's hand comes upon him. This marks the beginning of his prophetic calling among the exiled people in Babylon.

26:1.title: The Four Living Creatures
26:1.summary: Ezekiel sees four living creatures emerging from a stormy wind and great cloud, each with four faces (man, lion, ox, eagle), four wings, and human hands. They move in perfect unity with divine purpose, their appearance radiant like burning fire and lightning.

26:1.title: The Wheels Within Wheels
26:1.summary: Ezekiel sees wheels beside each of the four living creatures, each wheel having a wheel within it and rims covered with eyes. The wheels move in perfect coordination with the living creatures, lifted up and directed by the same spirit that moves the creatures.

26:1.title: The Expanse Above the Creatures
26:1.summary: Above the living creatures is an expanse like crystal, beneath which the creatures spread their wings. The sound of their wings is like the voice of the Almighty and the noise of a great army, and a voice speaks from above the expanse.

26:1.title: The Throne of God and the Glory of the Lord
26:1.summary: Above the expanse sits a throne of sapphire with a figure in human form surrounded by glowing fire and radiance like a rainbow. Ezekiel recognizes this as the appearance of the glory of Yahweh and falls on his face, hearing a voice speak to him.

26:2.title: Ezekiel Called to Stand and Receive God's Word
26:2.summary: God commands Ezekiel to stand on his feet, and the Spirit enters him, empowering him to hear God's message. This initiates Ezekiel's prophetic commissioning and establishes his readiness to receive divine instruction.

26:2.title: Ezekiel Sent to Rebellious Israel
26:2.summary: God sends Ezekiel to the rebellious house of Israel, a nation that has continuously transgressed against God from their fathers' time until the present. Regardless of whether they listen or refuse, they will know a prophet has been among them.

26:2.title: Courage in the Face of Opposition
26:2.summary: God instructs Ezekiel not to fear the rebellious house despite their harsh opposition, comparing their hostility to briers, thorns, and scorpions. Ezekiel must faithfully speak God's words whether the people listen or refuse.

26:2.title: Consuming the Scroll of Lamentations
26:2.summary: God commands Ezekiel to eat a scroll put forth by a divine hand, which is written inside and out with words of lamentation, mourning, and woe. This symbolic act represents Ezekiel's complete internalization of God's difficult message to deliver.

26:3.title: Ezekiel Eats the Scroll
26:3.summary: God commands Ezekiel to eat a scroll, which tastes sweet like honey in his mouth. This symbolic act represents Ezekiel's internalization of God's word before he speaks it to Israel.

26:3.title: Ezekiel's Commission to Speak to Israel
26:3.summary: God sends Ezekiel to speak His words to the house of Israel, assuring him that despite Israel's obstinacy and hardheartedness, his message will be heard. God hardens Ezekiel's face to give him courage against their resistance and commands him to receive God's words in his heart.

26:3.title: The Spirit Lifts Ezekiel and He Comes to the Exiles
26:3.summary: The Spirit of God lifts Ezekiel up, and he hears the glory of Yahweh and the sound of the heavenly creatures. Ezekiel travels in bitterness and arrives at Tel Aviv by the river Chebar where the Israelite captives live, and he sits overwhelmed among them for seven days.

26:3.title: Ezekiel Appointed as a Watchman
26:3.summary: God appoints Ezekiel as a watchman over Israel with the responsibility to warn both the wicked of their impending death and the righteous against turning from righteousness. Ezekiel's obedience in delivering warnings saves his own soul, while failure to warn makes him responsible for others' deaths.

26:3.title: God's Glory Appears and Ezekiel Receives Further Instructions
26:3.summary: God's glory appears to Ezekiel in the plain, and after he falls on his face, the Spirit empowers him to stand. God commands Ezekiel to shut himself inside his house and makes his tongue stick to his mouth so he cannot speak—except when God opens his mouth to deliver His word, emphasizing that only God's message, not Ezekiel's own words, matters.

26:4.title: The Siege of Jerusalem Symbolized
26:4.summary: Ezekiel is commanded to draw Jerusalem on a tile and enact a siege against it using an iron pan as a wall, creating a prophetic sign for Israel that demonstrates the coming judgment through visual representation.

26:4.title: Bearing the Iniquity of Israel and Judah
26:4.summary: Ezekiel must lie on his left side for 390 days bearing Israel's iniquity, then on his right side for 40 days bearing Judah's iniquity, symbolically acting out the weight and duration of their exile while remaining immobilized throughout.

26:4.title: Defiled Bread as a Sign of Captivity
26:4.summary: Ezekiel is commanded to eat limited rations of mixed grain bread baked with human dung, symbolizing how Israel will eat defiled food in exile; when he protests his ritual purity, God allows cow dung as a compromise, yet the defilement remains a sign of their degradation.

26:4.title: Famine and Despair in Jerusalem
26:4.summary: God declares He will break the staff of bread in Jerusalem, causing inhabitants to eat rationed food with fear and despair, ultimately leading to starvation, fear, and spiritual ruin as they waste away in their iniquity.

26:5.title: The Symbolic Act of the Divided Hair
26:5.summary: Ezekiel is commanded to shave his head and beard, then divide the hair into three parts to be burned, struck with a sword, and scattered to the wind—symbolic actions representing Jerusalem's coming destruction. A small remnant is bound in his skirts, with some of that remnant also cast into fire, signifying judgment that will spread throughout Israel.

26:5.title: Jerusalem's Wickedness and Unique Punishment
26:5.summary: God explains that Jerusalem, set in the center of nations, has rebelled against His ordinances more wickedly than surrounding nations and rejected His statutes. Because of her turbulence and abominations, God declares He will execute unprecedented judgments against her that exceed anything He has done before.

26:5.title: The Decree of Famine, Sword, and Scattering
26:5.summary: The specific judgments are enumerated: cannibalism will occur as fathers eat sons and sons eat fathers, a third will die by pestilence, a third by the sword, and a third will be scattered to the winds with the sword pursuing them. God's judgments will affect the whole remnant of Jerusalem's population.

26:5.title: The Completion of God's Wrath and Ultimate Desolation
26:5.summary: God's anger will be fully accomplished and His wrath satisfied when Jerusalem knows that the Lord has spoken in zeal; the city will become a desolation and reproach among nations. Multiple plagues—famine, evil beasts, pestilence, bloodshed, and the sword—will be sent as instruments of destruction until the judgment is complete.

26:6.title: The Prophet's Commission to Prophesy Against the Mountains
26:6.summary: God commands Ezekiel to turn his attention toward the mountains of Israel and prophesy judgment against them. This sets the stage for the pronouncement of God's coming judgment on the land.

26:6.title: Judgment on Israel's Idolatrous Worship Sites
26:6.summary: God announces that He will bring a sword against the mountains and destroy Israel's high places, altars, and idols, with the dead bodies scattered among them as testimony to His power. The survivors will recognize through this devastation that Yahweh is God.

26:6.title: The Remnant's Repentance in Exile
26:6.summary: Despite the judgment, God will preserve a remnant who escape the sword and are scattered among the nations, where they will remember God's faithfulness and their own unfaithfulness, loathing their idolatry and recognizing that God's judgment was not spoken in vain.

26:6.title: The Threefold Judgment and Desolation of the Land
26:6.summary: God commands signs of mourning and declares that judgment will come through sword, famine, and pestilence affecting all people regardless of distance, followed by the complete desolation of the land from the wilderness to Diblah. All will recognize through this comprehensive judgment that Yahweh is God.

26:7.title: The End Has Come Upon the Land
26:7.summary: God announces through Ezekiel that the final judgment has arrived for Israel. He will execute wrath upon the land according to their sinful ways and abominations, showing no mercy or pity, so that they will recognize His divine authority.

26:7.title: Reiteration of Inevitable Judgment
26:7.summary: The Lord emphasizes the certainty and severity of the coming judgment, repeating that calamity is imminent and will strike Israel according to their wicked ways. The judgment is unavoidable and executed without divine compassion.

26:7.title: The Day of Reckoning and Economic Collapse
26:7.summary: The day of judgment arrives with violence and wickedness blossoming into a rod of judgment. Economic life ceases as the trumpet sounds for war, but no one goes to battle because God's wrath has seized the entire community.

26:7.title: The Devastation of Siege and Suffering
26:7.summary: The judgment manifests through sword, famine, and pestilence that destroy those in field and city alike. The few survivors mourn in despair, physically weakened and dressed in sackcloth, covered in shame and horror.

26:7.title: The Uselessness of Wealth and Defilement of the Temple
26:7.summary: Israel's wealth—silver and gold—becomes worthless and cannot save them from God's wrath, having been a stumbling block to their sin. The temple's beauty, perverted into idolatry, becomes unclean, and God abandons it to be profaned by foreign invaders.

26:7.title: The Conquest by Foreign Nations and Collapse of Leadership
26:7.summary: Because the land is filled with violence and bloodshed, God brings the worst of the nations to conquer Israel, ending their pride and profaning their holy places. Desperate pleas for peace and prophetic guidance go unanswered, and leadership mourns in desolation as God judges them according to their own ways.

26:8.title: Ezekiel's Vision of God's Glory in Jerusalem
26:8.summary: In the sixth year, while elders of Judah sit before him, the Lord's hand falls upon Ezekiel and transports him in a vision to Jerusalem's temple. He sees the glory of God appearing as fire and brightness, the same manifestation he witnessed on the plain.

26:8.title: The Image of Jealousy at the Temple Gate
26:8.summary: God directs Ezekiel to observe an idol of jealousy placed at the temple's north gate entrance, asking if he sees the great abominations Israel commits that cause God to depart from his sanctuary.

26:8.title: Hidden Idolatry in Secret Chambers
26:8.summary: God leads Ezekiel to a concealed door in the temple wall, revealing a hidden chamber where seventy elders, including Jaazaniah, worship abominable creatures and idols in darkness, believing God cannot see them or has abandoned the land.

26:8.title: Women Weeping for Tammuz
26:8.summary: God brings Ezekiel to the temple's north gate where women weep for Tammuz, a pagan fertility god, representing yet another layer of abominable idolatry within Israel's worship.

26:8.title: Sun Worship in the Inner Court
26:8.summary: In the temple's inner court, Ezekiel witnesses about twenty-five men with their backs to God's temple, worshipping the sun toward the east—a profound defiance that fills the land with violence and provokes God's anger.

26:8.title: God's Promise of Judgment Without Mercy
26:8.summary: God declares he will deal with these abominations in wrath, showing neither mercy nor pity, and will not hear the cries of those who call out to him.

26:9.title: The Vision of Six Destroyers and the Man with the Inkhorn
26:9.summary: God summons six armed men to execute judgment on Jerusalem, accompanied by a seventh man clothed in linen with a writer's inkhorn. God's glory moves from the cherubim to the temple threshold as He calls to the man with the inkhorn.

26:9.title: The Marking and Slaughter of Jerusalem
26:9.summary: God instructs the man with the inkhorn to mark the foreheads of those who grieve over Jerusalem's abominations, while commanding the six destroyers to kill all others without mercy, beginning at God's sanctuary. The destroyers execute this judgment throughout the city.

26:9.title: Ezekiel's Intercession and God's Final Response
26:9.summary: Ezekiel falls on his face in anguish, pleading whether God will destroy all Israel, but God responds that Israel's sin is extreme and deserves judgment without pity. The man with the inkhorn reports that he has completed the marking as commanded.

26:10.title: The Glory of God and the Command to Scatter Fire
26:10.summary: Ezekiel sees the sapphire throne above the cherubim, and God commands a man clothed in linen to take coals of fire from between the cherubim and scatter them over the city as a sign of judgment. The divine glory fills the temple as this command is executed.

26:10.title: Description of the Cherubim and Wheels
26:10.summary: Ezekiel provides detailed description of the cherubim's form—including human-like hands under their wings, four faces each (cherub, man, lion, eagle), and the four wheels beside them covered with eyes. The wheels move in perfect coordination with the cherubim, guided by the spirit dwelling within them.

26:10.title: The Departure of God's Glory
26:10.summary: God's glory departs from the temple threshold and stands above the cherubim, who lift up their wings and rise from the earth with the wheels beside them, moving to the east gate of the temple. Ezekiel confirms these are the same living creatures he saw by the river Chebar.

26:11.title: Vision of the Wicked Counselors
26:11.summary: Ezekiel is transported to the east gate of the temple where he sees twenty-five men, including leaders like Jaazaniah and Pelatiah, who are devising wickedness and giving corrupt counsel in Jerusalem.

26:11.title: Prophecy Against the Princes of Jerusalem
26:11.summary: God commands Ezekiel to prophesy judgment against these wicked leaders, declaring that the city will not protect them as a caldron protects flesh, and they will face the sword at Israel's borders for abandoning God's statutes and following the ordinances of surrounding nations.

26:11.title: Death of Pelatiah and Ezekiel's Intercession
26:11.summary: As Ezekiel prophesies, Pelatiah dies, prompting Ezekiel to fall on his face and cry out to God, fearing complete destruction of Israel's remnant, while God indicates that the exiles themselves are being rejected by Jerusalem's inhabitants.

26:11.title: Promise of Restoration and Renewal
26:11.summary: God promises to gather Israel from exile, restore them to their land, and give them a new heart and spirit to obey His statutes, while those who persist in abomination will face judgment.

26:11.title: Departure of God's Glory and Return to the Exiles
26:11.summary: The glory of God departs from Jerusalem, ascending above the cherubim and standing on the mountain east of the city, after which the Spirit transports Ezekiel back to the captives in Babylon where he recounts all that God has shown him.

26:12.title: The Sign of Exile: Ezekiel's Symbolic Moving
26:12.summary: God commands Ezekiel to perform a symbolic act—packing and moving his belongings by day and exiting through a wall at night—to signify to the rebellious house of Israel that exile is coming. Ezekiel obeys the command, enacting the prophecy before the people's eyes.

26:12.title: Interpretation: The Prince's Exile and Scattering
26:12.summary: God explains that the symbolic act signifies the prince of Jerusalem and the house of Israel will go into exile in Babylon, unable to see the land they're fleeing. God will scatter them among nations, yet preserve a remnant who will declare God's judgment and know that Yahweh is Lord.

26:12.title: The Sign of Anxious Eating: Coming Desolation
26:12.summary: God commands Ezekiel to eat and drink with trembling and fear, symbolizing that Jerusalem's inhabitants will consume their food in dread as the land becomes desolate due to violence. Through this sign, the people will know that Yahweh is Lord.

26:12.title: The Proverb Reversed: Vision's Fulfillment at Hand
26:12.summary: God confronts the Israelite proverb that 'visions fail and days are prolonged,' declaring He will make this proverb cease because His word will be performed without delay. Every vision will be fulfilled in the rebellious house's own days.

26:12.title: Final Word: No More Deferral
26:12.summary: God reiterates His response to those claiming His visions concern a distant future, declaring that none of His words will be deferred any longer—what He speaks will be performed immediately.

26:13.title: Judgment Against False Prophets of Israel
26:13.summary: Ezekiel is commanded to prophesy against the false prophets of Israel who speak from their own hearts rather than from God's word. These prophets are like foxes in ruins, failing to strengthen Israel's defenses, and God declares His hand will be against them, removing them from His people and the land.

26:13.title: The Whitewashed Wall Parable
26:13.summary: Through the metaphor of a flimsy wall plastered with whitewash, Ezekiel depicts how false prophets mislead Israel by promising peace when there is no peace, and God will destroy this false security with overwhelming judgment. The prophets who prophesy deceptive visions of peace for Jerusalem will be exposed and consumed.

26:13.title: Judgment Against False Prophetesses
26:13.summary: Ezekiel is commanded to prophesy against women who prophesy from their own hearts and use magical practices (sewing pillows and making kerchiefs) to spiritually enslave people for personal gain. God will tear away their instruments of deception, rescue His people from their manipulation, and end their false visions.

26:14.title: Idolatry and False Prophecy
26:14.summary: Elders come to Ezekiel seeking God's word, but the Lord reveals they harbor idols in their hearts. God warns that He will answer such idolaters according to their idolatry, and declares judgment on false prophets who deceive them, so that Israel may return from idolatry and become God's people.

26:14.title: The Four Judgments and the Righteous Remnant
26:14.summary: God illustrates that even the righteousness of Noah, Daniel, and Job could not save a land under judgment; in famine, wild animals, sword, and pestilence, such righteous ones would save only themselves, not their families or the land. This demonstrates the severity and inescapability of God's judgment on sin.

26:14.title: Jerusalem's Judgment and Consoling Remnant
26:14.summary: God announces that Jerusalem will face all four judgments—sword, famine, wild animals, and pestilence—yet a remnant of sons and daughters will be spared and brought forth as a sign that God's judgment is righteous and not arbitrary. Their conduct will ultimately comfort those who witness it and vindicate God's purposes.

26:15.title: The Parable of the Useless Vine
26:15.summary: God asks Ezekiel to consider the vine tree: it is worthless as timber, unsuitable for any work even when whole, and completely useless once burned. The parable establishes that a vine's only value is in bearing fruit, not in its wood.

26:15.title: Jerusalem Compared to a Burned Vine
26:15.summary: God applies the parable to Jerusalem's inhabitants, declaring He will give them to the fire as fuel; those who escape the fire will still be consumed by it, and the land will become desolate because of their trespass. The judgment is absolute: like a burned vine, Jerusalem will be rendered completely useless and destroyed.

26:16.title: Command to Declare Jerusalem's Abominations
26:16.summary: God commands Ezekiel to confront Jerusalem with knowledge of her abominable deeds. This serves as the introduction to an extended allegory of Jerusalem's unfaithfulness.

26:16.title: The Allegory of Jerusalem's Foundling Origins and God's Covenant Care
26:16.summary: God depicts Jerusalem as an abandoned newborn of foreign (Canaanite, Amorite, Hittite) ancestry, left to die in blood until God passed by, rescued her, made a covenant with her, and lavished her with beauty, wealth, and royal status. This establishes the depth of God's unmerited grace and Jerusalem's obligation to faithfulness.

26:16.title: Jerusalem's Spiritual Prostitution and Idolatry
26:16.summary: Despite God's gifts, Jerusalem trusted in her beauty and engaged in widespread prostitution—idolatry with foreign nations (Egypt, Assyria, Chaldea) and with idols—even sacrificing her children and misusing God's provision for pagan worship. She became more debased than common prostitutes, actively pursuing forbidden alliances rather than being paid for them.

26:16.title: God's Judgment: Exposure, Humiliation, and Destruction
26:16.summary: God pronounces judgment: He will gather all of Jerusalem's lovers and expose her nakedness to them, judge her as one who commits adultery and murder, have them strip her of all her finery, stone and sword her, burn her houses, and execute public judgment—thus ending her prostitution and exhausting His wrath. Her punishment stems from her failure to remember God's mercy in her youth.

26:16.title: Comparison with Samaria and Sodom: Jerusalem's Exceptional Wickedness
26:16.summary: Jerusalem is compared unfavorably to her sisters Samaria and Sodom, being told she is worse than both; even Sodom, destroyed for pride and neglect of the poor, did not commit the abominations Jerusalem did. By her sins, Jerusalem has actually justified her wicked sisters, and she must bear her shame.

26:16.title: God's Ultimate Restoration and Covenant Renewal
26:16.summary: Though Jerusalem will bear her shame while her sisters are restored, God will remember His everlasting covenant with her from her youth, establish it forever, and restore her—along with her sisters—so that she may remember her ways, be ashamed, and know that God has forgiven her. This promise moves beyond judgment to mercy and restoration.

26:17.title: Commission to Speak a Riddle
26:17.summary: God commands Ezekiel to present a riddle and parable to the house of Israel. This introduction frames the prophetic message that follows.

26:17.title: The Parable of the Two Eagles and the Vine
26:17.summary: A great eagle takes the top of a cedar and plants its seed, which grows into a spreading vine; but another great eagle draws the vine toward itself, raising the question of whether it will prosper or wither. The parable illustrates the covenant relationship between Babylon and Judah's king, and the consequences of seeking another power's protection.

26:17.title: The Interpretation: Judah's Rebellion and Judgment
26:17.summary: God reveals that the parable refers to Nebuchadnezzar of Babylon taking Judah's king to Babylon and establishing a covenant, which the king then breaks by seeking Egypt's alliance; God swears the king will die in Babylon and cannot escape judgment, and his followers will be scattered. This passage condemns the king's oath-breaking and declares God's inevitable judgment.

26:17.title: Promise of Restoration and God's Dominion
26:17.summary: God promises to take a tender shoot from the high cedar and plant it on a lofty mountain in Israel, where it will flourish as a great tree sheltering all birds, demonstrating that God humbles the proud and exalts the humble. This concludes the passage with a reversal: judgment gives way to restoration and universal recognition of God's power.

26:18.title: Rejection of the Proverb About Fathers and Children
26:18.summary: God rejects the Israelite proverb that children suffer for their fathers' sins, declaring instead that every soul belongs to Him and that the individual soul who sins shall die. This establishes the principle of personal accountability before God.

26:18.title: The Righteous Person Shall Live
26:18.summary: God describes the characteristics of a just person: one who avoids idolatry and sexual sin, treats neighbors fairly, practices restitution and generosity, conducts honest business, and keeps God's statutes. Such a person shall surely live.

26:18.title: The Wicked Son of a Righteous Father
26:18.summary: If a righteous man's son becomes a violent criminal who commits all forms of wickedness—idolatry, sexual sin, robbery, and exploitation—that wicked son shall surely die for his own abominations, regardless of his father's righteousness.

26:18.title: The Righteous Grandson of a Wicked Father
26:18.summary: If the wicked man's son observes his father's sins, fears God, and instead practices righteousness and obedience, he shall live and not die for his father's iniquity. The wicked father, however, shall die for his own cruelty and oppression.

26:18.title: The Principle of Individual Judgment
26:18.summary: God clarifies that each soul bears only its own moral weight: the righteous person's righteousness belongs to him alone, and the wicked person's wickedness belongs to him alone. Neither child nor parent can inherit the other's moral standing.

26:18.title: The Wicked May Repent and Live
26:18.summary: If a wicked person repents from all his sins, keeps God's statutes, and does what is lawful and right, he shall surely live and his transgressions will not be remembered. God takes no pleasure in the death of the wicked but desires their repentance and life.

26:18.title: The Righteous May Fall and Die; God's Ways Are Just
26:18.summary: Conversely, if a righteous person turns from righteousness and commits wickedness, he shall die in his sin and his former righteous deeds will not be remembered. God responds to Israel's complaint that His way is unjust by declaring that His ways are equal and Israel's ways are unequal.

26:18.title: Call to Repentance and Life
26:18.summary: God calls Israel to turn from all transgressions, cast away their iniquities, and make themselves a new heart and new spirit, for God has no pleasure in death but desires that they turn and live. This is God's final exhortation to the people to choose life through repentance.

26:19.title: Introduction to the Lamentation
26:19.summary: Ezekiel is commanded to compose a lamentation (funeral song) specifically for the princes of Israel, setting the context for the poetic judgment that follows.

26:19.title: The Parable of the Lioness and Her Cubs
26:19.summary: Israel is depicted as a lioness who raised two lion cubs representing successive kings; both became violent and destructive, and both were captured and taken into exile—a metaphor for Israel's failed leadership and subsequent judgment by foreign powers.

26:19.title: The Parable of the Vine
26:19.summary: Israel's mother is described as a fruitful vine planted by waters with strong branches fit for rulers' scepters, but she was uprooted in God's fury, burned, replanted in a barren wilderness, and consumed by fire—leaving no strong rod to rule, completing the lamentation for Israel's destruction.

26:20.title: The Elders Come to Inquire; God Refuses
26:20.summary: Elders of Israel come to seek God's guidance, but Yahweh refuses to be inquired of by them, declaring this through Ezekiel the prophet.

26:20.title: Recital of Israel's Rebellions from Egypt to Canaan
26:20.summary: God commands Ezekiel to confront the elders by rehearsing Israel's pattern of disobedience: rebellion in Egypt despite God's covenant promises, rejection of the law and Sabbaths in the wilderness despite judgment warnings, and continued idolatry in the land—yet God acted for His name's sake and gave hardening statutes so they might know Him.

26:20.title: Contemporary Idolatry and Profanation in the Land
26:20.summary: Ezekiel accuses Israel of continuing their fathers' blasphemy by offering sacrifices at high places, defiling themselves with idols, and sacrificing their children to fire; God declares He will not be inquired of by them.

26:20.title: God's Promise of Judgment and Restoration
26:20.summary: God rejects Israel's desire to assimilate with pagan nations and vows to rule over them with mighty power, gathering them from exile, judging them in the wilderness, purging out rebels, and ultimately sanctifying His name through their restoration and obedience.

26:20.title: Future Worship and Reconciliation on God's Holy Mountain
26:20.summary: In the land of Israel, all the people will serve God acceptably; He will be glorified before the nations as He gathers His people, and they will remember and loathe their past sins while knowing that God acted for His name's sake, not their merit.

26:20.title: Prophecy Against the Forest of the South
26:20.summary: Yahweh commands Ezekiel to prophesy toward the Negev that a fire will consume all trees there—both green and dry—as a sign of God's judgment that all flesh will witness; Ezekiel then laments that people call him a speaker of parables.

26:21.title: Prophecy Against Israel and the Sign of the Prophet's Sighing
26:21.summary: God commands Ezekiel to prophesy against Jerusalem and the land of Israel, declaring that His sword will cut off both righteous and wicked. The prophet is instructed to sigh before the people as a sign of the coming judgment that will cause universal terror and despair.

26:21.title: The Song of the Sword
26:21.summary: Ezekiel prophesies about God's sword being sharpened and polished for slaughter, which will fall on Israel's princes and people. The prophet is commanded to cry out and strike his hands together, symbolizing the certainty and severity of God's judgment against all the land.

26:21.title: The King of Babylon's Judgment and the End of Judah's Monarchy
26:21.summary: God instructs Ezekiel to depict two paths for Babylon's sword—one to Jerusalem and one to Ammon—showing how the king of Babylon will divine his course and choose Jerusalem for destruction. God declares the end of Judah's kingship, overturning the throne until the coming of the one to whom it rightfully belongs.

26:21.title: Prophecy Against the Ammonites
26:21.summary: Ezekiel prophesies against the Ammonites, whose false prophets give them empty hopes, warning them of the sword drawn for slaughter. God declares judgment upon them in their land of birth, pouring out His indignation and delivering them to destruction, ensuring they will be remembered no more.

26:22.title: The Bloody City Indicted
26:22.summary: God commands Ezekiel to judge Jerusalem, the city that sheds blood and makes idols, listing her abominations. The city has become guilty, defiled, and brought shame upon herself, earning mockery from all nations.

26:22.title: Catalogue of Sins: Leaders and People
26:22.summary: Ezekiel details the specific wickedness of Israel's princes, priests, and people: bloodshed, dishonoring parents, oppression of foreigners and the vulnerable, desecration of holy things, sexual immorality, and bribery. All have forgotten God in their greed.

26:22.title: God's Hand Raised Against the City
26:22.summary: God declares He will strike against Jerusalem's dishonest gain and bloodshed, scattering the people among nations and consuming their filthiness. Through this judgment, they will know that God is Yahweh.

26:22.title: The Furnace Vision: Israel as Worthless Dross
26:22.summary: God reveals that Israel has become dross (worthless waste) and compares their judgment to metal being melted in a furnace. God will gather them in Jerusalem and melt them with the fire of His wrath until they acknowledge His judgment.

26:22.title: A Land Without Rain: Corruption of All Classes
26:22.summary: God describes Israel as an uncleansed land, where prophets prey like lions, priests violate God's law and profane holy things, princes shed blood for gain like ravening wolves, false prophets whitewash corruption with lies, and common people oppress the poor and foreigners.

26:22.title: No Intercessor Found; Judgment Poured Out
26:22.summary: God sought a righteous person to stand in the gap and intercede for the land, but found none. Therefore, He pours out His indignation and consumes them with the fire of His wrath, bringing their own ways upon their heads.

26:23.title: Introduction of the Two Sisters
26:23.summary: God commands Ezekiel to speak of two women, sisters and daughters of one mother, who prostituted themselves in Egypt. Named Oholah (Samaria) and Oholibah (Jerusalem), they became God's and bore sons and daughters.

26:23.title: Oholah's Apostasy and Punishment
26:23.summary: Oholah (Samaria) played the prostitute by doting on her Assyrian lovers and defiling herself with their idols, continuing her unfaithfulness from Egypt. God delivered her into the hands of the Assyrians, who exposed her nakedness, took her children, and killed her, making her a byword among women.

26:23.title: Oholibah's Greater Corruption
26:23.summary: Despite witnessing her sister's destruction, Oholibah (Jerusalem) became even more corrupt, lusting after Assyrians and then the Babylonians whose images she saw on walls. She multiplied her prostitution and remembered her youthful lewdness in Egypt, showing persistent unfaithfulness to God.

26:23.title: God's Judgment Against Oholibah
26:23.summary: God declares He will raise Oholibah's former lovers—Babylonians, Chaldeans, Assyrians—against her in fury. They will strip her, mutilate her, slay her children, and she will drink the bitter cup of her sister's judgment, filled with drunkenness and desolation.

26:23.title: The Sins of Both Sisters Declared
26:23.summary: God summons Ezekiel to judge Oholah and Oholibah, declaring their abominations: adultery, bloodshed, idolatry, sacrificing their children to idols, defiling God's sanctuary, and profaning His Sabbaths on the very day they murdered their children.

26:23.title: The Execution of Judgment
26:23.summary: Ezekiel describes how Oholibah prepared herself for men from afar, defiling God's sanctuary with idolatry and bloodshed. Righteous men will judge them as adulteresses; a company will stone and execute them by the sword, kill their children, and burn their houses, thereby ending lewdness in the land.

26:24.title: Divine Word and Historical Date
26:24.summary: God speaks to Ezekiel on the exact day the king of Babylon laid siege to Jerusalem, establishing the precise timing of this prophetic message. The date itself becomes a sign of God's sovereignty over history and judgment.

26:24.title: The Parable of the Bloody Caldron
26:24.summary: Ezekiel presents a parable of a caldron filled with choice meat and bones to represent Jerusalem's bloodguilt and corruption; God declares the city cannot be cleansed and will experience complete judgment. The imagery symbolizes God's inescapable wrath against the city's violence and moral filthiness.

26:24.title: Ezekiel's Wife Dies as a Sign
26:24.summary: God commands Ezekiel to remain emotionally composed when his beloved wife dies, performing no traditional mourning rituals to serve as a prophetic sign to the people. Ezekiel obeys immediately, demonstrating radical submission to God's word even in personal tragedy.

26:24.title: Interpretation of the Sign and Call to Israel
26:24.summary: When the people ask Ezekiel to explain his strange behavior, he reveals that his restrained mourning symbolizes Israel's future grief over the destruction of the Temple and their coming exile. Ezekiel becomes a living sign that demonstrates Israel's inescapable judgment and God's sovereign power.

26:24.title: Ezekiel's Mouth Opened and Recognition of God
26:24.summary: When Jerusalem falls and a fugitive brings news to Ezekiel, his mouth is opened to speak freely, ending his period of muteness and confirming his prophetic authority. The people will then recognize that Yahweh alone is God, validated through the fulfillment of Ezekiel's prophetic signs.

26:25.title: Introduction to Prophecies Against Nations
26:25.summary: God commands Ezekiel to receive His word and directs him to prophesy against the Ammonites. This sets the stage for a series of judgments against Israel's neighboring enemies.

26:25.title: Prophecy Against Ammon
26:25.summary: God pronounces judgment on Ammon for mocking Israel's sanctuary, land, and people during their suffering, promising to deliver them to eastern invaders and destroy them until they recognize His sovereignty. The Ammonites will experience humiliation and exile as punishment for their malicious rejoicing.

26:25.title: Prophecy Against Moab
26:25.summary: God judges Moab for treating Judah as merely another nation without special divine protection, promising to open its borders to eastern invaders and reduce it to obscurity. Moab's condescension toward God's people brings execution of judgments and recognition of God's power.

26:25.title: Prophecy Against Edom
26:25.summary: God condemns Edom for seeking perpetual vengeance against Judah, declaring that Israel itself will execute His judgment upon them from Teman to Dedan by the sword. God's wrath will be displayed through His own people as instruments of vengeance against Edom.

26:25.title: Prophecy Against Philistia
26:25.summary: God judges the Philistines for acting out of persistent malice and vengeful contempt against Judah, promising to destroy them and cut off their coastland through great vengeance. Their destruction will demonstrate that God executes judgment upon those who harbor eternal enmity toward His people.

26:26.title: Word of the Lord Against Tyre
26:26.summary: God speaks to Ezekiel in the eleventh year against Tyre, whose people rejoiced over Jerusalem's destruction, hoping to gain control of Jerusalem's trade routes and wealth.

26:26.title: Tyre's Destruction by Many Nations
26:26.summary: The Lord declares He will bring many nations against Tyre to destroy her walls, towers, and dust, making her a bare rock where nets are spread, and her people will be slain so they know Yahweh is God.

26:26.title: Nebuchadnezzar's Siege and Plunder of Tyre
26:26.summary: God will send Nebuchadnezzar, king of Babylon, from the north with a vast army to siege Tyre, slaughter her daughters, destroy her walls and buildings, plunder her wealth, and make her stones and timber sink into the waters.

26:26.title: Tyre's Songs and Harps Silenced
26:26.summary: God will silence Tyre's songs and harps, reduce her to a bare rock as a place for spreading nets, and ensure she is never rebuilt, all by Yahweh's decree.

26:26.title: The Island Nations Mourn Tyre's Fall
26:26.summary: All the island princes and seafaring peoples will tremble and mourn when they hear of Tyre's destruction, stripping their royal garments in shock and composing laments over the fall of this once-great city.

26:26.title: Tyre's Final Descent into the Pit
26:26.summary: God will make Tyre a desolate, uninhabited city covered by the deep waters, bringing her down with the ancient dead into the lower parts of the earth so she will never be found again, yet His glory will shine in the land of the living.

26:27.title: Commission to Lament over Tyre
26:27.summary: God commands Ezekiel, the son of man, to take up a lamentation over the city of Tyre. This sets the prophetic framework for the extended elegy that follows.

26:27.title: Tyre's Beauty and Construction
26:27.summary: Ezekiel describes Tyre's magnificent construction and beauty, portraying the city as a finely built ship with superior materials, skilled craftsmen from many nations, and military might. The passage emphasizes Tyre's arrogant claim to perfection while cataloging the international expertise that built her glory.

26:27.title: Tyre's Trading Partners and Merchants
26:27.summary: An extensive catalog of nations and regions that traded with Tyre, exchanging precious goods including metals, textiles, spices, animals, stones, and luxury items. This passage demonstrates Tyre's commercial dominance and the wealth flowing through her from across the known world.

26:27.title: Tyre's Glory and Destruction
26:27.summary: Tyre's prosperity reached its height as she became replenished and glorious through maritime trade, but the east wind breaks her in the midst of the sea, signaling her sudden catastrophic downfall. The passage marks the pivot from her magnificence to her ruin.

26:27.title: The Day of Tyre's Ruin
26:27.summary: In the day of her ruin, all of Tyre's inhabitants, merchants, sailors, and warriors sink into the sea and perish. Those who survive come ashore and mourn intensely with traditional expressions of grief, recognizing the magnitude of their loss.

26:27.title: Universal Mourning and Tyre's Become a Terror
26:27.summary: The survivors compose a funeral dirge asking who is like Tyre, now silenced in the sea; her former commercial partners and all island inhabitants are astonished and terrified by her fall. Tyre, once enriching kings with her wealth, becomes a hissing cautionary tale and terror with no future existence.

26:28.title: Judgment Against the Prince of Tyre
26:28.summary: God commands Ezekiel to pronounce judgment against the arrogant prince of Tyre, who has claimed to be a god based on his wisdom and wealth. Despite his human nature and finite understanding, his pride will lead to his destruction by foreign invaders, and he will die knowing he is merely man, not God.

26:28.title: Lament Over the King of Tyre
26:28.summary: God instructs Ezekiel to lament over the king of Tyre, revealing that this figure was once a perfect, exalted being—an anointed cherub in Eden adorned with precious stones—who fell into sin through pride in his beauty and corruption through violence and unrighteousness. His punishment is complete destruction and utter ruin.

26:28.title: Judgment Against Sidon and Restoration of Israel
26:28.summary: God commands Ezekiel to prophesy judgment against Sidon through pestilence and sword, declaring that through these executions God will be glorified and known as Yahweh. Additionally, Israel's neighbors who despised them will no longer threaten them, and Israel will recognize God as their Lord.

26:28.title: Promise of Israel's Restoration and Security
26:28.summary: God promises to gather the scattered house of Israel from among the nations, sanctify Himself in them before all peoples, and restore them to the land of Jacob where they will dwell securely, build houses, plant vineyards, and live without fear of their enemies.

26:29.title: God's Command to Prophesy Against Pharaoh
26:29.summary: The Lord commands Ezekiel to deliver a prophecy against Pharaoh and Egypt. This establishes the divine authority and purpose for the oracle that follows.

26:29.title: Pharaoh as a Great Monster—God's Judgment
26:29.summary: God denounces Pharaoh for his pride and false claim to the Nile River, comparing him to a monster that will be captured and destroyed; Egypt's weakness is exposed as a broken staff that has injured Israel rather than helped it. This passage indicts Pharaoh's arrogance and Egypt's unreliability as an ally.

26:29.title: Egypt's Desolation and Scattering
26:29.summary: God announces devastating judgment: Egypt will become a desolate wasteland for forty years, unpopulated and destroyed, with its people scattered among the nations as punishment for Pharaoh's blasphemous claim to have made the Nile. The comprehensive destruction emphasizes the totality of God's judgment against Egypt's pride.

26:29.title: Egypt's Restoration and Humiliation
26:29.summary: After forty years, God will restore Egypt but reduce it to a subordinate, powerless kingdom that will never dominate other nations again; it will no longer tempt Israel to rely on it instead of God.
