This promises restoration coupled with permanent diminishment, preventing Egypt from being a source of false confidence.

26:29.title: Nebuchadnezzar's Conquest of Egypt as Wages
26:29.summary: In a later dated oracle, God declares that Nebuchadnezzar of Babylon will conquer Egypt and plunder it as payment for his exhausting siege of Tyre, which brought him no spoils. This shows how God uses earthly powers to execute His judgments and reward His instruments.

26:29.title: Israel's Future Restoration and Voice
26:29.summary: God promises that in the day of Egypt's judgment, He will cause a 'horn' (sign of strength and honor) to sprout for Israel and give His people a voice among the nations. This promise of vindication for Israel stands in contrast to Egypt's humiliation, showing God's ultimate faithfulness to His chosen people.

26:30.title: Announcement of the Day of Yahweh Against Egypt
26:30.summary: Ezekiel receives God's word and is commanded to prophesy wailing over the imminent day of Yahweh, which will be a day of clouds and judgment upon the nations, particularly Egypt.

26:30.title: The Sword Against Egypt and Her Allies
26:30.summary: God declares that a sword will come upon Egypt and surrounding nations including Ethiopia, Put, Lud, and Cub, bringing death and destruction. Egypt's allies will fall, and messengers will spread fear among the Ethiopians.

26:30.title: God's Judgment on Egypt Through Nebuchadnezzar
26:30.summary: Yahweh will bring Egypt's population to an end through Nebuchadnezzar of Babylon and his armies, making the land desolate. God will destroy Egypt's idols, dry her rivers, set fires in her cities, and execute judgments on her strongholds so that Egypt will know Yahweh is God.

26:30.title: The Breaking of Pharaoh's Arms and Egypt's Scattering
26:30.summary: In a dated oracle, God declares He has already broken Pharaoh's arm and will break both his arms, causing the sword to fall from his hand. God will strengthen Babylon's king instead and scatter the Egyptians among the nations, so all will know that Yahweh is God.

26:31.title: Prophetic Introduction and Challenge to Pharaoh
26:31.summary: God commands Ezekiel to deliver a message to Pharaoh king of Egypt, asking whom he is like in his greatness. This sets up a parable that will compare Pharaoh's pride to a great tree.

26:31.title: The Parable of the Great Cedar
26:31.summary: Ezekiel describes the Assyrian as a magnificent cedar tree in Lebanon, nourished by abundant waters, surpassing all other trees in beauty and height, even those in God's garden. Its greatness was made by God himself, yet it became the object of envy.

26:31.title: The Judgment and Fall of the Proud Tree
26:31.summary: Because the great tree exalted itself in pride, God delivers it into the hands of a mighty foreign nation, which cuts it down and leaves it fallen across the mountains and valleys. All peoples abandon its shadow.

26:31.title: The Purpose of the Tree's Destruction
26:31.summary: The fallen tree becomes a dwelling place for birds and beasts, serving as a warning that no tree—no nation or ruler—should exalt itself in pride, for all such glory leads to death and the pit.

26:31.title: Universal Mourning and Descent to Sheol
26:31.summary: When the great tree descends to Sheol, God causes mourning throughout creation, the waters mourn, Lebanon grieves, and even the trees of Eden are comforted knowing the proud ruler is brought low. His allies and supporters descend with him to death.

26:31.title: The Application to Pharaoh
26:31.summary: Ezekiel directly applies the parable to Pharaoh, declaring that despite his glory and greatness, he will be brought down to the lower parts of the earth among the uncircumcised and the slain—a final warning of certain judgment.

26:32.title: Lamentation over Pharaoh's Fall
26:32.summary: God commands Ezekiel to compose a lamentation over Pharaoh of Egypt, comparing him to a young lion who became a monster in the sea. The oracle describes Egypt's destruction through God's judgment: Pharaoh will be caught in a net, his flesh spread on mountains, his blood watering the land, and cosmic darkness will cover the heavens at his fall, causing nations to tremble in fear.

26:32.title: Egypt Descends to Sheol with the Nations
26:32.summary: In a second word dated two weeks later, Ezekiel is commanded to wail over Egypt's multitude being cast down to Sheol to dwell uncircumcised with other slain nations. The passage catalogs Egypt among other great powers—Assyria, Elam, Meshech, Tubal, Edom, and the princes of the north—all of whom lie dishonored in the pit, having caused terror in life but now bearing shame in death, demonstrating the universal reach of God's judgment.

26:33.title: The Watchman's Responsibility
26:33.summary: God appoints Ezekiel as a watchman to warn Israel of coming judgment. Those who hear the warning but ignore it bear responsibility for their own death, while the watchman who fails to warn shares in the blood guilt of the unrepenitent.

26:33.title: Repentance and God's Justice
26:33.summary: God reveals His desire for the wicked to repent and live rather than die, and clarifies that righteousness or wickedness is not fixed—each person's current obedience or disobedience determines their fate. Israel falsely complains that God's way is unjust, but God will judge each person according to their own ways.

26:33.title: The Fugitive's Arrival and Ezekiel's Speech Restored
26:33.summary: A survivor from Jerusalem arrives at Ezekiel's location, and God opens Ezekiel's mouth, which had been bound silent, preparing him to speak God's word to the exiles.

26:33.title: Judgment on the Remnant in the Ruined Land
26:33.summary: Those remaining in the desolate land of Israel wrongly presume they will inherit it, but God condemns them for idolatry, violence, and sexual sin, promising complete desolation and death by sword, famine, and plague so they know He is Yahweh.

26:33.title: Ezekiel as a Pleasing but Unheeded Prophet
26:33.summary: The exiles gather to hear Ezekiel as though hearing pleasing entertainment and show verbal affection, but their hearts pursue selfish gain and they do not obey. Only when God's judgment comes will they acknowledge that a true prophet has been among them.

26:34.title: Prophecy Against the Unfaithful Shepherds of Israel
26:34.summary: God condemns the shepherds of Israel for feeding themselves rather than caring for the flock, allowing the sheep to become scattered and vulnerable to predators. The shepherds have neglected the weak, sick, and lost while ruling with harshness rather than compassion.

26:34.title: God's Judgment Against the Unfaithful Shepherds
26:34.summary: God declares judgment against the shepherds, holding them accountable for the scattered and preyed-upon flock and removing their authority to feed themselves from the sheep. God promises to rescue the sheep from the shepherds' destructive care.

26:34.title: God Himself as Shepherd—Seeking and Restoring the Flock
26:34.summary: God promises to personally search for, gather, and restore His scattered sheep from all places of exile, bringing them to good pasture and caring for the weak and lost while judging the strong who have oppressed others. This establishes God's direct shepherding role and His commitment to justice.

26:34.title: God's Judgment Between the Sheep—Protecting the Vulnerable
26:34.summary: God judges between the stronger and weaker sheep, condemning those who have consumed the best resources and trampled what remains, fouling water and pasture for others. God promises to save His flock from internal oppression and judge between them with justice.

26:34.title: God's Promise of the Davidic Shepherd and Covenant
26:34.summary: God promises to establish one shepherd—His servant David—over the restored flock, with God Himself as their God and David as prince. This signals the future messianic hope for unified, faithful leadership.

26:34.title: The Covenant of Peace and Restoration of the Land
26:34.summary: God establishes a covenant of peace with His restored people, ensuring security from wild beasts and enemies, transforming the land into abundance with rain and fruit, and bringing the people to know God as their God and themselves as His beloved flock. The passage culminates with God identifying Israel as His sheep and Himself as their shepherd God.

26:35.title: God's Judgment Against Mount Seir Pronounced
26:35.summary: Yahweh commands Ezekiel to prophesy against Mount Seir (Edom), announcing that He will make it a desolate wasteland and lay its cities bare so that its inhabitants will know He is Yahweh.

26:35.title: The Cause and Extent of Edom's Destruction
26:35.summary: Because Edom perpetually hated Israel and handed them over to the sword during their calamity, Yahweh swears to fill Edom's mountains and valleys with the slain and make it a perpetual desolation where no one will dwell.

26:35.title: Edom's Arrogance and Covetous Speech
26:35.summary: Edom claimed that the two nations and lands of Israel would become their possession, and they boasted against the mountains of Israel with contemptuous words; Yahweh has heard all their insults and proud speech against Him.

26:35.title: Edom's Desolation as Poetic Justice
26:35.summary: As Edom rejoiced over Israel's desolation and sought to devour its inheritance, so Yahweh will make Edom itself desolate; all of Edom shall know that He is Yahweh through this reversal of fortune.

26:36.title: Judgment Against the Nations Who Seized Israel's Land
26:36.summary: Ezekiel is commanded to prophesy to the mountains of Israel, announcing that God will judge the surrounding nations who mocked and seized Israel's desolate land, and will cause those nations to bear their own shame instead.

26:36.title: Restoration and Blessing of the Land of Israel
26:36.summary: God promises that the mountains of Israel will become productive again, be repopulated by the Israelites, and will no longer be reproached by the nations; the land will be restored to its former estate and even surpassed.

26:36.title: God's Judgment and Israel's Defilement
26:36.summary: God explains that He judged Israel's defilement and scattered them among the nations because they profaned His holy name, but now He will restore them primarily to sanctify His name and demonstrate His holiness before the watching nations.

26:36.title: Spiritual and Physical Restoration of Israel
26:36.summary: God promises to gather Israel from all nations, cleanse them spiritually through water and a new heart, give them His Spirit so they obey His law, restore them to their land, and assure them of provision while they remember their past wickedness.

26:36.title: Transformation of the Desolate Land into Paradise
26:36.summary: God declares that when He cleanses Israel from iniquity, the ruined cities will be rebuilt and the desolate land transformed into a fruitful paradise like the Garden of Eden, and the nations will recognize God's power; Israel will multiply abundantly like sacrificial flocks.

26:37.title: The Vision of the Valley of Dry Bones
26:37.summary: Ezekiel is transported by the Spirit into a valley full of very dry bones, and God asks him whether these bones can live. Ezekiel acknowledges that only God knows the answer, setting up the dramatic restoration to come.

26:37.title: The Resurrection of the Bones
26:37.summary: God commands Ezekiel to prophesy to the dry bones, which then miraculously reassemble with sinews, flesh, and skin; then he prophesies to the wind, and breath enters them, raising them as an exceedingly great army. This dramatic act demonstrates God's power to restore life to what is completely dead.

26:37.title: The Interpretation: Israel's Restoration
26:37.summary: God reveals that the bones represent the entire house of Israel, who feel their hope is lost and are cut off; He promises to open their graves, restore them to their land, place His Spirit in them, and they will know He is Yahweh. This passage transforms the vision from a literal resurrection into a prophecy of national and spiritual restoration.

26:37.title: The Sign of the Two Sticks
26:37.summary: God commands Ezekiel to take two sticks, write on them the names of Judah and Joseph (Ephraim), and join them into one stick as a sign to the people. This symbolic act represents God's intention to reunite the divided kingdoms of Israel.

26:37.title: The Promise of Reunification and Eternal Covenant
26:37.summary: God promises to gather Israel from all nations, make them one nation with one king (David), cleanse them from idolatry, establish an everlasting covenant of peace with them, place His sanctuary forever in their midst, and dwell with them as their God—so that the nations will know He is Yahweh. This passage culminates the prophecy with the fullness of restoration, spiritual transformation, and God's eternal presence.

26:38.title: The Lord's Command to Prophesy Against Gog
26:38.summary: The word of the Lord comes to Ezekiel commanding him to set his face against Gog, the prince of Rosh, Meshech, and Tubal, and to prophesy that God is against him. This introduces the prophecy of Gog's invasion and God's opposition to it.

26:38.title: Gog's Coalition and Invasion
26:38.summary: God will turn Gog around with hooks in his jaws and bring him with a vast, well-armed multitude from many nations to invade Israel after many days in the latter years, descending like a storm cloud upon the restored and secure land. This describes the gathering of Gog's army and the circumstances that provoke the invasion.

26:38.title: Gog's Evil Plan and Mercenary Concerns
26:38.summary: God reveals that Gog will devise an evil scheme to attack the peaceful, unwalled settlements of Israel to seize wealth and plunder, while merchants of distant lands question whether he has come merely for spoil. This exposes Gog's greedy and predatory motives.

26:38.title: The Prophecy's Fulfillment and God's Recognition
26:38.summary: Ezekiel is commanded to tell Gog that God will bring him against Israel when she dwells securely, so that the nations may know God's holiness, fulfilling what God had spoken through the ancient prophets of Israel. This emphasizes that this invasion was foretold and serves God's purpose of self-revelation.

26:38.title: God's Wrath and Judgment Against Gog
26:38.summary: When Gog comes against Israel, God's wrath will be kindled, causing a great earthquake that shakes all creation; God will bring judgment through internecine warfare, pestilence, blood, floods, hail, fire, and sulfur, thereby magnifying His name and making Himself known to all nations. This describes the catastrophic divine intervention that will destroy the invading force and vindicate God's holiness.

26:39.title: The Defeat of Gog
26:39.summary: God declares judgment against Gog, promising to disarm him, bring him down on the mountains of Israel, and give his body to scavengers. Through this destruction, God will make His holy name known to Israel and the nations, demonstrating His power and sovereignty.

26:39.title: The Cleansing of the Land
26:39.summary: After Gog's defeat, Israel will burn the weapons for seven years and spend seven months burying the dead to cleanse the land, with dedicated workers marking bones for proper burial in the valley of Hamon Gog. This extended process of purification demonstrates the complete devastation and the restoration of the land.

26:39.title: The Great Sacrifice on the Mountains
26:39.summary: God calls the birds and animals to gather for a great sacrificial feast on the mountains of Israel, where they will consume the flesh and blood of Gog's defeated army and princes. This vivid imagery portrays God's judgment as a sacrifice and the complete humiliation of the enemy.

26:39.title: Recognition of God's Justice and Israel's Past Captivity
26:39.summary: God declares that through His judgment on Gog, the nations will see His glory and justice, and Israel will know Him as their God. God reminds Israel that their past captivity resulted from their unfaithfulness and sin, which caused God to hide His face from them.

26:39.title: God's Promise of Restoration and the Outpouring of the Spirit
26:39.summary: God promises to restore the captives of Jacob, have mercy on Israel, and be jealous for His holy name as He brings them back to their land and sanctifies Himself before the nations. God will no longer hide His face and will pour out His Spirit on Israel, establishing an enduring relationship with them.

26:40.title: Ezekiel's Vision and Commission
26:40.summary: On the fourteenth anniversary of Jerusalem's destruction, the hand of the Lord comes upon Ezekiel and transports him in a vision to a high mountain in Israel where he encounters a divine messenger. The messenger instructs Ezekiel to carefully observe and remember everything he will be shown so that he may report it to the house of Israel.

26:40.title: Measurement of the Eastern Gate
26:40.summary: The angelic guide begins measuring the temple complex with a measuring reed, starting with the outer wall and the eastern gate. He records detailed dimensions of the gate's structure, including its lodges, thresholds, posts, and decorative features like palm trees and closed windows.

26:40.title: The Outer Court and Northern and Southern Gates
26:40.summary: The guide leads Ezekiel into the outer court with its pavement and thirty rooms, then measures the northern and southern gates, which are found to have the same dimensions and architectural features as the eastern gate, maintaining symmetry and divine proportion.

26:40.title: The Inner Court Gates
26:40.summary: The guide brings Ezekiel through the southern gate into the inner court and measures the southern, eastern, and northern gates of the inner court, each conforming to the same measurements with their lodges, arches, palm trees, and eight steps for ascent.

26:40.title: Sacrificial Preparation Areas
26:40.summary: The guide shows tables and washing facilities placed throughout the gates where priests would prepare and slaughter sacrifices for burnt offerings, sin offerings, and trespass offerings, with detailed descriptions of the stone tables and implements.

26:40.title: Priestly Chambers and the Inner Court
26:40.summary: The guide identifies rooms for singers and two priestly chambers dedicated to those who maintain the temple's duties and the altar, specifically noting the sons of Zadok who serve as ministers. He measures the inner court as a perfect square and notes the altar's position before the house.

26:40.title: The Temple Porch
26:40.summary: The guide measures the porch of the house itself, recording the dimensions of its posts, gateway width, length, and breadth, along with the pillars that flank its entrance, completing the detailed survey of the temple's outer structures.

26:41.title: Measurement of the Temple's Inner Sanctuary
26:41.summary: The angel guide measures the temple's outer posts, entrance, and inner chamber, identifying the innermost room as the most holy place. These precise measurements establish the sacred geometry of the temple's core.

26:41.title: Description of the Side Rooms and Their Access
26:41.summary: The angel describes the three-story side rooms surrounding the temple, their increasing breadth at higher levels, and their access points through doors to the north and south. These auxiliary chambers provided essential structural and functional support around the main sanctuary.

26:41.title: Measurement of the Separate Building and Overall Temple Complex
26:41.summary: The angel measures the western building adjacent to the sanctuary, the outer dimensions of the entire complex, and the surrounding galleries and structures. These measurements confirm the grand scale and symmetry of the restored temple.

26:41.title: Decorative Elements: Windows, Wood Ceilings, and Carved Ornaments
26:41.summary: The description details the temple's architectural features including closed windows, wooden ceilings, galleries, and decorative carvings of cherubim alternating with palm trees throughout the interior. These ornaments symbolize God's presence and creation's beauty.

26:41.title: The Temple Doors, Altar Table, and Final Decorative Details
26:41.summary: The angel describes the squared door posts, the double doors of the temple and sanctuary, the wooden altar table before Yahweh, and the final decorative elements including cherubim, palm trees, and windows on the porch. These details complete the vision of the restored temple's sacred furnishings and design.

26:42.title: Description of the Priestly Rooms
26:42.summary: The angel guide shows Ezekiel the rooms on the north and south sides of the inner court, describing their dimensions, layout, galleries, and entrances. These rooms were constructed in three stories with specific architectural features to serve the temple's priestly functions.

26:42.title: The Purpose of the Priestly Rooms
26:42.summary: The angel explains that the north and south rooms are holy spaces where priests who serve Yahweh eat the most holy offerings and store sacred items, requiring them to change garments and maintain ritual purity before returning to public areas.

26:42.title: Measuring the Outer Temple Boundary
26:42.summary: The angel completes the temple measurements by marking out the outer boundary wall on all four sides—five hundred reeds on each side, forming a perfect square that creates a sacred separation between the holy temple precinct and the common surrounding land.

26:43.title: The Return of God's Glory to the Temple
26:43.summary: Ezekiel is brought to the eastern gate where he witnesses the glory of the Lord returning from the east with a voice like many waters, filling the entire house. This vision fulfills and echoes his earlier visions by the river Chebar, confirming God's return to dwell in the restored temple.

26:43.title: God's Covenant Promise to Dwell Forever
26:43.summary: A divine voice declares that the temple is God's throne and that He will dwell among Israel forever, but only if they remove their idolatry and cease defiling His holy name as they did before. God promises to dwell in their midst permanently once they repent and abandon their abominable practices.

26:43.title: Ezekiel Commanded to Reveal the Temple Design
26:43.summary: Ezekiel is instructed to show the house of Israel the temple's pattern and design so they will be ashamed of their iniquities and, if repentant, can learn and keep all its ordinances. The temple on the mountain is declared most holy, establishing the law governing the sacred space.

26:43.title: The Measurements of the Altar
26:43.summary: Detailed architectural specifications are given for the altar in the restored temple, including its base, ledges, hearth, horns, and overall dimensions, all precisely measured in cubits. These measurements establish the precise construction required for the sacred altar.

26:43.title: Consecration Rituals and Initial Offerings
26:43.summary: God ordains the ceremonies for dedicating the altar, prescribing sin offerings and burnt offerings using a young bull and a male goat, with blood applied to the altar's horns and corners for cleansing and atonement. Salt is cast upon the animals and they are offered as burnt offerings to sanctify the altar.

26:43.title: Seven-Day Consecration and Restoration of Offerings
26:43.summary: For seven days, priests are to make daily sin offerings and prepare young bulls and rams without blemish to consecrate and purify the altar completely. On the eighth day and thereafter, regular burnt offerings and peace offerings may resume, signifying God's acceptance and restoration of normal temple worship.

26:44.title: The Eastern Gate Sealed and the Prince's Entrance
26:44.summary: Ezekiel is shown the outer eastern gate of the sanctuary, which is permanently sealed because Yahweh Himself entered through it. Only the prince may sit within this gate to eat bread before Yahweh, using an alternate entrance.

26:44.title: Ezekiel Commissioned to Observe and Teach the Temple Laws
26:44.summary: As Yahweh's glory fills the house, Ezekiel is commanded to carefully observe and remember all the ordinances, laws, and architectural details of the temple so he may teach them to Israel.

26:44.title: Condemnation of Israel's Profanation and the Rule Against Foreigners
26:44.summary: Ezekiel rebukes Israel for allowing uncircumcised foreigners into the sanctuary, profaning God's house and breaking His covenant through their abominations. Yahweh establishes that no foreigner uncircumcised in heart and flesh shall enter His sanctuary.

26:44.title: The Levites' Demotion and Restricted Service
26:44.summary: The Levites who abandoned Yahweh and followed idols will bear their shame and iniquity; they are demoted to gate-keeping and general temple service but forbidden to serve as priests or approach God's most holy things.

26:44.title: The Priests of Zadok: Qualifications, Duties, and Restrictions
26:44.summary: The faithful priests of Zadok who remained loyal shall serve Yahweh directly, enter the sanctuary, offer sacrifices, and teach Israel to distinguish holy from common and clean from unclean. Detailed regulations govern their clothing, behavior, marriage, purity, and conduct.

26:44.title: The Priests' Inheritance and Provision
26:44.summary: The priests receive Yahweh Himself as their inheritance rather than possessions in Israel, and are sustained by portions of offerings, firstfruits, and dough, with restrictions against eating unclean meat.

26:45.title: Division of the Land for the Holy Portion and City
26:45.summary: The land is to be divided with a holy portion set aside for priests and Levites, a city possession for all Israel, and remaining portions for the prince on either side. This ordered distribution ensures the prince no longer oppresses the people but gives them land according to their tribes.

26:45.title: Exhortation to Just Rulers and Fair Measurements
26:45.summary: The Lord commands the princes of Israel to cease violence and oppression, and to establish just weights and measures—the shekel standardized at twenty gerahs and the mina at sixty shekels—ensuring honest commerce and righteous governance.

26:45.title: Prescribed Offerings from the People to the Prince
26:45.summary: The people are to give regular offerings of grain, oil, and livestock to support the prince's sacrificial duties, with portions specified by standard measurements to maintain consistency and fairness in religious observance.

26:45.title: The Prince's Role in Festivals and Atonement
26:45.summary: The prince is responsible for presenting burnt offerings, meal offerings, and drink offerings at all appointed feasts, new moons, and Sabbaths, and for making atonement for the house of Israel through sin offerings, with special cleansing of the sanctuary on the first day of the year.

26:45.title: Observance of Passover and the Feast of Booths
26:45.summary: The prince is to oversee Passover in the first month (with unleavened bread and daily sacrifices) and similarly lead the Feast of Booths in the seventh month, presenting the required bulls, rams, goats, and meal offerings for seven days in each festival.

26:46.title: The Eastern Gate and Public Worship
26:46.summary: The inner court's east gate remains closed on workdays but opens on the Sabbath and new moon for worship. The prince and people of the land shall worship at this gate on these appointed times.

26:46.title: The Prince's Offerings on Sabbath and New Moon
26:46.summary: Specific instructions detail the prince's burnt and meal offerings: six lambs and a ram on the Sabbath, and a young bull, six lambs, and a ram on the new moon, all without blemish. These offerings establish the regular worship rhythm of the restored temple.

26:46.title: Gate Usage and Procession Rules for Worshippers
26:46.summary: The prince enters and exits by the same gate porch, while common people enter by one gate and exit by another during appointed feasts. The meal offering amounts remain consistent across these occasions.

26:46.title: Freewill Offerings and the Daily Burnt Offering
26:46.summary: The prince may present freewill offerings through the east gate, and Israel must prepare a perfect lamb daily as a continual burnt offering with its accompanying meal offering and oil, establishing perpetual worship.

26:46.title: Laws Governing the Prince's Inheritance and Land
26:46.summary: The prince's gifts to his sons are permanent inheritance, but gifts to servants revert to him in the year of liberty. The prince cannot dispossess the people but must distribute inheritance to his sons from his own holdings, protecting Israel's land distribution.

26:46.title: The Priests' Sacred Kitchens
26:46.summary: The guide shows Ezekiel the holy rooms for priests on the temple's north side, where priests boil trespass and sin offerings and bake meal offerings away from the outer court to maintain the people's sanctification.

26:46.title: The Four Cooking Courts in the Temple Corners
26:46.summary: The guide shows Ezekiel four identical enclosed courts at the temple's corners (40 cubits long, 30 cubits wide) with walls and boiling places beneath them where the temple ministers cook the people's sacrifices.

26:47.title: The Visionary River Flowing from the Temple
26:47.summary: An angelic guide shows Ezekiel waters issuing from beneath the temple threshold and flowing eastward. As they proceed eastward, the guide measures the river's depth at increasing intervals (ankles, knees, waist, and finally too deep to cross), demonstrating the river's miraculous growth and power.

26:47.title: Healing and Fertility Brought by the River
26:47.summary: Trees line both banks of the river, and the waters flow eastward into the Arabah toward the Dead Sea, bringing healing and life to all waters they touch. Fisheries will flourish, and trees producing perpetual fruit and healing leaves will grow along the banks, all sustained by water flowing from God's sanctuary.

26:47.title: The Borders of the Restored Land
26:47.summary: The Lord directs the division of the land among the twelve tribes of Israel according to an established inheritance promised to the fathers. Precise geographical boundaries are set for the north, east, south, and west sides of the restored territory.

26:47.title: Inheritance for Israel and Resident Aliens
26:47.summary: The land shall be divided by lot among the tribes of Israel, and foreigners residing in the land and raising families there shall receive inheritance equal to native-born Israelites, with each stranger inheriting within the tribe where he settles.

26:48.title: The Northern Tribes' Allotments
26:48.summary: Ezekiel lists the distribution of land among the northern tribes from Dan to Judah, each receiving an equal east-west portion. This represents the restoration and reorganization of Israel's tribal territories in the future kingdom.

26:48.title: The Sacred Offering and Priestly Portion
26:48.summary: A central 25,000 by 10,000 reed offering is designated with the sanctuary in its midst, belonging to the priests of Zadok's line who remained faithful and did not go astray. This holy portion is declared inalienable and most sacred to Yahweh.

26:48.title: The Levitical Portion and the City
26:48.summary: Levites receive an adjacent 25,000 by 10,000 reed portion, and the remaining 5,000 reeds serve the city with its suburbs and dwelling space. The entire holy offering measures 25,000 by 25,000 reeds foursquare as a unified sacred complex.

26:48.title: The Prince's Territory
26:48.summary: The prince receives land on both the east and west sides of the holy offering and city, with his territory also located between Judah and Benjamin. This grants the prince substantial holdings while the sanctuary remains central.

26:48.title: The Southern Tribes' Allotments
26:48.summary: The southern tribes—Benjamin, Simeon, Issachar, Zebulun, and Gad—receive equal east-west portions below the central holy zone, with Gad's border marking the southern boundary from Tamar to Egypt. This completes the territorial distribution of all twelve tribes.

26:48.title: The City's Gates and the Lord's Presence
26:48.summary: The city's perimeter measures 18,000 reeds with twelve named gates corresponding to the twelve tribes, three on each side, signifying access and belonging for all Israel. The vision concludes with the city renamed 'Yahweh is there,' declaring God's permanent dwelling with His people.

27:1.title: The Fall of Jerusalem and the Temple Vessels
27:1.summary: Nebuchadnezzar besieges Jerusalem in the third year of King Jehoiakim's reign, and the Lord allows him to capture the king and sacred vessels from God's house, which are carried to Babylon as spoils of war.

27:1.title: Selection and Renaming of Young Judeans
27:1.summary: Nebuchadnezzar orders that promising young men from Israel's royal and noble families, including Daniel and three companions, be trained in Chaldean learning and given Babylonian names to assimilate them into the empire.

27:1.title: Daniel's Resolve and the Test of Diet
27:1.summary: Daniel resolves to maintain his religious purity by refusing the king's food and wine; he persuades the steward to allow a ten-day trial of vegetables and water instead, and the four youths emerge healthier than those eating royal provisions.

27:1.title: Excellence and Recognition of the Four Youths
27:1.summary: God grants Daniel and his companions exceptional knowledge, wisdom, and skill; they surpass all other candidates and are found ten times superior to the king's magicians, earning permanent positions in the palace and continuing into the reign of Cyrus.

27:2.title: Nebuchadnezzar's Troubling Dream and the King's Impossible Demand
27:2.summary: King Nebuchadnezzar is troubled by a dream he cannot remember and demands that his wise men reveal both the dream and its interpretation or face execution. When the magicians, enchanters, and Chaldeans insist they need to hear the dream first, the king angrily recognizes their duplicity and orders the destruction of all the wise men of Babylon.

27:2.title: Daniel Seeks Divine Revelation Through Prayer
27:2.summary: When Daniel and his companions learn they are targeted for execution, Daniel requests time from the king and then calls his three friends to pray for God's mercy and revelation of the secret. God reveals the dream to Daniel in a night vision, and Daniel responds with a prayer blessing God's power to reveal deep mysteries and thanking Him for granting wisdom.

27:2.title: Daniel Prepares to Interpret the Dream Before the King
27:2.summary: Daniel stops the execution of the wise men and is brought before Nebuchadnezzar, where he humbly explains that human wisdom cannot reveal such secrets, but God in heaven reveals mysteries and has shown the king what will happen in the latter days. Daniel clarifies that his ability comes not from personal wisdom but from God's purpose to make the interpretation known.

27:2.title: The Vision of the Great Image
27:2.summary: Daniel describes Nebuchadnezzar's dream of a great and awesome statue made of gold, silver, brass, iron, and mixed iron-clay, which is struck by a stone cut without hands that shatters the entire image into dust carried away by the wind, while the stone becomes a great mountain filling the earth.

27:2.title: The Interpretation of the Image: Kingdoms and God's Eternal Kingdom
27:2.summary: Daniel interprets the image as representing successive earthly kingdoms—Nebuchadnezzar as the golden head, followed by inferior kingdoms of silver, brass, and iron—culminating in a divided kingdom of iron mixed with clay that cannot hold together. In the days of these kings, God will establish an eternal kingdom that destroys all earthly powers and stands forever, with the stone representing this divine kingdom.

27:2.title: Nebuchadnezzar's Recognition and Exaltation of Daniel
27:2.summary: Nebuchadnezzar bows before Daniel, offers him worship and sacrifices, and acknowledges that Daniel's God is the God of gods, the Lord of kings, and a revealer of secrets. The king elevates Daniel to great authority over Babylon and appoints his three companions to govern the province, while Daniel himself remains at the king's gate.

27:3.title: The Golden Image and the Command to Worship
27:3.summary: King Nebuchadnezzar erects a massive golden image and summons all officials to its dedication. A herald announces that all must worship the image when music sounds, on penalty of being thrown into a fiery furnace, and all the assembled peoples comply.

27:3.title: The Accusation Against the Three Jews
27:3.summary: Chaldean officials inform King Nebuchadnezzar that Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego, whom he appointed over Babylon's affairs, refuse to serve his gods or worship the golden image.

27:3.title: The Three Men's Defiant Response
27:3.summary: Nebuchadnezzar angrily summons the three men and threatens them with the furnace, but Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego declare that their God can deliver them and that regardless of the outcome, they will not worship his gods or the image.

27:3.title: The Furnace Heated and the Men Cast In
27:3.summary: Enraged by their refusal, Nebuchadnezzar orders the furnace heated seven times hotter than usual and commands his soldiers to bind and cast the three men into the fire; the soldiers are killed by the intense heat.

27:3.title: The Miraculous Deliverance
27:3.summary: Nebuchadnezzar observes four unbound men walking unharmed in the furnace, with the fourth appearing like a divine being. The three men emerge completely unharmed—no burns, singed hair, or smell of smoke—while the assembled officials witness the miracle.

27:3.title: The King's Conversion and Decree
27:3.summary: Nebuchadnezzar praises the God of the three men, acknowledging that He delivered His servants and sent His angel. He issues a decree that anyone who speaks evil against this God will be destroyed, and he promotes Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego in the province.

27:4.title: Nebuchadnezzar's Proclamation
27:4.summary: King Nebuchadnezzar addresses all peoples and declares his intention to testify about the signs and wonders that God has performed for him, acknowledging God's eternal kingdom and mighty dominion.

27:4.title: The Dream and the Wise Men
27:4.summary: While at peace in his palace, Nebuchadnezzar has a troubling dream and calls all the wise men of Babylon to interpret it, but only Daniel—filled with the spirit of the holy gods—can help.

27:4.title: The Vision of the Great Tree
27:4.summary: Daniel recounts Nebuchadnezzar's dream of a massive, fruitful tree that is cut down by a heavenly watcher, with only its roots left bound, until the king learns that the Most High rules over all kingdoms and appoints rulers.

27:4.title: Daniel's Interpretation and Warning
27:4.summary: Daniel interprets the tree as Nebuchadnezzar himself and explains that he will be driven into madness for seven times until he acknowledges God's rule, urging the king to repent and show mercy to escape the judgment.

27:4.title: The Judgment Fulfilled
27:4.summary: Twelve months after the warning, as Nebuchadnezzar boasts about his own power and glory, he is immediately driven mad, driven from his kingdom, and forced to live like an animal until his hair and nails grow wild.

27:4.title: Nebuchadnezzar's Restoration and Praise
27:4.summary: After his understanding returns, Nebuchadnezzar praises and honors God the Most High, acknowledging His eternal dominion, justice, and power to humble the proud, and he is restored to his kingdom with even greater majesty.

27:5.title: Belshazzar's Blasphemous Feast
27:5.summary: King Belshazzar hosts a great banquet where he commands that the sacred vessels from the Jerusalem temple be brought so that he and his court can drink from them while praising pagan gods. This act of desecration demonstrates the king's pride and contempt for the God of Israel.

27:5.title: The Mysterious Handwriting on the Wall
27:5.summary: A disembodied hand suddenly appears and writes cryptic words on the palace wall, terrifying Belshazzar. The king summons his wise men and magicians to read and interpret the writing, but none can decipher it, leaving the king and his court in profound distress.

27:5.title: The Queen Recommends Daniel
27:5.summary: The queen advises the panicked king to call upon Daniel, a man known for his exceptional wisdom and ability to interpret mysteries, whom King Nebuchadnezzar had formerly honored. She assures the king that Daniel can provide the interpretation.

27:5.title: Daniel Before the King
27:5.summary: Belshazzar brings Daniel before him and offers him rewards of purple clothing, a gold chain, and high position if he can read and interpret the writing. Daniel refuses the rewards but agrees to provide the interpretation and a message from God.

27:5.title: Daniel's Rebuke and Indictment
27:5.summary: Daniel recounts how God exalted Nebuchadnezzar but humbled him when he became proud, forcing him to learn that God rules the kingdoms of men. He then condemns Belshazzar for knowing this history yet still lifting himself against God, desecrating the temple vessels, and praising lifeless idols while failing to glorify the living God.

27:5.title: The Inscription Explained
27:5.summary: Daniel reveals the meaning of the three words written on the wall: MENE (God has numbered and ended Belshazzar's kingdom), TEKEL (Belshazzar is weighed and found wanting), and PERES (the kingdom is divided and given to the Medes and Persians). Each word pronounces God's judgment upon the king for his arrogance and blasphemy.

27:5.title: Daniel Honored and the Kingdom Falls
27:5.summary: Despite delivering a message of doom, Daniel is honored by Belshazzar with purple robes, a gold chain, and the rank of third ruler—though that very night Belshazzar is killed and Darius the Mede takes the kingdom. God's judgment is executed swiftly, and His word proves absolutely certain.

27:6.title: Daniel's Rise to Prominence
27:6.summary: King Darius establishes a government structure with 120 satraps overseen by three presidents, with Daniel as one of them. Daniel distinguishes himself through his excellent spirit and faithfulness, prompting the king to consider promoting him over the entire realm.

27:6.title: The Conspiracy Against Daniel
27:6.summary: Jealous officials seek to find fault with Daniel but discover none due to his complete integrity. They devise a scheme to trap him by manipulating the king into signing an irrevocable decree prohibiting prayer to any god or person except the king for thirty days.

27:6.title: Daniel's Faithful Defiance
27:6.summary: Despite the decree, Daniel continues his practice of praying toward Jerusalem three times daily. His enemies witness his prayer and immediately report him to the king, accusing him of disrespect and disobedience.

27:6.title: Daniel Cast into the Lions' Den
27:6.summary: King Darius, distressed by Daniel's predicament, attempts to rescue him but is bound by the unchangeable law of the Medes and Persians. He reluctantly commands that Daniel be cast into the lions' den, expressing faith that Daniel's God will deliver him.

27:6.title: Daniel's Miraculous Deliverance
27:6.summary: The king spends a sleepless night in anguish and hurries to the den at dawn to find Daniel alive and unharmed, protected by an angel who shut the lions' mouths. Daniel emerges without injury, his innocence proven before both God and the king.

27:6.title: Justice and the King's Proclamation
27:6.summary: The accusers and their families are cast into the lions' den and destroyed, while King Darius issues a decree throughout his realm acknowledging the God of Daniel as the living God whose kingdom cannot be destroyed. Daniel prospers under both Darius and Cyrus the Persian.

27:7.title: Daniel's Dream and the Four Winds
27:7.summary: In the first year of Belshazzar's reign, Daniel receives a night vision of four winds stirring up the great sea, from which four great and diverse animals emerge. This introduction sets the stage for an apocalyptic revelation about future kingdoms.

27:7.title: The Four Great Beasts
27:7.summary: Daniel describes four successive beasts rising from the sea: a lion with eagle's wings that becomes human-like, a bear raised on one side devouring flesh, a leopard with four wings and heads, and a terrifying fourth beast with iron teeth and ten horns, from which emerges a small horn with human eyes and a boastful mouth that displaces three of the original horns. These beasts represent successive empires that will dominate the earth.

27:7.title: The Ancient of Days and Divine Judgment
27:7.summary: Daniel witnesses the establishment of God's throne with the Ancient of Days clothed in white, attended by countless heavenly beings, as judgment is set and the books are opened. The fourth beast is slain and burned, while the other beasts lose their dominion but are allowed to live for a limited time.

27:7.title: The Son of Man Receives Eternal Kingdom
27:7.summary: One like a son of man comes before the Ancient of Days and is given dominion, glory, and an everlasting kingdom that all peoples and nations will serve. This figure represents the ultimate ruler whose kingdom shall never be destroyed.

27:7.title: Daniel Seeks and Receives Interpretation
27:7.summary: Troubled by the visions, Daniel approaches one of the heavenly attendants who interprets that the four beasts are four kings arising on earth, while the saints of the Most High will receive an eternal kingdom. The interpretation includes details of the fourth beast's dominion over the saints until the Ancient of Days executes judgment and grants the kingdom to the saints.

27:7.title: Detailed Interpretation of the Fourth Kingdom and the Blasphemous King
27:7.summary: The interpreter explains that the fourth beast is a fourth kingdom that will devour and destroy the earth, its ten horns represent ten kings, and a different king arising from them will subdue three kings, speak blasphemy against the Most High, and wear out the saints until his dominion is taken away and destroyed. Ultimately, the kingdom and dominion of all earthly powers will be given eternally to the people of the saints of the Most High, whose kingdom will be served by all nations.

27:7.title: Daniel's Response and Conclusion
27:7.summary: Daniel concludes his account by noting that while his thoughts deeply troubled him and his appearance was affected by the visions, he kept the matter in his heart, reflecting on the profound implications of what he had witnessed.

27:8.title: Daniel's Vision in Susa
27:8.summary: In the third year of King Belshazzar's reign, Daniel receives a vision while in the citadel of Susa by the river Ulai. This vision follows an earlier one he had received.

27:8.title: The Ram and the Goat Vision
27:8.summary: Daniel sees a two-horned ram conquering all directions, then a male goat from the west with a great horn that breaks the ram's horns and magnifies itself, until its great horn breaks and four horns replace it. These represent the rise and fall of successive kingdoms.

27:8.title: The Little Horn and Desecration
27:8.summary: A little horn grows from one of the four horns, becoming exceedingly great and casting down heavenly armies and stars, removing the continual offering and desecrating the sanctuary through mankind's disobedience. Truth is cast to the ground and the horn prospers in its evil.

27:8.title: The Heavenly Council and the 2,300 Days
27:8.summary: Holy ones in heaven discuss how long the vision concerning the continual offering and desolation will last, and are told the sanctuary will be cleansed after 2,300 evenings and mornings. This establishes a specific timeline for restoration.

27:8.title: Gabriel's Commission to Explain the Vision
27:8.summary: Daniel seeks understanding of the vision when the angel Gabriel appears before him; though initially frightened, Gabriel is sent to make him understand that the vision concerns the time of the end and the latter time of God's indignation.

27:8.title: Interpretation: The Ram and Four Kingdoms
27:8.summary: Gabriel reveals that the two-horned ram represents the kings of Media and Persia, the rough goat is the king of Greece with its great horn being the first king, and the four horns that replace it represent four kingdoms that shall arise but without the first king's power.

27:8.title: The Fierce King and His Actions
27:8.summary: In the latter time, a fierce-faced king of dark understanding shall arise, wielding great but divinely-limited power to destroy the mighty and holy people through cunning policy; he will magnify himself and destroy many in their security, but will be broken without human hand.

27:8.title: Sealing the Vision and Daniel's Response
27:8.summary: Gabriel confirms the vision's truth but commands Daniel to seal it because it concerns many days in the future; Daniel is overwhelmed and physically drained by the vision but resumes his duties while remaining bewildered, as none understand its full meaning.

27:9.title: Daniel's Understanding of Jeremiah's Prophecy
27:9.summary: In the first year of King Darius's reign, Daniel learns from the scriptures that Jerusalem's desolation will last seventy years as Jeremiah prophesied. This understanding prompts Daniel to seek God through prayer and intercession.

27:9.title: Daniel's Prayer of Confession and Intercession
27:9.summary: Daniel approaches God with fasting, sackcloth, and ashes, confessing the sins of Israel and pleading for God's mercy and restoration of Jerusalem. He acknowledges God's righteousness while admitting the nation's rebellion, disobedience, and rejection of the prophets.

27:9.title: Gabriel's Appearance and Message of Favor
27:9.summary: While Daniel is still praying, the angel Gabriel appears to him at the time of the evening offering, revealing that God has heard Daniel's prayer and sent Gabriel to give him wisdom and understanding because Daniel is greatly beloved. Gabriel commissions Daniel to understand the vision that follows.

27:9.title: The Seventy Weeks Prophecy
27:9.summary: Gabriel reveals that seventy weeks are decreed for Israel and Jerusalem to complete redemption, end sin, and bring everlasting righteousness. The prophecy details the timeline from the command to rebuild Jerusalem to the Anointed One, his rejection after sixty-two weeks, the destruction of the city, and a final covenant week involving cessation of sacrifice and desolation.

27:10.title: Daniel's Vision and Preparation Through Fasting
27:10.summary: In the third year of Cyrus's reign, Daniel receives a revelation about a great spiritual warfare and responds with a three-week fast of mourning, eating no pleasant food or wine and abstaining from self-care. His preparation through discipline and humility sets the stage for receiving divine understanding.

27:10.title: The Appearance of the Heavenly Messenger
27:10.summary: While standing by the river Hiddekel on the twenty-fourth day of the first month, Daniel sees a radiant figure clothed in linen with a brilliant, terrifying appearance like lightning and fire. Though only Daniel perceives the vision, the other men present experience overwhelming fear and flee, leaving Daniel alone and physically overwhelmed by the encounter.

27:10.title: The Messenger's Reassurance and Mission
27:10.summary: A hand touches Daniel, raising him to his knees, and the heavenly messenger identifies himself as sent in response to Daniel's prayer and humility from the first day he sought understanding. The messenger reveals that he was delayed twenty-one days by the prince of Persia but was aided by Michael, and has now come to reveal what will happen to Daniel's people in the latter days.

27:10.title: Daniel's Weakness and Strengthening
27:10.summary: Overwhelmed by the vision, Daniel becomes mute and loses all strength; a figure touches his lips enabling him to speak, but he confesses his anguish and powerlessness before the messenger. The heavenly being repeatedly strengthens Daniel, reassuring him not to fear and promising peace, until Daniel is emboldened to ask the messenger to continue speaking.

27:10.title: The Cosmic Spiritual Conflict and Final Assurance
27:10.summary: The messenger reveals the scope of spiritual warfare: he must return to fight the prince of Persia, and the prince of Greece will come next, but Michael, the chief prince over Israel, stands with Daniel's people. This concludes the messenger's mission statement, establishing the backdrop of heavenly conflict protecting God's covenant people.

27:11.title: Michael's Strengthening of Darius
27:11.summary: The angel Gabriel recalls how he strengthened and supported Darius the Mede in his first year of reign. This establishes divine involvement in the earthly kingdoms about to be described.

27:11.title: The Prophecy of Persian Kings and Alexander's Empire
27:11.summary: Gabriel reveals that four Persian kings will arise, with the fourth becoming exceptionally wealthy and provoking Greece; a mighty king (Alexander) will then conquer with great dominion, but his kingdom will be divided among four others after his death, not going to his heirs. This outlines the transition from Persian to Greek dominance.

27:11.title: Conflict Between the Kings of the North and South
27:11.summary: After Alexander's division, the southern and northern kingdoms engage in diplomatic and military conflicts, with an intermarriage alliance failing and one southern ruler conquering the northern kingdom's treasures before withdrawing. This depicts the early Hellenistic struggles between Egypt and Syria.

27:11.title: Escalating Wars and Greater Mobilization
27:11.summary: The northern king's sons prepare vast armies and launch multiple campaigns against the south, with increasing military strength and resources deployed after each conflict. This describes the intensifying Seleucid-Ptolemaic wars.

27:11.title: The King of the North's Dominance and Fall
27:11.summary: The northern king becomes unstoppable, conquering fortified cities and the glorious land, but after turning toward maritime conquests and facing resistance, he turns homeward only to stumble, fall, and vanish. This depicts the peak and decline of Seleucid power.

27:11.title: The Rise of a Contemptible King and His Aggression
27:11.summary: A despised figure (Antiochus IV Epiphanes) seizes the throne through flattery and cunning, conquers surrounding territories, and distributes plunder among his followers while plotting against strongholds. This introduces the most notorious persecutor of the Jewish people.

27:11.title: Conflict with the King of the South and Treachery
27:11.summary: Antiochus attacks Egypt with a large army but is betrayed by those close to the Egyptian king and his own forces are overwhelmed; both kings act deceitfully at negotiations, but their schemes fail because God has appointed a specific time. This depicts Antiochus's failed Egyptian campaigns and ultimate limitation by divine providence.

27:11.title: Antiochus's Return and Desecration of the Temple
27:11.summary: Returning with plunder, Antiochus turns against God's covenant, enters the southern kingdom again, but encounters resistance from ships from the west (Rome); enraged, he stations forces to desecrate the sanctuary, halt the daily sacrifice, and erect the abomination of desolation. This describes the historical persecution of Jews under Antiochus IV.

27:11.title: Persecution of the Faithful and Refinement Through Suffering
27:11.summary: Antiochus seduces the unfaithful through flattery, but those who know God stand firm and instruct others; the wise suffer greatly through violence and exile, receiving little visible help, yet this suffering refines and purifies them until God's appointed end-time. This portrays the Maccabean period's trials and the spiritual purification of the faithful remnant.

27:11.title: The King's Blasphemy and Idolatry
27:11.summary: Antiochus acts according to his will, exalting himself above all gods and speaking blasphemy against God; he abandons traditional gods to honor the god of fortresses (military power) with treasures, elevating his foreign-backed supporters and dividing territory for profit. This reveals his complete apostasy and self-deification.

27:11.title: The Final Conflict and the King's End
27:11.summary: At the time of the end, the kings of south and north clash, with the northern king sweeping through countries, conquering Egypt and its treasures, and even the lands of neighboring nations; alarmed by reports from east and north, he marches forth in fury to destroy, plants his royal pavilion between the sea and the holy mountain, yet comes to his end with none to help him. This culminates the prophecy in Antiochus's ultimate destruction and vindicates God's sovereignty.

27:12.title: Michael's Deliverance and the Resurrection
27:12.summary: Michael the prince will arise to deliver God's people during an unprecedented time of trouble, after which many will awake from death—some to eternal life and some to eternal shame. The wise and righteous will shine with brilliance like the stars forever.

27:12.title: Daniel Commanded to Seal the Prophecy
27:12.summary: Daniel is instructed to seal up the prophetic words until the end of time, when knowledge will increase and many will seek understanding of these mysteries.

27:12.title: The Duration of the Tribulation Revealed
27:12.summary: Daniel sees two angels by the river asking a heavenly figure how long the wonders will last; the figure swears that oppression of God's people will last for 'a time, times, and a half' before all things are finished.

27:12.title: Daniel's Inability to Understand and the Final Purification
27:12.summary: Daniel confesses he does not understand the outcome, and is told the words remain sealed until the end; meanwhile, the righteous will be purified and refined, while the wicked will remain in wickedness and ignorance.

27:12.title: The Specific Countdown to the End
27:12.summary: From the cessation of the daily offering and the setting up of the abomination of desolation, there shall be 1,290 days; blessed are those who endure to 1,335 days. Daniel is told to rest until the end of days when he will rise and receive his inheritance.

28:1.title: Hosea's Commission
28:1.summary: God calls the prophet Hosea during the reigns of multiple Judean and Israelite kings to marry a prostitute as a living sign of Israel's unfaithfulness to the Lord.

28:1.title: Birth of Jezreel and Coming Judgment
28:1.summary: Hosea marries Gomer, who bears a son named Jezreel; God declares this name symbolizes His coming judgment on the house of Jehu and the end of the northern kingdom of Israel.

28:1.title: Birth of Lo-Ruhamah and Selective Mercy
28:1.summary: A daughter is born and named Lo-Ruhamah ('not pitied') to signify God's withdrawal of mercy from Israel, though He promises to preserve Judah through His own power rather than military strength.

28:1.title: Birth of Lo-Ammi and Covenant Severance
28:1.summary: A third child, a son named Lo-Ammi ('not my people'), is born to symbolize God's complete rejection of Israel and the breaking of the covenant relationship.

28:1.title: Promise of Restoration and Reunion
28:1.summary: Despite present judgment, God promises Israel's descendants will be as countless as the sand of the sea and will be called 'sons of the living God,' with Judah and Israel reunited under one head in a great future day.

28:2.title: Call to Recognize God's People
28:2.summary: God instructs the people to address one another as "My people" and "My loved one," affirming their identity and relationship despite coming judgment. This opening frames the chapter's message of judgment and restoration within the context of God's covenant commitment.

28:2.title: God's Judgment on Unfaithful Israel
28:2.summary: Through the metaphor of an unfaithful wife, God pronounces judgment on Israel for abandoning Him to worship idols and false gods, stripping away her blessings and fertility until she recognizes that all provision comes from Him alone. Israel's idolatry—attributing gifts meant for the Lord to false lovers (Baal)—necessitates severe discipline and the removal of joy, celebration, and material prosperity.

28:2.title: God's Restoration and Renewed Covenant
28:2.summary: After judgment, God tenderly allures Israel back to Himself in the wilderness, restoring her vineyards, changing her relationship from servitude to intimate marriage, and establishing an eternal covenant based on righteousness, justice, lovingkindness, compassion, and faithfulness. This reversal demonstrates God's redemptive love that transcends deserved punishment.

28:2.title: Cosmic Renewal and Final Restoration
28:2.summary: God establishes a chain of divine response through creation—heaven and earth responding to each other, bringing forth abundance—culminating in God's sowing Israel as His own and extending mercy to those who were not His people, fully restoring the broken relationship. This eschatological vision encompasses all creation in Israel's redemption and the extension of God's people to include the Gentiles.

28:3.title: Hosea's Redemptive Marriage as God's Love for Israel
28:3.summary: God commands Hosea to marry an adulteress as a living parable of God's love for unfaithful Israel. Hosea purchases and commits to the woman, establishing conditions of faithfulness that mirror God's covenantal relationship with His people despite their idolatry.

28:3.title: Israel's Judgment and Future Restoration
28:3.summary: Israel will experience a period of exile and spiritual deprivation without religious institutions or royal leadership, but will ultimately return to seek God and their messianic king David, receiving His blessings. This prophecy depicts both judgment for idolatry and God's ultimate restoration of His people.

28:4.title: Yahweh's Charge Against Israel
28:4.summary: God brings a legal charge against the inhabitants of the land, declaring that truth, goodness, and knowledge of God are absent and replaced by lying, murder, stealing, and adultery. The consequences of this moral collapse will extend to the entire creation—the land will mourn and all living creatures will waste away.

28:4.title: Priests and Prophets Condemned
28:4.summary: God warns against accusing one another because the people are like those who bring charges against a priest; priests and prophets will stumble together, and God will destroy Israel's mother. The priests have rejected knowledge and forgotten God's law, so they feed on the people's sin and grow wealthy from iniquity, earning God's punishment.

28:4.title: Unfaithfulness and Idolatry
28:4.summary: Because Israel has abandoned Yahweh and turned to prostitution and wine, they will eat but not be satisfied and will not multiply; they consult wooden idols and sacrifice on high places with shrine prostitutes. God announces that he will not punish the people for their sexual unfaithfulness because the men themselves consort with prostitutes, leading the people to ruin.

28:4.title: Israel's Stubborn Rebellion and Final Judgment
28:4.summary: Though Israel plays the prostitute, Judah is warned not to follow; Israel, stubbornly fixed like a heifer, cannot be fed like a lamb. Ephraim clings to idols in continuous prostitution while rulers love shameful gain, and the wind will wrap them up in judgment—they will be disappointed by their sacrifices.

28:5.title: Indictment of Israel's Unfaithfulness
28:5.summary: God calls Israel's leaders to account for setting snares and spreading nets that have led the people into spiritual prostitution and defilement. Their sinful deeds have made them unable to return to God, and though they seek Him, they will not find Him because He has withdrawn Himself from their unfaithfulness.

28:5.title: The Coming Destruction and God's Consuming Judgment
28:5.summary: God commands the sounding of war alarms across Israel's cities, announcing the day of rebuke when Ephraim will become desolate and Judah's leaders will face God's wrath. God becomes to both kingdoms instruments of their destruction—like a moth and rottenness—consuming them from within because of their pursuit of idols.

28:5.title: False Healing and God's Fierce Judgment
28:5.summary: When Israel recognizes their sickness and wound, they turn to Assyria instead of to God, seeking help from a human king who cannot heal them. God responds as a consuming lion, tearing them to pieces, and withdraws until they acknowledge their sin and earnestly seek His face in their affliction.

28:6.title: A Call to Return and Repentance
28:6.summary: Israel is invited to return to Yahweh, who has wounded them but promises healing and revival. The people are urged to pursue intimate knowledge of God, whose coming is as certain and life-giving as the rising sun and spring rain.

28:6.title: God's Complaint: Ephemeral Love and Covenant Breaking
28:6.summary: God laments that Israel's and Judah's love is fleeting and shallow; He judges them through His prophets because He values mercy and knowledge of God over empty ritual. Their unfaithfulness echoes Adam's breach of covenant.

28:6.title: Indictment of Widespread Corruption and Wickedness
28:6.summary: God catalogs the systemic moral corruption in both Israel and Judah: cities stained with blood, priests committing murder and shameful crimes, and sexual immorality throughout the land.

28:6.title: Promise of Restored Fortune for Judah
28:6.summary: Despite the judgment pronounced, God promises Judah a harvest and restoration of fortunes when He gathers His people again.

28:7.title: Israel's Hidden Wickedness Exposed
28:7.summary: When God attempts to heal Israel, their iniquity and falsehood are uncovered—their leaders take pride in wickedness and lies while forgetting that God remembers all their deeds. This exposes the hypocrisy and corruption at the heart of the nation.

28:7.title: The Nation as a Burning Oven
28:7.summary: Israel's leaders are compared to adulterers and an overheated oven—their passions consume them unchecked, they devour their judges, and all their kings have fallen because no one calls upon God. This metaphor portrays a society consumed by unchecked desire and moral collapse.

28:7.title: Ephraim's Weakness Among Nations
28:7.summary: Ephraim mixes with foreign nations like an unturned pancake—weakened by outsiders yet unaware, aging without recognizing his decline. Despite these signs, Israel's pride prevents repentance and return to God.

28:7.title: Deception and Judgment Proclaimed
28:7.summary: Israel, foolish as a dove without understanding, calls to Egypt and Assyria for help instead of God; God declares judgment through a net upon them while lamenting that those He would redeem have spoken lies against Him. The passage announces woe and destruction for their unfaithfulness.

28:7.title: False Worship and Final Judgment
28:7.summary: Israel does not cry to God with true hearts but howl in distress on their beds; they assemble for material gain rather than faithfulness, plot evil despite God's teaching, and turn away. Their princes will fall by the sword because of their arrogant speech, becoming a mockery in Egypt.

28:8.title: The Alarm of Judgment
28:8.summary: God sounds the alarm like a trumpet warning of an eagle (enemy) descending upon His house because Israel has broken covenant and rebelled against His law. Despite their hypocritical cry acknowledging God, Israel has rejected what is good and faces enemy pursuit.

28:8.title: The Sin of Idolatry
28:8.summary: Israel has appointed kings and made idols of silver and gold without God's approval, acts that will lead to their destruction. God condemns the calf idol of Samaria as a human-made object, not a true god, destined to be shattered.

28:8.title: Reaping the Whirlwind
28:8.summary: Israel's sowing of wind (sin) will result in reaping whirlwind (judgment), leaving no harvest and being consumed by strangers. Israel becomes swallowed up among the nations like a valueless object, having gone to Assyria and hired lovers (foreign alliances) like a wild donkey.

28:8.title: Scattered and Forgotten
28:8.summary: Though Israel sold themselves among nations, God will gather them only for them to waste away under oppression from mighty kings. Despite God writing many laws for them, Israel treated His instruction as something strange and foreign.

28:8.title: Hollow Worship and Final Judgment
28:8.summary: Israel's sacrifices are rejected by God because their hearts are not aligned with Him; their iniquity will be remembered and punished, and they will return to Egypt in exile. Israel and Judah, having forgotten their Maker and built strength in palaces and fortified cities, will face God's fire consuming their fortresses.

28:9.title: Warning Against False Joy and Idolatry
28:9.summary: Hosea warns Israel not to celebrate like the nations, for they have been unfaithful to God through idolatry and spiritual prostitution. Their land will fail to provide, they will be exiled, and their worship will become defiled and unacceptable to the Lord.

28:9.title: The Day of Reckoning and Rejection of Prophets
28:9.summary: Hosea asks what Israel will do when they can no longer celebrate God's feasts, as judgment has come and they will be scattered and buried in foreign lands. The people have become so corrupt they mock and reject God's prophets, and God will bring visitation and punishment for their sins.

28:9.title: Israel's Corruption and Loss of Glory
28:9.summary: God recalls finding Israel as precious as early grapes and figs, but they turned to Baal worship and became abominable; now their glory will flee like a bird and their children will be taken from them. Though they raise children, God will bereave them, and Ephraim's former beauty will yield only destruction.

28:9.title: Complete Divine Rejection and Exile
28:9.summary: God pronounces judgment that Ephraim's wombs will miscarry and breasts dry up, their wickedness will cause them to be driven from God's house with no more love, and they will be cast away as wanderers among nations because they refused to listen to God. This is the ultimate consequence of their rebellion and covenant violation.

28:10.title: Israel's Prosperity Leads to Idolatry and Judgment
28:10.summary: Israel, like a luxuriant vine, has multiplied altars and sacred stones in proportion to their material prosperity, but their divided hearts will result in God demolishing their idolatrous shrines. Their abundance became an occasion for unfaithfulness rather than gratitude.

28:10.title: Israel's Rejection of God's Authority
28:10.summary: Israel claims they have no king and do not fear Yahweh, making false covenants and empty promises that produce only judgment like poisonous weeds in a field. Their words reveal their fundamental rejection of both divine and human authority.

28:10.title: The Fall of Samaria and Its Idols
28:10.summary: Samaria's inhabitants will mourn the loss of their golden calves of Beth Aven as they are carried away to Assyria, and the king and city will float away like a helpless twig on water. The glory they trusted in will depart, leaving only shame.

28:10.title: Destruction of Idolatrous Altars and Coming Judgment
28:10.summary: Israel's high places and altars will be destroyed, and the people in their desperation will cry to mountains to cover them, as God gathers nations to punish their persistent transgressions dating back to Gibeah. Their sin carries a long history of rebellion.

28:10.title: Ephraim as a Trained Heifer Under the Yoke
28:10.summary: Ephraim, like a heifer accustomed to threshing, will now experience the yoke and labor, with Judah plowing and Jacob breaking clods—a reversal from ease to toil as punishment. The metaphor shifts from animal comfort to forced servitude.

28:10.title: Call to Repentance and Accusation of Wickedness
28:10.summary: God calls Israel to sow righteousness and seek Him so that He may rain righteousness upon them, but Israel has instead plowed wickedness and reaped iniquity, trusting in their own might rather than in God. The contrast shows Israel's willful rejection of the path to redemption.

28:10.title: Total Destruction and the Fall of the King
28:10.summary: Battle will destroy Israel's fortresses as thoroughly as Shalman destroyed Beth Arbel, with mothers and children dashed to pieces, and at daybreak the king of Israel will be destroyed because of Bethel's great wickedness. The judgment is absolute and without mercy.

28:11.title: God's Tender Care for Israel in the Past
28:11.summary: God recalls how He loved Israel as a child, called them out of Egypt, taught them to walk, and nurtured them with cords of love and compassionate care. This passage emphasizes God's patient, parental instruction and tender devotion despite Israel's lack of understanding.

28:11.title: Israel's Refusal to Repent and Coming Judgment
28:11.summary: Because Israel refused to repent and turn back to God, they will face Assyrian conquest and military destruction as punishment for their rebellion. Their determination to turn away from God means even their prayers to the Most High will not be answered.

28:11.title: God's Heart of Compassion and Restraint of Wrath
28:11.summary: In tension with the coming judgment, God expresses His internal struggle and compassion, declaring He cannot give up Israel despite their unfaithfulness because His nature is merciful, not wrathful like man's. God's divine compassion restrains the full execution of His anger.

28:11.title: Israel's Future Restoration and Return
28:11.summary: Israel will eventually respond to God's call, returning trembling from Egypt and Assyria to be restored to their homes. This promise of restoration follows God's merciful decision to spare them, showing hope beyond judgment.

28:11.title: Israel's Present Unfaithfulness
28:11.summary: The oracle ends by noting that both Ephraim and Judah currently surround God with lies and deceit, remaining unfaithful and straying from Him. This concluding verse returns to Israel's present sinful state that necessitates the judgment and future redemption just described.

28:12.title: Ephraim's Futile Pursuits and God's Controversy
28:12.summary: Ephraim pursues empty alliances with Assyria and Egypt while multiplying lies, and Yahweh declares He will judge both Israel and Judah according to their ways. The passage establishes the theme of covenant unfaithfulness and impending divine judgment.

28:12.title: Jacob's Struggle with God and Divine Identity
28:12.summary: The passage recalls Jacob's wrestling with the angel at Bethel and his prevailing through persistence, establishing that Yahweh is the God who met Jacob and revealed Himself through this transformative encounter. This historical memory grounds Israel's identity in God's covenant faithfulness.

28:12.title: Call to Return and Warning Against False Security
28:12.summary: God exhorts Israel to return, practice kindness and justice, and wait for Him, but Ephraim defiantly claims wealth and innocence while being unaware of their spiritual corruption. The passage contrasts true repentance with false confidence in material prosperity.

28:12.title: God's Redemptive History and Prophetic Testimony
28:12.summary: Yahweh reminds Israel of His deliverance from Egypt and His repeated sending of prophets with visions and parables to call them back, demonstrating His patient grace and consistent efforts to restore the covenant people. This establishes God's faithfulness despite Israel's rejection.

28:12.title: Indictment of Wickedness and Remembrance of God's Preservation
28:12.summary: The passage condemns Gilead and Gilgal for their idolatrous practices and false worship, then contrasts this with God's preservation of Jacob and Israel through prophetic guidance from Egypt onward. The juxtaposition highlights Israel's ingratitude for God's sustained care.

28:12.title: Final Judgment on Ephraim's Provocation
28:12.summary: Yahweh declares that Ephraim's bitter provocation of His anger will result in bloodshed and divine repayment for their contempt, delivering the ultimate verdict of judgment that concludes the chapter's indictment.

28:13.title: Ephraim's Fall from Prominence
28:13.summary: Ephraim once held authority and respect in Israel, but through idolatry and unfaithfulness to God, he became guilty and will be destroyed as completely as morning mist, dew, chaff, and smoke that disappear.

28:13.title: God's Faithfulness and Israel's Ingratitude
28:13.summary: God reminds Israel of His exclusive claim as their God and Savior since Egypt, and recalls His provision in the wilderness; yet they became satisfied and proud, forgetting Him entirely.

28:13.title: God as Predator Against His People
28:13.summary: Because Israel has turned against God—their only true help—He will pursue them with the ferocity of a lion, leopard, and bereaved bear, tearing them apart in judgment.

28:13.title: The Futility of Human Kings
28:13.summary: Israel demanded a king and princes to save them, but the king God gave in anger He has now taken away in wrath, leaving them without salvation or protection.

28:13.title: Stored Guilt and Unexpected Redemption
28:13.summary: Ephraim's sin is accumulating and stored up like a woman in travail who cannot give birth, yet God paradoxically promises to ransom them from death and Sheol, though compassion is hidden from His eyes.

28:13.title: Complete Destruction of Samaria
28:13.summary: Though Ephraim was fruitful, the east wind of God's judgment will dry up all their resources, and Samaria will bear the full consequences of rebellion through sword, death of infants, and slaughter of pregnant women.

28:14.title: Call to Return to the Lord
28:14.summary: Israel is called to repent and return to God, confessing their sins and rejecting idolatry and false sources of help. The people are invited to approach God with words of repentance, acknowledging that only He can provide mercy and salvation.

28:14.title: God's Promise of Healing and Restoration
28:14.summary: God promises to heal Israel's unfaithfulness and love them freely, restoring them to beauty and vitality like flourishing plants and trees. This vision of abundant growth and fragrance depicts God's gracious renewal of His people.

28:14.title: Wisdom and the Way of the Righteous
28:14.summary: God renounces idolatry and declares His protective presence as Israel's source of life and fruitfulness, while a closing exhortation calls the wise to understand that God's ways are right and only the righteous can walk them successfully. The rebellious will stumble on the same paths.

29:1.title: The Word of the Lord and Call to Remember
29:1.summary: Joel receives God's word and calls the elders and all inhabitants to listen and witness an unprecedented disaster. He instructs them to pass this account down through generations as a memorial of God's judgment.

29:1.title: The Plague of Locusts and Agricultural Devastation
29:1.summary: A succession of locust swarms has completely consumed the land's vegetation, destroying vines, fig trees, and all cultivated plants, leaving the people stripped of food and joy. The account emphasizes the totality of destruction across all vegetation.
