41:12.summary: A scribe asks Jesus which commandment is greatest, and Jesus answers that loving God with all your heart, soul, mind, and strength is first, and loving your neighbor as yourself is second, with no greater commandments than these. The scribe affirms this wisdom, and Jesus tells him he is not far from God's kingdom.

41:12.title: Jesus Questions the Scribes About the Messiah's Identity
41:12.summary: While teaching in the temple, Jesus challenges the scribes' assumption that the Messiah is merely David's son by quoting Psalm 110, in which David himself calls the Messiah 'Lord,' implying the Messiah's superior status.

41:12.title: Warning Against the Scribes
41:12.summary: Jesus warns his followers to beware of scribes who seek honor and prestige through robes and greetings while exploiting widows and praying for show, and he pronounces that they will receive greater condemnation.

41:12.title: The Widow's Offering
41:12.summary: Jesus observes wealthy people giving large amounts into the temple treasury, then notices a poor widow giving only two small coins, and he teaches his disciples that she has given more than all the others because she gave everything she had to live on out of her poverty.

41:13.title: Jesus Predicts the Temple's Destruction
41:13.summary: A disciple marvels at the magnificent temple stones, but Jesus declares that not one stone will remain standing. This prophecy introduces Jesus's teaching about the end times and the coming judgment on Jerusalem.

41:13.title: The Disciples Ask About the End Times
41:13.summary: On the Mount of Olives, four disciples privately ask Jesus when the temple's destruction will occur and what sign will indicate it. Their question sets the stage for Jesus's extended eschatological discourse.

41:13.title: Warning Against Deception and Persecution
41:13.summary: Jesus warns the disciples to beware of false messiahs and to expect wars, famines, earthquakes, and persecution before the end comes. He promises that despite hardship and betrayal, those who endure to the end will be saved.

41:13.title: The Abomination of Desolation and the Flight from Judea
41:13.summary: Jesus instructs those in Judea to flee to the mountains when they see the abomination of desolation standing where it should not, signaling the destruction of Jerusalem. He emphasizes the urgency and severity of this time, noting that God will shorten these days for the sake of the elect.

41:13.title: False Christs and the Coming of the Son of Man
41:13.summary: Jesus warns against false christs and prophets who will perform signs and wonders to deceive. After cosmic disturbances, the Son of Man will appear in clouds with power and glory, sending angels to gather the chosen from all corners of creation.

41:13.title: The Parable of the Fig Tree and Certainty of Jesus's Words
41:13.summary: Jesus teaches that just as a fig tree's tender leaves signal summer's nearness, so the predicted signs will indicate that the end is near. He assures his disciples that this generation will witness these events and that his words will endure eternally.

41:13.title: The Command to Watch and Be Ready
41:13.summary: Jesus emphasizes that the day and hour of his return are unknown to all but the Father, therefore believers must continually watch, keep alert, and pray. He illustrates this with a parable of a man traveling abroad who leaves his servants responsible to watch for his return at any hour.

41:14.title: The Plot to Kill Jesus
41:14.summary: Two days before Passover, the chief priests and scribes plot to arrest and kill Jesus secretly, fearing a riot if they act during the feast.

41:14.title: The Anointing at Bethany
41:14.summary: A woman anoints Jesus' head with costly nard at Simon the leper's house; though some complain about waste, Jesus defends her act as preparation for his burial and promises her deed will be remembered.

41:14.title: Judas Agrees to Betray Jesus
41:14.summary: Judas Iscariot approaches the chief priests to hand Jesus over to them in exchange for money.

41:14.title: Preparation of the Passover
41:14.summary: Jesus sends two disciples into the city to find a man with a pitcher of water, who directs them to a prepared upper room where they set up the Passover meal.

41:14.title: Jesus Predicts His Betrayal
41:14.summary: At the Passover meal, Jesus announces that one of the twelve who eats with him will betray him, warning that though his betrayal is foretold, the betrayer faces terrible judgment.

41:14.title: Institution of the Lord's Supper
41:14.summary: Jesus breaks bread and gives it to his disciples as his body, then passes the cup as his blood of the new covenant poured out for many, vowing not to drink again until the kingdom of God.

41:14.title: Jesus Predicts the Disciples' Failure and Peter's Denial
41:14.summary: After singing a hymn, Jesus predicts all will stumble because of him that night but will be raised and go before them to Galilee; Peter insists he will not deny Jesus, and all the disciples agree, though Jesus warns Peter will deny him three times before dawn.

41:14.title: Jesus Prays in Gethsemane
41:14.summary: In Gethsemane, Jesus takes Peter, James, and John aside and prays three times that the cup might pass from him, yet submits to God's will; he returns each time to find the disciples sleeping, and finally announces the hour has come and the betrayer is at hand.

41:14.title: The Arrest of Jesus
41:14.summary: Judas arrives with an armed crowd and betrays Jesus with a kiss; a bystander strikes the high priest's servant, but Jesus submits to arrest, noting he was accessible in the temple yet Scripture must be fulfilled; all disciples flee, and a young man escapes naked.

41:14.title: Jesus Before the Sanhedrin
41:14.summary: At a nighttime trial before the chief priests, elders, and scribes, false witnesses cannot agree; when asked if he is the Messiah and Son of God, Jesus affirms it and declares he will sit at God's right hand, causing the high priest to condemn him for blasphemy; the council votes for death and mocks Jesus with spitting and beating.

41:14.title: Peter's Denial of Jesus
41:14.summary: As Jesus faces trial, Peter warms himself by a courtyard fire; confronted three times by servants and bystanders about knowing Jesus, Peter denies any association, and after the rooster crows a second time, he remembers Jesus' prediction and weeps in shame.

41:15.title: Jesus Brought Before Pilate
41:15.summary: The Jewish council delivers Jesus to Pilate, who questions him about being King of the Jews. Jesus responds minimally while the chief priests make many accusations, causing Pilate to marvel at his silence.

41:15.title: Barabbas Released, Jesus Condemned to Crucifixion
41:15.summary: Pilate offers to release a prisoner as he customarily does at the feast, but the chief priests manipulate the crowd to demand Barabbas be freed and Jesus crucified. Pilate, seeking to please the crowd, releases the murderer Barabbas and hands Jesus over to be flogged and crucified.

41:15.title: The Soldiers Mock Jesus
41:15.summary: Roman soldiers mock Jesus by dressing him in purple, crowning him with thorns, and hailing him as King of the Jews, then striking and spitting on him before stripping him and leading him out to crucifixion.

41:15.title: The Crucifixion of Jesus
41:15.summary: Simon of Cyrene is compelled to carry Jesus's cross to Golgotha, where Jesus is offered wine mixed with myrrh but refuses it. The soldiers crucify him at the third hour, divide his garments by casting lots, and place a superscription proclaiming him 'King of the Jews.'

41:15.title: Jesus Crucified Between Two Robbers
41:15.summary: Jesus is crucified between two robbers, fulfilling Scripture. Passersby and the chief priests mock him, taunting him to save himself and come down from the cross, even as those crucified with him insult him.

41:15.title: The Death of Jesus
41:15.summary: Darkness covers the land from the sixth to ninth hour; Jesus cries out in Aramaic, 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' After bystanders misunderstand his cry as calling Elijah, Jesus gives a loud cry and dies. The temple veil tears in two, and the centurion confesses Jesus as the Son of God.

41:15.title: The Burial of Jesus
41:15.summary: Faithful women, including Mary Magdalene, watch from afar as Joseph of Arimathaea, a council member seeking the Kingdom of God, boldly requests Jesus's body from Pilate, wraps it in linen, and lays it in a rock-cut tomb, with the women witnessing his burial place.

41:16.title: Women Visit the Tomb
41:16.summary: After the Sabbath, Mary Magdalene, Mary the mother of James, and Salome buy spices to anoint Jesus' body. Early on the first day of the week, they arrive at the tomb and discover that the large stone has been rolled away.

41:16.title: The Angel's Announcement of the Resurrection
41:16.summary: Inside the tomb, the women encounter a young man in white who announces that Jesus has risen and is not there, then instructs them to tell the disciples and Peter that Jesus will meet them in Galilee. Overwhelmed by fear and amazement, the women flee and initially say nothing to anyone.

41:16.title: Jesus Appears to Mary Magdalene
41:16.summary: Jesus appears first to Mary Magdalene, from whom he had cast out seven demons. She reports his resurrection to the grieving disciples, but they disbelieve her account.

41:16.title: Jesus Appears to Two Disciples
41:16.summary: Jesus appears in another form to two disciples as they travel in the countryside, and they report it to the other disciples, who also refuse to believe them.

41:16.title: The Great Commission
41:16.summary: Jesus appears to the eleven apostles at table, rebukes them for their unbelief and hardness of heart, then commissions them to preach the Gospel to all creation. He promises that signs—casting out demons, speaking new languages, handling serpents, immunity to poison, and healing the sick—will accompany believers.

41:16.title: The Ascension and the Disciples' Mission
41:16.summary: After speaking to the disciples, the Lord is taken up into heaven and sits at the right hand of God. The disciples go out and preach everywhere, with the Lord working through them and confirming the message with accompanying signs.

42:1.title: Luke's Preface
42:1.summary: Luke addresses his account to Theophilus, explaining that he has carefully investigated the fulfilled events concerning Jesus from eyewitness accounts in order to provide certainty about the gospel narrative.

42:1.title: The Annunciation to Zacharias and Birth of John the Baptist
42:1.summary: The righteous priest Zacharias receives a vision of the angel Gabriel announcing that his barren wife Elizabeth will bear a son named John, who will prepare Israel for the Lord; when Zacharias doubts, Gabriel strikes him mute until the birth occurs. Elizabeth conceives and hides herself, praising God for removing her reproach.

42:1.title: The Annunciation to Mary
42:1.summary: The angel Gabriel appears to the virgin Mary in Nazareth and announces that she will conceive and bear a son named Jesus, who will be the Son of the Most High and reign forever; Mary accepts this miraculous calling with obedient faith.

42:1.title: Mary Visits Elizabeth
42:1.summary: Mary travels to Elizabeth's home, and when Elizabeth hears Mary's greeting, the baby John leaps in her womb and Elizabeth is filled with the Holy Spirit, recognizing Mary as the mother of the Lord and blessing her faith.

42:1.title: The Magnificat (Mary's Song of Praise)
42:1.summary: Mary responds with a hymn celebrating God's mercy and mighty works, declaring that he exalts the humble, feeds the hungry, and fulfills his covenant promises to Abraham and Israel.

42:1.title: The Birth and Naming of John the Baptist
42:1.summary: Elizabeth bears a son; at his circumcision, despite family expectation, his mother insists he be called John, and when Zacharias confirms this in writing, his speech is restored and he blesses God, causing wonder throughout Judea.

42:1.title: Zacharias's Prophecy (The Benedictus)
42:1.summary: Filled with the Holy Spirit, Zacharias prophesies about God's redemption through David's line, declaring that his son John will prepare the way for the Lord to bring salvation and light to those in darkness.

42:1.title: John's Growth and Preparation
42:1.summary: John grows strong in spirit and lives in the wilderness, prepared by the Lord for his future role as forerunner to the Messiah.

42:2.title: The Census and Journey to Bethlehem
42:2.summary: Caesar Augustus issues a decree requiring enrollment throughout the Roman world. Joseph travels from Nazareth to Bethlehem with Mary, who is pregnant, to register according to their ancestral lineage.

42:2.title: The Birth of Jesus
42:2.summary: Mary gives birth to Jesus in Bethlehem and wraps him in cloth, laying him in a feeding trough because there is no room in the inn.

42:2.title: The Shepherds and the Heavenly Announcement
42:2.summary: Angels announce Jesus's birth to shepherds in the fields, proclaiming the good news of a Savior. The shepherds witness the heavenly host praising God, then visit the infant Jesus and spread the message widely, while Mary treasures these events in her heart.

42:2.title: Circumcision, Naming, and Presentation at the Temple
42:2.summary: Jesus is circumcised on the eighth day and named as the angel had commanded. His parents bring him to Jerusalem to present him to the Lord and offer the prescribed sacrifice according to the law.

42:2.title: Simeon's Testimony and Prophecy
42:2.summary: Simeon, a righteous and devout man filled with the Holy Spirit, recognizes Jesus as the promised Messiah and blesses him. He prophesies that Jesus will bring both falling and rising, and that suffering will pierce Mary's soul.

42:2.title: Anna's Witness and Thanksgiving
42:2.summary: Anna, an elderly prophetess who has devoted herself to temple worship and prayer, encounters the infant Jesus and gives thanks to God, proclaiming him to all who awaited redemption in Jerusalem.

42:2.title: Return to Nazareth and Jesus's Growth
42:2.summary: The family returns to Nazareth after fulfilling all requirements of the law. Jesus grows strong and is filled with wisdom and the grace of God.

42:2.title: Jesus at the Temple at Age Twelve
42:2.summary: When Jesus is twelve, his parents bring him to Jerusalem for Passover. He remains in the temple listening to and questioning the teachers, astounding all with his understanding. When his anxious parents find him and question his behavior, Jesus reveals his consciousness of being in his Father's house, a saying they do not understand.

42:2.title: Jesus's Return to Nazareth and Maturation
42:2.summary: Jesus returns to Nazareth with his parents and is subject to them. He increases in wisdom and stature, and in favor with God and men.

42:3.title: Historical Setting and John's Commission
42:3.summary: Luke establishes the historical context of John the Baptist's ministry during the reign of Tiberius Caesar and various regional rulers, and announces that God's word came to John in the wilderness.

42:3.title: John's Preaching and Isaiah's Prophecy
42:3.summary: John preaches baptism of repentance throughout the Jordan region, fulfilling Isaiah's prophecy about preparing the way of the Lord and making his paths straight for all flesh to see God's salvation.

42:3.title: John's Call to Repentance and Practical Obedience
42:3.summary: John rebukes the crowds for false confidence in their ancestry and demands genuine repentance through practical fruit—sharing possessions, honest taxation, and righteous conduct—warning that fruitless trees will be cut down.

42:3.title: John Identifies the Coming Messiah
42:3.summary: When people speculate whether John is the Christ, he clarifies he is unworthy even to serve the coming one, who will baptize with the Holy Spirit and fire, separating the righteous from those destined for judgment.

42:3.title: John's Imprisonment
42:3.summary: After preaching good news and rebuking Herod for his immorality, John is arrested and imprisoned, silencing his prophetic voice.

42:3.title: Jesus' Baptism and Divine Affirmation
42:3.summary: Jesus is baptized while praying, the Holy Spirit descends upon him in bodily form as a dove, and God's voice declares him to be the beloved Son in whom he is well pleased.

42:3.title: The Genealogy of Jesus
42:3.summary: Luke traces Jesus' lineage from Joseph through the line of David, back through the patriarchs and Old Testament figures, ultimately to Adam and God, establishing Jesus' humanity and his connection to God's redemptive history.

42:4.title: The Temptation of Jesus
42:4.summary: After his baptism, Jesus is led by the Spirit into the wilderness where he fasts for forty days and faces three temptations from the devil—to turn stones into bread, to worship Satan for worldly power, and to test God's protection by jumping from the temple. Jesus resists each temptation by quoting Scripture, demonstrating his dependence on God's word and his refusal to abandon his mission.

42:4.title: Jesus Returns to Galilee in Power
42:4.summary: Jesus returns from the wilderness filled with the power of the Holy Spirit and begins his public ministry, teaching in synagogues and gaining widespread recognition and praise.

42:4.title: Jesus Reads Isaiah in Nazareth
42:4.summary: In his hometown synagogue, Jesus reads from Isaiah 61, proclaiming that the Spirit has anointed him to bring good news to the poor, heal the brokenhearted, and free the captives, then declares that this Scripture is being fulfilled in their hearing.

42:4.title: Rejection at Nazareth
42:4.summary: The people initially marvel at Jesus' words but then express skepticism because he is Joseph's son, prompting Jesus to tell them that no prophet is accepted in his hometown and to warn them that God sometimes extends his favor to foreigners rather than to Israel. Enraged, the townspeople try to kill Jesus by throwing him off a cliff, but he miraculously escapes.

42:4.title: Demon Possession and Healing in Capernaum
42:4.summary: In Capernaum's synagogue on the Sabbath, Jesus casts out a demon from a man, and the demon recognizes Jesus as the Holy One of God. The people are amazed at Jesus' authoritative power over unclean spirits, and news of his deeds spreads throughout the region.

42:4.title: Evening Healings and Jesus' Mission Focus
42:4.summary: Jesus heals Simon's mother-in-law of fever, and at sunset crowds bring their sick and demon-possessed to him, which he heals. The next day, despite the multitude's pleas for him to stay, Jesus departs to preach the Kingdom of God in other cities, understanding that his mission extends beyond one location.

42:5.title: The Calling of Simon Peter
42:5.summary: Jesus teaches from Simon Peter's boat on the Sea of Galilee, then instructs him to cast nets into deep water, resulting in a miraculous catch of fish. Amazed and convicted of his sinfulness, Simon Peter and his partners James and John abandon everything to follow Jesus as fishers of men.

42:5.title: Jesus Cleanses a Leper
42:5.summary: A man full of leprosy begs Jesus to make him clean, and Jesus heals him by touch and word, then sends him to the priests with instructions to keep quiet. Despite this command, the report spreads widely and crowds gather to hear and be healed, prompting Jesus to withdraw for prayer.

42:5.title: The Healing of the Paralyzed Man
42:5.summary: While Jesus teaches with the Lord's power present to heal, four men lower a paralyzed friend through the roof. Jesus sees their faith and forgives the man's sins, prompting scribes and Pharisees to accuse him of blasphemy; he then heals the paralyzed man to prove his authority to forgive sins on earth.

42:5.title: The Calling of Levi and Meal with Sinners
42:5.summary: Jesus calls the tax collector Levi to follow him, and Levi hosts a great feast where Jesus eats with tax collectors and sinners. When religious leaders object, Jesus explains that he came not to call the righteous but sinners to repentance, positioning himself as a physician for the spiritually sick.

42:5.title: Teaching on Fasting and New Wine
42:5.summary: When questioned why his disciples don't fast like John's and the Pharisees' disciples, Jesus explains that fasting is inappropriate while the bridegroom is present, though it will come after his departure. He then teaches through three parables that his new covenant cannot be patched onto the old or contained in old forms—new wine requires new wineskins.

42:6.title: Plucking Grain on the Sabbath
42:6.summary: Jesus's disciples pluck and eat grain on the Sabbath, prompting Pharisees to challenge them. Jesus defends his disciples by citing David's precedent and declares that the Son of Man is lord of the Sabbath.

42:6.title: Healing the Withered Hand on the Sabbath
42:6.summary: Jesus heals a man with a withered hand in the synagogue on the Sabbath while being watched by scribes and Pharisees. His rhetorical question about doing good on the Sabbath silences their accusations, but they respond with rage and plot against him.

42:6.title: The Calling and Naming of the Twelve Apostles
42:6.summary: After praying all night, Jesus calls and names twelve disciples as apostles, including Peter, James, John, Matthew, and Judas Iscariot. This act establishes the foundational leadership structure of his movement.

42:6.title: Jesus Heals the Multitude
42:6.summary: Jesus descends with the apostles to a level place where large crowds from Judea, Jerusalem, and the surrounding regions gather to hear him and be healed. Power flows from Jesus and heals all who seek to touch him.

42:6.title: The Beatitudes and Woes
42:6.summary: Jesus pronounces blessings on the poor, hungry, and persecuted who will receive kingdom rewards, and woes on the rich, satisfied, and popular who have already received their consolation. This teaching inverts worldly values.

42:6.title: Love Your Enemies
42:6.summary: Jesus teaches his disciples to love enemies, do good to those who hate them, and give generously without expecting return. This radical love reflects God's mercy and kindness toward the ungrateful and wicked.

42:6.title: Do Not Judge; Give Generously
42:6.summary: Jesus forbids his followers from judging and condemning others, promising that the measure they use in giving will be returned to them in abundance. This teaching emphasizes mercy and reciprocal generosity.

42:6.title: The Blind Guide the Blind and the Fruit of the Heart
42:6.summary: Jesus teaches through parables that the blind cannot guide the blind, disciples become like their teachers, and people must remove their own faults before judging others. A good heart produces good fruit, while an evil heart produces evil fruit.

42:6.title: The Two Builders
42:6.summary: Jesus asks why people call him Lord but do not obey him, then contrasts the wise builder who hears and obeys his words with a foundation on rock, with the foolish builder who ignores them and has no foundation. Only obedience provides true security.

42:7.title: The Healing of the Centurion's Servant
42:7.summary: A Roman centurion whose servant is dying sends Jewish elders to Jesus, but then intercepts him with a message expressing unworthiness while demonstrating extraordinary faith in Jesus's word. Jesus heals the servant from a distance, marveling at the centurion's faith as greater than any found in Israel.

42:7.title: The Raising of the Widow's Son at Nain
42:7.summary: Jesus encounters a funeral procession for a widow's only son and, moved with compassion, raises the young man from the dead and restores him to his mother. The crowd recognizes Jesus as a great prophet and declares that God has visited his people.

42:7.title: John the Baptist's Question and Jesus's Response
42:7.summary: John the Baptist, imprisoned and hearing of Jesus's works, sends disciples to ask whether Jesus is the Messiah or if another is expected. Jesus responds by pointing to his miracles fulfilling messianic prophecies and declares a blessing on those not offended by him.

42:7.title: Jesus's Testimony Concerning John the Baptist
42:7.summary: Jesus praises John as the greatest prophet and messenger, greater than anyone born of women, yet affirms that even the least in God's kingdom surpasses John. He critiques his generation's rejection of both John and himself, illustrating their stubbornness with a parable of quarrelsome children.

42:7.title: The Sinful Woman Who Anointed Jesus's Feet
42:7.summary: A sinful woman anoints Jesus's feet with perfume and tears in a Pharisee's house; when the Pharisee questions whether Jesus is truly a prophet for allowing this contact, Jesus teaches him through a parable about forgiveness and love, then assures the woman that her faith has saved her and her sins are forgiven.

42:8.title: Jesus' Ministry with the Twelve and Faithful Women
42:8.summary: Jesus travels through cities and villages preaching the Kingdom of God, accompanied by the twelve apostles and women who have been healed of demons and infirmities, including Mary Magdalene and others who support them with their possessions.

42:8.title: The Parable of the Sower
42:8.summary: Jesus teaches a parable about a farmer sowing seed, then explains that the seed represents God's word and different types of soil represent how people receive it—some reject it, some believe temporarily, some are distracted by worldly concerns, and some bear fruit through faithful reception and perseverance.

42:8.title: The Lamp and the Imperative to Listen
42:8.summary: Jesus teaches that truth cannot remain hidden and will be revealed, and warns his listeners to pay careful attention to how they hear, because understanding increases what God gives while ignorance results in loss.

42:8.title: Jesus' True Family
42:8.summary: When Jesus learns that his mother and brothers seek him, he redefines family as those who hear and obey God's word rather than by biological relation.

42:8.title: Jesus Stills the Storm
42:8.summary: While crossing the lake with his disciples, Jesus sleeps as a violent storm arises; when the frightened disciples awake him, he rebukes the wind and water, bringing calm and prompting them to marvel at his authority over nature.

42:8.title: The Demon-Possessed Man of Gadara
42:8.summary: Jesus encounters a man possessed by many demons (Legion) in the land of the Gadarenes; he casts out the demons into a herd of pigs which plunge into the lake, and the healed man is found clothed and in his right mind, though the fearful townspeople ask Jesus to leave while the man becomes a witness to Jesus' greatness.

42:8.title: The Woman with the Flow of Blood and Jairus' Request
42:8.summary: Upon returning, Jesus is welcomed by crowds; Jairus, a synagogue ruler, asks Jesus to heal his dying daughter, but as they travel, a woman with a twelve-year hemorrhage touches Jesus' cloak and is healed by faith, prompting Jesus to affirm her faith and send her in peace.

42:8.title: The Raising of Jairus' Daughter
42:8.summary: When Jairus learns his daughter is dead, Jesus encourages faith rather than fear; he enters the house, dismisses the mourning crowd, takes the child's hand, and raises her to life, commanding the astonished parents to tell no one of this miracle.

42:9.title: The Twelve Commissioned and Sent Out
42:9.summary: Jesus gives the twelve apostles power and authority over demons and diseases, sends them to preach the Kingdom and heal, instructs them to travel without provisions and shake dust from their feet if rejected, and they depart to preach and heal throughout the villages.

42:9.title: Herod's Perplexity About Jesus
42:9.summary: Herod the tetrarch hears of Jesus' works and is confused about his identity, wondering if he is John the Baptist risen, Elijah, or another old prophet, having beheaded John himself, and seeks to see Jesus.

42:9.title: Feeding of the Five Thousand
42:9.summary: The apostles return and Jesus takes them to a deserted place near Bethsaida, but crowds follow; Jesus welcomes them, teaches about the Kingdom, and miraculously feeds five thousand men with five loaves and two fish, with twelve baskets of leftovers remaining.

42:9.title: Peter's Confession of Christ
42:9.summary: While praying, Jesus asks his disciples who the crowds and they themselves believe him to be; the disciples report various opinions (John the Baptist, Elijah, or an old prophet), and Peter confesses Jesus as the Christ of God.

42:9.title: The Passion Prediction and Cost of Discipleship
42:9.summary: Jesus commands silence about his identity, predicts he must suffer, be rejected, be killed, and be raised on the third day, then teaches that true followers must deny themselves, take up their cross, and lose their lives for his sake to save them, warning that rejection of him brings judgment, and assures some will see the Kingdom of God before death.

42:9.title: The Transfiguration
42:9.summary: About eight days after his passion prediction, Jesus takes Peter, John, and James up a mountain to pray; his appearance is altered with dazzling white clothing, Moses and Elijah appear in glory speaking of his coming departure, and God's voice from a cloud identifies Jesus as his beloved Son to be listened to, after which the disciples remain silent about the vision.
themes: communion with the law and prophets

42:9.title: The Healing of a Demon-Possessed Boy
42:9.summary: The next day, a man from the crowd begs Jesus to heal his only son, who is possessed by a spirit that convulses him; the disciples were unable to cast it out, but Jesus rebukes the demon, heals the boy, and returns him to his father, astounding the crowd at God's majesty.

42:9.title: Second Passion Prediction
42:9.summary: While all marvel at Jesus' deeds, he tells his disciples that the Son of Man will be delivered into the hands of men; the disciples do not understand this saying, its meaning is concealed from them, and they are afraid to ask about it.

42:9.title: True Greatness—Welcoming a Child
42:9.summary: The disciples argue about who is greatest; Jesus perceives their reasoning, sets a little child by his side, and teaches that whoever receives such a child in his name receives him and the Father, and that the least among them will be great.

42:9.title: The Unknown Exorcist
42:9.summary: John reports that the disciples forbade someone casting out demons in Jesus' name because he does not follow with them, but Jesus tells them not to forbid him, saying whoever is not against them is for them.

42:9.title: The Samaritan Rejection and Jesus' Rebuke of Vengeance
42:9.summary: As the days approach for Jesus to be taken up, he sets his face toward Jerusalem and sends messengers ahead; a Samaritan village rejects him because he is heading to Jerusalem, and when James and John ask to call fire down like Elijah, Jesus rebukes them, saying the Son of Man came to save, not destroy, and they go to another village.

42:9.title: Three Would-Be Followers and the Cost of Discipleship
42:9.summary: Three men express desire to follow Jesus: one volunteers but is told the Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head; another is called but asks to bury his father first, and is told to leave the dead to bury the dead and proclaim the Kingdom; a third wants first to say goodbye to his household, but is told no one putting his hand to the plow and looking back is fit for the Kingdom of God.

42:10.title: Jesus Appoints the Seventy and Sends Them Out
42:10.summary: Jesus appoints seventy disciples and sends them out in pairs to prepare cities for his coming, instructing them to pray for more laborers since the harvest is plentiful but workers are few.

42:10.title: Instructions for the Seventy Disciples
42:10.summary: Jesus gives the seventy detailed instructions for their mission: travel lightly, greet no one, bless houses of peace, accept hospitality, heal the sick and proclaim the kingdom, and pronounce judgment on cities that reject them.

42:10.title: Woes Against Unrepentant Cities
42:10.summary: Jesus pronounces woes against Chorazin, Bethsaida, and Capernaum for rejecting his mighty works, warning that their rejection of the disciples constitutes rejection of Jesus and the Father.

42:10.title: The Seventy Return with Joy; Jesus Redirects Their Rejoicing
42:10.summary: The seventy return celebrating their power over demons, but Jesus reorients their focus away from spiritual power and toward their eternal salvation, which is their true source of joy.

42:10.title: Jesus Rejoices and Reveals Hidden Wisdom; Blessing of the Disciples
42:10.summary: Jesus rejoices that the Father has revealed spiritual truth to humble believers rather than the wise, and he blesses the disciples for witnessing what prophets and kings longed to see.

42:10.title: The Lawyer's Question: Who Is My Neighbor?
42:10.summary: A lawyer tests Jesus by asking what he must do to inherit eternal life; Jesus directs him to the law's commands to love God and neighbor, prompting the lawyer to ask who qualifies as his neighbor.

42:10.title: The Parable of the Good Samaritan
42:10.summary: Jesus illustrates true neighborly love through a parable where a despised Samaritan shows mercy to a robbery victim, while religious leaders pass by, teaching that neighbor-love transcends ethnic and social boundaries.

42:10.title: Mary and Martha
42:10.summary: At Martha's house, Martha is anxious about serving while Mary sits at Jesus' feet listening to his word; Jesus affirms that Mary has chosen what is better—attentive devotion to him over busyness.

42:11.title: The Lord's Prayer
42:11.summary: A disciple asks Jesus to teach them to pray. Jesus teaches them a prayer focused on honoring God's name, seeking His kingdom, asking for daily provision, requesting forgiveness, and protection from temptation.

42:11.title: Teaching on Persistent Prayer
42:11.summary: Jesus uses the parable of a friend seeking bread at midnight and the example of earthly fathers to teach that God generously answers persistent prayer and gives the Holy Spirit to those who ask.

42:11.title: Jesus Defends His Authority over Demons
42:11.summary: After Jesus casts out a mute demon, critics accuse him of using demonic power. Jesus responds by explaining that a divided kingdom cannot stand, and that casting out demons by God's finger proves the kingdom of God has come; one must be with Him or against Him.

42:11.title: The Return of the Unclean Spirit
42:11.summary: Jesus warns that when a demon leaves a person, if that person does not fill the emptiness with God's presence, the demon may return with others, leaving the person in a worse condition than before.

42:11.title: True Blessedness
42:11.summary: A woman calls out a blessing on Jesus's mother, but Jesus redirects the focus, declaring that true blessing comes not from physical relationship but from hearing and keeping God's word.

42:11.title: The Sign of Jonah
42:11.summary: Jesus tells the crowd that this evil generation seeks a sign but will receive only the sign of Jonah—His resurrection. He warns that the Queen of the South and the Ninevites will rise in judgment against them for rejecting greater wisdom and repentance.

42:11.title: The Lamp of the Body
42:11.summary: Jesus teaches that a lamp is meant to give light, not be hidden. The eye is the lamp of the body; a good eye fills the body with light, while an evil eye brings darkness, and one must ensure the light within is not actually darkness.

42:11.title: Woes Against the Pharisees
42:11.summary: At a Pharisee's dinner, Jesus denounces the Pharisees for focusing on external cleanliness while neglecting justice, love of God, and having hearts full of wickedness. He condemns their pride and hidden corruption.

42:11.title: Woes Against the Lawyers
42:11.summary: A lawyer protests, and Jesus extends His denunciation to the lawyers as well, accusing them of burdening others while refusing to help, building monuments to prophets their fathers killed, hindering knowledge, and preventing people from entering God's kingdom.

42:11.title: Growing Opposition and Plotting
42:11.summary: The scribes and Pharisees become angry and begin to provoke Jesus aggressively, lying in wait to catch Him in something He says so they might accuse Him.

42:12.title: Warning Against the Hypocrisy of the Pharisees
42:12.summary: Jesus warns his disciples to beware of the hypocrisy of the Pharisees, teaching that nothing hidden will remain concealed—what is spoken in darkness will be revealed in the light. This sets the context for authentic discipleship based on truth rather than appearance.

42:12.title: Fear God Rather Than Man
42:12.summary: Jesus encourages his disciples not to fear those who can only kill the body, but rather to fear God who has power over the soul. He assures them of their infinite worth to God and promises that the Holy Spirit will enable them to confess Christ boldly and will teach them what to say when persecuted.

42:12.title: The Parable of the Rich Fool
42:12.summary: When asked to arbitrate an inheritance dispute, Jesus refuses and teaches through the parable of a wealthy farmer who builds bigger barns for his abundance only to die that night, teaching that life consists not in possessions but in being rich toward God. The parable warns against covetousness and the futility of laying up earthly treasure.

42:12.title: Do Not Be Anxious: Trust in God's Provision
42:12.summary: Jesus teaches his disciples to stop being anxious about food and clothing, using examples of ravens and lilies to demonstrate God's care for creation and by extension for them. He calls them to seek God's kingdom first, promises provision will follow, and urges them to give to the needy and store up treasure in heaven rather than on earth.

42:12.title: The Parable of the Waiting Servants
42:12.summary: Jesus calls his followers to be like servants waiting watchfully for their master's return from a wedding feast, dressed and ready to open the door immediately; blessed are those found watchful. The parable emphasizes readiness for the unexpected return of the Son of Man and warns against being caught unprepared.

42:12.title: The Parable of the Faithful Steward
42:12.summary: In response to Peter's question, Jesus teaches about the faithful and wise steward entrusted with household management who diligently tends to duties while awaiting his master's return, contrasting him with the unfaithful servant who abuses his position. The passage teaches that greater responsibility brings greater accountability and judgment according to one's knowledge.

42:12.title: Jesus Brings Division, Not Peace
42:12.summary: Jesus declares that he came to kindle fire on earth and that his mission will bring division rather than peace, even dividing families against themselves. He describes the inevitable conflict that his coming produces as households are split between those who accept and reject him.

42:12.title: Interpreting the Times and Making Peace with Adversaries
42:12.summary: Jesus rebukes the crowds for being able to interpret weather signs but unable to interpret the present time and what it demands. He urges them to judge what is right and to settle disputes with adversaries quickly, warning that unresolved conflict leads to imprisonment and severe consequences.

42:13.title: The Necessity of Repentance
42:13.summary: Jesus corrects the assumption that tragedy strikes the worst sinners by referencing the Galileans killed by Pilate and those crushed by the tower of Siloam. He emphasizes that all people must repent or face similar judgment.

42:13.title: The Parable of the Barren Fig Tree
42:13.summary: A man seeks fruit from a fig tree for three years but finds none and demands it be cut down; a gardener pleads for one more year to tend it, with the threat of removal if it remains unfruitful. The parable illustrates God's patience with Israel and the call to produce spiritual fruit or face judgment.

42:13.title: Healing of the Bent Woman on the Sabbath
42:13.summary: Jesus heals a woman crippled for eighteen years on the Sabbath, freeing her from satanic bondage; when a synagogue ruler protests, Jesus rebukes him for hypocrisy and affirms that works of compassion honor the Sabbath. The crowds rejoice at this display of divine power and compassion.

42:13.title: The Parables of the Mustard Seed and the Yeast
42:13.summary: Jesus compares the Kingdom of God to a mustard seed that grows into a large tree and to yeast that leavens a large amount of flour. Both parables depict how the Kingdom starts small but grows to fill and transform everything.

42:13.title: The Narrow Door and the Excluded
42:13.summary: Jesus teaches that entering the Kingdom requires strenuous effort through the narrow door, as many will seek entry but be unable; those outside will be rejected despite having eaten with Jesus, and unexpected reversals will occur where the last become first. The passage warns of future separation and emphasizes the urgency of genuine discipleship.

42:13.title: Jesus' Journey to Jerusalem and Lament over the City
42:13.summary: Pharisees warn Jesus that Herod seeks to kill him, but Jesus dismisses their concern and declares his intention to journey to Jerusalem where a prophet must perish. He laments over Jerusalem's rejection of prophets and God's desire to gather her children, pronouncing the city desolate until it acknowledges his coming.

42:14.title: Healing of the Man with Dropsy on the Sabbath
42:14.summary: Jesus heals a man with dropsy at a Pharisee's house on the Sabbath, then defends his action by pointing out that the Pharisees themselves would rescue a son or ox from danger on the Sabbath. His defense silences their objections.

42:14.title: Parable of the Guest Who Chooses the Lowest Seat
42:14.summary: Jesus teaches those reclining at the table to choose the lowest seat rather than the best, so that the host may honor them by inviting them higher; this reversal teaches that humility will be exalted and pride will be humbled. The parable illustrates the principle that self-abasement leads to true honor.

42:14.title: Teaching on Generosity to the Poor
42:14.summary: Jesus instructs his host to invite the poor, maimed, lame, and blind rather than friends and relatives, since the poor cannot repay but God will reward such generosity at the resurrection. This teaches radical hospitality grounded in God's future justice.

42:14.title: Parable of the Great Supper
42:14.summary: A man invites many to a great supper, but all make excuses and decline; angered, the master sends his servant to invite the poor, maimed, lame, and blind from the city, and then from the highways and hedges, filling the house while none of the original invitees will taste the supper. The parable depicts God's offer of salvation being rejected by the privileged but accepted by the outcast and humble.

42:14.title: The Cost of Discipleship
42:14.summary: Jesus tells the great crowds following him that discipleship requires renouncing all possessions, family ties, and one's own life, illustrated through two brief parables about counting the cost (building a tower and going to war). True discipleship demands total commitment and surrender of everything to follow him.

42:14.title: Salt Without Flavor
42:14.summary: Jesus declares that salt which has lost its saltiness is useless and fit only to be thrown out, concluding with a call to those with ears to hear. The saying warns that disciples who lose their distinctiveness and commitment are worthless.

42:15.title: Setting and Occasion for the Parables
42:15.summary: Tax collectors and sinners gather to hear Jesus, prompting the Pharisees and scribes to criticize him for associating with such people. Jesus responds by telling them parables to explain his ministry.

42:15.title: The Parable of the Lost Sheep
42:15.summary: A shepherd leaves ninety-nine sheep to search for one lost sheep, and upon finding it, celebrates with joy. Jesus teaches that heaven rejoices more over one sinner who repents than over ninety-nine righteous people.

42:15.title: The Parable of the Lost Coin
42:15.summary: A woman loses one of ten drachma coins and diligently searches for it, then celebrates its recovery with friends and neighbors. Jesus teaches that the angels of God rejoice over one sinner who repents.

42:15.title: The Parable of the Prodigal Son
42:15.summary: A younger son wastes his inheritance in reckless living, falls into poverty and desperation, and returns home in repentance. His father receives him with overwhelming love, compassion, and celebration, restoring him to the family.

42:15.title: The Elder Son's Resentment
42:15.summary: The elder son, who remained faithful and obedient, becomes angry and refuses to join the celebration, resenting his father's lavish welcome of his wayward brother. The father appeals to him, explaining that celebration is appropriate because the lost has been found and the dead restored to life.

42:16.title: The Parable of the Dishonest Manager
42:16.summary: A rich man's manager is accused of wasting his possessions and called to account. Before losing his position, the manager shrewdly reduces his lord's debtors' bills to secure future favor. The lord commends his cunning, illustrating how worldly people are shrewder than the righteous in pursuing their goals.

42:16.title: On Faithfulness with Wealth and Serving Two Masters
42:16.summary: Jesus teaches his disciples to use unrighteous wealth to make friends for eternity, emphasizing that faithfulness in small matters leads to faithfulness in great ones. He concludes that no one can serve both God and money—one master must be chosen.

42:16.title: Jesus Confronts the Pharisees on Self-Justification and the Law
42:16.summary: The money-loving Pharisees mock Jesus, but he exposes their hypocrisy: they justify themselves before people while God sees their corrupt hearts. Jesus affirms the enduring validity of the law and illustrates this with the teaching on divorce as adultery.

42:16.title: The Parable of the Rich Man and Lazarus
42:16.summary: A wealthy man ignores a suffering beggar at his gate; after death, their fates are reversed—Lazarus is comforted with Abraham while the rich man suffers in Hades. The rich man begs Abraham to warn his brothers, but Abraham replies that those who reject Scripture will not repent even if someone rises from the dead.

42:17.title: Teaching on Causing Stumbling and Forgiveness
42:17.summary: Jesus warns against causing others to sin and instructs his disciples to rebuke offenders and forgive repentant brothers repeatedly, without limit. This establishes the serious responsibility of believers toward one another and the necessity of unlimited forgiveness.

42:17.title: Faith Like a Mustard Seed and Unprofitable Servants
42:17.summary: When apostles ask for increased faith, Jesus teaches that even faith as small as a mustard seed can accomplish the impossible, then illustrates through a parable that servants who obey their master deserve no special thanks, only to perform their duty. The lesson emphasizes that faith, however small, is sufficient, and believers should maintain humble service without expectation of reward.

42:17.title: The Ten Lepers and the Grateful Samaritan
42:17.summary: Jesus heals ten lepers who cry out for mercy, but only one—a despised Samaritan—returns to thank him and glorify God. Jesus praises the Samaritan's faith and healing, highlighting the gratitude and faith of an outsider contrasted with the ingratitude of the nine.

42:17.title: The Kingdom of God is Within You
42:17.summary: When Pharisees ask when the Kingdom of God will come, Jesus declares it does not come with observable signs but is within them. He then turns to his disciples to warn that the future coming of the Son of Man will be sudden and unmistakable, but first he must suffer and be rejected.

42:17.title: Signs of the Son of Man's Coming and Judgment
42:17.summary: Jesus compares the coming judgment to the days of Noah and Lot, when people lived in complacency until sudden destruction came. He warns of the sudden, visible nature of the Son of Man's revelation and the need to abandon earthly possessions and life itself; in that day some will be taken and others left.

42:17.title: Where the Body Is, There the Vultures Gather
42:17.summary: When disciples ask where these events will occur, Jesus cryptically responds that judgment will gather where the dead body lies, indicating that the judgment is certain and inevitable, like vultures gathering over a carcass.

42:18.title: The Parable of the Persistent Widow
42:18.summary: Jesus teaches that believers must pray persistently without losing faith, using the example of a widow who wears down an unjust judge with her continual pleas. He assures that God will answer His chosen ones' prayers quickly, but questions whether faith will remain when the Son of Man returns.

42:18.title: The Parable of the Pharisee and the Tax Collector
42:18.summary: Jesus contrasts two men praying in the temple: a self-righteous Pharisee who boasts of his deeds, and a humble tax collector who merely asks for God's mercy. The tax collector is justified, teaching that humility and repentance, not self-exaltation, bring acceptance before God.

42:18.title: Jesus Welcomes Little Children
42:18.summary: When disciples rebuke people bringing babies to Jesus, he welcomes the children and teaches that the Kingdom of God belongs to those who receive it with childlike faith and dependence. Entry into God's kingdom requires this same receptive, humble attitude.

42:18.title: The Rich Ruler and the Cost of Discipleship
42:18.summary: A wealthy ruler asks Jesus how to inherit eternal life, but when Jesus commands him to sell his possessions and follow him, the man refuses because of his riches. Jesus teaches that wealth can hinder salvation, but what is impossible for humans is possible with God; Peter and the disciples, who have left everything, are assured of receiving far more in return.

42:18.title: Jesus Predicts His Passion
42:18.summary: Jesus tells the twelve disciples privately that they are going to Jerusalem where all prophecies about the Son of Man will be fulfilled through his betrayal to the Gentiles, mockery, abuse, scourging, death, and resurrection on the third day. The disciples fail to understand these words, and their meaning remains hidden from them.

42:18.title: Jesus Heals a Blind Beggar near Jericho
42:18.summary: A blind beggar outside Jericho recognizes Jesus and cries out for mercy despite attempts to silence him; Jesus grants his request and heals him through faith. The healed man follows Jesus and glorifies God, and the crowd praises God for the miracle.

42:19.title: Zacchaeus the Tax Collector
42:19.summary: Jesus encounters Zacchaeus, a rich chief tax collector in Jericho, who climbs a tree to see him. Jesus invites himself to Zacchaeus's house, and the tax collector responds by pledging to give half his wealth to the poor and restore fourfold anything he has wrongfully taken. Jesus declares that salvation has come to his house, affirming his mission to seek and save the lost.

42:19.title: The Parable of the Ten Minas
42:19.summary: Jesus tells a parable about a nobleman who gives ten servants minas to invest before traveling to receive a kingdom. Upon his return, he rewards the servants who multiplied their investments with authority over cities, but condemns the servant who buried his mina out of fear. The parable illustrates accountability, the use of gifts, and judgment on those who reject the master's rule.

42:19.title: The Triumphal Entry into Jerusalem
42:19.summary: Jesus sends two disciples to retrieve an unridden colt, which they bring to him. The disciples place their cloaks on the colt and Jesus rides toward Jerusalem as crowds spread cloaks in his way and praise him as the coming King. When Pharisees demand he rebuke his disciples, Jesus declares that the stones themselves would cry out in praise.

42:19.title: Jesus Weeps Over Jerusalem
42:19.summary: As Jesus approaches Jerusalem, he weeps over the city, lamenting that it has not recognized the time of God's visitation and the things that bring peace. He prophesies the coming siege and destruction of the temple, emphasizing the tragic consequences of rejecting him.

42:19.title: Jesus Cleanses the Temple
42:19.summary: Jesus enters the temple and drives out those engaged in buying and selling, declaring that the temple should be a house of prayer rather than a den of robbers. He teaches daily in the temple while the chief priests and scribes seek to destroy him, but they cannot act because the people hang on his every word.

42:20.title: Jesus' Authority Questioned
42:20.summary: The religious leaders challenge Jesus about his authority to teach and preach in the temple. Jesus responds by turning their question back on them, asking about John the Baptist's authority, which leaves them unable to answer without self-incrimination.

42:20.title: The Parable of the Wicked Tenants
42:20.summary: Jesus tells a parable about a vineyard owner who sends servants and then his son to collect fruit, but the tenants reject and kill them all. Jesus warns that the owner will destroy the tenants and give the vineyard to others, illustrating God's judgment on Israel's rejection of his messengers and his Son.

42:20.title: The Question About Paying Taxes to Caesar
42:20.summary: Jesus' opponents seek to trap him with a question about paying taxes to Caesar, but he perceives their malice and responds with the famous dictum to give Caesar what is Caesar's and God what is God's, silencing them before the people.

42:20.title: The Sadducees' Question About the Resurrection
42:20.summary: Sadducees who reject resurrection present Jesus with a hypothetical about a woman married to seven brothers, asking whose wife she would be in the resurrection. Jesus explains that the resurrected live like angels in a different order of existence where death and marriage no longer apply, proving that God is the God of the living.
themes: transformation in the age to come

42:20.title: The Question About the Messiah's Identity
42:20.summary: Jesus asks how the Christ can be David's son when David himself calls the Messiah his Lord in Psalm 110, implying that the Christ is greater than David and not merely his descendant.

42:20.title: Warning Against the Scribes
42:20.summary: Jesus warns his disciples to beware of scribes who seek honors and recognition while exploiting the vulnerable, saying they will receive greater condemnation for their hypocrisy and abuse of power.

42:21.title: The Widow's Offering
42:21.summary: Jesus observes a poor widow give two small coins to the temple treasury, declaring that she has given more than the rich people because she gave from her poverty rather than their abundance. This passage illustrates that true generosity is measured by sacrifice and devotion rather than the amount given.

42:21.title: Jesus Predicts the Temple's Destruction
42:21.summary: When disciples admire the temple's beautiful construction and decorations, Jesus prophesies that not one stone will be left standing on another. This foretells the Roman destruction of Jerusalem in 70 AD.

42:21.title: Signs of the End Times and Persecution of Believers
42:21.summary: Jesus warns his followers about false messiahs, wars, and natural disasters that will precede the end, but cautions them not to be terrified since the end will not come immediately. He then teaches that before these cosmic events, believers will face persecution before synagogues and rulers, but the Holy Spirit will give them wisdom in their defense, and endurance will lead to salvation.

42:21.title: The Siege of Jerusalem and Desolation of the City
42:21.summary: Jesus instructs believers to flee when they see Jerusalem surrounded by armies, describing this as the fulfillment of vengeance and judgment on the city. He warns of the suffering that will come, particularly for pregnant women and nursing mothers, and predicts that Jerusalem will fall by the sword and its people will be scattered among the nations.

42:21.title: Cosmic Signs and the Coming of the Son of Man
42:21.summary: Jesus describes the cosmic upheaval that will accompany his return—signs in the heavens, nations in distress and confusion, and the terrified reaction of people. He then promises that believers should look up with hope because their redemption draws near when the Son of Man comes in power and great glory.

42:21.title: The Parable of the Fig Tree and Assurance of Jesus' Words
42:21.summary: Jesus compares the signs of his coming to the budding of fig trees in spring, teaching that when believers see these things happening, they will know the Kingdom of God is near. He assures them that his generation will witness the fulfillment and that his words are eternally reliable, even when heaven and earth pass away.

42:21.title: Warning to Stay Alert and Pray
42:21.summary: Jesus warns disciples not to let their hearts be weighed down by excess living and worldly cares, for the day of judgment will come unexpectedly like a snare. He exhorts them to be watchful at all times and pray for worthiness to escape these judgments and stand before the Son of Man.

42:21.title: Jesus' Daily Ministry in Jerusalem
42:21.summary: Luke notes that during this period, Jesus spent his days teaching in the temple and his nights on the Mount of Olives, with crowds gathering early each morning to hear him teach. This passage establishes the routine of Jesus' final ministry days before his arrest.

42:22.title: The Plot to Kill Jesus and Judas's Betrayal
42:22.summary: As Passover approaches, the chief priests and scribes plot Jesus's death while fearing the people, and Satan enters Judas Iscariot to arrange his betrayal for money. The stage is set for Jesus's arrest through the treachery of one of his twelve apostles.

42:22.title: Preparation of the Passover
42:22.summary: Jesus sends Peter and John to prepare the Passover meal, directing them to find a man carrying water and securing an upper room, which they do exactly as he described. The disciples faithfully execute Jesus's instructions, demonstrating his supernatural knowledge.

42:22.title: The Last Supper and Institution of the Lord's Supper
42:22.summary: At the Passover meal with his twelve apostles, Jesus shares bread and wine as symbols of his body and blood given for them, establishing a memorial practice while announcing his betrayal. Jesus transforms the ancient Passover into a new covenant meal pointing to his sacrificial death.

42:22.title: Jesus Teaches on Greatness and Authority
42:22.summary: When the disciples argue over who is greatest, Jesus teaches that true greatness consists of humble service rather than dominion, modeling this by serving at the table, and promises his faithful disciples a share in his kingdom and authority. Jesus inverts worldly values by defining greatness through servanthood.

42:22.title: Jesus Predicts Peter's Denial
42:22.summary: Jesus warns Simon Peter that Satan has demanded to sift him like wheat, but assures him that Jesus has prayed for his faith to endure; Peter will deny knowing Jesus three times before the rooster crows, despite his bold claims of loyalty. Jesus foreknows Peter's failure yet promises ultimate restoration.

42:22.title: Jesus Prepares Disciples for Hardship
42:22.summary: Jesus recalls sending his disciples without provisions and receiving abundance, but now commands them to prepare for hardship by acquiring purses, wallets, and swords, fulfilling the scripture that he will be counted among transgressors. Jesus signals the shift from his protective ministry to a coming season of suffering and conflict.

42:22.title: Jesus Prays in Agony at Gethsemane
42:22.summary: Jesus goes to the Mount of Olives as his custom and prays earnestly, asking his Father to remove the cup of suffering yet submitting to God's will, while an angel strengthens him and his sweat falls like great drops of blood; he finds his disciples sleeping and urges them to pray against temptation. Jesus models surrender to God's purpose amid intense emotional and spiritual struggle.

42:22.title: The Arrest of Jesus
42:22.summary: Judas leads an armed multitude to Jesus and betrays him with a kiss; when a disciple strikes the high priest's servant's ear, Jesus heals him and rebukes the arresting party for coming with weapons as if against a criminal when he was accessible in the temple daily. Jesus's arrest fulfills the appointed hour when darkness has power, and even in capture he demonstrates healing and mercy.

42:22.title: Peter's Three Denials
42:22.summary: After Jesus is taken to the high priest's house, Peter follows at a distance and sits by the fire, but three times he denies knowing Jesus when questioned—first by a servant girl, then by another person, and finally by someone who recognizes his Galilean accent; a rooster crows, and Peter remembers Jesus's prediction and weeps bitterly. Peter's promised failure comes to pass exactly as Jesus foretold, leaving him with remorse and the weight of his broken oath.

42:22.title: Mockery and Beating of Jesus
42:22.summary: The men holding Jesus mock him, beat him, blindfold him, strike him on the face, and demand he prophesy who hit him, while insulting him with many other taunts. Jesus endures degradation and abuse at the hands of those who hold him in custody.

42:22.title: Jesus Before the Sanhedrin
42:22.summary: At daybreak, the council of elders, chief priests, and scribes question Jesus about whether he is the Christ; Jesus responds that they will not believe him either way, but announces that the Son of Man will be seated at God's right hand, leading them to ask if he claims to be God's Son, to which he affirms their accusation is correct. Jesus's direct claim to divine sonship gives the council the charge they need to condemn him.

42:23.title: Jesus Brought Before Pilate
42:23.summary: The Jewish council brings Jesus before Pilate and accuses him of perverting the nation, forbidding taxes to Caesar, and claiming to be Christ the king. Pilate questions Jesus and initially finds no basis for charges against him, but the accusers insist he stirs up the people throughout Judea.

42:23.title: Jesus Before Herod
42:23.summary: Pilate sends Jesus to Herod Antipas when learning he is a Galilean. Herod, long hoping to see a miracle, questions Jesus extensively but receives no answers, and his soldiers mock and humiliate him before sending him back to Pilate, resulting in Herod and Pilate becoming friends.

42:23.title: Pilate Condemns Jesus to Crucifixion
42:23.summary: Pilate assembles the people and declares Jesus innocent three times, but the crowd demands the release of Barabbas and insistently call for Jesus' crucifixion. Despite his reluctance, Pilate yields to their will and releases the murderer Barabbas while delivering Jesus to be crucified.

42:23.title: The Road to the Cross
42:23.summary: Simon of Cyrene is compelled to carry Jesus' cross as a great multitude follows, including mourning women. Jesus addresses the women, warning them to weep for themselves and their children rather than for him, as coming judgment will be severe.

42:23.title: The Crucifixion and the Penitent Thief
42:23.summary: Jesus is crucified at the Skull with two criminals, praying forgiveness for his killers as soldiers cast lots for his garments. Rulers and soldiers mock him, one criminal insults him, but the other rebukes the first, acknowledges their guilt, and asks Jesus to remember him, receiving the promise of paradise.

42:23.title: The Death of Jesus
42:23.summary: Darkness covers the land for three hours; the temple veil tears in two. Jesus commits his spirit to God and dies, prompting the centurion to recognize him as righteous. The multitudes return home in grief, while his acquaintances and the women from Galilee stand at a distance witnessing everything.

42:23.title: The Burial of Jesus
42:23.summary: Joseph of Arimathaea, a righteous council member who had not consented to Jesus' condemnation, requests Jesus' body from Pilate and buries it in an unused tomb. The women from Galilee witness the burial and prepare to anoint his body with spices after the Sabbath.

42:24.title: The Resurrection of Jesus
42:24.summary: Early on the first day of the week, women come to Jesus' tomb with spices but find it empty. Two angels announce that Jesus has risen, reminding them of his earlier words. The women report this to the apostles, but the disciples disbelieve until Peter runs to the tomb and sees the empty linen wrappings.

42:24.title: The Road to Emmaus
42:24.summary: Two disciples traveling to Emmaus encounter the risen Jesus but don't recognize him; he interprets the Scriptures concerning himself and joins them for a meal, where they finally recognize him in the breaking of bread. They immediately return to Jerusalem to tell the apostles that Jesus has risen.

42:24.title: Jesus Appears to the Eleven
42:24.summary: Jesus appears to the apostles in Jerusalem, offering peace and demonstrating his physical reality through his wounds, eating fish, and opening their minds to understand the Scriptures. He commissions them to preach repentance and forgiveness of sins to all nations and promises them power from the Holy Spirit.

42:24.title: The Ascension and the Disciples' Response
42:24.summary: Jesus leads the disciples to Bethany, blesses them, and is taken up into heaven. The disciples worship him and return to Jerusalem with great joy, remaining in the temple praising and blessing God.

43:1.title: The Word: Divine Nature and Function
43:1.summary: The Word (logos) exists eternally with God and is God, through whom all creation came into being. This Word is the source of life and light that shines in darkness and cannot be overcome.

43:1.title: John the Baptist's Witness
43:1.summary: John the Baptist was sent by God to testify about the light (Jesus), so that all might believe through his witness. John himself is not the light but merely a witness to it.

43:1.title: The Light Comes to the World
43:1.summary: The true light is coming into the world, yet the world rejects him, and those who were his own do not receive him. However, all who receive him gain the right to become God's children through belief in his name.

43:1.title: The Word Becomes Flesh
43:1.summary: The Word became flesh and dwelt among us, revealing the glory of God's only Son, full of grace and truth. Jesus surpasses Moses by bringing grace and truth, and he alone has revealed God to humanity.

43:1.title: John the Baptist's Interrogation and Self-Identification
43:1.summary: When Jerusalem's religious authorities question John about his identity, he denies being the Christ, Elijah, or the prophet, but identifies himself as the voice preparing the way for the Lord as Isaiah foretold. He baptizes in water while pointing to one greater who stands among them.

43:1.title: Jesus Revealed as the Lamb of God
43:1.summary: John identifies Jesus as the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world and testifies that he has seen the Spirit descend and remain upon Jesus, confirming him as the Son of God.

43:1.title: The First Disciples Follow Jesus
43:1.summary: Two of John's disciples follow Jesus after hearing his testimony; one of them is Andrew, who brings his brother Simon to Jesus, who is renamed Peter. These are among the first to recognize Jesus as the Messiah.

43:1.title: Philip and Nathanael Become Disciples
43:1.summary: Jesus calls Philip to follow him, and Philip brings Nathanael, identifying Jesus as the one promised in Moses and the prophets. Jesus demonstrates supernatural knowledge of Nathanael, who responds with faith, and Jesus promises him a vision of heaven opened and angels ascending and descending on the Son of Man.

43:2.title: Jesus Turns Water into Wine
43:2.summary: At a wedding in Cana, Jesus transforms water into wine when the supply runs out, performing his first miraculous sign. This act reveals his glory and causes his disciples to believe in him.

43:2.title: Jesus Goes to Capernaum and Jerusalem
43:2.summary: Jesus travels to Capernaum with his mother and disciples, then goes up to Jerusalem for the Passover feast.

43:2.title: Jesus Cleanses the Temple
43:2.summary: Jesus drives out merchants and money-changers from the temple, declaring it should not be a marketplace. His disciples recognize this act fulfills Scripture about zeal for God's house.

43:2.title: Jesus and the Sign of the Temple
43:2.summary: The Jews demand a sign justifying Jesus's actions; he cryptically refers to destroying and rebuilding the temple in three days, meaning his body's resurrection. His disciples later understand this prophecy after the resurrection.

43:2.title: Jesus's Discernment During the Feast
43:2.summary: Many people believe in Jesus when they see his miraculous signs during the Passover feast, yet Jesus does not commit himself to them because he knows all people and understands what is in human hearts.

43:3.title: Jesus Teaches Nicodemus about Being Born Again
43:3.summary: Nicodemus, a Pharisee and ruler, visits Jesus by night and acknowledges Him as a teacher from God. Jesus explains that one must be born anew—of water and Spirit—to enter God's kingdom, using the metaphor of wind to describe the mysterious work of the Holy Spirit.

43:3.title: Jesus Explains Judgment, Belief, and Light versus Darkness
43:3.summary: When Nicodemus struggles to understand, Jesus reveals that He is the Son of Man who descended from heaven and must be lifted up like the serpent in the wilderness, so that believers gain eternal life. God's love is demonstrated in giving His Son; judgment comes not from condemnation but from rejection of the light, as people prefer darkness because of their evil deeds.

43:3.title: Jesus and His Disciples Baptize in Judea
43:3.summary: Jesus moves to Judea with his disciples and begins a baptizing ministry there, while John the Baptist is also baptizing at Enon near Salim, having not yet been imprisoned.

43:3.title: John the Baptist Decreases; Jesus Increases
43:3.summary: A dispute arises among John's disciples about Jesus's growing baptizing ministry. John affirms that his role is to prepare the way, comparing himself to a friend of the bridegroom who rejoices in the bridegroom's voice; he declares that Jesus must increase while he himself must decrease.

43:3.title: The Authority and Witness of the Son from Heaven
43:3.summary: Jesus, who comes from above, is superior to all earthly things and testifies to what He has seen and heard from the Father. Those who receive His testimony confirm God's truthfulness; the Father loves the Son and has given all things into His hand, and eternal life belongs to those who believe in the Son, while God's wrath remains on those who disobey.

43:4.title: Jesus Departs Judea for Galilee
43:4.summary: Learning that the Pharisees have heard of his growing baptism ministry, Jesus leaves Judea and heads toward Galilee. This departure sets the stage for his encounter with the Samaritan woman.

43:4.title: Jesus at Jacob's Well
43:4.summary: Jesus arrives at a well in Samaria, near the city of Sychar, and sits down tired from his journey. The setting establishes the context for his transformative conversation with a Samaritan woman.

43:4.title: Jesus and the Samaritan Woman—Living Water
43:4.summary: A Samaritan woman comes to draw water, and Jesus asks her for a drink, breaking both gender and ethnic barriers. Jesus offers her 'living water' that will satisfy her spiritually forever, and she eagerly requests this gift.

43:4.title: Jesus Reveals Her Life and Identity
43:4.summary: Jesus reveals intimate knowledge of the woman's marital history and identifies himself as the Messiah she has been awaiting. His supernatural knowledge and self-disclosure lead her to recognize him as a prophet and Christ.

43:4.title: The Woman's Testimony and the City's Response
43:4.summary: The disciples return and are amazed to find Jesus speaking with a woman, but the Samaritan woman leaves her water pot and testifies to the townspeople about Jesus. Many Samaritans come out to see him based on her witness.

43:4.title: Jesus's Food: Doing God's Will and the Harvest
43:4.summary: When the disciples urge Jesus to eat, he explains that his true nourishment is completing God's work. He then teaches them about the spiritual harvest that is ripe and ready, instructing them to reap fruit for eternal life.

43:4.title: The Samaritans Believe and Confess Christ
43:4.summary: Many Samaritans from the city believe in Jesus because of the woman's testimony, and after Jesus stays with them for two days, even more believe through his own word. They confess him as the Savior of the world.

43:4.title: Jesus Travels to Galilee and Is Received
43:4.summary: After two days, Jesus departs for Galilee. Though a prophet lacks honor in his own country, the Galileans welcome him because they had witnessed his miraculous signs at the Jerusalem feast.

43:4.title: The Healing of the Nobleman's Son
43:4.summary: A royal official from Capernaum begs Jesus to heal his dying son; Jesus declares the boy will live, and the man believes and departs. The servants confirm the boy's fever left at the exact hour Jesus spoke, and the official and his household believe. This is recorded as Jesus's second sign.

43:5.title: The Healing of the Man at the Pool of Bethesda
43:5.summary: Jesus heals a man who has been paralyzed for thirty-eight years at the pool of Bethesda on the Sabbath, commanding him to take up his mat and walk. The immediate healing demonstrates Jesus's power over sickness and disability.

43:5.title: The Pharisees Question the Sabbath Healing
43:5.summary: The Jewish leaders accuse the healed man of breaking the Sabbath law by carrying his mat, then pursue and seek to kill Jesus when they discover he performed the healing, viewing his claim that God is his Father as blasphemous equality with God.

43:5.title: The Son's Authority and Judgment
43:5.summary: Jesus explains that he acts in perfect unity with the Father, possessing all the Father's authority including the power to give life and execute judgment, and that all people will hear his voice and be resurrected to either life or condemnation.

43:5.title: The Testimony About Jesus
43:5.summary: Jesus presents multiple witnesses to his identity—his own righteous judgment, John the Baptist's testimony, the works the Father gave him, and the Father's own witness—yet appeals to the Jews that they refuse to come to him for life despite searching the Scriptures that testify about him.

43:5.title: The Root of Unbelief
43:5.summary: Jesus exposes the spiritual condition of the Jews: their desire for human glory prevents belief, they lack God's love, they reject Jesus despite his coming in the Father's name, and they would believe Moses if they truly read his writings, which testify about Jesus.

43:6.title: The Feeding of the Five Thousand
43:6.summary: Jesus feeds a crowd of five thousand with five loaves and two fish, demonstrating his divine power and compassion. The people recognize him as the prophet promised to come into the world.

43:6.title: Jesus Walks on Water
43:6.summary: Jesus withdraws to the mountain while his disciples cross the sea, and he comes to them by walking on the water during a storm, calming their fears and bringing them safely to shore. This demonstrates his divine nature and authority over creation.

43:6.title: The Crowd Seeks Jesus and He Redirects Their Focus
43:6.summary: The crowd crosses the sea seeking Jesus and finds him, but he rebukes them for seeking him only for physical food rather than spiritual truth. Jesus calls them to believe in him as the one sent by God.

43:6.title: Jesus Declares Himself the Bread of Life
43:6.summary: Jesus claims to be the true bread from heaven, superior to the manna Moses provided, offering eternal life to all who believe in him. He assures that all given to him by the Father will come to him and be raised on the last day.

43:6.title: The Jews Question Jesus's Origin and Teaching
43:6.summary: The Jews murmur against Jesus's claim to have come from heaven, knowing his earthly parents, but Jesus teaches that belief comes through the Father's drawing and instruction. He further explains that he is the living bread whose flesh gives life to the world.

43:6.title: The Hard Teaching About Eating Jesus's Flesh and Drinking His Blood
43:6.summary: Jesus teaches the radical truth that believers must consume his flesh and drink his blood to have life, explaining that his flesh is true food and blood is true drink. He contrasts his life-giving bread with the manna that sustained the patriarchs but did not prevent their death.

43:6.title: Offense at Jesus's Words and Peter's Confession
43:6.summary: Many disciples find Jesus's teaching difficult and hard to accept, but Jesus explains that his words are spirit and life, not merely physical. Peter confesses Jesus as the Christ and Son of the living God, while Jesus reveals that one of the twelve—Judas—will betray him.

43:7.title: Jesus' Brothers Urge Him to Go to the Feast
43:7.summary: Jesus avoids Judea where the Jews seek to kill him, but his unbelieving brothers encourage him to go publicly to the Feast of Booths to reveal himself to the world. Jesus explains that his time has not yet come and remains in Galilee while they depart.

43:7.title: Jesus Goes to the Feast in Secret
43:7.summary: After his brothers leave, Jesus goes to the feast secretly while the Jews search for him and the crowds debate whether he is a good man or a deceiver, all speaking in fear.

43:7.title: Jesus Teaches and Justifies His Sabbath Healing
43:7.summary: At the midst of the feast, Jesus teaches in the temple and answers critics who question his authority and accuse him of breaking the law by healing on the Sabbath, arguing that circumcision on the Sabbath shows the priority of human wholeness.

43:7.title: Divided Responses to Jesus as the Christ
43:7.summary: Some in Jerusalem recognize that Jesus is the one they seek to kill yet speak openly without intervention, wondering if the rulers know he is the Christ, while others dispute his origins. Many believe based on his signs.

43:7.title: Arrest Plans and Jesus' Cryptic Words About Departure
43:7.summary: The Pharisees and chief priests send officers to arrest Jesus, but Jesus declares he will be with them only briefly before returning to the one who sent him, leaving the Jews confused about his cryptic meaning.

43:7.title: Jesus Offers Living Water and Speaks of the Spirit
43:7.summary: On the last day of the feast, Jesus cries out inviting the thirsty to come and drink, promising rivers of living water that refer to the Holy Spirit believers will receive after his glorification.

43:7.title: Division Over Jesus' Identity
43:7.summary: The crowd is divided, with some calling him the prophet and others the Christ, while some object based on his Galilean origin and messianic expectations tied to Bethlehem and David's lineage.

43:7.title: Officers Refuse to Arrest; Pharisees Dismiss the Crowd
