43:7.summary: The officers sent to arrest Jesus return without him, amazed at his speech; when mocked by the Pharisees, Nicodemus defends procedural justice while the Pharisees dismiss the unlearned crowd as cursed and claim no prophet arises from Galilee.

43:7.title: Dispersal at Day's End
43:7.summary: Everyone departs to their own homes, concluding the events of the feast day.

43:8.title: The Woman Caught in Adultery
43:8.summary: Jesus is brought a woman caught in adultery by the scribes and Pharisees, who test him with the Law's command to stone her. Jesus turns the accusers' attention inward by challenging anyone without sin to cast the first stone, and when all leave, he compassionately forgives the woman while calling her to sin no more.

43:8.title: Jesus as the Light of the World
43:8.summary: Jesus declares himself the light of the world and affirms that his testimony is valid because he knows his origin and destiny, backed by the Father's testimony. The Pharisees challenge his self-testimony, but Jesus explains the validity of his witness and teaches in the temple treasury.

43:8.title: Division Over Jesus's Origin and Departure
43:8.summary: Jesus announces his departure and warns that those who do not believe in him will die in their sins, creating confusion among the Jews about where he is going. Jesus clarifies the cosmic divide between his heavenly origin and their earthly nature, and declares that belief in him as 'I AM' is essential for salvation, resulting in many believing.

43:8.title: Truth, Freedom, and Spiritual Parentage
43:8.summary: Jesus teaches his believing disciples that remaining in his word leads to knowing the truth and being made free, but the Jews misunderstand, claiming Abrahamic descent exempts them from bondage. Jesus exposes their spiritual captivity to sin and reveals that their true father is the devil, not God, because they reject his truth.

43:8.title: Jesus's Claim to Eternal Existence and Attempted Stoning
43:8.summary: The Jews accuse Jesus of being a Samaritan and demon-possessed, but Jesus affirms his union with the Father and promises eternal life to those who keep his word. When he declares his existence before Abraham with the divine name 'I AM,' the Jews attempt to stone him, but he escapes through the temple.

43:9.title: Jesus Encounters the Man Born Blind
43:9.summary: Jesus encounters a man blind from birth and reframes the disciples' question about sin, explaining that the blindness exists to reveal God's works. Jesus declares himself the light of the world and his mission to do the work of the one who sent him.

43:9.title: Jesus Heals the Blind Man
43:9.summary: Jesus makes mud with saliva, anoints the man's eyes, and sends him to wash in the pool of Siloam, restoring his sight. Neighbors and acquaintances recognize the miracle, and the healed man testifies that Jesus opened his eyes.

43:9.title: The Pharisees Question the Healed Man
43:9.summary: The healed man is brought to the Pharisees, who are divided over whether Jesus could perform such a sign while breaking the Sabbath. The man testifies again and confesses that Jesus is a prophet.

43:9.title: The Pharisees Question the Man's Parents
43:9.summary: The Pharisees summon the parents to verify the miracle, but the parents, fearing expulsion from the synagogue, refuse to give details and defer to their son's testimony. Their response reveals the threat posed to anyone acknowledging Jesus as the Messiah.

43:9.title: The Healed Man Confronts the Pharisees
43:9.summary: Interrogated again, the man boldly defends Jesus, pointing out the logical impossibility that a sinner could perform such an unprecedented miracle, and questions whether the Pharisees wish to become disciples. The Pharisees insult him and expel him from the synagogue.

43:9.title: Jesus Restores and Teaches the Expelled Man
43:9.summary: Jesus finds the expelled man, reveals himself as the Son of God, and the man worships him. Jesus then pronounces judgment: those blind to truth may see, but those who claim to see remain in sin, condemning the Pharisees' spiritual blindness.

43:10.title: The Parable of the Shepherd and the Sheep
43:10.summary: Jesus teaches a parable about a shepherd who enters the fold by the door, calls his sheep by name, and leads them out while they recognize and follow his voice, rejecting strangers. The passage establishes the metaphor of authentic spiritual leadership versus theft and deception.

43:10.title: Jesus as the Door to Salvation
43:10.summary: Jesus reveals that he is the door of the sheep fold, through which entry brings salvation, freedom to go in and out, and abundant life, contrasting his life-giving purpose with the thief who comes only to steal, kill, and destroy. This passage explains the parable's meaning and Jesus's salvific role.

43:10.title: The Good Shepherd and His Sacrifice
43:10.summary: Jesus identifies himself as the good shepherd who lays down his life for his sheep, contrasting himself with hired hands who flee when danger comes, and promises to unite all his sheep into one flock while explaining his authority over his own death and resurrection. This passage reveals Jesus's sacrificial love and divine power.

43:10.title: Division and Debate About Jesus
43:10.summary: Jesus's words cause division among the Jews, with some claiming he is demon-possessed and insane, while others argue that demonic possession cannot produce the healing miracles Jesus has performed. The passage illustrates the conflicting responses to Jesus's identity and works.

43:10.title: Jesus's Identity at the Feast of Dedication
43:10.summary: During the Feast of the Dedication in winter, the Jews confront Jesus demanding he plainly declare whether he is the Christ; Jesus responds that his works testify about him and that his sheep hear his voice, follow him, and receive eternal life that no one can take from them, and declares his unity with the Father. This passage presents Jesus's implicit claim to messianic and divine identity.

43:10.title: Jesus Accused of Blasphemy and His Defense
43:10.summary: The Jews attempt to stone Jesus for blasphemy, claiming he makes himself God while being merely human; Jesus defends himself by appealing to Scripture's use of 'gods' for those who received God's word, then challenges his accusers to believe either in his identity or at least in his works as evidence of the Father's presence in him. This passage shows Jesus's legal and scriptural defense against the charge of blasphemy.

43:10.title: Jesus Withdraws and Many Believe
43:10.summary: Jesus departs beyond the Jordan to the place where John the Baptist first baptized, where many people come to him and testify that though John performed no signs, everything he said about Jesus proved true, and many believe in him there. This passage shows the contrast between John's testimony and Jesus's confirmatory works, resulting in faith.

43:11.title: Lazarus Falls Ill and Jesus Delays
43:11.summary: Lazarus of Bethany becomes ill, and his sisters Mary and Martha send word to Jesus. Jesus receives the news and declares the sickness is for God's glory, yet he delays two days before going to Lazarus.

43:11.title: Jesus Decides to Return to Judea
43:11.summary: Jesus tells his disciples they are returning to Judea despite the danger of being stoned. Using metaphors of light and darkness, Jesus speaks of going to awaken Lazarus, and the disciples misunderstand until he plainly states Lazarus is dead. Thomas expresses willingness to die with Jesus.

43:11.title: Jesus's Dialogue with Martha: 'I Am the Resurrection'
43:11.summary: Jesus arrives at Bethany where Lazarus has been dead four days. Martha meets him, expressing sorrow yet faith that God will grant his requests. Jesus reveals himself as the resurrection and life itself, and Martha confesses her belief that he is the Christ, the Son of God.

43:11.title: Mary's Grief and Jesus's Compassion
43:11.summary: Martha summons Mary, who rushes to Jesus and falls at his feet with the same lament as her sister. Witnessing the weeping of Mary and the Jews, Jesus is deeply moved with emotion. The crowd observes his tears as a sign of his love for Lazarus.

43:11.title: The Raising of Lazarus
43:11.summary: Jesus approaches the tomb and commands the stone removed. He prays, thanking the Father for hearing him so the witnesses might believe, then calls Lazarus forth by name. The dead man emerges bound in grave clothes, and Jesus commands him to be freed.

43:11.title: The Sanhedrin's Plot to Kill Jesus
43:11.summary: Many witnesses believe in Jesus after seeing Lazarus raised, but others report the miracle to the Pharisees. The chief priests and Pharisees convene, fearing Jesus will draw all people and incite Roman intervention. Caiaphas, the high priest, argues it is expedient for one man to die rather than lose the nation, unknowingly prophesying Jesus's sacrificial death. The council resolves to kill him.

43:11.title: Jesus Withdraws and the Search Intensifies
43:11.summary: Jesus withdraws from public ministry among the Jews and retreats to Ephraim near the wilderness. As the Passover approaches, people seek him in the temple, and the chief priests and Pharisees issue orders to report his whereabouts so they may arrest him.

43:12.title: Mary Anoints Jesus at Bethany
43:12.summary: At a supper in Bethany six days before Passover, Mary anoints Jesus' feet with expensive nard while Martha serves and Lazarus sits at table. When Judas objects to the waste, Jesus defends Mary, saying she has prepared his body for burial and that the poor will always be present but he will not.

43:12.title: The Plot Against Lazarus
43:12.summary: A large crowd comes to see Jesus and Lazarus, whose resurrection draws many Jews to believe in Jesus. The chief priests plot to kill Lazarus because his sign is causing people to turn to Jesus.

43:12.title: The Triumphal Entry into Jerusalem
43:12.summary: A great multitude welcomes Jesus into Jerusalem with palm branches and cries of 'Hosanna,' acclaiming him as the King of Israel. Jesus rides on a young donkey, fulfilling Zechariah's prophecy, while the Pharisees despair that everyone has gone after him.

43:12.title: Greeks Seek Jesus and Teaching on Glorification
43:12.summary: Greek worshippers ask Philip to see Jesus, and when this request is brought to Jesus, he announces that the time for the Son of Man's glorification has come. He teaches that bearing fruit requires dying like a grain of wheat, and those who serve him must follow him and will be honored by the Father.

43:12.title: Jesus' Prayer and the Voice from Heaven
43:12.summary: Jesus expresses that his soul is troubled but acknowledges his purpose in coming to this time, asking the Father to glorify his name. A voice from heaven responds that the name has been and will be glorified, and Jesus declares that his being lifted up will draw all people to himself.

43:12.title: Jesus as the Light and Call to Belief
43:12.summary: When the crowd questions how the Son of Man can be lifted up if the Christ remains forever, Jesus tells them to walk in the light while it is with them, urging belief in the light to become children of light before darkness overtakes them.

43:12.title: Unbelief and Isaiah's Prophecy Fulfilled
43:12.summary: Despite Jesus' many signs, the people do not believe, fulfilling Isaiah's prophecies about hardened hearts and blinded eyes. Even many rulers believe but do not confess their faith openly, choosing human praise over God's praise.

43:12.title: Jesus' Final Testimony to His Relationship with the Father
43:12.summary: Jesus proclaims that belief in him is belief in the Father who sent him, that seeing him is seeing the Father, and that he came as light to save rather than judge the world. He testifies that his words are the Father's commandment and that obedience to them leads to eternal life.

43:13.title: Jesus Washes the Disciples' Feet
43:13.summary: Before the Passover feast, knowing his time has come and that he came from God, Jesus humbly washes his disciples' feet as an act of sacrificial love. This menial task demonstrates his radical example of servant leadership.

43:13.title: Peter's Resistance and Jesus's Teaching on Cleansing
43:13.summary: Peter initially refuses to have Jesus wash his feet, but Jesus explains that accepting this service is necessary for having part in him. Jesus clarifies that one who has bathed needs only foot-washing, and notes that not all are clean, alluding to Judas's betrayal.

43:13.title: The Lesson of Footwashing and Mutual Service
43:13.summary: Jesus explains that as Lord and Teacher he has washed their feet to set an example, commanding the disciples to wash one another's feet and serve others humbly. Blessing comes to those who practice this teaching.

43:13.title: The Prophecy of Betrayal and Reception
43:13.summary: Jesus speaks cryptically about his betrayal, fulfilling Scripture, and explains that receiving his messengers is equivalent to receiving him and the Father. This establishes the cost of discipleship and the seriousness of the chosen group.

43:13.title: The Identification of the Betrayer
43:13.summary: Jesus announces one of them will betray him, troubling the disciples. The beloved disciple asks who it is; Jesus identifies Judas by giving him bread. Satan enters Judas, who immediately leaves to carry out the betrayal under cover of night.

43:13.title: The Final Discourse: Glorification, The New Commandment, and Peter's Denial Foretold
43:13.summary: With Judas gone, Jesus speaks of his imminent glorification and gives the new commandment to love one another as he has loved them, making this the mark of discipleship. Jesus predicts he will depart, tells Peter he cannot follow now but will later, and foreknows Peter's three denials.

43:14.title: Jesus Promises a Place in the Father's House
43:14.summary: Jesus comforts his troubled disciples by telling them to trust in God and in him, assuring them he is going to prepare a place for them in his Father's house and will return to bring them there. He indicates that his disciples already know where he is going and the way to get there.

43:14.title: Jesus the Way, the Truth, and the Life
43:14.summary: When Thomas expresses confusion about the way, Jesus declares himself to be the exclusive way to the Father, identifying himself as the truth and the life. Jesus explains his oneness with the Father and promises that believers will do greater works through faith in his name and prayer.

43:14.title: Love, Obedience, and the Promised Holy Spirit
43:14.summary: Jesus teaches that love for him is demonstrated through keeping his commandments, and promises to send the Holy Spirit as an eternal Counselor and advocate. He assures his disciples of his continued presence and reveals that obedience to him leads to being loved by the Father.

43:14.title: Jesus's Self-Revelation and the Spirit's Teaching Role
43:14.summary: When Judas (not Iscariot) asks why Jesus will reveal himself only to believers and not to the world, Jesus explains that love and obedience determine who receives his self-revelation, and promises that the Holy Spirit will teach all things and recall his teachings. He emphasizes that his words come from the Father who sent him.

43:14.title: Jesus's Parting Gift of Peace and Departure
43:14.summary: Jesus offers his peace to the disciples as a parting gift, distinct from worldly peace, and calls them to remove fear and trust in his departure and return to the Father. He announces that the ruler of the world has no hold over him and that his actions demonstrate his love for the Father, then signals his imminent departure.

43:15.title: The Vine and the Branches
43:15.summary: Jesus identifies himself as the true vine and his Father as the farmer, explaining that branches must remain in him to bear fruit, and that fruitfulness glorifies the Father and proves true discipleship. The imagery emphasizes complete dependence on Jesus and the necessity of pruning for spiritual growth.

43:15.title: Remaining in Jesus's Love
43:15.summary: Jesus calls his disciples to remain in his love by keeping his commandments, just as he remains in his Father's love, so that his joy may be fully realized in them. This passage connects obedience, love, and joy as mutually dependent spiritual realities.

43:15.title: The Command to Love One Another
43:15.summary: Jesus commands his disciples to love one another as he has loved them, exemplified by his willingness to lay down his life; he elevates them from servants to friends by sharing with them all that he heard from the Father. This redefines discipleship as intimate friendship based on sacrificial love.

43:15.title: Chosen and Appointed to Bear Fruit
43:15.summary: Jesus reminds his disciples that he chose them and appointed them to go and bear lasting fruit, with the assurance that the Father will grant whatever they ask in his name, and reiterates his command that they love one another. This establishes the disciples' mission as fruit-bearing rooted in being chosen and in mutual love.

43:15.title: The World's Hatred and Its Cause
43:15.summary: Jesus warns that the world will hate his disciples just as it hated him because they no longer belong to the world, and that persecution will come for his name's sake; he explains that the world's hatred is without excuse since he revealed the Father's works and words to them. The hatred fulfills the Old Testament prophecy that the Messiah would be hated without cause.

43:15.title: The Spirit's Testimony and the Disciples' Witness
43:15.summary: Jesus promises to send the Holy Spirit (the Counselor, the Spirit of truth) from the Father to testify about him, and he calls his disciples to also testify as those who have been with him from the beginning. This passage establishes the dual witness of the Spirit and the apostles to Jesus's identity and work.

43:16.title: Jesus Prepares His Disciples for Persecution
43:16.summary: Jesus warns his disciples that they will face persecution, expulsion from synagogues, and even death from those who think they serve God. He explains that his earlier warnings are meant to help them remember and understand these events when they occur.

43:16.title: The Necessity of Jesus' Departure and the Coming of the Holy Spirit
43:16.summary: Jesus explains that his return to the Father is essential because only then can he send the Holy Spirit (Counselor) to the disciples. The Spirit will convict the world of sin, righteousness, and judgment, revealing truth about unbelief, Christ's ascension, and Satan's defeat.

43:16.title: The Spirit's Role in Revealing Truth
43:16.summary: Jesus indicates that the Spirit of truth will guide disciples into all truth, speaking what he hears from the Father and glorifying Christ by declaring the things of Christ. The Spirit's teaching flows from the unity and equality of all things between the Father and Son.

43:16.title: Sorrow Turned to Joy
43:16.summary: Jesus cryptically speaks of the disciples not seeing him for a little while, then seeing him again. When the confused disciples question this, Jesus explains that their grief at his departure will be transformed into inexpressible joy at his resurrection, like the pain of childbirth replaced by the joy of new life.

43:16.title: Prayer in Jesus' Name and the Father's Love
43:16.summary: Jesus teaches that in the future, disciples will receive direct answers to prayer asked in his name without needing his intercession, because the Father himself loves them for their love and belief in Christ. This reflects the intimate relationship established through faith.

43:16.title: The Disciples' Confession of Faith
43:16.summary: Jesus plainly declares his origin from God the Father and his return to the Father. The disciples respond with confidence that Jesus knows all things and came from God, expressing their complete belief in him.

43:16.title: Jesus Predicts Scattering and Offers Peace
43:16.summary: Jesus questions whether the disciples truly believe and predicts they will scatter in fear during his arrest, leaving him alone. Yet he assures them that the Father remains with him, and he gives them his peace—not the world's peace, but the confidence that he has overcome the world.

43:17.title: Jesus Prays for His Own Glorification
43:17.summary: Jesus lifts his eyes to heaven and asks the Father to glorify him as the appointed time has come, so that he may in turn glorify the Father. He recalls completing the work given to him and requests to be restored to the glory he possessed before the world existed.
themes: mutual honor between Father and Son

43:17.title: Jesus Testifies to His Disciples' Faith
43:17.summary: Jesus describes how he has revealed God's name to his chosen disciples, who have kept God's word and come to know that all things come from the Father, believing that the Father sent Jesus into the world.

43:17.title: Jesus Prays for the Protection and Joy of His Disciples
43:17.summary: Jesus prays specifically for his disciples rather than the world, asking the Father to protect them through his name and keep them unified as he departs to return to the Father, so they may experience his joy fully.

43:17.title: Jesus Prays for His Disciples' Sanctification and Mission
43:17.summary: Jesus acknowledges that his disciples are hated by the world because they do not belong to it, just as he does not. He prays that they be kept from evil and sanctified in God's truth, and that they be set apart for their mission as he has been sent into the world.

43:17.title: Jesus Prays for the Unity of All Future Believers
43:17.summary: Jesus extends his prayer to include all future believers who will come to faith through the disciples' word, praying that they all be one with each other and with the Father and Son, so that the world may recognize that the Father sent Jesus and loves them as he loves the Son.

43:17.title: Jesus' Final Prayer: Desire for Believers to Behold His Glory
43:17.summary: Jesus expresses his desire for his followers to be with him eternally to see the glory given to him by the Father since before creation. He concludes by affirming that though the world does not know the Father, he knows the Father and will continue making him known so that his love may dwell in believers.

43:18.title: Jesus Goes to Gethsemane and Judas Arrives with Armed Forces
43:18.summary: After finishing his discourse, Jesus leads his disciples across the Kidron valley to a garden where he often meets with them. Judas, knowing the location and having secured soldiers and officers from the Jewish leaders, arrives to arrest Jesus.

43:18.title: Jesus's Arrest and Peter's Sword
43:18.summary: Jesus, fully aware of what is happening, confronts the arresting party and identifies himself; they fall back at his declaration. Peter draws a sword and cuts off the servant's ear, but Jesus rebukes him, submitting to the will of the Father and his appointed suffering.

43:18.title: Jesus Bound and Led Before Annas
43:18.summary: The soldiers seize and bind Jesus, then lead him first to Annas, the father-in-law of Caiaphas the high priest. A brief note explains Caiaphas's earlier counsel that one man should die for the people.

43:18.title: Peter Follows and Denies Jesus at the Door
43:18.summary: Peter and another disciple follow Jesus to the high priest's courtyard; the other disciple, known to the high priest, enters while Peter initially waits outside. The other disciple brings Peter in, but when questioned by the door-keeper, Peter denies being a disciple.

43:18.title: Jesus Before Annas and His Interrogation
43:18.summary: Annas questions Jesus about his disciples and teaching. Jesus boldly responds that he taught openly in synagogues and the temple, inviting Annas to question those who heard him. When an officer strikes him, Jesus challenges the charge, and Annas sends him bound to Caiaphas.

43:18.title: Peter's Second and Third Denial
43:18.summary: As Peter stands warming himself, he is questioned again about being a disciple and denies it. A relative of Malchus (whose ear Peter cut off) recognizes Peter from the garden, prompting another denial just as the rooster crows.

43:18.title: Jesus Led to Pilate; Refusal to Enter the Praetorium
43:18.summary: Jesus is brought from Caiaphas to the Roman Praetorium early in the morning. The Jewish leaders refuse to enter the building so as not to be defiled before eating the Passover, indicating their concern for ceremonial purity even as they pursue Jesus's death.

43:18.title: Jesus Before Pilate: The Nature of His Kingdom
43:18.summary: Pilate questions Jesus about being King of the Jews. Jesus clarifies that his kingdom is not of this world and does not rely on military force; he explains he was born and came to testify to the truth, and those of the truth hear his voice.

43:18.title: Pilate Finds No Fault; Barabbas Released Instead
43:18.summary: After questioning Jesus about truth, Pilate announces he finds no basis for a charge against him. He offers the crowd a choice to release someone at Passover, but they choose Barabbas, a robber, over Jesus, sealing his fate.

43:19.title: Jesus Flogged and Mocked
43:19.summary: Pilate has Jesus flogged, and the soldiers mock him by crowning him with thorns, dressing him in purple, and repeatedly striking him while hailing him as "King of the Jews." This scene displays the cruelty and contempt of those who reject Jesus.

43:19.title: Pilate's Interrogation and Attempted Release
43:19.summary: Pilate presents the beaten Jesus to the crowd, declaring he finds no fault in him, but the chief priests demand crucifixion based on the law against claiming to be God's Son. When threatened that releasing Jesus would make him disloyal to Caesar, Pilate becomes afraid and questions Jesus privately, learning that his authority comes from above.

43:19.title: Pilate Delivers Jesus to Crucifixion
43:19.summary: Faced with the crowd's threat that releasing Jesus would make him no friend of Caesar, Pilate capitulates and formally delivers Jesus to be crucified, despite his repeated declarations of Jesus' innocence. Political expediency triumphs over justice.

43:19.title: The Crucifixion and Division of Garments
43:19.summary: Jesus is led to Golgotha and crucified between two criminals, with Pilate placing an inscription declaring him "King of the Jews." The soldiers divide his garments by casting lots for his seamless coat, fulfilling Old Testament Scripture.

43:19.title: Jesus Entrusts His Mother to the Beloved Disciple
43:19.summary: Jesus sees his mother and the beloved disciple standing at the cross and entrusts his mother's care to the disciple, who takes her into his home from that hour. This act demonstrates Jesus' love and provision even in his dying moments.

43:19.title: Jesus' Death
43:19.summary: Knowing that all things are accomplished, Jesus says "I am thirsty," receives vinegar on a sponge, declares "It is finished," and then bows his head and dies. His final words indicate the completion of his redemptive work.

43:19.title: Breaking of Legs and Piercing of Side
43:19.summary: To hasten death before the Sabbath, the soldiers break the legs of the other two crucified men but find Jesus already dead, so they pierce his side instead, fulfilling Scripture that none of his bones would be broken and that they would look upon him whom they pierced. The eyewitness testimony is given so readers may believe.

43:19.title: The Burial of Jesus
43:19.summary: Joseph of Arimathaea, a secret disciple, asks Pilate for Jesus' body and receives permission; Nicodemus joins him with spices, and they wrap Jesus' body according to Jewish burial custom and lay it in a new, nearby tomb. Even in death, Jesus receives honor and careful preparation for resurrection.

43:20.title: The Disciples Discover the Empty Tomb
43:20.summary: Mary Magdalene discovers the stone rolled away from Jesus's tomb early on Sunday morning and reports it to Peter and John. Both disciples run to the tomb, where John arrives first but Peter enters first; seeing the graveclothes arranged orderly, John believes in the resurrection, though they do not yet understand the Scripture.

43:20.title: Jesus Appears to Mary Magdalene
43:20.summary: Mary remains weeping at the tomb and encounters two angels, then turns to see the risen Jesus, whom she initially mistakes for a gardener. Jesus calls her by name, and she recognizes him; he directs her to tell the disciples that he is ascending to his Father and their Father, God and their God.

43:20.title: Jesus Appears to the Disciples and Commissions Them
43:20.summary: Jesus appears to the gathered disciples on the evening of Resurrection Sunday, greets them with peace, shows them his wounded hands and side, and empowers them with the Holy Spirit. He commissions them to forgive or retain sins, extending his redemptive work through their ministry.

43:20.title: Thomas Believes After Seeing the Risen Jesus
43:20.summary: Thomas, absent from the first appearance, doubts the disciples' testimony and demands physical evidence; eight days later, Jesus appears again and invites Thomas to touch his scars. Thomas declares his faith completely, and Jesus pronounces a blessing on those who believe without seeing.

43:20.title: Purpose Statement for the Gospel
43:20.summary: John concludes by noting that Jesus performed many other signs not recorded in this Gospel, but these signs are written so that readers may believe Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and through believing find eternal life in his name.

43:21.title: Jesus Appears at the Sea of Tiberias
43:21.summary: After his resurrection, Jesus appears to his disciples at the Sea of Tiberias. The disciples, including Simon Peter, decide to go fishing but catch nothing that night.

43:21.title: The Miraculous Catch of Fish
43:21.summary: Jesus appears on the beach without being recognized and directs the disciples to cast their net on the right side of the boat, resulting in a miraculous catch so large they cannot haul it in. The beloved disciple recognizes Jesus, and Peter jumps into the sea to reach him.

43:21.title: Breakfast with the Risen Jesus
43:21.summary: The disciples land with their catch and find Jesus has prepared a meal with fish and bread on a fire. Jesus invites them to eat breakfast, an intimate meal that marks the third resurrection appearance and strengthens their faith.

43:21.title: Jesus Restores Peter and Commissions Him
43:21.summary: Jesus asks Peter three times if he loves him, paralleling Peter's three denials, and each time commissions Peter to feed and tend his sheep. Peter is grieved by the repeated questioning but affirms his love and receives his pastoral commission.

43:21.title: Jesus Predicts Peter's Martyrdom
43:21.summary: Jesus cryptically predicts that Peter will one day be bound and carried where he does not wish to go, signifying the manner of his death and glorification of God. Jesus calls Peter to follow him.

43:21.title: Jesus Clarifies Peter's Concern About the Beloved Disciple
43:21.summary: Peter asks Jesus about the fate of the beloved disciple who is following them. Jesus responds that Peter should not concern himself with others' destinies but focus on his own calling to follow, clarifying that his words about the disciple staying until his return were conditional.

43:21.title: Attestation of the Gospel's Witness
43:21.summary: The passage affirms that the beloved disciple is the one who testifies about these things and wrote them, with assurance that his witness is true. It concludes by noting that Jesus did many other things not recorded, as the world could not contain all the books that could be written.

44:1.title: Prologue and the Risen Jesus
44:1.summary: Luke addresses Theophilus, explaining that his first account covered all that Jesus began to do and teach until his ascension. After his suffering, Jesus showed himself alive to the apostles over forty days, providing many proofs and speaking about God's kingdom.

44:1.title: The Promise of the Holy Spirit and the Great Commission
44:1.summary: Jesus commands the apostles to remain in Jerusalem to wait for the Father's promise of baptism in the Holy Spirit, assuring them they will receive power to be witnesses from Jerusalem to the ends of the earth.

44:1.title: The Ascension and Promise of Return
44:1.summary: As the apostles watch, Jesus is taken up into a cloud and lifted out of sight; two men in white assure them that Jesus will return in the same manner as he ascended.

44:1.title: The Disciples Return to Jerusalem and Pray
44:1.summary: The apostles return from the Mount of Olives to Jerusalem and gather in the upper room with other believers, including Jesus' mother and brothers, devoting themselves to prayer and supplication.

44:1.title: Peter's Discourse on Judas's Betrayal and Replacement
44:1.summary: Peter addresses about one hundred twenty believers and explains that Scripture required Judas's betrayal and fall from apostleship to be fulfilled; he recounts Judas's tragic death and quotes Psalms concerning his desolation and office passing to another.

44:1.title: The Selection of Matthias as Judas's Replacement
44:1.summary: The apostles determine that a replacement for Judas must be chosen from those who accompanied Jesus throughout his ministry and witnessed the resurrection; they present Joseph Barsabbas and Matthias, pray for God's guidance, and cast lots, with Matthias being chosen and numbered among the eleven apostles.

44:2.title: The Day of Pentecost and the Filling with the Holy Spirit
44:2.summary: On the day of Pentecost, the disciples gathered together in Jerusalem are suddenly filled with the Holy Spirit, accompanied by a sound like rushing wind and visible tongues like fire. They begin to speak in languages they have not learned, empowered by the Spirit's work.

44:2.title: The Bewilderment of the Crowd
44:2.summary: Devout Jews from many nations hear the disciples speaking in their own languages about God's mighty works, amazed and perplexed by what they are witnessing. Some mock the disciples, suggesting they are drunk with new wine.

44:2.title: Peter's First Apostolic Discourse: Joel's Prophecy Fulfilled
44:2.summary: Peter stands up to address the crowd, defending the disciples against charges of drunkenness by explaining that the events fulfill Joel's prophecy about God pouring out his Spirit on all people in the last days. He declares that whoever calls on the Lord's name will be saved.

44:2.title: Peter's Testimony: Jesus' Resurrection and Exaltation
44:2.summary: Peter proclaims that Jesus of Nazareth, approved by God through miracles, was crucified by lawless men but raised from the dead by God and exalted to God's right hand. He uses David's psalms to show that the resurrection and exaltation of the Messiah were prophesied, declaring Jesus is both Lord and Christ.

44:2.title: The Call to Repentance and the First Converts
44:2.summary: Moved by Peter's words, the crowd asks what they should do; Peter calls them to repent and be baptized for forgiveness of sins and to receive the Holy Spirit. About three thousand people are baptized and added to the new community of believers.

44:2.title: The Life of the Early Church Community
44:2.summary: The new believers devote themselves to apostolic teaching, fellowship, breaking bread together, and prayer, sharing all possessions and meeting daily in the temple. The community grows with signs and wonders, characterized by joy and gratitude, as the Lord continually adds to their number those being saved.

44:3.title: The Healing of the Lame Beggar
44:3.summary: Peter and John encounter a man lame from birth begging at the temple gate. Peter commands him to walk in the name of Jesus Christ, and the man is immediately healed, leaping and praising God as he enters the temple.

44:3.title: The People's Amazement
44:3.summary: Everyone recognizes the formerly lame beggar now walking and praising God, and they gather in amazement in Solomon's porch, wondering at what has happened.

44:3.title: Peter's Speech: Jesus Glorified and Calling to Repentance
44:3.summary: Peter addresses the gathered crowd, redirecting their focus from the apostles to Jesus, whom God raised from the dead. He proclaims that Jesus is the fulfillment of Old Testament prophecies, calls the people to repent, and promises that Jesus is ordained to bless them by turning them from their wickedness.

44:4.title: Arrest of Peter and John
44:4.summary: As Peter and John speak to the people about Jesus' resurrection, the priests and Sadducees arrest them. Despite their imprisonment, about five thousand believe in Jesus.

44:4.title: Peter's Defense Before the Council
44:4.summary: Peter, filled with the Holy Spirit, boldly testifies before the Jewish council that the crippled man was healed in Jesus' name, declaring that salvation comes through Jesus Christ alone.

44:4.title: The Council's Response and the Healed Man
44:4.summary: The council recognizes Peter and John's boldness and perceives they had been with Jesus; seeing the healed man present, they cannot deny the miracle and note he was over forty years old.

44:4.title: The Apostles Report and the Church Prays
44:4.summary: After being released, Peter and John report to the church, which responds with a prayer quoting Psalm 2, acknowledging that Jesus' suffering was foreordained and requesting boldness to continue proclaiming God's word with signs and wonders.

44:4.title: The Holy Spirit's Filling and Communal Sharing
44:4.summary: God answers their prayer by shaking the meeting place and filling them with the Holy Spirit; the believers live in radical unity, sharing all possessions and selling property to meet each other's needs, with Barnabas exemplifying this generosity.

44:5.title: Ananias and Sapphira
44:5.summary: A couple named Ananias and Sapphira sell property but secretly keep back part of the proceeds while claiming to give it all to the apostles. Peter exposes their deception as lying to the Holy Spirit, and both fall dead in judgment, bringing great fear upon the entire assembly.

44:5.title: Signs and Wonders Among the People
44:5.summary: The apostles perform many miracles and signs, healing the sick and those afflicted by demons, with such power that even Peter's shadow brings healing. Multitudes gather from surrounding cities seeking healing.

44:5.title: Apostles Arrested and Released by an Angel
44:5.summary: The high priest and Sadducees, filled with jealousy, arrest the apostles and put them in prison. An angel miraculously opens the prison doors and tells them to speak in the temple; when officers find the prison empty and report the apostles are teaching in the temple, the captain brings them before the council without violence.

44:5.title: Peter's Defense Before the Council
44:5.summary: The high priest commands the apostles not to teach about Jesus, but Peter declares they must obey God rather than men and proclaims Jesus as risen Lord, exalted Savior, and giver of repentance and forgiveness, with the apostles and Holy Spirit as witnesses.
themes: authority of God over human authority

44:5.title: Gamaliel's Counsel and the Council's Response
44:5.summary: The council is enraged by Peter's words, but the respected Pharisee teacher Gamaliel counsels them to leave the apostles alone, arguing that if their work is human it will fail, but if it is from God they cannot stop it. The council beats the apostles and commands them not to teach in Jesus's name, then releases them.
themes: obedience to God over human authority

44:5.title: The Apostles' Joy in Persecution
44:5.summary: The apostles depart from the council rejoicing that they were counted worthy to suffer for Jesus's name, and continue daily teaching and preaching about Jesus in the temple and from house to house.

44:6.title: The Selection of Seven Men to Serve
44:6.summary: A dispute arises in the Jerusalem church over the neglect of Hellenist widows in the daily distribution of aid. The apostles delegate this administrative task to seven Spirit-filled men of good reputation, freeing themselves to focus on prayer and preaching the word.
themes: unity in the body of Christ

44:6.title: The Church's Growth and Stephen's Ministry
44:6.summary: The word of God spreads and disciples multiply in Jerusalem, with many priests coming to faith. Stephen, one of the chosen seven, performs great wonders and signs among the people.

44:6.title: Stephen's Arrest and False Accusation
44:6.summary: Synagogue members from various regions dispute with Stephen but cannot withstand his wisdom and Spirit-empowered words. They incite false witnesses to accuse him of blasphemy against Moses and God, bringing him before the council where his face appears like an angel's.

44:7.title: The High Priest's Question
44:7.summary: The high priest asks Stephen if the accusations against him are true, setting the stage for Stephen's defense before the council.

44:7.title: Abraham's Call and Covenant
44:7.summary: Stephen traces Israel's history from Abraham's call in Mesopotamia, his journey to Canaan, God's promise of descendants and land, and the covenant of circumcision. This establishes God's faithfulness across generations despite Abraham having no heir.

44:7.title: Joseph in Egypt and the Patriarchs' Burial
44:7.summary: Joseph is sold into slavery by his jealous brothers, but God preserves him, granting him wisdom and authority in Egypt. During the famine, Jacob and the patriarchs settle in Egypt, and their bodies are eventually buried in Shechem. This demonstrates God's protection and providence despite human jealousy and suffering.

44:7.title: Oppression of Israel in Egypt
44:7.summary: As the population of Israel grows in Egypt, a new pharaoh arises who does not know Joseph and begins to oppress the Hebrews, forcing them to expose their newborn sons. This sets the context for the need for deliverance.

44:7.title: Moses' Birth, Education, and Flight
44:7.summary: Moses is born and rescued by Pharaoh's daughter, educated in Egyptian wisdom, and at forty attempts to deliver his people by killing an Egyptian oppressor. When his brothers reject his leadership and mock him, Moses flees to Midian, where he lives as a stranger for forty years.

44:7.title: Moses and the Burning Bush
44:7.summary: After forty years in Midian, an angel appears to Moses in a flaming bush on Mount Sinai, revealing himself as the God of the patriarchs and commissioning Moses to deliver Israel from Egyptian bondage. God acknowledges He has witnessed Israel's suffering and groaning.

44:7.title: Moses as Ruler, Deliverer, and Prophet
44:7.summary: Stephen affirms that the Moses whom Israel rejected has been sent by God as both ruler and deliverer, performing wonders and leading them through the Red Sea and wilderness. Moses prophesied that God would raise up a prophet like him from among the people.

44:7.title: Israel's Rebellion and Idolatry at Sinai
44:7.summary: Though Moses was with the angel at Sinai and received the living oracles to give to Israel, the people rejected him, turned their hearts back to Egypt, and fashioned a golden calf to worship while praising the false god that led them out. This reveals Israel's persistent disobedience and unfaithfulness.

44:7.title: God's Judgment Through the Prophets
44:7.summary: God turned away from Israel's false worship and allowed them to serve false gods, as prophesied: despite forty years of sacrifices in the wilderness, they adopted Moloch and other idols, and God will carry them beyond Babylon in exile. This shows God's judgment on persistent idolatry.

44:7.title: The Tabernacle, Temple, and David
44:7.summary: The patriarchs carried the tabernacle of testimony through the wilderness as God commanded Moses, and later brought it into Canaan under Joshua. David found favor with God and sought to build a dwelling place for God; Solomon eventually built a temple.

44:7.title: God Does Not Dwell in Man-Made Temples
44:7.summary: Stephen quotes the prophet to show that the Most High does not dwell in temples made by human hands, as heaven is His throne and earth His footstool; His hand made all things, implying God transcends all human structures and institutions.

44:7.title: Stephen's Accusation Against Israel's Leaders
44:7.summary: Stephen directly accuses the council of resisting the Holy Spirit and stubbornness like their fathers, persecuting the prophets who foretold the Righteous One and becoming His betrayers and murderers, while having received but not kept God's law.

44:7.title: Stephen's Martyrdom
44:7.summary: Enraged by Stephen's words, the council grinds their teeth at him; but Stephen, full of the Holy Spirit, sees a vision of Jesus standing at the right hand of God and declares it. They drag him outside the city and stone him to death while a young man named Saul watches. As Stephen dies, he prays for forgiveness for his killers, echoing Jesus' example.

44:8.title: Saul's Persecution of the Church
44:8.summary: Following Stephen's execution, Saul actively persecutes the Jerusalem church while believers scatter throughout Judea and Samaria, except for the apostles. This marks the beginning of widespread Christian diaspora driven by violent opposition.

44:8.title: Philip's Ministry in Samaria
44:8.summary: Scattered believers preach the word, and Philip proclaims Christ in Samaria with miraculous signs—healing the paralyzed and casting out demons—resulting in great joy in the city. God's word advances despite persecution through apostolic witness and supernatural power.

44:8.title: Simon the Sorcerer
44:8.summary: Simon, a sorcerer who previously amazed Samaria, believes Philip's message about the Kingdom and Jesus, is baptized, and follows Philip, marveling at the signs and miracles. His faith appears genuine but will be tested regarding his true motivations.

44:8.title: The Apostles Send Peter and John; Gift of the Holy Spirit
44:8.summary: The Jerusalem apostles send Peter and John to Samaria, who pray for the new believers to receive the Holy Spirit; through the apostles' laying on of hands, the Samaritans receive the Spirit. The Holy Spirit's coming confirms and completes the Samaritan believers' acceptance into God's family.

44:8.title: Simon's Sin and Peter's Rebuke
44:8.summary: Simon, seeing the Holy Spirit given through apostolic laying on of hands, offers money for this power; Peter harshly rebukes him, declaring his heart is wrong and calling him to repent of his wickedness and bitterness. Simon asks Peter to pray for his forgiveness, revealing the gravity of attempting to commodify God's gift.

44:8.title: Peter and John Return and Minister in Samaria
44:8.summary: After testifying and speaking the Lord's word in Samaria, Peter and John return to Jerusalem while preaching the Gospel to many Samaritan villages on their way. The gospel continues to spread throughout the region.

44:8.title: Philip and the Ethiopian Eunuch
44:8.summary: An angel directs Philip south to Gaza, where he meets an Ethiopian eunuch official returning from Jerusalem worship. Philip joins him in his chariot, explains that the Isaiah 53 passage about a lamb led to slaughter refers to Jesus, and preaches Jesus to him. The encounter demonstrates how the Spirit guides divine appointments for witnessing.

44:8.title: The Ethiopian Eunuch's Baptism
44:8.summary: The eunuch asks what prevents his baptism; they stop at water and Philip baptizes him. When they come up from the water, the Spirit catches Philip away, and the eunuch continues on his way rejoicing, having found salvation and eternal hope in Christ.

44:8.title: Philip's Continued Ministry
44:8.summary: Philip is found at Azotus and travels through the region preaching the Gospel to all cities until he reaches Caesarea, continuing his apostolic witness across the land.

44:9.title: The Conversion of Saul on the Damascus Road
44:9.summary: Saul, a fierce persecutor of Christians, encounters the risen Jesus in a blinding light while traveling to Damascus. He falls to the ground, hears Jesus ask why he persecutes him, and is struck blind for three days, beginning his transformation from persecutor to apostle.

44:9.title: Ananias Restores Saul's Sight and the Holy Spirit
44:9.summary: The Lord directs Ananias to visit the blinded Saul and pray for his healing. Though fearful because of Saul's reputation, Ananias obeys and lays hands on Saul, restoring his sight and filling him with the Holy Spirit; Saul is baptized and strengthened.

44:9.title: Saul's Bold Preaching and Escape from Damascus
44:9.summary: Saul immediately begins proclaiming Jesus in the synagogues, astonishing those who knew him as a persecutor and confounding the Jews with his arguments. When the Jews plot to kill him, his disciples help him escape by lowering him through the city wall in a basket.

44:9.title: Saul Joins the Apostles in Jerusalem
44:9.summary: When Saul arrives in Jerusalem, the disciples fear and doubt him; Barnabas vouches for him and brings him to the apostles, recounting his conversion and ministry. Saul preaches boldly but faces opposition, so the brothers send him away to Tarsus for safety.

44:9.title: Peace and Growth Throughout Judea, Galilee, and Samaria
44:9.summary: Following Saul's departure, the churches experience a season of peace and multiplication, strengthened by walking in the fear of the Lord and the comfort of the Holy Spirit.

44:9.title: Peter Heals Aeneas at Lydda
44:9.summary: Peter travels throughout the region and encounters Aeneas, a paralyzed man bedridden for eight years. In the name of Jesus Christ, Peter heals him, and this miracle leads many in Lydda and Sharon to turn to the Lord.

44:9.title: Peter Raises Tabitha from the Dead at Joppa
44:9.summary: Peter is called to Joppa where the beloved disciple Tabitha (Dorcas), known for her charitable works, has died. Peter prays and commands her to rise, and she is restored to life; this miracle becomes known throughout Joppa and brings many to faith in the Lord.

44:10.title: Cornelius's Vision and Commission
44:10.summary: Cornelius, a devout Roman centurion in Caesarea, receives a vision from an angel instructing him to send for Peter in Joppa. His prayers and generosity have been noticed by God, and the angel directs him to fetch Simon Peter to receive a message.

44:10.title: Peter's Vision of the Sheet
44:10.summary: While praying on a rooftop, Peter receives a trance vision of a sheet containing unclean animals and is told by a voice to kill and eat them. When he protests, the voice tells him that what God has cleansed he must not call unclean, and the vision repeats three times before the sheet is taken back to heaven.

44:10.title: Peter's Confusion and Obedience
44:10.summary: As Peter ponders the meaning of his vision, Cornelius's messengers arrive seeking him. The Spirit directs Peter to go with them without doubt, and Peter welcomes them and agrees to go to Caesarea with some Jewish believers accompanying him.

44:10.title: Peter Arrives at Cornelius's House
44:10.summary: Peter arrives in Caesarea where Cornelius has gathered his family and friends. Cornelius falls at Peter's feet in worship, but Peter corrects him, saying he is only a man. Peter then declares that God has shown him not to call any person unclean or unholy, and Cornelius recounts his angelic vision and asks Peter to share God's message.

44:10.title: Peter's Gospel Message to the Gentiles
44:10.summary: Peter proclaims that God shows no favoritism and that anyone who fears God and works righteousness is acceptable to Him. He then preaches the gospel of Jesus Christ—His anointing, works of healing, death, resurrection, and coming judgment—declaring that through faith in Jesus, all who believe receive forgiveness of sins.

44:10.title: The Holy Spirit Falls on the Gentiles
44:10.summary: While Peter is still speaking, the Holy Spirit falls upon all the Gentiles who heard his message, evidenced by their speaking in tongues and praising God. Peter then commands them to be baptized in Jesus's name, and they ask him to stay with them for several days.

44:11.title: The Jerusalem Church Questions Peter
44:11.summary: News reaches Jerusalem that Gentiles have received God's word. When Peter arrives, the circumcision party challenges him for eating with uncircumcised men, raising the fundamental question of whether the gospel extends beyond Jewish boundaries.

44:11.title: Peter Defends His Actions with the Vision of the Sheet
44:11.summary: Peter explains his encounter with Cornelius by recounting his vision of a sheet with unclean animals and God's directive to eat, followed by the Spirit's command to go with Gentile visitors without discrimination, and his witnessing the Holy Spirit falling on Gentiles just as it did on Jews at Pentecost. He argues that if God has accepted them, he cannot reject them.

44:11.title: The Jerusalem Church Accepts God's Plan for Gentiles
44:11.summary: The apostles and brothers accept Peter's testimony and glorify God, recognizing that God has granted Gentiles repentance leading to eternal life.

44:11.title: The Gospel Spreads to Antioch and Bears Fruit
44:11.summary: Scattered believers from the Stephen persecution travel to Phoenicia, Cyprus, and Antioch, speaking the gospel initially only to Jews, but some men from Cyprus and Cyrene preach to Hellenists with great success. The Jerusalem church sends Barnabas to Antioch, where he encourages the believers and many are added to the Lord through his ministry.

44:11.title: Barnabas Recruits Saul and They Minister in Antioch
44:11.summary: Barnabas goes to Tarsus, finds Saul, and brings him to Antioch, where they teach and build up the church for a year, and the believers are first called Christians.

44:11.title: Agabus Prophesies Famine and Antioch Provides Relief
44:11.summary: The prophet Agabus prophesies a coming famine that will affect the whole world. The disciples in Antioch, each according to their means, determine to send relief to the brothers in Judea, which they do through Barnabas and Saul.

44:12.title: Herod's Persecution: James Killed and Peter Arrested
44:12.summary: King Herod begins persecuting the church, executing James the apostle and arresting Peter, intending to try him after Passover. The church responds with fervent, constant prayer for Peter's deliverance.

44:12.title: Peter's Miraculous Escape from Prison
44:12.summary: An angel of the Lord appears to Peter in his cell, his chains fall away, and he is led out of the prison through locked gates, miraculously freed from Herod's custody. Peter realizes the deliverance is real and not merely a vision.

44:12.title: Peter's Arrival at the Prayer Meeting
44:12.summary: Peter goes to the house of Mary where Christians are praying for him; a servant named Rhoda recognizes his voice but in joy forgets to let him in, causing confusion among those praying. Peter finally enters, recounts his miraculous escape, and then departs to another location.

44:12.title: Herod's Death and God's Justice
44:12.summary: When Herod discovers Peter's escape, he executes the guards in anger; later, during a public speech when people hail him as a god, an angel strikes him down and he dies of worms for accepting divine honor that belongs only to God. Meanwhile, God's word continues to grow.

44:12.title: The Gospel's Growth and the Missionaries' Return
44:12.summary: Despite persecution and death, God's word multiplies throughout the region; Barnabas and Saul return to Jerusalem after completing their relief mission, bringing John Mark with them.

44:13.title: The Holy Spirit Calls Barnabas and Saul
44:13.summary: The church at Antioch includes prophets and teachers, and while they worship and fast, the Holy Spirit directs them to set apart Barnabas and Saul for missionary work. After prayer, fasting, and the laying on of hands, the church commissions and sends them out.

44:13.title: Paul and Barnabas in Cyprus: Elymas the Sorcerer Struck Blind
44:13.summary: Sent by the Holy Spirit, Paul and Barnabas travel to Cyprus and preach in synagogues with John Mark as their assistant. When the proconsul Sergius Paulus seeks to hear God's word, the sorcerer Elymas opposes them; Paul, filled with the Spirit, pronounces blindness on Elymas, and the astonished proconsul believes.

44:13.title: Journey to Antioch of Pisidia
44:13.summary: Paul's company sails from Paphos to Perga in Pamphylia, where John Mark abandons them and returns to Jerusalem. Paul and Barnabas continue inland to Antioch of Pisidia and enter the synagogue on the Sabbath.

44:13.title: Paul's Sermon on Jesus as Fulfillment of Promise
44:13.summary: Invited to speak, Paul delivers a comprehensive address tracing God's redemptive history from Israel's exodus through the judges and kings to Jesus, presenting Jesus as the promised Savior whose resurrection fulfills Scripture and offers justification to all who believe. He concludes with a warning from the prophets for those who reject this message.

44:13.title: Initial Response and Growing Opposition
44:13.summary: Gentiles eagerly request another sermon, and many Jews and proselytes follow Paul and Barnabas. But the following week, as nearly the whole city gathers to hear, the jealous Jews oppose and blaspheme the message, leading Paul to declare that God's word goes now to the Gentiles. The Gentiles rejoice, and God's word spreads throughout the region.

44:13.title: Persecution and Rejoicing
44:13.summary: Jewish opponents incite prominent citizens to persecute Paul and Barnabas and expel them from the city, but the apostles shake the dust from their feet and move to Iconium. Despite persecution, the disciples remain filled with joy and the Holy Spirit.

44:14.title: Preaching in Iconium and Flight to Lycaonia
44:14.summary: Paul and Barnabas preach in Iconium's synagogue with great success, but opposition from unbelieving Jews stirs up Gentiles against them. After an attempted attack, they flee to the cities of Lycaonia where they continue preaching the Gospel.

44:14.title: The Healing at Lystra and Mistaken Worship
44:14.summary: Paul heals a crippled man at Lystra, which prompts the crowd to believe Paul and Barnabas are gods and attempt to offer them sacrifices. The apostles forcefully redirect the people to worship the living God alone, emphasizing His creation and providential care.

44:14.title: Paul Stoned at Lystra and Rises to Continue
44:14.summary: Hostile Jews from nearby cities convince the crowd to stone Paul and drag him out of the city, leaving him apparently dead. However, Paul miraculously recovers and returns to the city, demonstrating God's protection and strengthening of His messenger.

44:14.title: Return Journey and Establishment of Church Leadership
44:14.summary: Paul and Barnabas complete their missionary circuit by preaching in Derbe and returning to strengthen the new believers in Lystra, Iconium, and Antioch, appointing elders and commending them to God's care while exhorting them toward perseverance through tribulation.

44:14.title: Return to Antioch and Report to the Sending Church
44:14.summary: Paul and Barnabas travel through Pisidia and Pamphylia back to Antioch, where they gather the assembly and report all that God accomplished through them, particularly opening the door of faith to the Gentiles, and spend time with the disciples.

44:15.title: The Circumcision Controversy
44:15.summary: Men from Judea teach that Gentiles must be circumcised to be saved, causing sharp dispute with Paul and Barnabas, who are appointed to bring the question before the apostles and elders in Jerusalem.

44:15.title: Journey to Jerusalem
44:15.summary: Paul and Barnabas travel to Jerusalem, reporting the conversion of the Gentiles and bringing joy to believers, where they meet with the apostles and elders to address the circumcision question.

44:15.title: Peter's Speech
44:15.summary: Peter testifies that God chose him to preach to the Gentiles, gave them the Holy Spirit without distinction, and saved them through grace alone—not through the law of Moses which no one could bear.

44:15.title: James's Decision
44:15.summary: After hearing Paul and Barnabas report signs and wonders among the Gentiles, James cites the prophets to affirm God's plan to call the Gentiles, then proposes they not burden Gentile believers with circumcision but ask them to avoid idolatry, sexual immorality, and certain foods.

44:15.title: The Jerusalem Letter
44:15.summary: The apostles, elders, and assembly send Judas and Silas with Paul and Barnabas to Antioch with a letter affirming that Gentiles need not be circumcised or keep the Mosaic law, only abstain from idolatry, sexual immorality, and certain foods.

44:15.title: Delivering the Letter and Strengthening the Church
44:15.summary: The letter is delivered to Antioch where it brings great encouragement; Judas and Silas strengthen the believers through prophecy, and Paul and Barnabas continue teaching and preaching the word.

44:15.title: Paul and Barnabas Separate
44:15.summary: Paul proposes revisiting churches they planted, but he and Barnabas sharply disagree over taking John Mark; they separate, with Barnabas sailing to Cyprus with Mark while Paul travels through Syria and Cilicia with Silas, both strengthening the churches.

44:16.title: Timothy Joins Paul's Missionary Team
44:16.summary: Paul meets Timothy at Lystra, a young disciple with a believing Jewish mother and Greek father. After circumcising him to avoid offense to local Jews and delivering the apostolic decrees from Jerusalem, Paul takes Timothy as a traveling companion, and the churches are strengthened in faith and grow in number.

44:16.title: The Macedonian Vision
44:16.summary: As Paul and his team travel through Phrygia and Galatia, the Holy Spirit redirects them away from preaching in Asia and Bithynia, guiding them instead to Troas where Paul receives a vision of a Macedonian man calling them to help. Paul recognizes this as God's call to bring the gospel to Macedonia.

44:16.title: Lydia's Conversion and Hospitality
44:16.summary: Paul's team sails to Philippi in Macedonia and on the Sabbath encounter Lydia, a wealthy purple merchant and God-worshiper, by a riverside prayer place. The Lord opens her heart to receive Paul's message, and she and her household are baptized, then she insists on hosting the missionaries in her home.

44:16.title: Deliverance of the Demon-Possessed Slave Girl and Imprisonment
44:16.summary: A slave girl possessed by a spirit of divination repeatedly follows Paul, proclaiming their message, but Paul casts out the demon in Jesus' name. Her enraged masters, losing their profitable fortune-telling business, drag Paul and Silas before the magistrates with false accusations, leading to their public beating and imprisonment in stocks in the inner prison.

44:16.title: The Philippian Jailer's Conversion
44:16.summary: At midnight, Paul and Silas pray and sing hymns while imprisoned, and a sudden earthquake frees all prisoners and opens all doors. The jailer, thinking the prisoners have escaped, prepares to kill himself, but Paul stops him. Witnessing this divine power, the jailer asks what he must do to be saved, believes in Jesus with his household, and is baptized that very night.

44:16.title: Paul Asserts His Roman Rights and Departs
44:16.summary: The magistrates attempt to quietly release Paul and Silas the next day, but Paul insists on a public apology, asserting that they were beaten without trial despite being Roman citizens. The frightened magistrates comply and escort them out, after which Paul and Silas visit Lydia's house to encourage the brothers before departing the city.

44:17.title: Paul's Ministry in Thessalonica
44:17.summary: Paul enters the Thessalonian synagogue and reasons from Scripture for three Sabbaths, proving that Jesus is the Christ who suffered and rose from the dead. Some Jews and a great multitude of God-fearing Greeks, including prominent women, are persuaded and join Paul and Silas.

44:17.title: Opposition and Uproar in Thessalonica
44:17.summary: Unpersuaded Jews incite wicked men to create a city uproar, dragging Jason and other believers before the city rulers and falsely accusing them of sedition against Caesar. The rulers release them after taking security from Jason.

44:17.title: Reception and Faith in Beroea
44:17.summary: Paul and Silas are sent to Beroea, where the Jews receive the word with eagerness and examine the Scriptures daily to verify the message. Many believe, including prominent Greek women and men.

44:17.title: Persecution Spreads and Paul Departs
44:17.summary: Jews from Thessalonica follow Paul to Beroea and agitate the crowds, prompting the brothers to send Paul toward Athens while Silas and Timothy remain behind. Those accompanying Paul escort him to Athens and receive instructions to send the others quickly.

44:17.title: Paul in Athens: Provocation and Engagement
44:17.summary: While waiting for Silas and Timothy in Athens, Paul is deeply troubled by the city's pervasive idolatry. He reasons daily in the synagogue and marketplace, encountering Epicurean and Stoic philosophers who dismiss him as a babbler or accuse him of promoting foreign gods, then bring him to the Areopagus to explain his new teaching.

44:17.title: Paul's Areopagus Speech
44:17.summary: Paul addresses the Athenian council, commending their religiosity while revealing that their worship of an unknown god is actually ignorance of the true God. He proclaims the Creator who transcends temples, sustains all life, has appointed all nations, and now calls all people to repent because He has appointed a day of righteous judgment through the resurrected Christ.

44:17.title: Response to the Gospel in Athens
44:17.summary: The message of resurrection produces divided responses: some mock while others request further teaching. Paul departs, but certain believers, including Dionysius the Areopagite and Damaris, join him in faith.

44:18.title: Paul meets Aquila and Priscilla in Corinth
44:18.summary: After leaving Athens, Paul arrives in Corinth where he meets Aquila and Priscilla, Jewish tentmakers who had been expelled from Rome. Paul joins them in their trade while beginning his ministry.

44:18.title: Paul's ministry in Corinth and the Lord's encouragement
44:18.summary: Paul reasons in the synagogue every Sabbath, and when Silas and Timothy arrive, he intensifies his witness to Jesus as the Messiah despite Jewish opposition. After being rejected, Paul shifts to the Gentiles, and many Corinthians believe and are baptized. The Lord reassures Paul in a vision that he is protected and has many believers in the city, and Paul continues teaching there for a year and a half.

44:18.title: Gallio's dismissal of charges against Paul
44:18.summary: The Jews bring Paul before Gallio, the proconsul of Achaia, accusing him of persuading people to worship God contrary to Jewish law. Gallio refuses to judge matters of Jewish custom and law, dismissing the case. The Greeks then beat Sosthenes, the synagogue ruler, before the judgment seat while Gallio remains indifferent.

44:18.title: Paul's journey to Jerusalem and Antioch
44:18.summary: After staying in Corinth longer, Paul departs with Priscilla and Aquila, sailing for Syria with a vow. He stops in Ephesus to reason with the Jews, then continues to Caesarea and Jerusalem, finally going down to Antioch.

44:18.title: Paul's third missionary journey begins
44:18.summary: After spending time in Antioch, Paul departs on his third missionary journey, traveling through Galatia and Phrygia to strengthen and establish the disciples.

44:18.title: Apollos's ministry and the instruction of Priscilla and Aquila
44:18.summary: Apollos, an eloquent and learned Jew from Alexandria who knows only John's baptism, comes to Ephesus and speaks boldly about Jesus in the synagogue. Priscilla and Aquila take him aside and explain the way of God more accurately. The brothers encourage him to go to Achaia, and there he powerfully helps believers and refutes the Jews by showing from Scripture that Jesus is the Christ.

44:19.title: Paul Encounters John's Disciples and Baptizes Them in Jesus' Name
44:19.summary: Paul finds disciples in Ephesus who knew only of John's baptism of repentance and had not heard of the Holy Spirit. After instructing them about Jesus, Paul baptizes them in His name, and they receive the Holy Spirit, speaking in tongues and prophesying.

44:19.title: Paul's Ministry in Ephesus
44:19.summary: Paul boldly teaches in the synagogue for three months about God's kingdom, but when opposition hardens, he separates the disciples and teaches daily in the school of Tyrannus for two years, resulting in the gospel spreading throughout all Asia.

44:19.title: Miracles, False Exorcists, and the Burning of Magic Books
44:19.summary: God performs extraordinary miracles through Paul, even healing the sick through cloths touched by him. Jewish exorcists attempt to use Jesus' name but are defeated by a demon-possessed man; this humiliating incident causes fear to fall on the city, and many believers confess their sins while practitioners of magic publicly burn their books, demonstrating the triumph of the gospel over spiritual darkness.

44:19.title: Paul Plans His Future Travels
44:19.summary: Paul determines to travel through Macedonia and Achaia before going to Jerusalem and Rome, sending Timothy and Erastus ahead while he remains in Asia.

44:19.title: The Riot of the Silversmiths
44:19.summary: A silversmith named Demetrius incites a riot in Ephesus by stirring up craftsmen's fears that Paul's preaching against idols threatens their trade and the worship of Artemis. The confused mob seizes Paul's companions and chants for two hours in the theater, but Alexander (a Jew) is unable to address the crowd.

44:19.title: The Town Clerk Restores Order
44:19.summary: The town clerk quiets the assembly by appealing to civic pride and legality, noting that Paul's companions have committed no offense and that grievances should be settled in court, not through mob action, thereby preventing further violence and dismissing the gathering.

44:20.title: Paul's Journey Through Macedonia and Greece
44:20.summary: After the uproar in Ephesus subsides, Paul travels through Macedonia and Greece, encouraging the believers. When a plot against him forces a change of plans, he returns through Macedonia instead of sailing directly to Syria.

44:20.title: Paul's Travel Companions and Journey to Troas
44:20.summary: Paul is accompanied by representatives from various churches as he sails from Philippi to Troas, where the group reunites after five days of travel and stays for a week.

44:20.title: Eutychus Falls from the Window and Is Restored
44:20.summary: During Paul's lengthy discourse on the first day of the week, a young man named Eutychus falls asleep and plummets from a third-story window; Paul miraculously restores him to life, comforting the terrified disciples.

44:20.title: Paul's Sea Journey to Miletus
44:20.summary: Paul travels by land to Assos to meet the ship, then sails to Miletus via Mitylene, Chios, and Samos, deliberately bypassing Ephesus to reach Jerusalem by Pentecost.

44:20.title: Paul's Farewell Address to the Ephesian Elders
44:20.summary: Paul summons the Ephesian elders to Miletus and delivers a comprehensive farewell address, recounting his faithful ministry, warning of future dangers to the flock, and entrusting them to God's grace while modeling sacrificial service and generosity.

44:20.title: Paul's Emotional Farewell
44:20.summary: Paul kneels in prayer with the elders, and all weep deeply, grieving that they will not see his face again as he departs for the ship.

44:21.title: Paul's Journey to Tyre and Farewell with Disciples
44:21.summary: After parting from the Ephesian elders, Paul and his companions sail to Phoenicia, stopping at Tyre where they find disciples who warn Paul through the Spirit not to go to Jerusalem. After seven days, the believers accompany Paul and his group to the beach, where they kneel and pray together before departing.

44:21.title: Journey from Tyre to Jerusalem via Caesarea
44:21.summary: Paul's company travels from Tyre through Ptolemais to Caesarea, where they stay with Philip the evangelist and his four prophesying daughters. A prophet named Agabus performs a symbolic action, binding himself with Paul's belt to warn that Paul will be bound and delivered to the Gentiles in Jerusalem, but Paul remains resolved to go.

44:21.title: Paul's Report to Jerusalem Elders and Compromise on the Law
44:21.summary: Upon arriving in Jerusalem, Paul reports to James and the elders about God's work among the Gentiles, and they discuss concerns that Paul is teaching Jews to abandon the Mosaic law. To address these rumors, the elders suggest Paul participate in a purification ceremony with four men under a vow, demonstrating that he himself upholds the law while maintaining that Gentile believers are not bound by it.

44:21.title: Riot in the Temple and Paul's Arrest
44:21.summary: During the purification ceremony, Jews from Asia recognize Paul in the temple and incite a riot, falsely accusing him of teaching against the law and bringing Greeks into the holy place. The mob drags Paul from the temple and attempts to kill him until the Roman commander arrives with soldiers and arrests him, binding him with two chains while the crowd cries for his death.

44:21.title: Paul's Identification and Request to Address the Crowd
44:21.summary: As Paul is about to be taken into the barracks, he identifies himself to the Roman commander as a Jew from Tarsus (a citizen of a significant city), not the Egyptian rebel the commander suspected. Paul requests permission to address the crowd, which the commander grants, and Paul stands on the stairs gesturing for silence before preparing to speak in Hebrew.

44:22.title: Paul Requests Permission to Speak
44:22.summary: Paul asks the hostile Jewish crowd for permission to make his defense, and gains their attention by addressing them in Hebrew.

44:22.title: Paul's Background as a Persecutor
44:22.summary: Paul establishes his Jewish credentials and describes his former zealous persecution of Christians, even receiving authorization from the high priest to arrest believers in Damascus.

44:22.title: Paul's Encounter with the Risen Jesus
44:22.summary: While traveling to Damascus, Paul experiences a blinding vision of light and hears Jesus identify himself, asking why Paul persecutes him; Paul's companions witness the light but not the voice, and he is led blindly into the city.

44:22.title: Ananias Restores Paul's Sight and Calls Him to Mission
44:22.summary: A devout Christian named Ananias visits Paul, restores his sight, reveals that God has appointed Paul as a witness to the Gentiles, and baptizes him.

44:22.title: Jesus Directs Paul to Leave Jerusalem for the Gentiles
44:22.summary: In a temple vision, Jesus instructs Paul to leave Jerusalem because the city will reject his testimony, and tells him he will be sent to preach to the Gentiles.

44:22.title: The Crowd's Violent Rejection and Roman Intervention
44:22.summary: The crowd erupts in fury at Paul's mention of the Gentile mission and demands his death; Roman soldiers arrest him and prepare to interrogate him by scourging.

44:22.title: Paul Claims Roman Citizenship
44:22.summary: Paul invokes his Roman citizenship to prevent his illegal scourging, causing the centurion and commander to fear they have violated the rights of a Roman citizen.

44:22.title: Paul Is Released and Brought Before the Sanhedrin
44:22.summary: The next day, the commander releases Paul from chains and arranges a hearing before the Jewish council to determine the nature of the charges against him.

44:23.title: Paul Before the Sanhedrin
44:23.summary: Paul declares his clear conscience before the council, but the high priest Ananias orders him struck. Paul rebukes Ananias for violating the law, then apologizes upon learning his identity, demonstrating respect for proper authority.

44:23.title: Paul Divides the Council
44:23.summary: Paul cleverly identifies himself as a Pharisee and raises the question of resurrection, which creates a heated dispute between Pharisees and Sadducees. The riot becomes so severe that Roman soldiers must extract Paul from the assembly to protect him.

44:23.title: The Lord Encourages Paul
44:23.summary: In the barracks, the Lord appears to Paul and assures him that as he has testified in Jerusalem, so he must testify in Rome, giving him divine comfort and confirmation of his mission.

44:23.title: The Jewish Plot to Kill Paul
44:23.summary: More than forty Jews conspire under a curse to kill Paul, enlisting the chief priests and elders to request that the commanding officer bring Paul down for another hearing where they plan to ambush and murder him.

44:23.title: The Plot Exposed
44:23.summary: Paul's nephew overhears the assassination plot and brings the news to Paul, who arranges for the young man to inform the commanding officer. The commander learns of the forty assassins and their oath, and instructs the nephew to keep the matter confidential.

44:23.title: Paul's Protective Escort to Caesarea
44:23.summary: Claudius Lysias organizes a large armed force to transport Paul safely to Felix the governor at Caesarea, accompanying him with a letter explaining that Paul is a Roman citizen wrongly accused on religious matters rather than criminal ones.

44:23.title: Paul Delivered to the Governor
44:23.summary: The soldiers transport Paul by night to Antipatris and then to Caesarea, where they present him and Lysias's letter to Governor Felix. Felix, learning Paul is from Cilicia, promises a full hearing once the accusers arrive and places Paul under custody in Herod's palace.

44:24.title: Tertullus Accuses Paul Before Felix
44:24.summary: The high priest Ananias and elders arrive with the orator Tertullus to present charges against Paul to the Roman governor Felix. Tertullus flatters Felix and accuses Paul of being a plague, an instigator of insurrections, and a ringleader of the Nazarene sect who attempted to profane the temple, with the Jewish council affirming these accusations.

44:24.title: Paul's Defense Before Felix
44:24.summary: Paul responds to the charges by respectfully addressing Felix, denying that he stirred up crowds or violated Jewish law, and asserting that his only offense is his faith in the resurrection and his practice of maintaining a clear conscience before God and men. He challenges his accusers to produce evidence and defends his worship at the temple and his service to his nation.

44:24.title: Felix Defers Decision and Grants Paul Privileges
44:24.summary: Felix, knowing about the Way, postpones his ruling until the commanding officer Lysias arrives, and orders that Paul be kept in custody with reasonable privileges and freedom to receive visitors and friends.

44:24.title: Paul Speaks to Felix and Drusilla; Felix's Corruption
44:24.summary: Felix summons Paul to speak about faith in Christ Jesus, and as Paul reasons about righteousness, self-control, and coming judgment, Felix becomes terrified and dismisses him, though he continues to summon Paul hoping to receive a bribe for his release.

44:24.title: Paul Remains in Bonds Under Festus
