44:24.summary: After two years, Felix is replaced by Porcius Festus, and Felix, seeking to gain favor with the Jews, leaves Paul imprisoned without trial.

44:25.title: Festus Arrives and the Jews' Plot Against Paul
44:25.summary: Festus arrives in the province and is immediately approached by Jewish leaders who request that Paul be transferred to Jerusalem, plotting to kill him on the way. Festus refuses and invites the leaders to accompany him to Caesarea to formally accuse Paul.

44:25.title: Paul's Trial Before Festus
44:25.summary: Festus conducts a formal hearing where the Jews bring serious charges against Paul that they cannot prove. Paul maintains his innocence, and when Festus suggests moving the trial to Jerusalem, Paul appeals to Caesar, which Festus grants.

44:25.title: Festus Reports Paul's Case to King Agrippa
44:25.summary: King Agrippa and Bernice arrive in Caesarea, and Festus explains Paul's case to Agrippa, noting that the charges concern Jewish religious matters rather than criminal wrongdoing. Agrippa expresses interest in hearing Paul speak for himself.

44:25.title: Paul Brought Before Agrippa
44:25.summary: Paul is formally brought before King Agrippa and the assembled officials with great ceremony. Festus summarizes the situation, acknowledging Paul's innocence but the need for formal charges before sending him to Rome as Paul has requested.

44:26.title: Paul Granted Permission to Speak
44:26.summary: King Agrippa grants Paul permission to speak in his own defense, and Paul stretches out his hand to begin. Paul expresses gratitude for the opportunity to defend himself before Agrippa, who is knowledgeable about Jewish customs and questions.

44:26.title: Paul's Background as a Pharisee
44:26.summary: Paul describes his strict Pharisaic upbringing in Jerusalem and his current trial for believing in God's promise of resurrection. He challenges Agrippa by asking why it seems incredible that God would raise the dead.

44:26.title: Paul's Former Persecution of Christians
44:26.summary: Paul recounts his previous campaign against followers of Jesus, during which he imprisoned many saints in Jerusalem and even voted for their execution, pursuing them even to foreign cities. He describes his violent opposition driven by religious zeal.

44:26.title: Paul's Damascus Road Conversion
44:26.summary: Paul describes his encounter with the risen Jesus on the road to Damascus—a blinding light at noon, a voice calling him, and Christ commissioning him as an apostle to open the eyes of both Jews and Gentiles to turn from darkness to light. This experience transformed Paul's entire mission and calling.

44:26.title: Paul's Obedience to the Heavenly Vision
44:26.summary: Paul declares his faithful obedience to the vision he received, proclaiming repentance and works of repentance throughout Damascus, Jerusalem, Judea, and to the Gentiles. He emphasizes that his message aligns with what Moses and the prophets predicted about Christ's suffering and resurrection.

44:26.title: Festus Accuses Paul of Madness; Paul Appeals to Agrippa
44:26.summary: Festus interrupts Paul's defense, loudly accusing him of insanity driven by excessive learning. Paul respectfully denies the charge and appeals directly to King Agrippa's knowledge of these events, suggesting that the king believes the prophets and therefore should understand Paul's message.

44:26.title: Agrippa's Response and Paul's Prayer
44:26.summary: Agrippa responds with irony, suggesting Paul is nearly persuading him to become a Christian. Paul responds with a heartfelt prayer that all who hear him—whether with little or much persuasion—might become Christians like himself, except for his bonds.

44:26.title: The Verdict: Paul Is Innocent
44:26.summary: After the hearing concludes and the officials withdraw, they converse among themselves and conclude that Paul has done nothing worthy of death or imprisonment. Agrippa remarks to Festus that Paul could have been released if he had not appealed to Caesar.

44:27.title: Paul's Journey to Rome Begins
44:27.summary: Paul and other prisoners are handed over to a Roman centurion named Julius for transport to Italy. The ship begins its voyage, stopping at Sidon where Julius treats Paul kindly, and proceeds along the coast of Asia Minor, eventually reaching Fair Havens near Crete after difficult sailing.

44:27.title: Paul's Warning and Divine Assurance
44:27.summary: As winter approaches and sailing becomes dangerous, Paul warns the ship's crew and passengers that continuing the voyage will result in loss of cargo, ship, and lives. When his warning is ignored and a storm strikes, Paul receives a vision from an angel assuring him that no one will die and that he must appear before Caesar, though the ship will run aground.

44:27.title: The Storm and Paul's Leadership
44:27.summary: For fourteen days the ship is driven about by a violent storm in the Adriatic Sea. When the sailors attempt to abandon ship, Paul alerts the centurion and soldiers, preventing their escape. Paul encourages everyone to eat and maintain strength, and after giving thanks to God, his calm faith restores hope to all aboard.

44:27.title: Shipwreck and Safe Deliverance
44:27.summary: As daylight breaks, the crew spots a bay and attempts to run the ship ashore, but it strikes a reef and begins breaking apart. The soldiers plan to kill the prisoners to prevent their escape, but the centurion, wanting to save Paul, stops them and orders everyone to jump overboard or use debris to reach the shore safely, resulting in all 276 persons being saved.

44:28.title: Paul Bitten by a Viper and Healed on Malta
44:28.summary: After shipwreck on Malta, Paul gathers firewood and is bitten by a viper, but shakes it off unharmed. The natives first suspect him of being a murderer under divine judgment, then revere him as a god when they see he suffers no harm.

44:28.title: Paul Heals Publius's Father and Others on Malta
44:28.summary: Paul is received by Publius, the island's chief official, and heals his father of fever and dysentery through prayer and laying on of hands; other sick islanders are subsequently healed, and the people honor Paul and his companions with provisions.

44:28.title: Journey from Malta to Rome
44:28.summary: After three months, Paul and his companions board a ship and sail to Syracuse, Rhegium, and Puteoli, where they find Christian brothers who invite them to stay seven days before continuing to Rome.

44:28.title: Paul's Arrival in Rome
44:28.summary: Brothers from Rome meet Paul at The Market of Appius and The Three Taverns to welcome him; Paul thanks God and is encouraged, then is placed under house arrest with a guarding soldier.

44:28.title: Paul Explains His Circumstances to the Jewish Leaders
44:28.summary: Paul calls together the leaders of the Jews and explains that though innocent of charges against the Jewish people, he was delivered to the Romans and forced to appeal to Caesar, but appeals to them now for their understanding.

44:28.title: Jewish Leaders Respond and Request to Hear Paul
44:28.summary: The Jewish leaders report they received no accusations against Paul from Judea and ask him to explain his views on the Christian faith, which they note is spoken against everywhere.

44:28.title: Paul Testifies to the Jewish Leaders about Jesus and God's Kingdom
44:28.summary: Paul spends a full day testifying to the Jews about the Kingdom of God and Jesus from the law and prophets; when they are divided in belief, he quotes Isaiah's prophecy about hardened hearts and declares that God's salvation is sent to the nations.

44:28.title: Paul's Ministry in Rome Under House Arrest
44:28.summary: After the Jews depart in dispute, Paul remains in Rome for two years under house arrest, receiving all visitors and boldly preaching the Kingdom of God and teaching about Jesus Christ without hindrance.

45:1.title: Salutation and Greeting
45:1.summary: Paul introduces himself as an apostle set apart to proclaim the Gospel of God concerning Jesus Christ, the Son of God, and extends grace and peace to the church at Rome. He establishes his apostolic authority and the continuity of God's redemptive plan through the Old Testament prophets.

45:1.title: Paul's Prayer and Desire to Visit Rome
45:1.summary: Paul expresses thanksgiving for the Romans' faith and describes his earnest desire and repeated attempts to visit them so that he might share spiritual gifts and bear fruit among them. He emphasizes his sense of obligation to proclaim the Gospel to all peoples, both educated and simple.

45:1.title: The Power of the Gospel and Righteousness by Faith
45:1.summary: Paul declares that the Gospel is God's powerful means of salvation for all who believe and reveals God's righteousness that comes through faith from beginning to end. This principle—that the righteous live by faith—is the foundation of his entire message.

45:1.title: God's Wrath Against Ungodliness and Idolatry
45:1.summary: Paul explains that God's wrath is revealed against those who suppress the truth through unrighteousness, despite God's clear self-revelation through creation. Humanity's refusal to honor God or give thanks led them into futile reasoning, spiritual darkness, and the foolish exchange of God's glory for idolatrous images.

45:1.title: Divine Judgment: Abandonment to Sinful Desires
45:1.summary: As consequence for rejecting God's truth, God delivered humanity over to degrading passions and dishonorable behaviors, including unnatural sexual acts that carry within themselves the penalty of their error. This judicial abandonment represents God's response to humanity's deliberate exchange of truth for lies.

45:1.title: A Depraved Mind and Catalog of Sins
45:1.summary: Because people refused to acknowledge God, He gave them over to a corrupted mind resulting in every kind of unrighteousness—from murder and deceit to malice and covenant-breaking. The passage concludes by condemning not only those who commit such sins but also those who approve of them, despite knowing God's judgment.

45:2.title: The Condemning Judgment of the Self-Righteous
45:2.summary: Paul confronts those who judge others while committing the same sins, exposing their hypocrisy and warning that God's judgment is true and inescapable. He calls them to recognize God's goodness as an invitation to repentance rather than an excuse for continued sin.

45:2.title: God's Impartial Judgment According to Works
45:2.summary: God will render to each person according to their deeds—eternal life to those who persevere in goodness, and wrath to those who pursue evil—with no distinction between Jew and Greek. This impartial judgment reflects that God shows no favoritism.

45:2.title: The Law and Conscience: Justified by Doing, Not Hearing
45:2.summary: Those who have the law will be judged by it; Gentiles without the written law are a law to themselves through their conscience. True righteousness comes from doing the law, not merely possessing or hearing it, and God will judge all hearts by Christ according to the gospel.

45:2.title: Jewish Privilege Brings Greater Responsibility and Hypocrisy
45:2.summary: Paul exposes the contradiction of Jewish teachers who boast in the law and God's will while breaking that very law through theft, adultery, and idolatry. Their disobedience causes God's name to be blasphemed among the Gentiles, negating any privilege their heritage provides.

45:2.title: True Jewishness and Circumcision of the Heart
45:2.summary: Circumcision is valuable only if one obeys the law; a lawbreaker's circumcision becomes uncircumcision. Conversely, an uncircumcised Gentile who keeps the law demonstrates true circumcision of the heart—the inward transformation that makes one truly Jewish before God.

45:3.title: Jewish Advantage and God's Faithfulness
45:3.summary: Paul affirms that Jews have the great advantage of being entrusted with God's word, and that human faithlessness cannot nullify God's faithfulness or justice. The objection that sin might glorify God's righteousness is rejected, along with the heresy that evil should be done for good to come.

45:3.title: Universal Sinfulness—Catena of Scripture
45:3.summary: Paul establishes through a chain of Old Testament quotations that all people—Jews and Gentiles alike—are under sin and characterized by spiritual corruption, moral failure, and absence of fear of God. No one is righteous or seeks God.

45:3.title: The Law's Function: Knowledge of Sin, Not Justification
45:3.summary: The law stops every mouth and brings all humanity under God's judgment, but it cannot justify anyone; its purpose is to reveal the knowledge of sin.

45:3.title: Righteousness Apart from the Law Revealed in Christ
45:3.summary: God's righteousness is now revealed apart from the law through faith in Jesus Christ, available to all who believe; Christ is the atoning sacrifice whose blood demonstrates God's justice in forgiving prior sins and justifying the faithful.

45:3.title: Boasting Excluded; Faith and the Law Established
45:3.summary: All grounds for boasting are excluded by the principle of faith rather than works; justification comes by faith alone, the same faith applies to both Jews and Gentiles, and faith in Christ upholds rather than nullifies the law.

45:4.title: Abraham Justified by Faith, Not Works
45:4.summary: Paul asks what Abraham found through human effort, then demonstrates from Scripture that Abraham was justified by faith in God, not by works. David also pronounces blessing on those whose sins are forgiven apart from works.

45:4.title: Abraham as Father of All Who Believe
45:4.summary: Abraham received the sign of circumcision while already justified in uncircumcision, establishing him as the father of all believers—both circumcised and uncircumcised—who follow his faith. This shows that faith, not circumcision, defines Abraham's true offspring.

45:4.title: The Promise Comes Through Faith and Grace, Not Law
45:4.summary: The promise that Abraham would be heir of the world came through righteousness of faith, not through the law; the law actually produces wrath, while faith ensures the promise rests on grace and is secure for all believers. This shows why the promise must be by faith rather than by law—so that it may be guaranteed to all of Abraham's spiritual descendants.

45:4.title: Abraham's Faith in God's Power to Give Life
45:4.summary: Abraham believed God's promise to make him father of many nations despite his aged body and Sarah's barrenness, trusting in God's power to raise the dead. His unwavering faith in God's promise was reckoned to him as righteousness, demonstrating the nature of saving faith.

45:4.title: Abraham's Justification as Pattern for Our Justification
45:4.summary: Abraham's justification was recorded not only for his own account but also for believers in the God who raised Jesus from the dead. Christ's death for our trespasses and resurrection for our justification fulfill the pattern established in Abraham's faith.

45:5.title: Justification Brings Peace and Access to Grace
45:5.summary: Through faith in Christ, believers are justified and have peace with God, gaining access to grace and the hope of sharing in God's glory. This establishes the foundation of the Christian's new standing before God.

45:5.title: Suffering Produces Hope Through the Holy Spirit
45:5.summary: Believers can rejoice even in suffering because hardship produces perseverance, which develops character and strengthens hope—a hope that will not disappoint because God's love has been poured into our hearts through the Holy Spirit. This reveals how suffering serves a redemptive purpose in Christian maturity.

45:5.title: The Demonstration of God's Love in Christ's Death
45:5.summary: Christ died for the weak, the ungodly, and sinners—a love far exceeding human willingness to die even for the righteous, thereby proving God's unique love toward us. Through Christ's death, enemies of God are reconciled, justified by his blood, and saved from wrath, giving believers reason to rejoice in their restored relationship with God.

45:5.title: Sin and Death Enter Through Adam
45:5.summary: Sin entered the world through one man (Adam), bringing death to all humanity because all sinned, with death reigning even over those whose sins differed from Adam's original disobedience. Adam's transgression foreshadows Christ, who would come to reverse this universal condition.

45:5.title: Christ's Righteousness Overcomes Adam's Trespass
45:5.summary: God's grace and gift through Christ far exceed the destructive impact of Adam's one trespass; where Adam's disobedience made many sinners, Christ's obedience justifies many to life and establishes them as righteous. This parallel structure shows Christ's redemptive work as vastly superior to and reversing the effects of the Fall.

45:5.title: Grace Reigning Through Righteousness to Eternal Life
45:5.summary: Though the law came in to increase awareness of trespass, grace abounded even more abundantly in response; just as sin reigned through death, grace now reigns through righteousness, leading to eternal life through Jesus Christ. This concludes Paul's argument by showing grace's ultimate triumph and the believer's inheritance of eternal life.

45:6.title: The Problem: Should We Continue in Sin?
45:6.summary: Paul addresses a misunderstanding of grace: should believers continue sinning so grace can abound? He firmly rejects this notion, asserting that those who died to sin cannot live in it any longer.

45:6.title: Death and Resurrection with Christ
45:6.summary: Through baptism, believers are united with Christ's death and resurrection, dying to sin and receiving new life. This union means freedom from sin's bondage and the power to live for God, since Christ's resurrection demonstrates victory over death.

45:6.title: Call to Live as Dead to Sin
45:6.summary: Believers must not allow sin to reign in their bodies but instead present their members as instruments of righteousness to God, assured that sin has no dominion over them because they live under grace, not law.

45:6.title: Freedom from Law Does Not Mean Freedom to Sin
45:6.summary: Paul clarifies that living under grace rather than law does not permit sinning; instead, believers have transferred their allegiance from sin to righteousness, becoming servants of obedience with the outcome of sanctification.

45:6.title: From Servants of Sin to Servants of Righteousness
45:6.summary: Just as believers once presented their bodies to sin, producing shame and death, they now present themselves to righteousness, producing sanctification and eternal life. The contrast concludes with the fundamental truth: sin's wage is death, but God's gift is eternal life through Christ.

45:7.title: Freedom from the Law Through Death in Christ
45:7.summary: Paul uses the analogy of marriage to explain that just as death frees a widow from her husband's law, believers are freed from the law's dominion through Christ's death, so they may be joined to the resurrected Christ and bear fruit for God in the Spirit rather than in fleshly passions.

45:7.title: The Law Reveals Sin But Is Not Sin
45:7.summary: Paul clarifies that the law itself is not sinful but holy and good; rather, the law functions to reveal and expose sin by showing us what we should not do, and sin uses the commandment to deceive and kill us, thereby demonstrating sin's extreme sinfulness.

45:7.title: The Struggle Between Flesh and Spirit
45:7.summary: Paul describes his personal struggle with the tension between his desire to obey God's law and his inability to do so because of sin dwelling in his flesh; though he delights in God's law inwardly, he finds himself enslaved to sin's law, and only Jesus Christ can deliver him from this wretchedness.

45:8.title: No Condemnation in Christ
45:8.summary: Those in Christ Jesus experience no condemnation because they walk by the Spirit rather than the flesh. God accomplished what the law could not by sending his Son to condemn sin in the flesh, so that the law's righteous requirement is fulfilled in those who follow the Spirit.

45:8.title: Life in the Spirit
45:8.summary: Those oriented toward the Spirit experience life and peace, while those oriented toward the flesh experience spiritual death. Believers possess Christ's Spirit dwelling within them, which gives eternal life and promises resurrection of their mortal bodies.

45:8.title: Children of God and Heirs with Christ
45:8.summary: Believers are obligated to live by the Spirit, not the flesh, and those led by God's Spirit are his children. Through the Spirit of adoption, believers cry out to God as Father, are confirmed as God's children, and become joint heirs with Christ, sharing in both his sufferings and his future glory.

45:8.title: Present Suffering and Future Glory
45:8.summary: Present sufferings are incomparable to the glory to come; all creation groans in anticipation of redemption, and believers likewise groan while awaiting the resurrection of their bodies. The hope we possess is invisible and sustains us through patient waiting.

45:8.title: The Spirit's Intercession and God's Sovereign Purpose
45:8.summary: The Spirit aids believers in their weakness by interceding for them with groanings too deep for words, and God knows the Spirit's mind and works all things together for good for those called according to his purpose. God's redemptive plan includes foreknowledge, predestination, calling, justification, and glorification.

45:8.title: God's Love Conquers All Opposition
45:8.summary: God's sacrifice of his own Son assures believers that nothing can separate them from Christ's love—not condemnation, nor any earthly trial, nor any spiritual force or cosmic power. Believers are more than conquerors through Christ's love, and nothing in creation can sever them from God's love in Christ.

45:9.title: Paul's Sorrow for Israel
45:9.summary: Paul solemnly affirms his deep grief over Israel's rejection of Christ, expressing such profound love for his people that he would be willing to forfeit his own salvation for their sake. He emphasizes Israel's unique spiritual privileges—adoption, glory, covenants, law, temple service, and promises—and notes that Christ himself came from the Jewish people.

45:9.title: God's Election and the True Israel
45:9.summary: Paul clarifies that God's word has not failed because true Israel consists not of all physical descendants of Abraham, but of those whom God has chosen according to promise. He illustrates this through examples of Isaac over Ishmael and Jacob over Esau, showing that divine election operates according to God's sovereign call, not human merit or ancestry.

45:9.title: God's Sovereignty and Justice
45:9.summary: Paul addresses the objection that God's selective mercy is unjust by affirming that God's will is not determined by human effort but by His own merciful purposes. He cites God's dealings with Moses and Pharaoh as proof that God extends mercy according to His own design and hardens whom He chooses.

45:9.title: The Potter and the Clay
45:9.summary: Paul uses the metaphor of a potter and clay to defend God's absolute right to deal with His creatures according to His purpose, whether to display His wrath through vessels of destruction or to reveal His glory through vessels of mercy prepared for honor. He notes that God's calling extends to both Jews and Gentiles.

45:9.title: The Remnant and the Gentiles
45:9.summary: Paul cites Old Testament prophecies showing that God's mercy extends to the Gentiles—calling them His people though they were not—while only a remnant of Israel will be saved. The prophets Isaiah and Hosea affirm that God preserves a seed and accomplishes His purposes despite Israel's unfaithfulness.

45:9.title: Israel's Stumbling and the Stumbling Stone
45:9.summary: Paul concludes by contrasting Gentiles who pursued no righteousness yet attained it through faith with Israel who pursued righteousness through the law but failed, because they sought it by works rather than faith. Christ, the stumbling stone, is the crucial issue—those who believe in Him will not be disappointed, but those who reject Him stumble.

45:10.title: Paul's Burden for Israel's Salvation
45:10.summary: Paul expresses his passionate desire and prayer for Israel's salvation, acknowledging that while they have zeal for God, it lacks knowledge because they are ignorant of God's righteousness and seek to establish their own instead of submitting to His.

45:10.title: Christ the End of the Law and Righteousness through Faith
45:10.summary: Paul explains that Christ is the fulfillment of the law for righteousness to all who believe, contrasting law-based righteousness (which requires works) with faith-based righteousness (which is accessible through confession and belief in the heart). Salvation comes through confessing Jesus as Lord and believing in His resurrection.

45:10.title: Salvation Available to All Without Distinction
45:10.summary: Scripture affirms that believers in Christ will not be disappointed, and Paul emphasizes that there is no distinction between Jew and Greek—the same Lord is rich toward all who call upon Him, and whoever calls on the Lord's name will be saved.

45:10.title: The Chain of Salvation: Hearing, Believing, Calling, and Preaching
45:10.summary: Paul presents a logical chain: people must call on Christ, believe in Him, hear about Him, and hear from those who are sent to preach. Faith itself comes through hearing the word of God, establishing the necessity of Gospel proclamation for salvation.

45:10.title: Israel's Rejection Despite God's Outreach
45:10.summary: Paul demonstrates that Israel has indeed heard the Gospel (the message has gone throughout the world), yet they have largely rejected it. Scripture shows God's persistent outreach to Israel—appearing to those who didn't seek Him, yet they remain a disobedient and contrary people despite God stretching out His hands to them all day long.

45:11.title: God's Faithfulness to Israel: The Remnant
45:11.summary: Paul affirms that God has not rejected Israel despite their rejection of Christ, pointing to the example of Elijah when God preserved a faithful remnant of seven thousand. Even in Paul's present time, a remnant exists chosen by grace, not works, while the rest were hardened according to Scripture.

45:11.title: The Olive Tree: Israel and the Gentiles
45:11.summary: Israel's stumbling brought salvation to the Gentiles, yet their fall is not final; rather, it provokes them to jealousy and offers hope for restoration. Paul uses the metaphor of an olive tree to show how Gentiles were grafted in among the natural branches (Israel), warning against pride and showing that God can graft the natural branches back in.

45:11.title: The Mystery of Israel's Salvation
45:11.summary: Paul reveals that Israel's partial hardening is temporary, lasting until the fullness of the Gentiles enters God's kingdom, after which all Israel will be saved through Christ. God's mercy extends to all people through a pattern of disobedience and subsequent grace, orchestrated to bring universal mercy.

45:11.title: Doxology: Praise to God's Infinite Wisdom
45:11.summary: Paul concludes with a hymn of praise acknowledging the unsearchable depths of God's wisdom, knowledge, and judgments, emphasizing that all things exist through God and for his glory. This doxology celebrates God's transcendence and ultimate authority over all creation.

45:12.title: Present Your Bodies as a Living Sacrifice
45:12.summary: Paul urges believers to offer themselves wholly to God as a spiritual act of worship, rejecting worldly conformity and seeking transformation through renewed minds to discern God's will.

45:12.title: Gifts and Function in the Body of Christ
45:12.summary: Paul teaches that believers should have humble self-assessment and recognize their place in Christ's body, where diverse spiritual gifts are distributed to all members for specific functions to be exercised faithfully.

45:12.title: Christian Virtue and Community Life
45:12.summary: Paul outlines the marks of genuine Christian love: sincere devotion to good, mutual affection and honor among believers, diligent service, spiritual fervor, hopeful joy, patient endurance, prayerfulness, generosity to the needy, and hospitable welcome.

45:12.title: Love Toward Enemies and Humility
45:12.summary: Paul calls believers to bless persecutors rather than curse them, to practice empathetic fellowship with others' joys and sorrows, and to maintain humble unity without pride or arrogance.

45:12.title: Overcoming Evil with Good
45:12.summary: Paul instructs believers to avoid retaliation, pursue peace with all people, leave judgment to God's justice, and actively overcome evil through kindness—feeding and helping even enemies as a means of spiritual victory.

45:13.title: Submission to Governing Authorities
45:13.summary: Paul instructs believers to submit to civil authorities as ordained by God, emphasizing that rulers serve God's purposes to reward good conduct and punish evil. Believers should pay taxes and give respect to those in authority as a matter of conscience and civic duty.

45:13.title: Love as the Fulfillment of the Law
45:13.summary: Paul teaches that the primary debt believers owe is love for one another, which fulfills all the commandments of the law. Love for one's neighbor summarizes and accomplishes the entire moral law.

45:13.title: Awakening to the Day and Putting On Christ
45:13.summary: Paul exhorts believers to awaken from spiritual sleep, recognizing that salvation's completion is nearer than ever, and to cast off works of darkness while putting on Christ and the armor of light. Living as children of the day means rejecting worldly indulgences and fleshly desires.

45:14.title: Accept the Weak in Faith
45:14.summary: Paul instructs the strong in faith to welcome those with weaker consciences without quarreling over disputable matters like diet. Believers should not despise or judge one another, recognizing that God has accepted all who believe.

45:14.title: All Belong to the Lord
45:14.summary: Paul emphasizes that Christians belong to God alone and will answer to Him, not to one another; Christ's death and resurrection established His lordship over both living and dead. Therefore, believers should not presume to judge fellow servants who stand or fall before God's throne.

45:14.title: The Judgment Seat of Christ
45:14.summary: Paul reminds believers that all will stand before Christ's judgment seat and give account of themselves to God, making mutual judgment among believers both presumptuous and unnecessary. This future accountability should shape present conduct and relationships.

45:14.title: Pursue Peace and Build Up Others
45:14.summary: Rather than judging each other, believers should focus on not causing others to stumble; Christian freedom in morally neutral matters must yield to love and the other's conscience. The kingdom of God consists not of external practices but of righteousness, peace, and joy in the Holy Spirit.

45:14.title: Pursue Peace and Avoid Causing Offense
45:14.summary: Paul exhorts believers to pursue peace and mutual edification, not destroying God's work through insistence on personal preferences in indifferent matters. Whatever is not done in faith is sin, and believers should act according to their own conscience without judging others.

45:14.title: Doxology: Glory to God Through Christ
45:14.summary: Paul concludes with a doxology ascribing glory to God who can establish believers through the gospel of Christ and the revelation of the mystery now made known to all nations. This ascription of praise affirms God's eternal wisdom and power.

45:15.title: Obligation to Bear with the Weak
45:15.summary: Paul exhorts the strong to bear with the weak, following Christ's example of self-denial and acceptance. Unity among believers is achieved through accepting one another as Christ has accepted us, for God's glory.

45:15.title: Christ as Servant to Both Jews and Gentiles
45:15.summary: Christ became a servant of the Jews to confirm God's promises to the patriarchs, and through him the Gentiles glorify God for his mercy. Paul invokes Old Testament testimony that both Jews and Gentiles are called to praise God together.

45:15.title: Paul's Apostolic Ministry to the Gentiles
45:15.summary: Paul affirms his confidence in the Romans' spiritual maturity and explains his bold writing as flowing from his apostolic grace to serve Christ among the Gentiles. He has preached the gospel comprehensively from Jerusalem to Illyricum, seeking always to pioneer new territory.

45:15.title: Paul's Travel Plans and Hoped Visit to Rome
45:15.summary: Paul explains that he has been prevented from visiting Rome but now desires to travel there en route to Spain. He first must deliver a collection from Macedonia and Achaia to the poor saints in Jerusalem, completing this ministry before coming to the Romans.

45:15.title: Request for Prayer and Apostolic Benediction
45:15.summary: Paul earnestly requests the Romans to pray for him—that he be protected from enemies in Judea and that his service to Jerusalem be acceptable to the saints. He closes with a prayer for God's peace upon them.

45:16.title: Commendation of Phoebe
45:16.summary: Paul commends Phoebe, a deacon of the church at Cenchreae, to the Roman church, asking them to receive her worthily and assist her as she has helped many others including Paul himself.

45:16.title: Greetings to Roman Christians
45:16.summary: Paul sends greetings to numerous individuals and households in Rome, commending their faith, service, and sacrifice in Christ, and emphasizing the unity and mutual love within the church.

45:16.title: Warning Against False Teachers
45:16.summary: Paul warns the Romans to avoid those who cause divisions and stumbling blocks contrary to true doctrine, identifying them as self-serving deceivers, and assures them that God will defeat Satan and grant them grace.

45:16.title: Final Greetings and Benediction
45:16.summary: Paul relays greetings from his companions Timothy, Lucius, Jason, Sosipater, Tertius, Gaius, Erastus, and Quartus, and concludes with a benediction of grace from Jesus Christ.

46:1.title: Salutation
46:1.summary: Paul greets the church at Corinth, identifying himself as an apostle called by God's will, and pronounces grace and peace upon them from God the Father and Jesus Christ.

46:1.title: Thanksgiving for the Corinthians' Spiritual Gifts
46:1.summary: Paul thanks God for the grace given to the Corinthians in Christ, noting their enrichment in speech and knowledge, their spiritual gifts, and God's faithfulness to sustain them until Christ's revelation.

46:1.title: Appeal for Unity and Warning Against Division
46:1.summary: Paul urgently appeals to the Corinthians to speak in unity and end their divisions into factions following Paul, Apollos, and Cephas, asserting that Christ cannot be divided and reminding them that he was sent to preach the Gospel, not to baptize.

46:1.title: The Wisdom of God versus the Wisdom of the World
46:1.summary: Paul contrasts the world's wisdom with God's wisdom, explaining that the message of the cross—foolishness to the world—is God's power to save, and that God's foolishness and weakness surpass human wisdom and strength.

46:1.title: God's Choice of the Weak and Lowly
46:1.summary: Paul reminds the Corinthians that God has chosen what the world considers foolish, weak, and despised to shame the wise and mighty, so that no one boasts before God but only in the Lord, who has become their wisdom, righteousness, sanctification, and redemption.

46:2.title: Paul's Apostolic Method: Spiritual Power Over Human Wisdom
46:2.summary: Paul reminds the Corinthians that he came to them not with eloquent speech or worldly wisdom, but in weakness and dependence on God's Spirit, so their faith would rest on God's power rather than human persuasion. His preaching demonstrated the Spirit's power rather than relying on rhetorical skill.

46:2.title: God's Hidden Wisdom Revealed in Christ
46:2.summary: Paul explains that while worldly rulers know nothing of God's deep wisdom—a mystery hidden before the ages and foreordained for believers' glory—this hidden wisdom was ultimately demonstrated in Christ's crucifixion, which the rulers failed to understand. God's wisdom surpasses human comprehension and includes blessings beyond what any natural eye has seen or ear heard.

46:2.title: The Spirit's Role in Revealing God's Wisdom
46:2.summary: Paul teaches that God's Spirit reveals to believers the deep things of God that would otherwise remain hidden, just as a person's own spirit knows their private thoughts. Through the Spirit, believers receive and speak God's wisdom, not in human-taught words but in Spirit-taught language that communicates spiritual truths to spiritual people.

46:2.title: Spiritual Discernment and the Mind of Christ
46:2.summary: Paul contrasts the natural person who cannot receive God's Spirit's truths—finding them foolish—with the spiritual person who discerns all things and stands under God's judgment. Believers possess Christ's mind, enabling them to understand and evaluate all things according to God's wisdom.

46:3.title: Spiritual Immaturity and Divisive Factions
46:3.summary: Paul rebukes the Corinthians for remaining spiritually immature, unable to receive solid teaching, and for creating factional divisions by following human leaders like Paul and Apollos rather than Christ. Their jealousy, strife, and party spirit reveal they are still controlled by fleshly desires rather than the Spirit.

46:3.title: God's Workers and God's Building
46:3.summary: Paul clarifies that apostles like himself and Apollos are merely servants through whom God works, each with different roles in planting and watering, but God alone provides spiritual growth. The Corinthians themselves are God's field and building, made holy through His work.

46:3.title: Building on the Foundation with Accountability
46:3.summary: Paul describes himself as a master builder who laid the foundation of Jesus Christ in Corinth, and warns others to build carefully upon it, for the Day of Judgment will test the quality of each person's work by fire—some will be rewarded, others will suffer loss but be saved. Every worker will give account for how they built upon Christ.

46:3.title: The Temple of God and Divine Judgment
46:3.summary: Paul reminds the Corinthians that they collectively are God's temple indwelt by His Spirit and therefore holy; anyone who destroys this temple—through division or immorality—will face God's destruction. The church's sanctity demands protection.

46:3.title: Worldly Wisdom Exposed as Folly
46:3.summary: Paul warns against self-deception through worldly wisdom, calling the Corinthians to become fools by the world's standards in order to gain true wisdom from God. Scripture confirms that God sees through human cunning and finds the world's reasoning worthless.

46:3.title: Everything Belongs to You in Christ
46:3.summary: Paul concludes by forbidding boasting in human leaders, declaring that all things—whether apostles, the world, life, death, present, or future—belong to the Corinthians as God's people, and that they belong to Christ, who belongs to God. True wealth comes not from following individuals but from belonging to Christ.

46:4.title: Servants and Stewards of God's Mysteries
46:4.summary: Paul instructs the Corinthians to regard him and other apostles as Christ's servants entrusted with God's mysteries, requiring faithfulness. He emphasizes that human judgment of him is insignificant and that only the Lord's judgment matters, which will reveal hidden things and give proper praise at the appointed time.

46:4.title: Warning Against Arrogance and Sectarianism
46:4.summary: Paul uses himself and Apollos as examples to teach the Corinthians not to go beyond what Scripture says and to avoid pride in following particular leaders. He rebukes their spiritual pride by sarcastically noting they think themselves already mature and exalted without the apostles.

46:4.title: The Apostles' Suffering and Hardship
46:4.summary: Paul contrasts the Corinthians' comfort with the apostles' afflictions, describing themselves as condemned men displayed as a spectacle and enduring hunger, weakness, homelessness, labor, persecution, and disgrace. This vivid account reveals the cost of apostolic ministry and true Christian discipleship.

46:4.title: Paul's Paternal Relationship and Example
46:4.summary: Paul clarifies his harsh words are meant to admonish rather than shame his spiritual children, emphasizing his unique role as their father in Christ through the gospel. He sends Timothy to remind them of his apostolic ways and to reinforce his teachings across all churches.

46:4.title: Paul's Promised Visit and Appeal for Obedience
46:4.summary: Paul warns against those who are puffed up, assuming he won't come, and promises to visit soon to demonstrate God's power rather than mere words. He appeals to the Corinthians about whether he should come with correction or with love and gentleness, leaving the choice dependent on their response.

46:5.title: Discipline of the Immoral Brother
46:5.summary: Paul addresses a case of sexual immorality in the Corinthian church—a man in a relationship with his stepmother—and commands the church to deliver him to Satan through removal from the community so that his spirit may be saved on the day of judgment.

46:5.title: Leaven and Passover Imagery
46:5.summary: Paul warns that a little yeast corrupts the whole batch and calls the church to purge out the old yeast and live as the unleavened people of God, since Christ our Passover has been sacrificed; believers must therefore celebrate with sincerity and truth rather than malice.

46:5.title: Clarification on Association with Sinners
46:5.summary: Paul clarifies his previous instruction about not associating with sexual sinners—he means believers who are sexually immoral, not unbelievers in the world, since judging outsiders belongs to God alone; the church must judge and remove the wicked from among themselves.

46:6.title: Lawsuits Among Believers
46:6.summary: Paul rebukes the Corinthians for taking disputes before pagan courts instead of settling them among themselves, arguing that believers' future role as judges should motivate them to resolve conflicts internally rather than bringing shame to the church through public litigation.

46:6.title: The Unrighteous Will Not Inherit God's Kingdom
46:6.summary: Paul lists sins that exclude people from God's kingdom—sexual immorality, idolatry, theft, drunkenness, and more—but reassures the Corinthians that through Christ they have been washed, sanctified, and justified, transforming their identity and future.

46:6.title: Christian Freedom and Bodily Resurrection
46:6.summary: Paul clarifies that while all things are lawful, not all are beneficial, and the believer should not be enslaved by anything; he contrasts temporary bodily appetites with God's plan to raise believers' bodies, establishing the body's eternal significance.

46:6.title: The Body as Temple of the Holy Spirit
46:6.summary: Paul teaches that believers' bodies are members of Christ and temples of the Holy Spirit, making sexual immorality a sin against one's own body; he calls Christians to flee immorality and glorify God with their bodies, which they do not own but were purchased by Christ's price.

46:7.title: Marriage and Celibacy: Paul's Opening Teaching
46:7.summary: Paul addresses questions about marriage, affirming celibacy as good but recognizing marriage as a remedy against sexual immorality and as divinely gifted. He expresses his personal preference for celibacy while acknowledging that each person has their own calling from God.

46:7.title: Counsel to the Unmarried and Married
46:7.summary: Paul advises the unmarried and widows that remaining single is good, but those lacking self-control should marry rather than struggle with sexual temptation. For the married, he commands (citing the Lord's authority) that spouses not separate, or if they do, remain unmarried or be reconciled.

46:7.title: Mixed Marriages and the Unbelieving Spouse
46:7.summary: Paul instructs believers married to unbelievers not to initiate divorce if the unbeliever is willing to remain; the believing spouse's faith sanctifies the household and children. However, if the unbeliever departs, separation is permitted and the believer is not bound; believers should remain hopeful that their faithfulness might lead to the spouse's conversion.

46:7.title: Remain in Your Calling
46:7.summary: Paul establishes a principle applicable across all circumstances: each person should remain in the condition to which God has called them—whether circumcised or uncircumcised, bondservant or free—because ultimate identity is found in Christ, not in social status. True freedom and slavery are spiritual realities, not merely social ones.

46:7.title: Judgment Concerning Virgins and the Present Distress
46:7.summary: Paul offers pastoral advice to virgins (unmarried women) and those responsible for them, recommending singleness because of present troubles and to allow undistracted devotion to the Lord. However, marriage is not sinful; he aims to spare people from the practical difficulties marriage brings in times of crisis.

46:7.title: The Freedom and Virtue of Both Marriage and Singleness
46:7.summary: Paul clarifies that a guardian or father may give a virgin in marriage without sin, but he who steadfastly keeps her unmarried does better. He concludes with teaching on widows' freedom to remarry (only in the Lord) but notes that remaining unmarried brings greater happiness—a judgment Paul believes reflects the Spirit's wisdom.

46:8.title: Knowledge versus Love
46:8.summary: Paul contrasts knowledge, which inflates pride, with love, which builds up others. True knowledge of God is not mere intellectual understanding but is demonstrated through loving God in return.

46:8.title: The Nature of God and Christ
46:8.summary: Paul affirms monotheism—that idols have no real existence and there is only one true God the Father and one Lord Jesus Christ through whom all things exist. This theological foundation establishes that eating food sacrificed to idols poses no spiritual danger to the knowledgeable believer.

46:8.title: Freedom Must Not Become a Stumbling Block
46:8.summary: Not all believers have the knowledge that idols are powerless; some with weak consciences are defiled when they eat such food. Though food itself doesn't affect our standing with God, Christians must use their freedom responsibly to avoid causing weaker believers to stumble.

46:8.title: The Danger of Emboldening the Weak
46:8.summary: When a knowledgeable believer eats food sacrificed to idols before a weaker brother, it may embolden that weak brother to act against his own conscience, leading to his spiritual harm. Such an action sins not only against the brother but against Christ himself.

46:8.title: Paul's Commitment to His Weaker Brothers
46:8.summary: Paul concludes by personally pledging to abstain from meat entirely if it would prevent his brothers from stumbling, demonstrating that love and the welfare of fellow believers takes priority over personal freedom.

46:9.title: Paul's Apostolic Authority
46:9.summary: Paul asserts his freedom and apostolic credentials, having seen the risen Jesus and having established the Corinthian church as proof of his apostleship. His authority, though questioned by some, is confirmed by the reality of his work among them.

46:9.title: The Right to Financial Support
46:9.summary: Paul defends apostolic rights to food, drink, marriage, and financial support through Old Testament examples and Jesus's teaching, but explains he voluntarily foregoes these rights to avoid hindering the Gospel. He demonstrates that privilege must sometimes be surrendered for the sake of others.

46:9.title: Paul's Voluntary Renunciation of Rights
46:9.summary: Though the Lord ordained that Gospel proclaimers should be supported, Paul chose to preach without charge, wanting his boasting in ministry to rest on faithful proclamation rather than on receiving compensation. His constraint comes from inner necessity rather than external obligation.

46:9.title: Becoming All Things to All People
46:9.summary: Paul describes his missionary strategy of cultural and religious accommodation—becoming like a Jew to Jews, like the lawless to the lawless, like the weak to the weak—all to win more people to Christ and become a participant in the Gospel's blessings. His aim is not personal gain but the salvation of others.

46:9.title: Discipline and Perseverance in the Christian Race
46:9.summary: Paul exhorts believers to run the Christian race with certainty and discipline, comparing it to athletic competition: just as athletes exercise self-control for a perishable prize, believers must discipline their bodies and lives to obtain an imperishable reward and avoid disqualification. Personal integrity and perseverance to the end are essential.

46:10.title: Israel's Wilderness Experience as a Warning
46:10.summary: Paul recounts how the Israelites experienced God's miraculous provisions (cloud, sea crossing, spiritual food and drink from Christ) yet failed to please Him through idolatry, sexual immorality, testing God, and grumbling. These historical examples serve as warnings for believers living in the final age to avoid similar sins.

46:10.title: God's Faithfulness in Temptation
46:10.summary: Paul assures believers that God is faithful and will not allow them to be tempted beyond their capacity, but will always provide a way of escape so they can endure temptation with strength.

46:10.title: Flee Idolatry and Avoid Demonic Fellowship
46:10.summary: Paul commands believers to flee idolatry and explains that participation in the Lord's Supper establishes a covenant bond with Christ, just as eating temple sacrifices creates fellowship with an altar. Since Gentile sacrifices are offered to demons, believers cannot participate in both God's table and demons' table without provoking the Lord's jealousy.

46:10.title: Christian Liberty Exercised in Love and Conscience
46:10.summary: Though all things may be lawfully permitted, Paul teaches that believers should prioritize the spiritual welfare of others and their conscience over personal freedom; when dining with unbelievers or when someone indicates food was sacrificed to idols, one should abstain for the sake of that person's conscience and growth in faith.

46:10.title: All Actions Done for God's Glory and Others' Salvation
46:10.summary: Paul concludes that believers should conduct all of life—eating, drinking, and every action—to the glory of God and for the benefit and salvation of others, avoiding offense to Jews, Greeks, and the church.

46:11.title: Imitate Paul as He Imitates Christ
46:11.summary: Paul urges the Corinthians to follow his example as he follows Christ, and commends them for maintaining the traditions he delivered to them.

46:11.title: Head Coverings in Worship
46:11.summary: Paul establishes the principle of headship (Christ over man, man over woman, God over Christ) and applies it to the custom of head coverings during prayer and prophecy, instructing that men should pray uncovered and women should pray covered, reflecting God's created order.

46:11.title: Divisions at the Lord's Supper
46:11.summary: Paul rebukes the Corinthians for coming together in a way that exacerbates divisions rather than unity, condemning their practice of eating individual meals and shaming those without food, rather than partaking of the Lord's Supper together.

46:11.title: The Institution of the Lord's Supper
46:11.summary: Paul recounts the words and actions of Jesus at the Last Supper, establishing the sacrament of bread and cup as a memorial of Christ's body broken and blood shed, to be repeated until his return.

46:11.title: Unworthy Participation and Self-Examination
46:11.summary: Paul warns that consuming the bread and cup unworthily brings judgment, and calls believers to examine themselves and discern the Lord's body before eating, noting that failure to do so has resulted in weakness, sickness, and death among the Corinthians.

46:11.title: Instructions for Proper Order at the Supper
46:11.summary: Paul instructs the Corinthians to wait for one another when gathering to eat and directs the hungry to eat at home, preventing the meal from becoming an occasion of judgment, with the promise to address remaining matters when he visits.

46:12.title: Introduction to Spiritual Gifts and the Test of the Spirit
46:12.summary: Paul introduces the topic of spiritual gifts and establishes that genuine confession of Jesus as Lord comes only through the Holy Spirit, distinguishing true spiritual speech from false.

46:12.title: Varieties of Gifts, Services, and Workings from One Spirit
46:12.summary: Paul explains that though there are many different spiritual gifts and ministries, they all come from the same Spirit, Lord, and God who distributes them individually according to his will for the common good.

46:12.title: The Body of Christ and Baptism into One Spirit
46:12.summary: Just as a physical body has many members but is one whole, so Christ's church is one body composed of many members; all believers are baptized into this one body and share one Spirit regardless of background.

46:12.title: Every Member Necessary to the Body
46:12.summary: Paul uses the metaphor of body parts to show that every member is necessary and none can claim insignificance; diversity of function strengthens rather than weakens the whole.

46:12.title: Mutual Care and Honor Among Body Members
46:12.summary: Members should not despise one another; weaker or less honorable parts deserve greater honor and care, and all members should suffer and rejoice together in genuine solidarity and compassion.

46:12.title: Apostolic Roles and the Desire for the Best Gifts
46:12.summary: Paul reminds the Corinthians they are Christ's body with individual roles, lists the ordained gifts in order of importance, emphasizes not all have the same gifts, and exhorts them to earnestly desire the greatest gifts while pointing to a more excellent way.

46:13.title: Love is Superior to All Spiritual Gifts
46:13.summary: Without love, even the most impressive spiritual gifts—eloquent speech, prophecy, knowledge, faith, and sacrificial generosity—are worthless and unprofitable. Paul establishes that love is the essential foundation that gives all spiritual abilities their true value.

46:13.title: The Character of Love
46:13.summary: Paul describes love's defining qualities: it is patient, kind, humble, and selfless; it does not envy, boast, act rudely, or seek its own interests; it endures all hardships and always believes the best in others. These verses paint a portrait of love as both a virtue and an active force in relationships.

46:13.title: Love's Permanence and the Temporary Nature of Spiritual Gifts
46:13.summary: While prophecy, languages, and knowledge are temporary and will cease when God's complete revelation comes, love never fails and will endure forever. Paul explains that partial spiritual gifts will be superseded by complete spiritual understanding.

46:13.title: From Childhood to Maturity in Understanding
46:13.summary: Paul uses the metaphor of growing from childhood to adulthood to illustrate humanity's progression from partial knowledge to complete understanding; currently we see God only dimly as in a mirror, but ultimately we will see him face to face with full knowledge.

46:13.title: Love is the Greatest Virtue
46:13.summary: Of the three transcendent virtues—faith, hope, and love—that will remain eternally, love is the greatest because it encompasses and supersedes the others in its supreme importance to God's character and human relationships.

46:14.title: Prophecy Preferred Over Tongues
46:14.summary: Paul urges the Corinthians to pursue love and earnestly desire spiritual gifts, particularly prophecy, which is superior to speaking in tongues because it edifies the whole assembly rather than the individual speaker alone.

46:14.title: The Necessity of Intelligibility
46:14.summary: Through illustrations from musical instruments and human speech, Paul demonstrates that communication without clarity is useless, and the Corinthians should seek spiritual gifts that build up the assembly rather than themselves.

46:14.title: Spirit and Understanding in Prayer and Worship
46:14.summary: Paul teaches that while praying in tongues engages the spirit, believers should also pray with understanding so that others are edified; he illustrates this by saying he would rather speak five understandable words than ten thousand in tongues.

46:14.title: Tongues and Prophecy as Signs
46:14.summary: Paul cites Old Testament law to explain that tongues function as a sign for unbelievers (often hardening them), while prophecy convicts and converts believers and unbelievers alike by revealing the secrets of their hearts and drawing them to worship.

46:14.title: Order in Worship and Use of Gifts
46:14.summary: Paul establishes practical regulations for orderly worship: tongues should be limited to two or three with interpretation, prophets should speak in turn with discernment, and all things must be done decently for mutual edification, since God is a God of peace not confusion.

46:14.title: Women's Silence and Final Exhortation
46:14.summary: Paul instructs women to remain silent in assemblies and submit according to the law, with permission to ask their husbands at home; he closes by affirming that the Corinthians should desire prophecy and not forbid tongues, but let all things be done decently and in order.

46:15.title: The Gospel Message Received and Held
46:15.summary: Paul reminds the Corinthians that the gospel he preached is the message they received and in which they stand, and by which they are saved if they hold firmly to it. Their salvation depends on maintaining faith in this foundational proclamation.

46:15.title: The Core Gospel: Christ's Death, Resurrection, and Appearances
46:15.summary: Paul recites the essential gospel tradition: Christ died for sins, was buried, was raised on the third day according to Scripture, and appeared to numerous witnesses including Peter, the twelve, five hundred believers, James, and all the apostles, finally to Paul himself. Though unworthy due to his past persecution, Paul testifies that grace enabled him to labor faithfully alongside the other apostles in proclaiming this unified message.

46:15.title: The Problem: Denying the Resurrection of the Dead
46:15.summary: Paul addresses the contradiction some Corinthians make by preaching Christ's resurrection while denying the general resurrection of the dead; if there is no resurrection, then Christ was not raised, making the apostolic message and the believers' faith meaningless, leaving them still in their sins and making Christians the most pitiable of all people. The logical inconsistency threatens the very foundation of Christian hope.

46:15.title: Christ's Resurrection as First Fruits and the Order of Resurrection
46:15.summary: Paul affirms that Christ has indeed been raised and is the first fruits guaranteeing that all believers will be made alive in their proper order: Christ first, then those belonging to him at his coming, and finally the end when Christ delivers the kingdom to God after abolishing all enemies and subjecting all things to himself. This eschatological structure shows Christ's reign will culminate in the complete victory of God over all opposition.

46:15.title: Practical Arguments from Resurrection Hope
46:15.summary: Paul appeals to concrete Christian practices and Paul's own suffering to demonstrate the absurdity of denying resurrection: believers practice baptism for the dead, Paul endures daily danger, and the Corinthians would have no reason to resist sinful living if the dead are not raised. He warns them against being deceived by empty philosophies and calls them to righteous living grounded in belief in resurrection.

46:15.title: The Nature of the Resurrection Body
46:15.summary: Addressing the question of how the dead are raised and in what form, Paul uses the analogy of a seed dying and being transformed into a living plant to explain that the resurrection body is a real but glorified form, not merely spiritual. Drawing on Adam and Christ as representatives of natural and spiritual existence, Paul shows that believers will be transformed from earthly, mortal bodies into heavenly, immortal bodies bearing Christ's image.

46:15.title: The Mystery of Transformation and Victory Over Death
46:15.summary: Paul reveals the mystery that not all believers will experience death but all will be transformed in a moment at the trumpet sound, when the corruptible puts on incorruption and mortality becomes immortal, fulfilling the promise that death is defeated. He celebrates that through Christ's victory, sin's power is broken and exhorts believers to labor steadfastly knowing their work in the Lord will not be in vain.

46:16.title: Instructions for the Collection for Jerusalem
46:16.summary: Paul instructs the Corinthians to set aside money weekly for the poor saints in Jerusalem, following the example of the Galatian churches, so that no special collection will be needed when he arrives. Approved messengers will deliver the gift to Jerusalem, and Paul may accompany them if appropriate.

46:16.title: Paul's Travel Plans
46:16.summary: Paul announces his travel itinerary, planning to visit the Corinthians after passing through Macedonia, hoping to stay longer rather than just pass through. He will remain at Ephesus until Pentecost because of an open door for ministry despite many adversaries.

46:16.title: Paul's Commendation of Timothy and Apollos
46:16.summary: Paul commends Timothy, who is doing the Lord's work, asking the Corinthians to receive him without fear and send him on safely. He also addresses Apollos, noting that while he did not wish to come now, he will visit when the opportunity arises.

46:16.title: Exhortation to Steadfastness and Love
46:16.summary: Paul issues a brief but powerful exhortation calling the Corinthians to vigilance, firm faith, courage, and strength, while emphasizing that all their actions must be motivated by love.

46:16.title: Recognition of Stephanas and His Household
46:16.summary: Paul urges the Corinthians to submit to and honor Stephanas and his household, who were the first converts in Achaia and are devoted to serving the saints; he commends them for supplying what was lacking and refreshing both his spirit and theirs.

46:16.title: Final Greetings and Benediction
46:16.summary: Paul relays greetings from the Asian churches and notably from Aquila and Priscilla and the assembly meeting in their house; he instructs a holy kiss among believers, personally signs the letter, pronounces a curse on those who do not love Christ, calls for His coming, and closes with grace, love, and blessing.

47:1.title: Salutation
47:1.summary: Paul and Timothy greet the church at Corinth and all the saints in Achaia, offering them grace and peace from God the Father and Jesus Christ. This establishes the apostolic authority and the recipient community for the epistle.

47:1.title: God of All Comfort
47:1.summary: Paul blesses God as the Father of mercies who comforts believers in affliction so they can comfort others, explaining that shared suffering and comfort strengthen the church's faith and hope. This foundational doxology establishes the theological framework for understanding trials as opportunities for ministry.

47:1.title: Affliction in Asia and Divine Deliverance
47:1.summary: Paul recounts a severe affliction in Asia where he and his companions despaired of life, but God delivered them from death to teach them reliance on God rather than themselves. He asks the Corinthians to support them through prayer so that thanksgiving may abound for their deliverance.

47:1.title: Paul's Conduct and Testimony
47:1.summary: Paul affirms that his conscience testifies to his holy and sincere behavior guided by God's grace rather than worldly wisdom, and he appeals to the Corinthians' own acknowledgment of his apostolic integrity. This establishes mutual confidence between Paul and the church.

47:1.title: Paul's Travel Plans and Accusation of Fickleness
47:1.summary: Paul explains his planned visits to Corinth and subsequent travel to Macedonia and Judea, defending himself against implications of inconsistency or worldly motives in his decision-making. This addresses criticism about his reliability as an apostle.

47:1.title: God's Faithfulness and the Yes in Christ
47:1.summary: Paul argues that God's faithfulness ensures his word is reliable, declaring that Jesus Christ embodies God's trustworthiness as the final 'Yes' to all God's promises, and that believers are sealed and assured by the Holy Spirit. This theological affirmation grounds Paul's credibility in God's nature.

47:1.title: Paul's Pastoral Intent and Authority
47:1.summary: Paul calls God as witness that he spared the Corinthians from a visit not out of harshness but to work alongside them as fellow servants for their joy, affirming their standing in faith. This clarifies his apostolic role as supportive rather than tyrannical.

47:2.title: Paul's Sorrowful Letter Written in Love
47:2.summary: Paul determined not to visit the Corinthians in sorrow, and explains that his previous letter, written with great affliction and tears, was motivated by his abundant love for them, not to cause pain but to demonstrate his deep care.

47:2.title: Forgiveness and Restoration of the Offender
47:2.summary: Paul addresses a specific offender in the congregation, urging the Corinthians to move beyond the punishment already administered to forgive and comfort him, so that Satan cannot exploit excessive sorrow; Paul also assures them of his own forgiveness and urges obedience to prevent Satan's advantage.

47:2.title: Paul's Anxiety Over Missing Titus
47:2.summary: Despite finding an open door for the Gospel in Troas, Paul could find no relief in his spirit and left to Macedonia because he was troubled at not finding his brother Titus, showing Paul's deep pastoral concern for his companions.

47:2.title: Apostles as the Aroma of Christ
47:2.summary: Paul declares that God leads believers in Christ's triumph and that apostles are a sweet aroma of Christ's knowledge to God—both saving and condemning depending on response—and affirms that unlike those peddling God's word for profit, the apostles speak with sincerity from God's presence in Christ.

47:3.title: The Corinthians as Paul's Letter of Commendation
47:3.summary: Paul dismisses the need for letters of commendation, stating that the Corinthian believers themselves are his letter of recommendation—written on his heart and known to all—and that they are Christ's letter written by the Spirit on hearts of flesh rather than stone tablets.

47:3.title: Sufficiency Through Christ and the New Covenant
47:3.summary: Paul affirms that Christian confidence comes through Christ, not from personal adequacy, and establishes that God's sufficiency enables apostles to serve the new covenant of the Spirit rather than the letter, for the Spirit gives life while the letter brings death.

47:3.title: The Greater Glory of the Spirit Over the Old Covenant
47:3.summary: Paul compares the glory of the old covenant (written on stone, with Moses' face shining) to the far greater and permanent glory of the new covenant of the Spirit, arguing that if the temporary ministry brought such glory, the eternal Spirit-ministry must surpass it immeasurably.

47:3.title: Boldness in Contrast to Moses' Veil
47:3.summary: Paul contrasts his bold proclamation with Moses, who veiled his face to hide the fading glory of the old covenant, and explains that Israel's minds remain veiled when the old covenant is read because understanding comes only in Christ.

47:3.title: The Veil Removed and Transformation in the Lord
47:3.summary: Paul teaches that the veil remains on people's hearts when Moses is read, but when anyone turns to the Lord, the veil is removed and believers—with unveiled faces—are transformed from glory to glory into Christ's image through the Spirit's power.

47:4.title: Apostolic Ministry with Integrity
47:4.summary: Because Paul and his fellow ministers have received mercy, they do not lose heart in their ministry. They have rejected deceit and hidden shame, instead commending themselves through truthful proclamation of the gospel to people's consciences before God.

47:4.title: The Gospel Veiled to the Perishing
47:4.summary: Though the gospel message is veiled to those destined to perish—whose minds have been blinded by the god of this world—God's light has shone in the hearts of believers to reveal the glory of Christ, the image of God. This divine illumination comes through God's creative power, the same power that called light into existence at creation.

47:4.title: Treasure in Earthen Vessels
47:4.summary: Paul describes how God's surpassing power dwells in fragile human vessels so that the power clearly belongs to God rather than to humans. Despite constant pressure, persecution, and hardship, believers are not crushed or destroyed because they carry the dying of Jesus in their bodies, through which Christ's resurrection life is revealed.

47:4.title: Death and Life at Work
47:4.summary: Though death works in the apostles through their suffering, life works in the Corinthian believers through their benefit. Filled with the same faith that moved the psalmist to speak, Paul and his companions believe and therefore speak, knowing that God who raised Jesus will also raise believers and present them before Him, so that multiplied grace produces abundant thanksgiving to God's glory.

47:4.title: Inward Renewal and Eternal Perspective
47:4.summary: Though the outward body decays, the inward self is renewed each day, and present afflictions—though momentary—produce eternal glory far exceeding them. Believers must focus not on visible, temporal things but on invisible, eternal realities.

47:5.title: Earthly Tent and Heavenly Building
47:5.summary: Paul assures believers that when their earthly bodies are destroyed, God has prepared an eternal dwelling in heaven, and the Holy Spirit serves as a down payment guaranteeing this future inheritance. Though believers groan under the burden of mortal existence, they long not for non-existence but for resurrection bodies that will swallow up death with eternal life.

47:5.title: Walking by Faith and Judgment
47:5.summary: Paul explains that faith, not physical sight, governs the believer's walk in this present separation from the Lord, and this reality motivates Christians to live to please Christ, knowing all will appear before his judgment seat to receive according to their deeds. Believers are called to courage, willing to endure bodily absence from Christ in order to be present with him eternally.

47:5.title: Motivated by the Fear of the Lord
47:5.summary: Paul's ministry of persuasion is driven by reverence for God and transparency before him, and whether he appears ecstatic or calm, his actions serve either God's glory or the Corinthians' edification. He distinguishes his apostolic motivation from self-commendation, instead offering himself as a resource for the believers to defend against boasters who value external appearance over inner substance.

47:5.title: The Love of Christ and New Creation
47:5.summary: The constraining love of Christ, demonstrated in his death for all, transforms the believer's understanding: Christ's death and resurrection mean believers no longer live for themselves but for him, and personal knowledge of Christ shifts from earthly categories to spiritual reality. Anyone united to Christ enters new creation reality where all old things pass away and everything becomes new.

47:5.title: Ministry of Reconciliation and Exchange of Righteousness
47:5.summary: God initiated reconciliation through Christ and entrusts believers with the message and ministry of reconciliation, urging others to be reconciled to God whose grace does not count their trespasses against them. The gospel's climax is Christ's substitutionary exchange: he, the sinless one, became sin itself so that believers might become God's righteousness.

47:6.title: Appeal to Receive God's Grace
47:6.summary: Paul urges the Corinthians not to receive God's grace in vain, emphasizing that now is the acceptable time and day of salvation. He appeals to them to respond to God's saving offer in the present moment.

47:6.title: Paul's Defense of His Apostolic Ministry
47:6.summary: Paul commends himself as a servant of God through endurance in afflictions, spiritual virtues, and paradoxical experiences of weakness and strength, demonstrating the authenticity of his ministry despite opposition. He presents a catalog of hardships and character qualities that validate his apostolic authority.

47:6.title: Appeal for Openness and Reciprocal Love
47:6.summary: Paul expresses his open heart toward the Corinthians and appeals to them to reciprocate his affection and openness, speaking to them as beloved children. He invites them to move beyond their own emotional restrictions toward fuller fellowship.

47:6.title: Command Against Unequal Yoking with Unbelievers
47:6.summary: Paul forbids believers from being unequally yoked with unbelievers, presenting a series of contrasts between righteousness and wickedness, light and darkness, Christ and Belial. He emphasizes that believers are God's temple and promises God's covenant presence to those who separate themselves from idolatry.

47:7.title: Call to Cleanse Ourselves from Defilement
47:7.summary: Paul exhorts the Corinthians to cleanse themselves from all spiritual and physical impurity, perfecting their holiness in reverent fear of God. This follows from the promises of God's presence and relationship with His people.

47:7.title: Paul's Defense of His Integrity and Affection
47:7.summary: Paul appeals to the Corinthians to open their hearts to him, asserting his integrity—that he wronged, corrupted, or exploited no one—and expressing his deep affection for them. Despite afflictions, he is filled with comfort and joy because of his confidence in them.

47:7.title: Comfort Through Titus's Arrival
47:7.summary: Paul describes the anxiety he experienced in Macedonia—outward conflict and inward fear—but rejoices that God comforted him through Titus's arrival, who brought news of the Corinthians' longing, grief, and zeal for Paul. This report multiplied his joy.

47:7.title: Godly Sorrow Leading to Repentance
47:7.summary: Paul explains that though his stern letter caused sorrow, he does not regret it because the Corinthians' sorrow was godly and led to repentance rather than destruction. Their repentance produced earnest care, self-defense, righteous indignation, fear, longing, zeal, and justice—proving their purity in the matter.

47:7.title: Vindication of Paul's Apostolic Ministry and the Corinthians' Faithfulness
47:7.summary: Paul clarifies that his letter was written to demonstrate the Corinthians' earnest care and vindicate them before God, not to address the wrongdoer alone. He rejoices in the comfort that came from their response and from Titus's affirmation of their obedience, fear, and trembling reception of him, expressing his full confidence in them.

47:8.title: The Macedonian Churches' Generous Example
47:8.summary: Paul describes how the churches of Macedonia, despite severe affliction and poverty, gave abundantly and cheerfully to support other believers. Their generosity was so remarkable that they exceeded their means and gave themselves first to the Lord, demonstrating genuine Christian commitment.

47:8.title: The Exhortation to Complete the Collection
47:8.summary: Paul urges the Corinthians to complete their own charitable giving, reminding them that Christ's self-emptying poverty made spiritual riches available to them. He appeals not by command but by appealing to their sincerity and love, using Christ's example as the ultimate model of generous sacrifice.

47:8.title: Completing the Gift According to Ability
47:8.summary: Paul judges it right for the Corinthians to complete what they began a year prior, giving according to their means rather than beyond them. What matters is not the amount but the willing readiness and ability to give from what one possesses.

47:8.title: Mutual Support and Economic Equality
47:8.summary: Paul explains that the collection is not meant to burden the Corinthians unfairly but to create mutual aid and equality among churches—as the wealthy help the poor now, so the formerly poor may help them in future need. He appeals to the Old Testament principle of fair distribution shown in the manna story.

47:8.title: Commendation of the Collection Messengers
47:8.summary: Paul praises Titus for his willingness to carry the collection and commends the unnamed brother chosen by the churches to accompany him. These messengers have been selected for their trustworthiness and reputation to ensure the collection's integrity and to glorify the Lord.

47:8.title: Maintaining Integrity in Financial Administration
47:8.summary: Paul emphasizes the importance of handling the collection transparently to avoid criticism and to maintain good conduct before both God and people. He commends the messengers and calls on the Corinthians to demonstrate their love and vindicate Paul's confidence in them before the assembled churches.

47:9.title: Paul's Appeal for Readiness in Giving
47:9.summary: Paul explains that he is sending brothers ahead to ensure the Corinthians have prepared their promised generous gift, so that their reputation for willingness to give will not be undermined by unpreparedness when Macedonian delegates arrive.

47:9.title: Sowing and Reaping; Cheerful Giving
47:9.summary: Paul teaches that generous giving produces generous returns, and emphasizes that God values gifts given willingly from the heart rather than those given reluctantly or under pressure.

47:9.title: God's Grace Enables Abundant Giving
47:9.summary: Paul assures the Corinthians that God's grace will abundantly supply all they need and enable them to overflow in good works, ultimately enriching them so they can give liberally in all circumstances.

47:9.title: The Results and Impact of Charitable Giving
47:9.summary: Paul explains that their generous giving not only meets material needs among the saints but also produces thanksgiving to God and strengthens spiritual bonds, as recipients pray for the givers and recognize God's grace working through them.

47:9.title: Doxology: Thanksgiving for Christ
47:9.summary: Paul concludes with praise to God for his indescribable gift, culminating the discussion of giving by pointing to Christ as the supreme gift.

47:10.title: Paul's Appeal for Understanding
47:10.summary: Paul appeals to the Corinthians by Christ's humility and gentleness, acknowledging that he appears weak in person but bold in letters, and requests that his boldness not be misconstrued as arrogance when he addresses certain critics.

47:10.title: Spiritual Warfare and Divine Power
