66:14.summary: Another angel with a sharp sickle comes from the temple, and an angel with power over fire commands him to gather the earth's grapes into God's winepress. The angel gathers the vintage and throws it into the winepress of God's wrath, resulting in a massive outpouring of judgment.

66:15.title: The Sign of Seven Angels and the Sea of Glass
66:15.summary: John sees a great sign in heaven: seven angels with the seven last plagues representing God's completed wrath, and the victorious saints standing on a sea of glass mixed with fire, holding harps of God.

66:15.title: The Song of Moses and the Lamb
66:15.summary: The victorious saints sing a hymn combining the song of Moses with worship of the Lamb, praising God's greatness, righteousness, and holiness, and declaring that all nations will come to worship Him.

66:15.title: The Release of the Seven Plagues
66:15.summary: The heavenly temple opens and the seven angels emerge clothed in righteousness to receive bowls of God's wrath from one of the living creatures; the temple fills with smoke from God's glory, making it inaccessible until the plagues are complete.

66:16.title: The Command to Pour Out the Seven Bowls
66:16.summary: A loud voice from the temple commands seven angels to pour out the bowls of God's wrath upon the earth, initiating the final series of apocalyptic judgments.

66:16.title: The First Three Bowls and Divine Justice Affirmed
66:16.summary: The first angel causes painful sores on beast-worshippers; the second turns the sea to blood killing all life; the third pollutes all fresh water. The angel of waters and the altar voice affirm God's righteous judgment in response to the shedding of saints' blood.

66:16.title: The Fourth and Fifth Bowls: Heat and Darkness
66:16.summary: The fourth bowl scorches people with intense heat from the sun, and the fifth bowl plunges the beast's kingdom into darkness with painful sores, yet people blaspheme God and refuse to repent despite these overwhelming judgments.

66:16.title: The Sixth Bowl: Preparation for Armageddon
66:16.summary: The sixth bowl dries up the Euphrates to prepare the way for kings from the east, while demonic spirits like frogs emerge from the dragon, beast, and false prophet to gather the world's kings for battle at Megiddo.

66:16.title: The Seventh Bowl: The End of All Things
66:16.summary: The seventh bowl is poured into the air as a voice declares 'It is done!' Catastrophic earthquakes destroy cities, Babylon is judged, islands and mountains vanish, and massive hailstones kill multitudes as people continue to blaspheme God.

66:17.title: Vision of the Great Prostitute and the Scarlet Beast
66:17.summary: An angel summons John to witness the judgment of the great prostitute Babylon, who has corrupted the kings and peoples of the earth. John sees a woman dressed in luxury sitting on a scarlet beast, holding a cup of abominations, marked with the name identifying her as the mother of prostitutes, and he observes that she is drunk with the blood of the saints and martyrs of Jesus.

66:17.title: Interpretation of the Beast's Identity and the Final Victory
66:17.summary: The angel explains the mystery: the beast represents a kingdom that was, is not, and will emerge from the abyss to destruction, with its seven heads representing seven kings (five fallen, one present, one yet to come) and its ten horns representing ten kings who briefly unite to war against the Lamb. The Lamb will ultimately overcome them, and those faithful to him will also overcome.

66:17.title: Destruction of the Prostitute and God's Sovereign Purpose
66:17.summary: The angel reveals that the waters where the prostitute sits represent peoples and nations, and explains that the ten horns and beast will hate and destroy her, making her desolate and burning her with fire—all according to God's purpose to accomplish his will until his words are fulfilled. The woman is identified as the great city that ruled over the kings of the earth.

66:18.title: The Angel's Proclamation of Babylon's Fall
66:18.summary: A powerful angel descends from heaven and announces that Babylon the great has fallen, becoming a demonic stronghold. The city's destruction is presented as judgment for her sexual immorality and corruption of the nations through luxury and wine.

66:18.title: Call to Separation and Swift Judgment
66:18.summary: A heavenly voice calls God's people to flee Babylon and escape her coming plagues, which will arrive suddenly in a single day through death, famine, and fire. God's judgment is presented as just recompense for her pride and presumption of security.

66:18.title: Lament of the Kings, Merchants, and Seafarers
66:18.summary: Earthly rulers, merchants, and seafarers who profited from Babylon's wealth weep and mourn from a distance as they witness her complete destruction in a single hour, bewailing the loss of their source of riches and luxury goods.

66:18.title: Heaven's Rejoicing and Babylon's Complete Desolation
66:18.summary: Heaven and the saints are called to rejoice over God's judgment upon Babylon, as a mighty angel symbolically casts the city down like a millstone into the sea, and all signs of her culture, commerce, and life are permanently silenced. Her destruction is justified by her persecution of the prophets and saints.

66:19.title: The Heavenly Multitude Celebrates God's Judgment
66:19.summary: A great multitude in heaven praises God with Hallelujahs, celebrating His righteous judgment against the great prostitute (Babylon) and the vindication of His servants' blood. The twenty-four elders and four living creatures join in worship, affirming God's justice and majesty.

66:19.title: The Marriage of the Lamb and the Beatitude of the Guests
66:19.summary: A voice from the throne calls all God's servants to praise Him, and the multitude rejoices at the arrival of the Lamb's marriage and the readiness of His bride, clothed in righteous acts. John is instructed to record a beatitude blessing those invited to the marriage supper, and when he attempts to worship an angel, he is corrected to worship God alone, emphasizing that testimony of Jesus is the essence of prophecy.

66:19.title: The Vision of the Rider on the White Horse
66:19.summary: John sees heaven opened and beholds a figure called Faithful and True riding a white horse, with eyes of fire, many crowns, and a name known only to Himself, clothed in blood-sprinkled garments and identified as the Word of God. He judges and wages war in righteousness, with armies following Him, wielding a sharp sword to strike nations and rule with an iron rod, bearing the title 'King of Kings and Lord of Lords.'

66:19.title: The Battle of Armageddon and the Defeat of the Beast
66:19.summary: An angel calls the birds of heaven to God's great supper to consume the flesh of those slain in battle. The beast, false prophet, and kings of the earth gather to war against the Rider and His army, but the beast and false prophet are captured and cast alive into the lake of fire, while the rest are killed by the Rider's sword and consumed by the birds.

66:20.title: The Binding of Satan
66:20.summary: An angel from heaven seizes the dragon (Satan) with a great chain and imprisons him in the abyss for a thousand years, preventing him from deceiving the nations until his temporary release.

66:20.title: The Reign of the Saints and the First Resurrection
66:20.summary: John sees thrones with those seated on them judging, and witnesses the souls of martyrs and those who refused to worship the beast come to life and reign with Christ for a thousand years, blessed and freed from the second death.

66:20.title: Satan's Final Rebellion and Defeat
66:20.summary: After the thousand years, Satan is released and deceives the nations of the four corners of the earth (Gog and Magog), gathering them for war against God's people, but divine fire consumes them and Satan is cast into the lake of fire forever.

66:20.title: The Final Judgment at the Great White Throne
66:20.summary: John sees a great white throne with the Judge seated upon it before whom all the dead stand to be judged according to their works, with those not found in the book of life cast into the lake of fire.

66:21.title: The New Heaven and Earth, and God's Dwelling with His People
66:21.summary: John witnesses the creation of a new heaven and earth with no sea, and sees the holy city New Jerusalem descending from God. God announces that He will dwell with humanity forever, wiping away all tears and ending death, mourning, crying, and pain.

66:21.title: The Promise to Overcomers and Warning to the Unbelieving
66:21.summary: Christ declares He is making all things new and offers the water of life freely to the thirsty; those who overcome will become His children and inherit these blessings. However, the cowardly, unbelieving, and those who practice sin will be cast into the lake of fire, the second death.

66:21.title: The Vision of the Holy City's Structure and Gates
66:21.summary: An angel invites John to see the Lamb's bride, the New Jerusalem, and shows him the city descending from heaven with a great wall, twelve gates inscribed with the names of Israel's tribes, and twelve foundations bearing the names of the twelve apostles.
themes: unity of old and new covenants

66:21.title: The Measurements and Materials of the Holy City
66:21.summary: The angel measures the city with a golden reed, revealing it is perfectly cubic (12,000 stadia in each dimension) with walls of jasper and streets of pure gold like glass. The city's twelve foundations are adorned with precious stones, and its twelve gates are each made of a single pearl.

66:21.title: The Glory and Function of the Heavenly City
66:21.summary: The city needs no temple because God and the Lamb themselves are its temple, and they illuminate it with divine glory, eliminating the need for sun or moon. The nations and kings bring their glory into the city through its always-open gates, while only those whose names are written in the Lamb's book of life may enter.

66:22.title: The Vision of the New Jerusalem's Eternal Glory
66:22.summary: John sees the river of life flowing from God's throne with the tree of life bearing perpetual fruit for healing, where God's servants will see His face in eternal light and reign forever without curse or night. This depicts the ultimate restoration and perfect communion between God and His redeemed people.

66:22.title: The Angel Confirms the Prophecy and Rejects Worship
66:22.summary: The angel assures John that the prophecy is faithful and true, coming soon, and when John attempts to worship him, the angel corrects him by identifying himself as a fellow servant and directing worship to God alone. This emphasizes the authority and reliability of the revelation while reinforcing proper worship.

66:22.title: Instructions Not to Seal the Prophecy and Christ's Promise
66:22.summary: John is commanded not to seal the prophecy because the time is near, and he is told that people's moral conditions will be finalized as they persist in their ways, while Jesus proclaims His imminent return with His reward and identifies Himself as the Alpha and Omega. This underscores the urgency of the message and Christ's eternal divine nature.

66:22.title: Blessings for the Righteous and Jesus's Final Testimony
66:22.summary: Those who keep God's commandments are blessed with access to the tree of life and the eternal city, while the wicked and unrighteous are excluded; Jesus testifies that He has sent this message to the churches and identifies Himself as the root and offspring of David and the Bright Morning Star. This presents the final call to righteousness and Jesus's comprehensive identity.

66:22.title: The Final Invitation and Warning Against Altering Scripture
66:22.summary: The Spirit and the Bride invite all who thirst to freely receive the water of life, followed by a solemn warning that adding to or removing from the prophecy's words will result in divine judgment through plagues and exclusion from the tree of life. This emphasizes the open invitation to salvation alongside the sanctity and immutability of God's Word.

66:22.title: Final Affirmation and Benediction
66:22.summary: Jesus affirms His imminent return with "Amen," and John responds with the prayer "Come, Lord Jesus," concluding the revelation with a benediction of grace from the Lord Jesus to all saints. This closes the entire book with hope, longing, and benediction.
